**Step 1 — Define the chirp structure.**
A linear-FMCW chirp has instantaneous frequency varying linearly across sweep time $T_c$:

$$
f(t) \;=\; f_0 \;+\; \frac{B}{T_c}\, t \quad \text{for} \;\; 0 \le t \le T_c
$$

**Step 2 — Express the transmit (TX) and echo (RX) phases.**
The transmitted chirp starts at $t = 0$. The echo from a target at range $R$ arrives after round-trip delay $\tau$:

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{2R}{c}
$$

The received echo is a delayed, attenuated replica:

$$
f_{rx}(t) \;=\; f_0 \;+\; \frac{B}{T_c}(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the beat (IF) frequency.**
Mixing multiplies the two chirps; after low-pass filtering we keep the difference, which is a constant-frequency tone:

$$
f_b(t) \;=\; f_{tx}(t) - f_{rx}(t) \;=\; \frac{B}{T_c}\, \tau
$$

Substituting $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$
f_b \;=\; \frac{2 R B}{c\, T_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Rayleigh resolution criterion.**
Two targets separated in range by $\Delta R$ produce two beat tones differing by:

$$
\Delta f_b \;=\; \frac{2 B}{c\, T_c}\, \Delta R
$$

The FFT can resolve tones separated by at least $\Delta f_b \ge 1/T_c$ (one bin per sweep duration):

$$
\frac{2 B}{c\, T_c}\, \Delta R \;\ge\; \frac{1}{T_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Delta R$.**
The factor $T_c$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$
\Delta R \;\ge\; \frac{c}{2B}
$$

Hence the **range resolution** is:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta R \;=\; \frac{c}{2B}\;}
$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:** For $B = 4$ GHz and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$
\Delta R \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 4 \times 10^9} \;=\; 0.0375 \text{ m} \;=\; 3.75 \text{ cm}
$$

This is why modern 77 GHz automotive radars can distinguish two vehicles even when parked bumper-to-bumper with only **~4 cm gap**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Doppler Velocity from FMCW Beat Frequency

**Statement:** Show that for an FMCW chirp, the moving-target beat frequency is $f_b = S\tau + f_D$, leading to a coupling that must be resolved via 2-D FFT.

**Step 1 — Add a radial velocity term to the round-trip delay.**
For a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ *toward* the radar, the time-varying range is:

$$
R(t) \;=\; R_0 \;-\; v_r\, t
$$

Therefore the instantaneous round-trip delay is:

$$
\tau(t) \;=\; \frac{2(R_0 - v_r t)}{c} \;=\; \frac{2R_0}{c} \;-\; \frac{2 v_r t}{c}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous beat frequency.**
Differentiating the delay's effect on the chirp's instantaneous frequency difference:

$$
f_b(t) \;=\; S\, \tau(t) \;+\; f_D
$$

where $S = B/T_c$ is the chirp slope and $f_D = 2 v_r / \lambda$ is the classical Doppler frequency:

$$
f_b(t) \;=\; \frac{2 B R_0}{c\, T_c} \;-\; \frac{2 B v_r t}{c\, T_c} \;+\; \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Observe the joint Range-Doppler coupling.**
The first term is the **range beat**, the second is a small **Doppler-induced frequency drift within a single chirp**, and the third is the classical Doppler shift. When $T_c \ll 1/f_D$, the range-Doppler coupling term is negligible, and:

$$
f_b \;\approx\; \underbrace{\frac{2 B R_0}{c\, T_c}}_{\text{range beat}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}}_{\text{Doppler beat}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 2-D FFT to decouple.**
A **Fast-Time FFT** across samples within one chirp extracts the range beat. A **Slow-Time FFT** across $N_c$ consecutive chirps (at sweep repetition interval $T_{\text{PRI}}$) extracts the Doppler beat because the range beat remains constant while the phase of $f_D$ accumulates at $\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_D T_{\text{PRI}}$ per chirp.

**Final coupled formula used in 2-D FFT:**

$$
\boxed{\;f_{b,\text{fast}}(R) \;=\; \frac{2 B R}{c\, T_c}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{D,\text{slow}} \;=\; \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Maximum Unambiguous Range

**Statement:** Show that the maximum unambiguous range of an FMCW radar is $R_{\max} = c \cdot T_{\text{PRI}} / 2$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the round-trip constraint.**
A target must complete its echo return **before the next chirp begins**, otherwise the echo is mis-assigned to the *next* chirp's fast-time window:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; T_{\text{PRI}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau = 2R/c$:**

$$
\frac{2 R_{\max}}{c} \;=\; T_{\text{PRI}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_{\max}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;R_{\max} \;=\; \frac{c\, T_{\text{PRI}}}{2}\;}
$$

For $T_{\text{PRI}} = 40\;\mu\text{s}$:

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \;=\; 6000 \text{ m} \;=\; 6 \text{ km}
$$

In practice, **signal-processing SNR**, **antenna gain** and **regulatory EIRP limits** clip $R_{\max}$ to 150–300 m for LRR units.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Angular Resolution of a Uniform Linear Array (ULA)

**Statement:** Derive the azimuth resolution of a radar with $N_a$ receive antennas uniformly spaced by $d$.

**Step 1 — Define the array geometry.**
The phase difference between adjacent elements for a far-field wave arriving at angle $\theta$ off broadside is:

$$
\Delta\phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi d \sin\theta}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the array steering vector.**
Across $N_a$ elements, the spatial frequency is:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{2\pi d \sin\theta}{\lambda}
$$

The first-null beamwidth of a uniform array of length $L = (N_a - 1)d$ is:

$$
\theta_{\text{null-null}} \;=\; \frac{2\lambda}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the half-power (3 dB) Rayleigh criterion.**
The 3-dB beamwidth is approximately 0.886 times the null-null width for a uniformly-weighted aperture:

$$
\Delta\theta \;\approx\; \frac{0.886\,\lambda}{N_a d \cos\theta}
$$

For a broadside target ($\theta = 0$):

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\theta \;\approx\; \frac{0.886\,\lambda}{N_a d}\;}
$$

**Numerical Example:** With $N_a = 8$, $d = \lambda/2$, $\lambda = 3.9$ mm:

$$
\Delta\theta \;\approx\; \frac{0.886 \times 3.9}{8 \times 1.95} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;\approx\; 12.7^{\circ}
$$

This motivates the use of **MIMO virtual arrays** ($N_t \times N_r$) to enlarge the effective aperture without increasing physical antenna count.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Radar Parameters vs ADAS Function

| ADAS Function | Required Range | Required $\Delta R$ | Required $v_{\text{res}}$ | Preferred Radar Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) | 0–250 m | ~1 m | 0.5 m/s | LRR 77 GHz |
| Forward Collision Warning (FCW) | 0–150 m | ~0.5 m | 0.3 m/s | LRR/MRR |
| Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) | 0–80 m | ~0.3 m | 0.2 m/s | MRR 76–77 GHz |
| Blind Spot Detection (BSD) | 0–30 m | ~0.2 m | 0.5 m/s | SRR 77 GHz corner |
| Lane Change Assist (LCA) | 0–70 m | ~0.5 m | 0.5 m/s | SRR/MRR corner |
| Rear Cross-Traffic Alert (RCTA) | 0–50 m | ~0.3 m | 0.5 m/s | SRR corner |
| Parking / Park-Assist | 0.1–10 m | ~0.05 m | 0.1 m/s | URR 77–81 GHz |
| Traffic Jam Assist | 0–80 m | ~0.5 m | 0.2 m/s | MRR front + corner |
| Vulnerable Road User (VRU) Detection | 0–60 m | ~0.1 m | 0.3 m/s | MRR 77 GHz + camera |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Automotive Radar Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VCO Chirp Generator] --> B[Power Amplifier TX]
    B --> C[TX Antenna Array]
    C --> D[Free Space Propagation]
    D --> E[Target Object Car Pedestrian]
    E --> D
    D --> F[RX Antenna Array MIMO]
    F --> G[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    G --> H[Mixer with LO Reference]
    A --> H
    H --> I[IF Amplifier and LPF]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    J --> K[Range FFT]
    K --> L[Doppler FFT]
    L --> M[Angle FFT CFAR]
    M --> N[Point Cloud Output]
    N --> O[Object Tracking Kalman Filter]
    O --> P[ADAS Function ACC AEB BSD]
```

## 4.2 Mapped Applications of Automotive Radar Around the Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Vehicle Top View]
    V --> F[Front Bumper LRR 77GHz]
    V --> B1[Front Left Corner SRR 77GHz]
    V --> B2[Front Right Corner SRR 77GHz]
    V --> R1[Rear Left Corner SRR 77GHz]
    V --> R2[Rear Right Corner SRR 77GHz]
    V --> RC[Rear Centre MRR 77GHz]

    F -->|0 to 250m| ACC[Adaptive Cruise Control]
    F -->|0 to 150m| FCW[Forward Collision Warning]
    F -->|0 to 80m| AEB[Automatic Emergency Braking]
    F -->|0 to 80m| TJA[Traffic Jam Assist]

    B1 --> LCA1[Lane Change Assist Left]
    B2 --> LCA2[Lane Change Assist Right]
    B1 --> BSD1[Blind Spot Detection Left]
    B2 --> BSD2[Blind Spot Detection Right]

    R1 --> RCTA1[Rear Cross Traffic Alert Left]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Radar Limitations] --> W[Environmental]

    W --> W1[Heavy Rain Attenuation 1 dBkm]

    W --> W3[Road Salt Clutter]
    W --> W4[Tunnel and Overpass Multipath]

    T --> T1[Small Object Detection Pedestrian RCS 0dBsm]

    I --> I1[Mounting Misalignment Calibration Drift]

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Sample]`
**List any three automotive ADAS functions that primarily rely on 77 GHz FMCW radar and justify why radar is preferred over a camera for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — requires **direct radial-velocity measurement** of the preceding vehicle. Radar measures $v_r$ via Doppler at frame rates up to **20 Hz**, whereas a camera must estimate $v_r$ from optical flow (error-prone at night, in glare, or when features are sparse). *(1 mark)*
2. **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** — operates in **all weather, all lighting** (fog, tunnel, oncoming headlights). Radar's 77 GHz mmWave suffers negligible attenuation (~**1 dB/km** in fog), while cameras suffer complete degradation. *(1 mark)*
3. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — corner SRR units must detect motorcycles (RCS ~**5–10 dBsm**) in adjacent lanes even when the B-pillar physically blocks the camera's view. Radar's radio waves penetrate the B-pillar geometry. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Sample]`
**Explain the meaning and significance of "radar cross-section" (RCS). State typical RCS values in dBsm for a pedestrian, a bicycle, and a heavy truck.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, $\sigma$)** is the equivalent *projected area* of a target that intercepts the transmitted electromagnetic wave and re-radiates it isotropically back toward the radar, expressed in **m²** or logarithmically in **dBsm** where $\sigma_{\text{dBsm}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma / 1\text{ m}^2)$. *(1 mark)*

RCS depends on the target's geometry, material, and aspect angle relative to the radar line of sight.

Typical values:

| Target | RCS (m²) | RCS (dBsm) |
|---|---|---|
| Pedestrian | ~ 0.5 – 1.0 | **−3 to 0 dBsm** *(1 mark)* |
| Bicycle | ~ 1 – 3 | **0 to +5 dBsm** *(0.5 mark)* |
| Heavy Truck (rear) | ~ 200 – 1500 | **+23 to +32 dBsm** *(0.5 mark)* |

RCS directly affects received power via $P_r \propto \sigma$, so **pedestrian detection requires much higher SNR** than truck detection — driving up radar hardware sensitivity requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A — `[KTU ESE Dec 2023 Module-2 Sample]`
**(a)** Derive the radar range equation and explain each term. Compute the maximum detectable range of a 77 GHz automotive radar given: $P_t = 10$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 20$ dBi, $\sigma = 10$ dBsm, $\lambda = 3.9$ mm, $L_s = 6$ dB, and minimum detectable SNR $\text{SNR}_{\min} = 13$ dB at the ADC input with $kT_0 = −204$ dBW/Hz and receiver bandwidth $B_{\text{noise}} = 10$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four limitations** of automotive radar with respect to vulnerable road user (VRU) detection in an urban ADAS scenario, and explain how **sensor fusion with camera and LiDAR** mitigates each limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Radar range equation (derivation).**
The Friis transmission equation gives the power captured by a receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ in the far field of a transmitting antenna of gain $G_t$ at distance $R$:

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{4\pi R^2} \cdot A_e
$$

Using $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$:

$$
P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

Including system losses $L_s$:

$$
\boxed{\;P_r \;=\; \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L_s}\;}
$$

*(2 marks for derivation + 1 mark for term explanation)*

**Term Explanations:**
- $P_t$ — peak/average transmit power; limited by **EIRP regulation** (typically **55 dBm** max at 77 GHz).
- $G_t, G_r$ — antenna gains; set by aperture size and beamwidth.
- $\lambda^2$ — favouring **higher frequency (77 GHz)** yields smaller $\lambda$, but the 4th-power $R$ term still dominates.
- $\sigma$ — target RCS, in m².
- $R^4$ — bidirectional path loss; the most critical term.
- $L_s$ — total ohmic + atmospheric + miscalibration losses. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Convert all parameters to linear scale.**
- $P_t = 10$ dBm $= 10$ mW $= 0.01$ W.
- $G_t = G_r = 20$ dBi $= 100$ (linear).
- $\sigma = 10$ dBsm $= 10$ m².
- $L_s = 6$ dB $\approx 3.98$ (linear).
- $\text{SNR}_{\min} = 13$ dB $\approx 19.95$.
- $kT_0 B_{\text{noise}} = 10^{(-204/10)} \times 10^4 = 3.98 \times 10^{-17}$ W.

**Step 3 — Noise floor.**

$$
N \;=\; k T_0 B_{\text{noise}} \;=\; 3.98 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Required received power.**

$$
P_{r,\min} \;=\; \text{SNR}_{\min} \times N \;=\; 19.95 \times 3.98 \times 10^{-17} \;\approx\; 7.94 \times 10^{-16} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the range equation for $R_{\max}$.**

$$
R_{\max} \;=\; \left[\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 L_s P_{r,\min}}\right]^{1/4}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.01 \times 100 \times 100 \times (3.9 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10 \;\approx\; 0.01 \times 10^{4} \times 1.521 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \;\approx\; 1.521 \times 10^{-1}
$$

Denominator:

$$
(4\pi)^3 \times 3.98 \times 7.94 \times 10^{-16} \;\approx\; 1984 \times 3.16 \times 10^{-15} \;\approx\; 6.27 \times 10^{-12}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{1.521 \times 10^{-1}}{6.27 \times 10^{-12}} \;\approx\; 2.43 \times 10^{10}
$$

Fourth root:

$$
\boxed{\;R_{\max} \;\approx\; 125 \text{ m}\;}
$$

*(2 marks for numerical computation)*

This is consistent with a **Mid-Range Radar (MRR)** specification.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (b)

**Limitation 1 — Small RCS of pedestrians ($\sigma \approx −3$ dBsm).**
Pure radar alone yields low SNR for pedestrians at 50 m+. **Fusion mitigation:** camera provides **classification** (CNN-based pedestrian detector) which the radar then validates via **Doppler consistency** before AEB triggers. *(2 marks)*

**Limitation 2 — Radar cannot classify object type.**
A radar point cloud of, say, 4 points could be a pedestrian, a traffic cone, or a stray animal. **Fusion mitigation:** camera-YOLO network provides **semantic class**; radar point provides **3-D location + velocity**; combined into a **tracked fused object**. *(2 marks)*

**Limitation 3 — Multipath in urban canyons (false echoes from walls).**
Radar generates spurious detections that can trigger phantom AEB events. **Fusion mitigation:** LiDAR's narrow beam rejects wall multipath; camera object detector rejects radar-only false alarms via **geometric consistency check**. *(1.5 marks)*

**Limitation 4 — Radar is blind to visual cues (red traffic light, lane markings).**
A pedestrian may be *legally* crossing on a green signal — radar alone cannot stop a vehicle at a traffic light. **Fusion mitigation:** camera reads **signal phase and timing (SPaT)**, providing semantic context that radar's kinematics alone cannot supply. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU ESE May 2024 Module-2 Sample]`
**(a)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, explain the signal-processing chain of a 77 GHz FMCW automotive radar. Show how **range, velocity and angle** are extracted step-by-step, and derive the formulas for range and velocity resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications** of automotive radar in detail, covering at least **six ADAS functions**. For each function, state the radar class (LRR/MRR/SRR), typical detection range, and a real-world vehicle model that uses it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Block diagram description.**
*(3 marks for block diagram with explanation)*

The signal-processing chain is:

**Chirp Generator (VCO) → Power Amplifier → TX Antenna → Free-space propagation → RX Antenna Array → LNA → Mixer (with LO reference from VCO) → IF Amplifier → LPF → ADC → Range-FFT → Doppler-FFT → Angle-FFT → CFAR Detection → Cluster/Tracker → Object List → ADAS ECU.**

Each block is explained as:

1. **VCO** generates a linear-FMCW chirp with slope $S = B/T_c$.
2. **Power amplifier** boosts chirp to ~10 dBm.
3. **TX antenna** radiates the chirp into space; **RX antenna array** captures echoes.
4. **LNA** amplifies weak echoes (noise figure ~10 dB).
5. **Mixer** multiplies RX with LO; produces **IF beat signal** at $f_b = S \cdot 2R/c$.
6. **IF amplifier + LPF** filters out high-frequency products.
7. **ADC** digitises IF at ≥ 20 MHz.
8. **Range-FFT** converts beat frequencies to range bins: $R = f_b c / (2S)$.
9. **Doppler-FFT** across $N_c$ consecutive chirps gives radial velocity: $v_r = f_D \lambda / 2$.
10. **Angle-FFT** across MIMO virtual array gives azimuth $\theta$.
11. **CFAR** adaptively thresholds to declare detections.

**Step 2 — Range resolution derivation** *(already in §3.1)*:
$\Delta R = c/(2B)$. For $B = 4$ GHz, $\Delta R = 3.75$ cm. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Velocity resolution derivation.**
Phase change between consecutive chirps due to Doppler is $\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_D T_{\text{PRI}}$. Across $N_c$ chirps of duration $T_c$ each, the observation window is $T_{\text{obs}} = N_c T_c$. The Doppler-FFT bin width is $1/T_{\text{obs}}$. Setting $f_D \ge 1/(N_c T_c)$:

$$
\frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} \;\ge\; \frac{1}{N_c T_c}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta v \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2 N_c T_c}\;}
$$

For $\lambda = 3.9$ mm, $N_c = 256$, $T_c = 40\;\mu$s:

$$
\Delta v \;=\; \frac{3.9 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 256 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 0.19 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(2.5 marks for derivation + numerical)*

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (b)

Six ADAS functions of automotive radar with detailed comparison:

| ADAS Function | Radar Class | Range (m) | Real Vehicle Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** | LRR | 0–250 | **Tata Harrier, Mahindra XUV700** | Maintains set time-gap to preceding vehicle; uses Doppler to track $v_r$. *(1 mark)* |
| **2. Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** | LRR | 0–150 | **Hyundai Creta 2024 ADAS** | Issues visual + audible warning if TTC < 2.5 s. *(1 mark)* |
| **3. Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** | MRR | 0–80 | **Toyota Fortuner, MG Gloster** | Applies full brake authority if driver ignores FCW. *(1 mark)* |
| **4. Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** | SRR corner | 0–30 | **Kia Seltos, BYD Atto 3** | Corner SRR monitors adjacent lanes; warns during lane-change. *(1 mark)* |
| **5. Rear Cross-Traffic Alert (RCTA)** | SRR corner | 0–50 | **Volvo XC60, Hyundai Verna** | Detects crossing vehicles while reversing out of parking bays. *(1 mark)* |
| **6. Traffic Jam Assist (TJA) / Highway Assist** | MRR front + corner | 0–80 | **Mercedes-Benz E-Class DRIVE PILOT, Honda Accord** | Level-2 longitudinal + lateral control in dense traffic. *(1 mark)* |

**Additional Context (1 mark):**
Beyond these six, **park-assist URR** (ultra-short-range radar at < 10 m, on bumpers) enables automatic perpendicular and parallel parking in vehicles such as the **BMW 7-Series** and **Audi A8**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse LRR with SRR.** LRR operates at 76–77 GHz with ~250 m range for ACC; SRR is *also* 77 GHz but with shorter chirp durations and shorter ranges (~30 m) for BSD. Students often write "SRR uses 24 GHz" — this is now obsolete (24 GHz UWB was discontinued for automotive use in EU/India by 2022).
> - **Always state units.** Writing "$c = 3 \times 10^8$" without "m/s" loses 0.5 marks.
> - **Range equation** requires **derivation, not just statement** for full marks.
> - **Range vs. velocity resolution** are independent — confusing them costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- [ ] Automotive radar uses **77 GHz** (not 24 GHz anymore).
- [ ] Waveform: **linear FMCW chirp** with sweep slope $S = B/T_c$.
- [ ] Beat frequency: $f_b = 2 R B / (c T_c)$.
- [ ] Range resolution: $\boxed{\Delta R = c/(2B)}$ — independent of $T_c$.
- [ ] Doppler frequency: $f_D = 2 v_r / \lambda$.
- [ ] Velocity resolution: $\Delta v = \lambda / (2 N_c T_c)$.
- [ ] Max unambiguous velocity: $v_{\max} = \lambda / (4 T_c)$.
- [ ] Radar equation: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4 L_s]$.
- [ ] SNR scales as $1/R^4$ — **the 4th-power law dominates all link-budget design**.
- [ ] **Six key applications**: ACC, FCW, AEB, BSD, LCA/RCTA, TJA, plus parking URR.
- [ ] **Five key limitations**: small RCS pedestrians, multipath in tunnels, no classification, weather (heavy rain/snow), mutual interference from other vehicles.
- [ ] **Sensor fusion** with camera + LiDAR overcomes radar's classification and resolution weaknesses.
- [ ] **MIMO virtual arrays** (e.g., 3 TX × 4 RX = 12 virtual elements) enhance angular resolution without adding hardware.
- [ ] RCS in **dBsm**: pedestrian ≈ **0 dBsm**, car ≈ **10–20 dBsm**, truck ≈ **25–30 dBsm**.
- [ ] **Regulatory EIRP** at 77 GHz is typically limited to **55 dBm** to avoid interference with neighbouring vehicles.
- [ ] **Cost driver** of modern radars is the **number of TX/RX channels** (MIMO rank) and **compute silicon** (DSP/CNN co-processors).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** is a remote sensing technology that employs pulsed laser light to measure variable distances (ranges) to the Earth's surface or to objects in its field of view. From an Autonomous Driving and ADAS perspective, LiDAR is an active optical perception sensor that emits near-infrared (NIR) laser pulses (~**905 nm** or **1550 nm** wavelength) and precisely measures the round-trip Time of Flight (ToF) of each reflected photon to construct a high-resolution, three-dimensional point cloud of the vehicle's surroundings in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> LiDAR is one of the three primary perception modalities for Level 3+ Autonomous Driving (alongside Camera and Radar). It is the *only* sensor that directly provides centimeter-level accurate 3D geometric depth information independent of ambient illumination.

## 1.2 Core Operating Principle — One-Line Summary

LiDAR determines the distance $R$ to a target by emitting a short laser pulse and measuring the time $\Delta t$ it takes for the pulse to return after reflecting off the target.

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c$ is the speed of light in the propagation medium (approximately $3 \times 10^8$ m/s in air), and the factor of 2 accounts for the two-way (round-trip) propagation path.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bat and the Echo:**
> Imagine you are standing in a dark, foggy tunnel and you shout loudly. Your shout travels, hits the wall, and the echo comes back. By measuring *how long* you had to wait for the echo, you instantly know how far the wall is. If you shout repeatedly while turning your head, you can build a complete "map" of the tunnel.
>
> **LiDAR works exactly this way**, except:
> - Instead of *sound waves*, it uses **pulses of laser light** (which travel a million times faster, so the timing electronics must be extraordinarily precise — picosecond level).
> - Instead of your *ears*, it uses an ultra-sensitive photodetector (typically an Avalanche Photodiode — APD, or a Single-Photon Avalanche Diode — SPAD).
> - Instead of turning your *head*, it uses a **mechanical rotating mirror**, a **MEMS scanner**, or a solid-state Optical Phased Array (OPA) to sweep the beam across the scene.

## 1.4 LiDAR vs. Other ADAS Sensors — At a Glance

| Parameter | Camera (Passive Optical) | Radar (Radio) | LiDAR (Active Optical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active / Passive | Passive | Active | Active |
| Signal Type | Visible/NIR light | Microwave (~77 GHz) | Laser (~905/1550 nm) |
| Range Resolution | Low (stereo depth = poor) | Medium (~10–30 cm) | **High (~2–5 cm)** |
| Works in Fog/Rain | Poor | **Excellent** | Degraded (heavy fog/rain) |
| Works at Night | Poor (needs IR illuminator) | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Object Classification | **Excellent** (texture/color) | Poor | Medium (geometry-based) |
| Cost | Low | Low | **High** (historically) |
| Angular Resolution | High (megapixels) | Low (~1°–2°) | **High (~0.1°–0.03°)** |

## 1.5 Classification of Automotive LiDAR Systems

1. **Mechanical Spinning LiDAR** — e.g., Velodyne HDL-64E. Full 360° coverage, high resolution. Bulk and expensive.
2. **MEMS-based LiDAR** — Micro-Electro-Mechanical mirror steers the beam. Compact, lower cost. Limited FOV per unit.
3. **Flash LiDAR** — Illuminates the entire scene with a single wide laser pulse; uses a focal-plane array (FPA) detector. No moving parts. Short range typically (~50–100 m).
4. **Optical Phased Array (OPA) — Solid-State LiDAR** — No moving parts; electronically steered. Most promising for mass production.
5. **Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) LiDAR** — Measures both range *and* velocity via Doppler shift. Coherent detection.

> [!NOTE]
> The **1550 nm** wavelength (Eye-Safe Class 1) is increasingly preferred for automotive LiDAR over **905 nm** (Silicon-based, cheaper detectors but Class 1 eye-safety limit restricts peak power and hence range).

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Building Blocks of a LiDAR System

Every LiDAR — regardless of architecture — is composed of these five functional blocks:

1. **Laser Transmitter** — Generates short optical pulses (typically 1–10 ns wide) at high Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF), often 100 kHz–1 MHz.
2. **Beam Steering / Scanning Mechanism** — Mechanical (spinning motor + mirror), MEMS, Risley prism, OPA, or Flash (no steering).
3. **Receiver Optics** — Telescope/lens that collects returning photons from the scene and focuses them onto the photodetector.
4. **Photodetector** — Converts returning optical energy into an electrical signal. APDs or SPADs are most common.
5. **Signal Processing & Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC)** — Measures $\Delta t$ with picosecond resolution and assembles the **point cloud** $(x, y, z, \text{intensity})$.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Emission:** The laser diode fires a short, high-peak-power pulse (peak power ~10–100 W, but the average power is low — eye-safe duty cycle < 0.1%).
- **Step 2 — Propagation:** The pulse travels through free space at $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. The beam diverges at an angle $\theta_{div}$ (typically 0.1°–1°).
- **Step 3 — Reflection / Scattering:** Upon striking a target, the pulse is reflected (specular) or scattered (diffuse, Lambertian). For automotive targets (cars, pedestrians, road), diffuse Lambertian scattering dominates.
- **Step 4 — Collection:** Returning photons are gathered by the receive aperture of diameter $D_r$.
- **Step 5 — Detection:** The photodetector produces an electrical pulse whose leading edge marks the arrival time $\Delta t$.
- **Step 6 — Geolocation:** Knowing $\Delta t$, the scanner's current azimuth $\phi$ and elevation $\theta$, the system computes the target's 3D coordinates:
$$
x = R \cos\theta \cos\phi, \quad y = R \cos\theta \sin\phi, \quad z = R \sin\theta
$$
- **Step 7 — Point Cloud Assembly:** Successive pulses and scan positions are aggregated into a dense point cloud (typically 1–10 million points/second for automotive).

## 2.3 Why is Timing Resolution So Critical?

For a target at distance $R = 100$ m, the round-trip time is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 100}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 667~\text{ns}
$$

To achieve a range resolution of $\Delta R = 5$ cm, the required timing resolution $\delta t$ is:

$$
\delta t = \frac{2 \cdot \Delta R}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 333~\text{ps}
$$

This is precisely why **Time-to-Digital Converters (TDCs)** with picosecond resolution are mandatory for LiDAR systems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Range from Time of Flight | m | Core LiDAR equation |
| 2 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \delta t}{2}$ | Range Resolution | m | $\delta t$ = timing jitter |
| 3 | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 R_{\max}}$ | Max Pulse Repetition Frequency (no range ambiguity) | Hz | Ensures next pulse fires after previous echo returns |
| 4 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \cdot \rho \cdot D_r^2 \cdot \eta_{sys}}{4 R^2 \cdot \theta_{div}^2 \cdot \tan^2(\theta_{div}/2)}$ | Received optical power (simplified LiDAR link budget) | W | $\rho$ = target reflectivity |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{N_s}{\sqrt{N_s + N_b + N_d}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dimensionless | $N_s$ = signal photons, $N_b$ = background, $N_d$ = dark counts |
| 6 | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{\theta_{FOV}}{N_{pixels}}$ | Angular Resolution | rad | Determines cross-range resolution |
| 7 | $A_{footprint} = \pi \left(R \tan\dfrac{\theta_{div}}{2}\right)^2$ | Beam footprint area at range $R$ | m² | Determines how "spread out" the pulse is on the target |
| 8 | $\Delta f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Doppler frequency shift (FMCW LiDAR only) | Hz | $v_r$ = radial velocity |
| 9 | $E_{pulse} = P_{peak} \cdot \tau_{pulse}$ | Pulse Energy | J | $\tau$ = pulse width (typically 1–10 ns) |
| 10 | $P_{avg} = E_{pulse} \cdot \text{PRF}$ | Average Optical Power | W | Must satisfy Class 1 eye-safety |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Long-Range Automotive Detection:** LiDAR mounted on the roof of an autonomous vehicle detects vehicles and obstacles up to **200–250 m** away, providing critical reaction time at highway speeds.
- **HD Map Generation:** Survey-grade LiDAR (e.g., Velodyne, Hesai, Leica) is used to pre-map road networks at centimeter accuracy, against which live point clouds are matched via SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Vulnerable Road User (VRU) Protection:** Pedestrian detection at intersections under any lighting condition — critical for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) at night.
- **Free Space Detection:** Distinguishing drivable from non-drivable regions of the road surface — a task where LiDAR's geometric precision excels over cameras.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eye-Safety (IEC 60825-1):** All automotive LiDARs must be **Class 1** (eye-safe under all conditions). This limits either the wavelength (905 nm is restricted in peak power), the pulse energy, or pushes manufacturers toward **1550 nm** (eye-safe at higher powers because the cornea absorbs it before reaching the retina).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Range Equation from Time of Flight

**Statement:** Derive the LiDAR range equation starting from the principle of constant light velocity in free space.

**Step 1:** The pulse travels from the LiDAR to the target along the outward path of length $R$.

$$
\text{Outward travel time} = \frac{R}{c}
$$

**Step 2:** After reflection/scattering, the returning photons travel the *same* distance $R$ back to the receiver.

$$
\text{Return travel time} = \frac{R}{c}
$$

**Step 3:** The total round-trip time is the sum of outward and return travel times.

$$
\Delta t = \frac{R}{c} + \frac{R}{c} = \frac{2R}{c}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for $R$ gives the fundamental LiDAR range equation.

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

This is the most fundamental equation in the entire LiDAR domain — examiners expect you to write it from memory in seconds.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Range Resolution from Timing Jitter

**Statement:** Show that range resolution depends linearly on timing jitter.

**Step 1:** Differentiate the range equation with respect to $\Delta t$.

$$
\frac{dR}{d(\Delta t)} = \frac{c}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Replace the infinitesimal $d$ with finite error increments ($\delta R$ = range error, $\delta t$ = timing jitter).

$$
\delta R = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \delta t
$$

**Step 3:** This means the *smallest distinguishable* range difference is half the speed of light multiplied by the smallest distinguishable time difference.

$$
\Delta R_{min} = \frac{c}{2} \cdot \delta t_{min}
$$

**Numerical Evaluation:** For a typical automotive SPAD-based TDC with $\delta t_{min} = 100$ ps:

$$
\Delta R_{min} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 0.015~\text{m} = 1.5~\text{cm}
$$

This is why modern SPAD-based LiDARs boast centimeter-level depth precision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Unambiguous Range and PRF

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between maximum unambiguous range and maximum pulse repetition frequency.

**Step 1:** Consider the constraint that the next laser pulse must NOT be emitted until the previous pulse has safely returned from the maximum intended range $R_{max}$.

$$
\Delta t_{round-trip,max} = \frac{2 R_{max}}{c}
$$

**Step 2:** The inter-pulse interval $T_{PRF}$ must satisfy $T_{PRF} \geq \Delta t_{round-trip,max}$.

$$
T_{PRF} \geq \frac{2 R_{max}}{c}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\text{PRF} = 1/T_{PRF}$, we obtain:

$$
\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 R_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** For $R_{max} = 250$ m:

$$
\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 250} = 6 \times 10^{5}~\text{Hz} = 600~\text{kHz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This trade-off is fundamental: **higher PRF = denser point cloud = better angular resolution**, BUT it also **caps the maximum range** of the LiDAR. Designers must balance this against the use of multiple pulses per direction (pulse-train encoding).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — LiDAR Link Budget (Received Power)

**Statement:** Derive the simplified received power equation for a pulsed LiDAR.

**Step 1:** The laser emits peak power $P_t$ within a beam divergence angle $\theta_{div}$ (radians).

**Step 2:** At range $R$, the beam illuminates a footprint area $A_{foot}$ on the target:

$$
A_{foot} = \pi \left(R \tan\frac{\theta_{div}}{2}\right)^2 \approx \frac{\pi R^2 \theta_{div}^2}{4} \quad \text{(for small angles)}
$$

**Step 3:** The fraction of optical power intercepted by a Lambertian target of reflectivity $\rho$ is multiplied by an effective bidirectional reflectance factor.

**Step 4:** The reflected radiation scatters into the entire hemisphere. Only a tiny fraction enters the receiver aperture of diameter $D_r$. The solid angle subtended by the aperture at the target is:

$$
\Omega_r = \frac{\pi D_r^2 / 4}{R^2} = \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4 R^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Combining all factors and including system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ (transmitter optics × receiver optics × detector quantum efficiency):

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot \rho \cdot D_r^2 \cdot \eta_{sys} \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{4 R^2 \cdot \theta_{div}^2 \cdot R^2}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the beam's surface-normal and the target's surface-normal. For a head-on target, $\alpha = 0$ and $\cos(\alpha) = 1$.

**Step 6 — The famous "$1/R^4$" law:** Because the beam spreads over $R^2$ on the way out *and* the receiver subtends only $1/R^2$ solid angle on the way back, the received power falls off as:

$$
P_r \propto \frac{1}{R^4}
$$

This is why **doubling the range reduces the received signal by a factor of 16**, dramatically reducing SNR. This is the principal engineering challenge for long-range automotive LiDAR.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Beam Footprint and Cross-Range Resolution

**Statement:** Derive the cross-range (lateral) resolution of a LiDAR at distance $R$.

**Step 1:** A laser pulse with divergence angle $\theta_{div}$ strikes a target at distance $R$, producing an illuminated footprint of diameter $d_{foot}$:

$$
d_{foot} = 2 R \tan\left(\frac{\theta_{div}}{2}\right) \approx R \cdot \theta_{div}
$$

**Step 2:** Two adjacent targets can be distinguished only if their footprints do not overlap. Thus the cross-range resolution $\Delta x_{cross}$ is:

$$
\Delta x_{cross} = R \cdot \theta_{div}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:** For $\theta_{div} = 0.03° = 5.24 \times 10^{-4}$ rad and $R = 200$ m:

$$
\Delta x_{cross} = 200 \times 5.24 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.105~\text{m} = 10.5~\text{cm}
$$

This explains why automotive LiDARs targeting highway use cases demand sub-0.05° angular resolution.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Wavelength

| Property | 905 nm (Silicon-based) | 1550 nm (InGaAs-based) |
|---|---|---|
| Detector Material | Silicon (mature, cheap) | InGaAs (expensive) |
| Solar Background | Higher (closer to peak solar irradiance) | Lower (water absorption peak) |
| Eye Safety Limit | Lower (retina hazard) | **Higher (cornea-absorbing)** |
| Atmospheric Attenuation | Lower (clear air) | Slightly higher (some water vapor absorption) |
| Fiber Laser Compatibility | Poor | **Excellent** (telecom-grade) |
| Range Achievable | ~150–200 m | **>250 m** at equal eye-safety class |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trend in 2024+:** Industry (Luminar, Aeva, Innoviz, Hesai) is rapidly migrating to **1550 nm** for premium long-range sensing, while **905 nm** dominates mass-market Tier-2 suppliers due to silicon CMOS compatibility.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive LiDAR System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal and data flow across all major functional blocks of a pulsed Time-of-Flight LiDAR — from laser emission to point cloud output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller and FPGA] --> B[Laser Diode Driver]
    B --> C[Laser Source 905nm or 1550nm]
    C --> D[Transmit Optics Collimator]
    D --> E[Beam Scanner Mechanical, MEMS, or OPA]
    E --> F[Target Scene Vehicles, Pedestrians, Road]
    F --> G[Receive Optics Telescope Aperture Dr]
    G --> H[Narrow Bandpass Optical Filter]
    H --> I[Photodetector APD or SPAD]
    I --> J[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    J --> K[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    K --> L[Signal Processor DSP or SoC]
    L --> M[Point Cloud Output X, Y, Z, Intensity]
    A -. Timing Trigger .-> K
    A -. Scan Position phi, theta .-> E
    M --> N[Perception Stack Object Detection, SLAM, Tracking]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** represent the optical signal path (top half) and the electrical signal path (bottom half).
- The **dashed arrows** represent *synchronization and metadata* — the scan angle at the moment of firing must be logged alongside the measured ToF, otherwise the point cloud cannot be reconstructed.
- The single most important feedback is the **timing trigger** from the FPGA to the TDC: this defines the $t = 0$ reference for the round-trip measurement.

---

## 4.2 LiDAR Scan Pattern Comparison — Conceptual Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SPIN["Mechanical Spinning"]
        SPIN_A[360 degree horizontal sweep] --> SPIN_B[Multiple laser channels stacked vertically]
        SPIN_B --> SPIN_C[Rich dense point cloud]
    end

    subgraph MEMS["MEMS Mirror Scanning"]
        MEMS_A[Single laser source] --> MEMS_B[2D MEMS mirror raster scan]
        MEMS_B --> MEMS_C[FOV limited to 60 to 120 degrees]
    end

    subgraph FLASH["Flash LiDAR"]
        FLASH_A[Diffuser expands pulse to wide cone] --> FLASH_B[2D Focal Plane Array FPA captures echo]
        FLASH_B --> FLASH_C[Single shot 3D frame, no scanning]
    end

    subgraph OPA["Optical Phased Array OPA Solid State"]
        OPA_A[Grating coupler emits beam] --> OPA_B[Phase shifters steer electronically]
        OPA_B --> OPA_C[No moving parts, mass production friendly]
    end
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Spinning** = best resolution today, worst for automotive-grade reliability.
- **MEMS** = good balance, dominant in mid-range ADAS.
- **Flash** = best for short range (blind-spot, in-cabin monitoring).
- **OPA** = future-proof solid-state direction; still emerging in 2024.

---

## 4.3 LiDAR Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw ADC Stream from Detector] --> AMP[Amplification and Baseline Restoration]
    AMP --> CFD[Constant Fraction Discriminator CFD]
    CFD --> TDC[Time to Digital Conversion ps resolution]
    TDC --> HIST[Histogram Builder for Multi Pulse Accumulation]
    HIST --> PEAK[Peak Detection for Range Bin]
    PEAK --> XYZ[Cartesian Projection using Scan Angles]
    XYZ --> MOTION[Motion Compensation ego vehicle motion correction]
    MOTION --> OUT[Final Registered Point Cloud]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why histograms?** A single laser pulse may return only a few photons. To achieve meter-level precision at 200 m, LiDARs typically fire **multiple pulses per direction** and accumulate them into a histogram. The peak of the histogram marks the true range — a technique called **photon-counting LiDAR**.
