# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define LiDAR. With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a pulsed Time-of-Flight LiDAR used in autonomous vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an active remote sensing technology that determines the distance to a target by measuring the round-trip time of flight of a laser pulse emitted towards the target and reflected back to the sensor.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):** A pulsed laser diode emits short optical pulses (typically 1–10 ns wide at 905 nm or 1550 nm) toward the target scene. The pulses strike objects (vehicles, pedestrians, road surface) and scatter back. A photodetector (APD or SPAD) senses the returning photons. The Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$. The range is computed as:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

Combined with the beam's azimuth and elevation scan angles, the LiDAR processor generates a 3D point cloud $(x, y, z)$ of the surroundings, which is fed to the perception stack for object detection, classification, and tracking.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Compare 905 nm and 1550 nm LiDAR wavelengths with respect to eye safety, detector technology, and maximum achievable range.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 905 nm LiDAR | 1550 nm LiDAR |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye Safety** | Retinal hazard; restricted peak power | Cornea-absorbed; higher permissible power |
| **Detector** | Silicon (cheap, mature) | InGaAs (expensive) |
| **Max Range** | ~150–200 m | **>250 m** |
| **Solar Background** | Higher | Lower |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

**Conclusion:** 905 nm is preferred for cost-sensitive ADAS, while 1550 nm is preferred for premium autonomous driving requiring long range and high eye-safety power budgets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an automotive LiDAR system. Describe the role of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Derive the LiDAR range equation and the maximum unambiguous range equation. A 905 nm LiDAR has a timing resolution of 200 ps. Calculate (i) the range resolution, and (ii) the maximum pulse repetition frequency for $R_{max} = 200$ m. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q(a) — Functional Architecture (7 Marks)

A pulsed ToF automotive LiDAR consists of the following blocks:

**(1) Laser Transmitter (1 Mark):** A laser diode (905 nm silicon-based, or 1550 nm fiber/solid-state) emits short pulses (1–10 ns) at high peak power. The Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) is typically 100 kHz–1 MHz.

**(2) Beam Steering / Scanning Mechanism (1 Mark):** A mechanical rotor, MEMS mirror, or Optical Phased Array directs the beam across the Field of View (FoV). For spinning LiDARs, this achieves 360° horizontal coverage at typically 5–20 Hz rotation.

**(3) Transmit and Receive Optics (1 Mark):** Collimating lenses expand and shape the outgoing beam. The receive telescope (aperture diameter $D_r$) collects returning photons. A narrow-band optical filter rejects solar background.

**(4) Photodetector (1 Mark):** An Avalanche Photodiode (APD) or Single-Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD) converts returning optical photons into an electrical current pulse.

**(5) Signal Processing and TDC (1 Mark):** A Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA) boosts the signal. A Constant Fraction Discriminator (CFD) extracts a precise timing mark. The Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) measures $\Delta t$ with picosecond resolution.

**(6) Host Processor (1 Mark):** An FPGA or SoC computes $(x, y, z)$ for each measurement using the range and current scan angles, then assembles the point cloud for downstream perception.

**(7) Synchronization (1 Mark):** The FPGA must synchronize laser emission, scan angle, and TDC measurement. A single shared clock domain is mandatory; otherwise, point cloud geometry is corrupted.

---

### Model Solution to Q(b) — Derivations & Numerical Problems (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Range Equation (2 Marks):**

Let $R$ be the one-way distance to the target. The laser pulse travels outward in time $R/c$ and returns in another $R/c$. The total round-trip time $\Delta t$ is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c} \implies R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Range Equation (1 Mark):** To avoid range ambiguity, the next pulse can be emitted only after the previous echo has returned from $R_{max}$:

$$
T_{PRF} \geq \frac{2 R_{max}}{c} \implies \text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{c}{2 R_{max}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 Marks):**

**(i) Range Resolution** for $\delta t = 200$ ps:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \cdot \delta t}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 0.03~\text{m} = 3~\text{cm}
$$

**(ii) Maximum PRF** for $R_{max} = 200$ m:

$$
\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{c}{2 R_{max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 200} = 7.5 \times 10^{5}~\text{Hz} = 750~\text{kHz}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) Range resolution = **3 cm**, (ii) Maximum PRF = **750 kHz**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q (a)** Explain the principle of operation of an FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) LiDAR. How does it differ from a pulsed ToF LiDAR in terms of velocity measurement capability? **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A 1550 nm LiDAR has a transmit aperture of 25 mm diameter, beam divergence of 0.05°, peak pulse power of 50 W, pulse width 5 ns, and target reflectivity 0.1. Compute the received optical power for a target at $R = 150$ m, assuming the target surface is perpendicular to the beam. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q(a) — FMCW LiDAR (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (4 Marks):** Unlike pulsed LiDAR which fires discrete optical pulses, FMCW LiDAR continuously emits a laser whose optical frequency is linearly swept (chirped) over a bandwidth $B$ (typically tens of GHz). The returning signal is coherently mixed with a local oscillator on a photodetector. The resulting beat frequency $f_b$ is directly proportional to the target range $R$:

$$
f_b = \frac{2 R \cdot S}{c}
$$

where $S$ is the chirp rate (Hz/s). The range is recovered by FFT analysis of the beat signal.

**Velocity Measurement (Doppler) (3 Marks):** When the target moves with radial velocity $v_r$, the returned signal experiences a Doppler frequency shift:

$$
\Delta f_{Doppler} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

By measuring both $f_b$ (range) and $\Delta f_{Doppler}$ (velocity) simultaneously, FMCW LiDAR directly outputs a 4D point cloud $(x, y, z, v_r)$. Pulsed ToF LiDAR requires *multiple* sequential frames and tracking algorithms to infer velocity — FMCW does it in a single measurement, making it inherently robust against interference from other LiDARs.

---

### Model Solution to Q(b) — Link Budget Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D_r = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- $\theta_{div} = 0.05° = 0.05 \times \pi/180 = 8.727 \times 10^{-4}$ rad
- $P_t = 50$ W
- $\tau = 5$ ns (not directly needed for instantaneous power)
- $\rho = 0.1$
- $R = 150$ m

**Step 1 — Pulse Energy (1 Mark):**

$$
E_{pulse} = P_t \cdot \tau = 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 250~\text{nJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Beam Footprint Area at $R = 150$ m (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{foot} = \pi \left( R \tan\frac{\theta_{div}}{2} \right)^2 \approx \frac{\pi R^2 \theta_{div}^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{foot} = \frac{\pi \times (150)^2 \times (8.727 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = 1.347 \times 10^{-2}~\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solid Angle Subtended by Receiver (2 Marks):**

$$
\Omega_r = \frac{\pi D_r^2}{4 R^2} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4 \times (150)^2} = 2.18 \times 10^{-8}~\text{sr}
$$

**Step 4 — Received Power (Assuming $\eta_{sys} = 1$, head-on target) (2 Marks):**

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\Omega_r}{\pi \theta_{div}^2 / 4}
$$

Equivalently, using the simplified $1/R^4$ form:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot \rho \cdot D_r^2}{4 R^2 \cdot \theta_{div}^2 \cdot R^2} = \frac{50 \times 0.1 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times (150)^2 \times (8.727 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (150)^2}
$$

Computing denominator step by step:

$$
4 \times 22500 \times 7.616 \times 10^{-7} \times 22500 \approx 4 \times 22500 \times 0.01714 \approx 1542.4
$$

Numerator:

$$
50 \times 0.1 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = 3.125 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{-3}}{1542.4} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-6}~\text{W} \approx 2.03~\mu\text{W}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_r \approx \mathbf{2.03~\mu W}$ (assuming ideal $\eta_{sys} = 1$). With realistic $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.5$–$0.7$, the actual value will be ~1–1.4 µW.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $R = c \Delta t / 2$ — costs **1 full mark** instantly.
> 2. **Unit conversion trap:** $\theta_{div}$ is given in **degrees** but must be used in **radians** in the formula. A wrong conversion can produce an answer off by a factor of ~3300.
> 3. **Range ambiguity:** Students often forget that $\text{PRF}_{max}$ assumes no overlapping echoes. In multi-target scenarios with pulse-coding, the formula is more complex.
> 4. **Eye-safety:** Always state the LiDAR class (Class 1) when discussing wavelength choice — examiners reward this contextual awareness.
> 5. **Block diagrams:** Always draw **arrow-headed, labelled blocks** — vague boxes with no arrows lose 1–2 marks even if the explanation is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** LiDAR = Light Detection and Ranging. An *active optical* sensor that uses laser pulses and Time-of-Flight for 3D ranging.
- **Master Equation:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ — non-negotiable; must be on your fingertip.
- **Two Key Wavelengths:** **905 nm** (cheap silicon detectors, restricted range due to eye-safety) and **1550 nm** (long range, eye-safe, expensive InGaAs).
- **Five Building Blocks:** Laser → Scanner → Optics → Detector → TDC + Processor. Memorize this sequence.
- **Range Resolution:** $\Delta R = c \cdot \delta t / 2$. For 100 ps jitter → 1.5 cm range resolution.
- **Maximum Unambiguous Range:** $\text{PRF}_{max} = c / (2 R_{max})$. Trade-off between density and reach.
- **The $1/R^4$ Law:** Received power falls off as the **fourth power** of range. This is the dominant engineering challenge for long-range automotive LiDAR.
- **Angular Resolution:** $\theta_{res} = \theta_{FOV} / N_{pixels}$. Determines lateral (cross-range) precision: $\Delta x_{cross} = R \cdot \theta_{div}$.
- **FMCW vs. Pulsed:** Pulsed measures **range only**; FMCW measures **range + velocity** simultaneously via Doppler.
- **Eye Safety:** Class 1 (IEC 60825-1) is mandatory for automotive. 1550 nm is preferred because the cornea absorbs it before the retina is damaged.
- **Scanning Technologies:** Spinning (best resolution), MEMS (mid-range), Flash (short range), OPA (future solid-state).
- **Advantages over Camera:** Works in darkness; provides direct 3D depth without calibration; robust to texture-less targets.
- **Disadvantages vs. Radar:** Degraded in heavy rain/fog/snow; higher cost; lower angular resolution than dense cameras.
- **Standard Performance Targets (2024):** Range > 200 m at 10% reflectivity, angular resolution < 0.05°, point rate > 5 million points/sec, FoV 120° × 25°, Class 1 eye-safe, automotive-grade reliability (AEC-Q102).
- **Key Application Areas:** Object detection, SLAM, free-space detection, HD map generation, pedestrian/Vru safety.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lidar Applications in 3D Mapping and Obstacle Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lidar Applications in 3D Mapping and Obstacle Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** is an active remote sensing modality that employs coherent or incoherent laser pulses in the near-infrared (NIR ~**905 nm** or **1550 nm**) spectrum to determine the range, bearing, and reflectivity of target objects. In the context of **autonomous driving and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**, vehicular LiDAR functions as a **3D Time-of-Flight (ToF) scanner** that emits photon pulses, measures the round-trip echo delay, and reconstructs a dense **3D point cloud** representing the drivable environment. The point cloud is then segmented, clustered, and classified to perform **obstacle detection, road boundary extraction, dynamic object tracking, and HD-map localization** (essential for Levels 3–5 of SAE J3016 autonomy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Pearl:** LiDAR is *not* a camera. It is a ranging sensor that geometrically measures the world. It is *complementary* to cameras (which capture photometric/color information) and RADAR (which measures Doppler velocity but has poor angular resolution).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a blindfolded person standing in a busy parking lot, holding a **laser torch and a stopwatch**:
- They fire the torch (**emit a laser pulse**),
- The light bounces off a car, a curb, and a pedestrian,
- The stopwatch records how long each echo took to return (**time of flight**).
- Multiplying the time by the speed of light and dividing by **2** (because the light had to go *and come back*) gives the distance to each obstacle.
- Now, instead of just pointing straight, imagine the torch slowly rotating in a circle (**azimuth scan**) and tilting up/down (**elevation scan**).
- Thousands of such measurements, each tagged with (distance, angle, angle), assemble into a **3D dotted replica of the lot** — that is the **point cloud**.

That is exactly what a rooftop LiDAR (e.g., Velodyne HDL-64E, Hesai Pandar XT-32, RoboSense RS-LiDAR-M1) does for a self-driving car, at rates of **millions of points per second**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Speed of Light:** Light travels **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**. Going 100 m takes only 0.33 µs. That is why LiDAR electronics must be sub-nanosecond precise — a 1 ns timing error equals **15 cm of range error**.

### 1.3 The Three Foundational Constants in LiDAR Physics

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|-------|------------------|
| Speed of light (vacuum) | c | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | Maximum possible pulse speed |
| Wavelength (typical automotive NIR) | λ | **905 nm** or **1550 nm** | Determines eye-safety and atmospheric loss |
| Beam divergence | θ_div | **~0.03° – 0.5°** | Dictates angular resolution and footprint size |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (Why & How)

The complete LiDAR signal chain is a five-stage process. Each stage is critical to the final point cloud fidelity.

**Stage 1 — Laser Pulse Emission**
- A semiconductor laser diode (GaAs for 905 nm, InGaAs for 1550 nm) is pulsed at peak power levels of **~25–100 W** (peak) for nanosecond durations.
- The pulse width τ determines the **range resolution**: ΔR_min = (c · τ) / 2. A 1 ns pulse ⇒ ΔR = 15 cm. A 4 ns pulse ⇒ ΔR = 60 cm.
- *Why this matters:* Shorter pulses give finer depth resolution but require wider receiver bandwidth and higher peak power to maintain SNR.

**Stage 2 — Beam Steering (Mechanical vs. Solid-State)**
- **Spinning (mechanical) LiDAR:** A motor rotates the laser-receiver stack at 5–20 Hz. Multi-channel designs (16, 32, 64, 128, 128 layers) emit multiple vertical beams simultaneously.
- **MEMS LiDAR:** A micro-mirror deflects the beam in 2D; compact and cheaper.
- **Optical Phased Array (OPA):** Solid-state, no moving parts; uses interference of multiple emitters to steer the beam electronically.
- **Flash LiDAR:** Floods the entire FOV in one wide pulse; uses a focal-plane array (FPA) detector.

**Stage 3 — Detection of the Returned Echo**
- A photodiode (Avalanche Photodiode — APD, or SPAD for single-photon counting) detects the returning photons.
- The detector converts optical energy into a measurable current/voltage pulse, whose leading edge is timestamped.

**Stage 4 — Time-of-Flight (ToF) Measurement**
- The time interval Δt between emission timestamp t_emit and detection timestamp t_recv is measured using a high-precision TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) with picosecond resolution.
- The range R is then computed.

**Stage 5 — Georeferencing and Point Cloud Assembly**
- The range R is converted to 3D Cartesian coordinates using the LiDAR's mounting pose (azimuth φ, elevation θ, height h, roll/pitch/yaw).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU ESE — they are asked verbatim almost every year.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | **Range from Time-of-Flight** | metres (m) |
| 2 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | **Range resolution from pulse width τ** | m |
| 3 | $R_{max}^{4} = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot A_r \cdot \sigma \cdot \eta_{atm}^{2}}{(4 \pi)^{2} \cdot P_{min}}$ | **LiDAR Link / Range Equation** (radar-like form) | m⁴ |
| 4 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_r \cdot \tau}{h \nu \cdot NEP}$ | **Photon-limited Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{\lambda}{D_{aperture}}$ | **Diffraction-limited angular resolution** | radians |
| 6 | $\phi(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \pi S t^{2}$ | **FMCW chirp phase** (for FMCW LiDAR) | radians |
| 7 | $R_{FMCW} = \dfrac{f_b \cdot c}{2 \cdot S}$ | **Range from FMCW beat frequency f_b** | m |
| 8 | $v_{radial} = \dfrac{f_{Doppler} \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | **Radial velocity (FMCW Doppler)** | m/s |
| 9 | $D_{footprint} = 2 R \tan\left(\dfrac{\theta_{div}}{2}\right)$ | **Laser footprint diameter at range R** | m |
| 10 | $\vert P \vert = \sqrt{P_x^{2} + P_y^{2} + P_z^{2}}$ | **Euclidean norm of a 3D point** (used in nearest-neighbor, ICP) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where it is used in real engineering:** Equations 1–3 form the foundation of any LiDAR datasheet. Equation 4 governs the **maximum detection range** of a self-driving car LiDAR (typically 200–300 m for premium units). Equations 6–8 represent the **FMCW LiDAR** generation (e.g., **Aeva, Mobileye, Innoviz**) which simultaneously measures **range AND velocity per point** — a major advantage over Time-of-Flight LiDAR.

### 2.3 Why 3D Mapping and Obstacle Detection are Inseparable

3D mapping is essentially the *output* of obstacle detection. They are two sides of the same coin:
- **Geometric map** (point cloud) → **spatial data structure**.
- **Obstacle detection** → **algorithm that consumes the map and outputs a labelled bounding box / segmented cluster**.

A high-resolution 3D map enables obstacle detection by providing:
- **Ground-plane separation** (RANSAC-based plane fitting).
- **Euclidean / DBSCAN clustering** of points above the ground plane.
- **Bounding box regression** (e.g., using PointPillars, CenterPoint, PointNet++).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Range Equation from the Speed of Light

We start from the kinematic definition of velocity.

**Step 1 — Definition of velocity:**
For a photon travelling from the LiDAR to a target and back, the round-trip distance $d_{RT}$ is related to time $\Delta t$ by:

$$d_{RT} = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Decompose round-trip into one-way range:**
Because the light must travel **outbound** and **inbound**, the one-way range R is exactly half of the round-trip distance:

$$2R = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for R:**

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for a car at 100 m:**
Assume Δt = 666.7 ns (a typical reading for a 100 m obstacle).

$$R = \frac{(2.998 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}) \times (666.7 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s})}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator:**

$$(2.998 \times 10^{8}) \times (666.7 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.998 \times 666.7 \times 10^{-1} = 199.89 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Divide by 2:**

$$R = \frac{199.89}{2} = 99.945 \, \text{m} \approx 100 \, \text{m} \checkmark$$

> **Logic Note:** The factor of **1/2** is the single most-forgotten term in KTU exams. Writing $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ (forgetting the division) **loses 1 mark immediately** in the valuation key.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Range Resolution from Pulse Width

A LiDAR cannot distinguish two targets that are closer together than the spatial length of its own pulse.

**Step 1 — A pulse of width τ occupies a spatial length c·τ in vacuum.**
The pulse leading edge travels c·τ in time τ.

**Step 2 — Two targets separated by ΔR will produce return pulses that overlap until the second echo has been delayed by an extra round trip of 2ΔR.**

**Step 3 — Setting the echo-arrival delay equal to the pulse width:**

$$2 \cdot \Delta R_{min} = c \cdot \tau$$

**Step 4 — Solve for ΔR_min:**

$$\Delta R_{min} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (a high-end LiDAR with τ = 4 ns):**

$$\Delta R_{min} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \times (4 \times 10^{-9})}{2} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \, \text{m} = 60 \, \text{cm}$$

**Step 6 — Implication for self-driving cars:**
A 60 cm range resolution is too coarse to distinguish the front and rear of a motorcycle. Premium LiDARs use **pulse widths of 1–2 ns**, achieving **ΔR_min = 15–30 cm**, sufficient for object discrimination.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: 3D Coordinate Reconstruction from Polar Measurements

Each LiDAR pulse returns three quantities: range R, azimuth φ, elevation θ. From these, the 3D Cartesian coordinates of a point $P$ are computed as follows.

**Step 1 — Project the range onto the horizontal plane:**

$$R_{horiz} = R \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the horizontal x and y components:**

$$x = R_{horiz} \cdot \cos(\phi) = R \cos(\theta) \cos(\phi)$$

$$y = R_{horiz} \cdot \sin(\phi) = R \cos(\theta) \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the vertical (height) z component:**

$$z = R \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Compact vector form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta)\cos(\phi) \\ \cos(\theta)\sin(\phi) \\ \sin(\theta) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Add the LiDAR mounting pose (height h, roll α, pitch β, yaw γ) via rotation-translation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{world} = T_{mount} \cdot P_{sensor} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{xyz}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) & \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ h \end{bmatrix} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (φ = 30°, θ = 1°, R = 50 m):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= 50 \cdot \cos(1^\circ) \cdot \cos(30^\circ) = 50 \cdot 0.9998 \cdot 0.8660 = 43.29 \, \text{m} \\
y &= 50 \cdot \cos(1^\circ) \cdot \sin(30^\circ) = 50 \cdot 0.9998 \cdot 0.5000 = 24.99 \, \text{m} \\
z &= 50 \cdot \sin(1^\circ) = 50 \cdot 0.01745 = 0.872 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

This point is approximately **43.3 m forward, 25.0 m to the right, and 0.87 m above** the LiDAR — a typical pedestrian on a sidewalk.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: FMCW LiDAR Beat Frequency (Bonus)

**Step 1 — A linear chirp has instantaneous frequency $f(t) = f_c + S t$, where S = B/T_s is the chirp slope (bandwidth B over sweep time T_s).**

**Step 2 — The transmitted and received chirps are mixed (multiplied) in the detector. The output is a beat signal whose frequency is the difference in instantaneous frequencies:**

$$f_b = S \cdot \tau = S \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for R:**

$$R = \frac{f_b \cdot c}{2 S}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (B = 4 GHz, T_s = 1 ms, f_b = 10 MHz):**

$$S = \frac{4 \times 10^{9}}{10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{12} \, \text{Hz/s}$$

$$R = \frac{(10^{7}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{8})}{2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{12})} = \frac{3 \times 10^{15}}{8 \times 10^{12}} = 375 \, \text{m}$$

This demonstrates the long-range capability of FMCW LiDAR.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: LiDAR vs. Camera vs. RADAR

| Parameter | LiDAR | Camera (RGB) | mmWave RADAR |
|-----------|-------|--------------|--------------|
| Active/Passive | Active (emits light) | Passive (ambient) | Active (emits RF) |
| Output | 3D point cloud | 2D image + colour | Range, velocity, RCS |
| Range (vehicular) | 100–300 m | 50–250 m | 200–350 m |
| Angular resolution | **0.1° – 0.4°** | High (pixels) | Poor (1°–3°) |
| Velocity accuracy | Moderate (tracking) | Requires optical flow | **Excellent (Doppler)** |
| Works at night | **Yes** | No (without IR) | Yes |
| Weather robustness | Degraded in fog/rain | Degraded in fog | **Excellent** |
| Cost per unit | $500 – $10000+ | $50 – $300 | $100 – $500 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LiDAR System Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end functional architecture of an automotive LiDAR, from pulse generation to obstacle classification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Generator - TDC Clock] --> B[Laser Diode Driver 905nm or 1550nm]
    B --> C[Beam Steering Unit - Mechanical MEMS OPA Flash]
    C --> D[Environment - Atmosphere and Targets]
    D --> E[Return Optics - Receiver Aperture]
    E --> F[Photodetector - APD or SPAD]
    F --> G[Amplifier - Transimpedance TIA]
    G --> H[Time of Flight Processor - TDC]
    A --> H
    H --> I[Range Calculator - R = c dt over 2]
    I --> J[3D Point Cloud Builder - X Y Z Intensity]
    J --> K[Ground Plane Segmentation - RANSAC]
    K --> L[Clustering - DBSCAN Euclidean]
    L --> M[Object Classification - PointPillars CenterPoint]
    M --> N[Tracking - Kalman Filter Hungarian Assignment]
    N --> O[Output - Bounding Boxes with Class and Velocity]
```

**Interpretation of the pipeline:**
- The **Trigger Generator** fires the laser and timestamps the emission simultaneously.
- The **Photodetector** timestamps the echo arrival.
- The **TDC** measures Δt with picosecond resolution.
- The **3D Point Cloud** is the digital twin of the surroundings.
- The **RANSAC + DBSCAN + Classifier** chain converts raw points into *semantic obstacles* (cars, pedestrians, cyclists).
- The **Kalman Filter** maintains object identity across frames.

### 4.2 3D Mapping and Obstacle Detection Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Point Cloud Input] --> B0[Preprocessing - ROI Filter Remove Self Vehicle Points]
    B0 --> C0[Ground Plane Fitting - RANSAC Plane Model ax plus by plus cz plus d equals 0]
    C0 --> D0[Non Ground Point Extraction - Height above Ground > 0.3 m]
    D0 --> E0[Voxel Downsampling - 5 cm Cubes for Speed]
    E0 --> F0[Euclidean Clustering - DBSCAN eps = 0.5 m min samples = 10]
    F0 --> G0[Cluster Bounding Box - 3D AABB or OBB]
    G0 --> H0[Feature Extraction - Point Count BBox Volume Aspect Ratio Intensity Histogram]
    H0 --> I0[Classifier - SVM OR Random Forest OR Deep Network]
    I0 --> J0[Labelled Obstacle - Car Pedestrian Cyclist Static]
    J0 --> K0[Multi Object Tracker - Kalman Filter]
    K0 --> L0[Output - Tracked Objects with ID Class Velocity Trajectory]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "explain obstacle detection using LiDAR," always walk through the pipeline **Voxel Down → Ground Removal → Clustering → Classification → Tracking**. This shows the examiner you understand the *complete* system, not just one algorithm.

### 4.3 Coordinate Frame Transformation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sensor Frame Origin] --> B1[Range R from ToF]
    B1 --> C1[Azimuth phi from Encoder]
    C1 --> D1[Elevation theta from Channel Index]
    D1 --> E1[Cartesian XYZ in Sensor Frame]
    E1 --> F1[Mounting Pose h alpha beta gamma]
    F1 --> G1[Vehicle Frame]
    G1 --> H1[World Frame via GPS and IMU]
    H1 --> I1[Global HD Map Coordinates]
```

### 4.4 Comparison: Spinning vs. Solid-State LiDAR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[LiDAR Architectures] --> B2[Spinning 360 deg]
    A2 --> C2[MEMS Mirror]
    A2 --> D2[Optical Phased Array OPA]
    A2 --> E2[Flash LiDAR]
    B2 --> F2[Advantages Full 360 FOV High Channels 64 128]
    B2 --> G2[Disadvantages Moving Parts Expensive Large]
    C2 --> H2[Advantages Compact Lower Cost Automotive Grade]
    C2 --> I2[Disadvantages Smaller FOV Mirror Reliability]
    D2 --> J2[Advantages Solid State No Moving Parts Fast]
    D2 --> K2[Disadvantages Early Tech Side Lobes Limited Range]
    E2 --> L2[Advantages Single Pulse Full Frame]
    E2 --> M2[Disadvantages Short Range High Peak Power]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**"Define LiDAR and explain its principle of operation in the context of autonomous vehicles." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
LiDAR stands for **Light Detection and Ranging**. It is an active remote sensing technology that operates on the principle of **Time-of-Flight (ToF)**. A laser diode emits short pulses of near-infrared light (typically **905 nm** or **1550 nm**). These pulses travel to a target, reflect, and return to a photodetector (APD/SPAD). The round-trip time Δt is measured by a Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC). The range R to the target is computed using:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where c = speed of light. By measuring R at thousands of azimuth and elevation angles, a **3D point cloud** of the surroundings is constructed, which is essential for obstacle detection, localization, and mapping in autonomous vehicles.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for principle, 1 for equation + 3D point cloud explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"List and briefly explain three advantages of LiDAR over cameras and RADAR for 3D mapping." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Direct 3D range measurement:** LiDAR provides accurate (cm-level) range data at every point, whereas a camera requires complex stereo vision or depth-learning algorithms to estimate depth.
2. **Day-night robustness:** LiDAR is *active* (it emits its own light), so it works equally well in bright sunlight and total darkness, unlike cameras which fail in low light.
3. **High angular resolution with range:** Compared to mmWave RADAR, LiDAR provides dense, fine-grained angular information (0.1°–0.4°), enabling precise shape and edge detection of small objects such as pedestrians and poles.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per advantage with one-sentence justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — From Dec 2023 Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) Derive the range equation used in a Time-of-Flight (ToF) LiDAR system and compute the range for a measured round-trip time of 500 ns. Assume c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. (7 Marks)**
**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 3D LiDAR system for obstacle detection in an autonomous vehicle. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** State the principle (1 mark):
A ToF LiDAR emits a short laser pulse and measures the time Δt elapsed until the reflected echo is detected at the receiver.

**Step 2:** Establish the round-trip relation (2 marks):
The total distance travelled by the photon in time Δt is:

$$d_{RT} = c \cdot \Delta t$$

Since this distance equals the **outbound** plus the **inbound** path, and the target is at a one-way range R from the LiDAR:

$$2R = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3:** Solve for R (1 mark):

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the values (1 mark):
Given Δt = 500 ns = 500 × 10⁻⁹ s.

$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \times (500 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate (2 marks):

$$R = \frac{3 \times 500 \times 10^{-1}}{2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** The target is at a range of **75 m** from the LiDAR.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the goal: To convert raw laser echoes into labelled 3D obstacles in real time.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Laser emission stage:** A laser diode produces 905 nm / 1550 nm pulses at kHz–MHz repetition rates. A beam-steering mechanism (spinning head, MEMS mirror, or OPA) deflects the beam across the FOV.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Detection and ranging stage:** The returning photons are focused onto an APD/SPAD. A TDC measures Δt; range R is computed as $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. The angles (φ, θ) come from the encoder and channel index.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **3D point cloud assembly:** Each (R, φ, θ) is converted to (X, Y, Z) using spherical-to-Cartesian transform, forming the point cloud.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Obstacle detection algorithms:**
- RANSAC ground-plane fit.
- Euclidean / DBSCAN clustering of non-ground points.
- Bounding-box regression and classification (PointPillars / SVM).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Diode] --> B[Beam Steering]
    B --> C[Targets in FOV]
    C --> D[Receiver APD]
    D --> E[TDC - Measure dt]
    E --> F[Range R = c dt over 2]
    F --> G[3D Point Cloud Builder]
    G --> H[Ground Removal RANSAC]
    H --> I[Clustering DBSCAN]
    I --> J[Classifier]
    J --> K[Tracked Obstacle Output]
```

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative Set**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a) Explain the principle of FMCW LiDAR. Derive the relation between the beat frequency and the target range. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Compare Spinning, MEMS, OPA, and Flash LiDAR architectures in a tabular form. List two real-world automotive LiDAR products for each category. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave):
Unlike pulsed ToF LiDAR, FMCW LiDAR emits a **continuously frequency-modulated laser** (a "chirp") whose optical frequency varies linearly with time over a bandwidth B during a sweep duration T_s.

The instantaneous frequency is:

$$f(t) = f_c + S \cdot t, \quad \text{where } S = \frac{B}{T_s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain the beat-frequency concept:
The transmitted and returned chirps are mixed in a coherent photodetector. The mixer output is a low-frequency **beat signal** whose frequency $f_b$ is the difference between the instantaneous frequencies at the moment of mixing. Because the received chirp is delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$, the beat frequency is:

$$f_b = S \cdot \tau = S \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Solve for R:

$$R = \frac{f_b \cdot c}{2 S} = \frac{f_b \cdot c \cdot T_s}{2 B}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Doppler velocity:
FMCW LiDAR can also extract radial velocity from the same chirp using the Doppler shift $f_D$:

$$v_{radial} = \frac{f_D \cdot \lambda}{2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mention the advantage: **Simultaneous range + velocity per point** in a single measurement, plus immunity to interference from other LiDARs.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (use \vert instead of \mid where needed):**

| Architecture | Steering Mechanism | FOV | Range | Cost | Pros | Cons | Real Products |
|--------------|-------------------|-----|-------|------|------|------|---------------|
| Spinning (Mechanical) | Motor rotates 360° | 360° × 30° | 200–300 m | High | Full 360°, mature | Bulky, moving parts | Velodyne HDL-64E, Hesai Pandar XT-32, RoboSense RS-Ruby |
| MEMS | Micro-mirror oscillation | 60°–120° | 100–200 m | Medium | Compact, automotive-grade | Smaller FOV, mirror wear | Innoviz One, Robosense M1 |
| OPA (Solid-State) | Electronic phase array | 60°–120° | 50–150 m | High (early) | No moving parts, fast | Side lobes, immature | Quanergy S3 (early), Analog Photonics |
| Flash | Single wide pulse + FPA | 60°–90° | 30–100 m | Medium | No scanning, robust | Short range, high peak power | Continental 3D Flash, Argo AI sensor |

*(7 marks: 4 for table completeness, 2 for product examples, 1 for summary comment)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the range equation. $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ is **wrong** — always write $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. The examiner specifically docks 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing "angular resolution" with "field of view."** FOV is the total scanning range; angular resolution is the smallest step the LiDAR can distinguish. E.g., a 360° FOV does not imply 360° resolution.
> 3. **Mixing up RANSAC and DBSCAN roles.** RANSAC is for **plane fitting (ground segmentation)**; DBSCAN is for **clustering remaining points**. Many students swap them.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Always write units in intermediate steps (m, s, m/s). The KTU 2024 scheme follows "units audit" strictly.
> 5. **Writing "LiDAR is a camera."** It is a *ranging* sensor. The examiner circles this and deducts 1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring FMCW LiDAR** in a comparison question. The 2024 syllabus explicitly lists FMCW as a sub-topic. Omitting it costs at least 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **LiDAR** = **Light Detection and Ranging**, an active 3D ToF ranging sensor.
- [x] **Core equation:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$, with **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**.
- [x] **Pulse width τ** sets the **range resolution** $\Delta R = c\tau/2$.
- [x] **Two dominant wavelengths:** 905 nm (silicon-friendly) and 1550 nm (eye-safe, longer range).
- [x] **3D point cloud** is built from (R, φ, θ) using spherical-to-Cartesian transform.
- [x] **FMCW LiDAR** uses chirp beat frequency to measure range AND velocity simultaneously: $R = f_b c / (2S)$, $v = f_D \lambda / 2$.
- [x] **Obstacle detection pipeline:** Voxel Down → RANSAC Ground Removal → DBSCAN Clustering → Bounding Box → Classifier (SVM/PointPillars) → Kalman Tracker.
- [x] **Architectures:** Spinning, MEMS, OPA, Flash — know the trade-offs (FOV, range, cost, moving parts).
- [x] **LiDAR ≠ Camera ≠ RADAR.** LiDAR gives dense 3D geometry; cameras give colour; RADAR gives long-range velocity.
- [x] **Eye-safety:** 1550 nm is safer for the retina (absorbed by cornea) and allows higher peak power; 905 nm is cheaper but eye-safety-limited.
- [x] **Weather degradation:** Fog, heavy rain, and snow scatter and attenuate the laser — performance drops at >50 mm/hr rain.
- [x] **Real-world products:** Velodyne, Hesai, RoboSense (spinning); Innoviz, Luminar (MEMS/solid-state); Aeva, Mobileye (FMCW).
- [x] **Famous equations to memorize:** Range, range resolution, range equation (R⁴), FMCW beat-frequency, Doppler velocity.
- [x] **For a 14-mark answer**, always include: (i) the equation, (ii) a numerical example, (iii) a labelled block diagram, and (iv) a comparison table if relevant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camera Systems and Image Processing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📷 Camera Systems and Image Processing in ADAS

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Camera Systems and Image Processing

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Camera Systems in ADAS** refer to the optical-electronic perception modules mounted on a vehicle that capture 2-D or 3-D visual data of the surrounding environment for downstream perception, decision-making, and actuation tasks. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these systems encompass the **image sensor**, **optics (lens assembly)**, **image signal processor (ISP)**, and the associated **image-processing algorithms** that convert raw photons into structured semantic information (lanes, vehicles, pedestrians, traffic signs, free-space).

**Image Processing** is the computational pipeline that transforms a raw captured frame $I(x, y, t)$ into a higher-level representation suitable for perception algorithms, using operations drawn from linear algebra, signal processing, and computer vision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly maps to **CO2** — *"Apply image-processing and perception algorithms to interpret vehicle environment."* Questions typically test (a) camera geometry, (b) filtering/edge detection, (c) feature extraction, and (d) calibration mathematics.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Eye vs. An Automotive Camera

Imagine you are driving down a Kochi bypass at dusk. Your **eye** (the camera sensor) gathers light rays bouncing off a pedestrian's white shirt. The **retina** (the CMOS/CCD pixel array) converts these photons into electrical signals. The **visual cortex** (the on-board ECU / neural accelerator) then performs a sophisticated sequence of operations:

1. **Focus adjustment** (the lens changing focal length).
2. **Noise filtering** (ignoring irrelevant static).
3. **Edge sharpening** (recognising the silhouette of the pedestrian).
4. **Object recognition** (classifying the silhouette as "human").
5. **Depth estimation** (judging the distance using stereo disparity).

An automotive camera replicates *all five* of these biological stages in silicon and software, but with **far stricter latency constraints** (typically **< 50 ms** end-to-end for L2+ ADAS functions like AEB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** A modern ADAS front camera (e.g., Mobileye EyeQ6, NVIDIA Drive Orin paired camera) operates at **30–60 FPS**, with a **latency budget of 20–100 ms** and a **dynamic range > 120 dB** to handle backlit tunnel exits.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Automotive Cameras

| Camera Class | Mounting Location | Primary ADAS Function | Typical FOV | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mono Front Camera** | Behind windshield | Lane, traffic sign, vehicle detection | 50° – 100° | 0–250 m |
| **Stereo Camera Pair** | Windshield (baseline ≈ 200 mm) | Depth, obstacle ranging, 3-D reconstruction | 45° – 70° | 0–120 m |
| **Surround-View (Fish-eye)** | 4× side/rear bumpers | Parking, blind-spot, 360° bird's-eye | 180° – 200° | 0–10 m |
| **Rear-View Camera** | License-plate bezel | Reverse AEB, parking | 130° | 0–8 m |
| **In-Cabin DMS** | Steering column / A-pillar | Driver monitoring, gaze tracking | 60° – 90° | 0–1.5 m |
| **Thermal (LWIR) Camera** | Front grille (premium) | Pedestrian detection at night | 24° – 50° | 0–300 m |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pinhole Camera Model — Foundation of All ADAS Vision

The **pinhole camera model** is the *canonical abstraction* used in KTU-level computer vision. It assumes light from a 3-D world point passes through an infinitesimally small aperture and projects onto an image plane.

### 2.1.1 Geometric Derivation of Projection

Let a 3-D world point be expressed in camera coordinates as:

$$
\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}
$$

The corresponding 2-D image-plane coordinates $(x, y)$ are obtained by **similar triangles**:

$$
x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

where $f$ is the **focal length** (in mm or pixels). In homogeneous form, the projection is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The pinhole model is *linear in homogeneous coordinates* but *non-linear in Euclidean coordinates* due to the perspective divide $Z^{-1}$. This is the central reason why **camera calibration** and **inverse-depth parameterisation** are unavoidable in monocular SLAM.

---

### 2.2 Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Parameters

The complete **camera projection matrix** decomposes into:

$$
\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]
$$

where the **intrinsic matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ is:

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- $f_x, f_y$: focal length in pixel units (typically 500–1500 pixels).
- $c_x, c_y$: **principal point** (image centre, ideally $W/2$, $H/2$).
- $\gamma$: **skew coefficient** (≈ 0 for modern CMOS sensors).

The **extrinsic matrix** $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ encodes the 6-DoF pose (3 rotation + 3 translation) of the camera relative to the vehicle (or world) frame.

---

### 2.3 Lens Distortion Models — Real Cameras Are Not Pinholes

| Distortion Type | Mathematical Form | Visual Symptom | Correction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial** | $\Delta_r = 1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6$ | Barrel/pincushion warping | Iterative undistortion |
| **Tangential** | $\Delta_t$ due to de-centred lens | Asymmetric tilt | Calibration-resolved |
| **Chromatic** | Wavelength-dependent focal shift | Colour fringing | ISP lens-shading correction |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically restricts questions to **radial distortion** with two coefficients $(k_1, k_2)$. The distorted pixel coordinates $(x_d, y_d)$ relate to undistorted $(x_u, y_u)$ as:
>
> $$x_d = x_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4), \quad y_d = y_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4)$$
>
> where $r^2 = x_u^2 + y_u^2$.

---

### 2.4 Stereo Vision & Depth from Disparity

For a **rectified stereo pair** with baseline $B$ and focal length $f$, the depth $Z$ of a world point is recovered from its **disparity** $d = x_L - x_R$:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disparity is inversely proportional to depth.** A close object has high disparity; a far object has near-zero disparity. This is the *single most-tested formula* in KTU Module 2 numericals.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole projection | $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$ | $f$ in mm or px |
| 2 | Intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 5 DoF |
| 3 | Camera matrix $\mathbf{P}$ | $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]$ | 3×4 |
| 4 | Stereo depth | $Z = fB/d$ | $d \neq 0$ required |
| 5 | Disparity range | $d \in [d_{\min}, d_{\max}] \Rightarrow Z \in [Z_{\max}, Z_{\min}]$ | pixels |
| 6 | Field of View (FOV) | $\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan\bigl(w / (2f)\bigr)$ | radians |
| 7 | Optical Flow (LK) | $\mathbf{u} = -\bigl(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J}\bigr)^{-1} \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{b}$ | Lucas–Kanade constraint |
| 8 | Image gradient magnitude | $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Sobel / Prewitt kernels |
| 9 | Canny edge threshold | Hysteresis: $T_{\text{high}}$ retains, $T_{\text{low}}$ drops weak | Two thresholds |
| 10 | Harris corner response | $R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - \alpha \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$ | $\alpha \approx 0.04$ |
| 11 | Camera exposure (EV) | $\text{EV} = \log_2 \bigl(N^2 / t \bigr) - \log_2(\text{ISO}/100)$ | Stops |
| 12 | Sensor SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | Photon-shot limited |
| 13 | Pixel pitch & diffraction | $d_{\text{pixel}} \le 1.22 \lambda (f/\#)$ | Nyquist sampling |
| 14 | Rotation matrix property | $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, $\det \mathbf{R} = +1$ | SO(3) |
| 15 | Epipolar constraint | $\mathbf{x}_R^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ | Fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical pipe symbol rule:** Absolute values in the formulas above are written as `\vert` (e.g., `$\vert \nabla I \vert$`) to preserve Markdown table integrity. Do **not** use `\|` or `|` inside table cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stereo Depth from Disparity

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 2.

### Setup

Consider a rectified stereo rig with two cameras $\mathcal{C}_L$ (left) and $\mathcal{C}_R$ (right), separated along the X-axis by a baseline $B$. Both cameras share identical intrinsic parameters $\mathbf{K}$ and identical focal length $f$.

A world point $P$ has 3-D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ in the *left camera frame*. Its projections are:

$$
\mathbf{p}_L = (x_L, y_L), \quad \mathbf{p}_R = (x_R, y_R)
$$

---

### Step 1 — Project onto Left Image Plane

Applying the pinhole model:

$$
x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Reasoning:** By definition of perspective projection, the image-plane coordinates scale linearly with $X$ and inversely with depth $Z$.

---

### Step 2 — Project onto Right Image Plane

The same point in the *right camera frame* has coordinates $(X - B, Y, Z)$ (baseline shifts origin along X).

$$
x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}, \quad y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Reasoning:** Rectification guarantees $y_L = y_R$ (epipolar lines are horizontal), so the only difference between left and right projections is along the X-axis.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Disparity

The disparity $d$ is the horizontal pixel offset:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\
&= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
&= f \cdot \frac{X - X + B}{Z} \\
&= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Subtracting cancels the unknown $X$, leaving a *clean expression* in $Z$ only.

---

### Step 4 — Solve for Depth

Inverting the disparity equation:

$$
\boxed{\,Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}\,}
$$

**Reasoning:** This shows depth is *inversely* proportional to disparity — an algebraic cornerstone of stereo vision.

---

### Step 5 — Error Sensitivity Analysis (KTU-favourite follow-up)

Differentiating $Z$ w.r.t. $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} &= -\frac{f \cdot B}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Depth error grows *quadratically* with distance. Doubling range quadruples the error. This is why **long-range ADAS cameras prefer mono+radar fusion** over stereo.

---

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** $f = 800$ pixels, $B = 200$ mm, observed disparity $d = 16$ pixels.

Compute depth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} \\
&= \frac{800 \times 200}{16} \\
&= \frac{160\,000}{16} \\
&= 10\,000 \text{ mm} = \mathbf{10 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always carry units. KTU valuators award a half-mark for correct dimensional consistency ($Z$ in metres, $f$ in pixels, $B$ in metres → $Z$ in metres requires $f$ dimensionless *or* $B$ in same units as desired $Z$).

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Harris Corner Detection Response

### Step 1 — Image Gradient & Structure Tensor

For an image patch $W$, compute gradients $I_x$, $I_y$ and form the **second-moment matrix** (structure tensor):

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reasoning:** $\mathbf{M}$ captures the *local intensity distribution*. Its eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ describe principal curvatures of the image intensity surface.

---

### Step 2 — Eigenvalue Interpretation

| Condition | Eigenvalues | Patch Type |
|---|---|---|
| Both small | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Flat region |
| One large, one small | $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | Edge |
| Both large | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \gg 0$ | **Corner** |

---

### Step 3 — Corner Response Function

To avoid explicit eigendecomposition (costly), Harris proposed:

$$
R = \det(\mathbf{M}) - \alpha \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2
$$

Expanding:

$$
R = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - \alpha (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2
$$

**Reasoning:** Corners yield $R > 0$ and large magnitude; edges yield $R < 0$; flats yield $\vert R \vert \approx 0$. The empirical constant $\alpha \in [0.04, 0.06]$ balances sensitivity.

---

### Step 3a — Alternative Shi-Tomasi Variant (KTU may ask)

$$
R_{\text{ST}} = \min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)
$$

This is **monotonic in corner-ness** and is preferred in modern **visual-odometry pipelines** (e.g., ORB-SLAM).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Image Processing Operations vs. ADAS Application

| ADAS Application | Image Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Lane detection | Edge + Hough transform | Curved line parameters |
| Vehicle detection | Haar cascade / CNN | Bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$ |
| Pedestrian detection | HOG + SVM | Pedestrian probability |
| Traffic sign recognition | Colour threshold + template | Sign class |
| Free-space estimation | Optical flow + v-disparity | Drivable polygon |
| Visual odometry | Feature tracking + PnP | 6-DoF ego-motion |
| SLAM | Bundle adjustment | Globally consistent map |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End ADAS Camera Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene Light Rays] --> B[Lens Assembly]
    B --> C[CMOS Image Sensor]
    C --> D[ISP Image Signal Processor]
    D --> E[Raw Bayer Frame]
    E --> F[ISP Demosaic and Denoise]
    F --> G[Color Space Convert RGB to YUV]
    G --> H[Preprocessing Rectify and Undistort]
    H --> I{Feature Stage}
    I --> J[Edge Detection Canny]
    I --> K[Corner Detection Harris]
    I --> L[Deep CNN Backbone ResNet]
    J --> M[Classical Lane Detector]
    K --> N[Visual Odometry PnP]
    L --> O[Object Detection YOLO]
    M --> P[ADAS Decision Layer]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Actuator Output AEB LKA ACC]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Light enters at node `A`, propagates through optical front-end (`B` → `C`), is digitised (`D` → `E`), then ISP cleans and colourises (`F` → `G`), rectified (`H`), and finally forks into *classical* (`J`, `K`) and *deep-learning* (`L`) branches that converge at the ADAS decision layer `P`.

---

## 4.2 Pinhole Camera Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ[3D World Point X Y Z] -->|Light ray| AP[Pinhole Aperture f]
    AP -->|Projected ray| IMG[Image Plane at distance f]
    IMG -->|Pixel coordinates| UV[2D Image Point x y]

    subgraph Optical Axis
    AP
    IMG
    end

    FL[Focal Length f mm] -.-> AP
    ZD[Depth Z] -.-> AP
```

> **Reading Guide:** Node `AP` represents the aperture. The principal-axis relationship $x = fX/Z$ is governed by the right-triangle similarity enforced by `FL` and `ZD`.

---

## 4.3 Stereo Camera Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StereoRig[Rectified Stereo Baseline B]
        CL[Left Camera f px]
        CR[Right Camera f px]
    end
    CL -->|Image IL| RECT[Rectification Stage]
    CR -->|Image IR| RECT
    RECT --> MATCH[Stereo Matching Block Census SGM]
    MATCH --> DISP[Disparity Map d x y]
    DISP --> DEPTH[Depth Map Z equals fB over d]
    DEPTH --> APP[Application Layer Obstacle Detection Free Space]
```

---

## 4.4 Image Processing Algorithm Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Pixel Level Operations] --> L1[Threshold and Histogram]
    L0 --> L2[Filtering Gaussian Median Bilateral]
    L1 --> M1[Edge Detection Canny Sobel]
    L2 --> M1
    L1 --> M2[Morphology Erode Dilate]
    L2 --> M2
    M1 --> F1[Feature Level Corners Blobs Descriptors]
    M2 --> F1
    F1 --> S1[Structural Level Object Segmentation Tracking]
    S1 --> SEM[Semantic Level Lane Sign Vehicle Pedestrian]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The diagram ascends the classical *image-understanding hierarchy*: `L0` (pixels) → `L1`/`L2` (low-level operators) → `M1`/`M2` (mid-level features) → `F1` (features) → `S1` (structures) → `SEM` (semantics). Each tier builds abstraction from the previous.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between CCD and CMOS image sensors used in automotive cameras. Mention two ADAS-relevant trade-offs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---|---|
| Read-out | Charge shifted row-by-row to common output | Per-pixel transistor active read-out |
| Noise | Lower fixed-pattern noise, higher read noise | Higher FPN, lower read noise |
| Speed | Slower, global shutter | Faster, supports global + rolling shutter |
| Power | Higher | **Lower** (preferred for EV ADAS) |
| Cost | Higher | **Lower** (mass-market) |

**Two ADAS-relevant trade-offs:**
1. **Rolling-shutter artefacts** in CMOS distort fast-moving objects — critical for highway AEB. Global-shutter CMOS mitigates this.
2. **Power budget**: CMOS consumes ~10× less power, vital for always-on surround-view systems in electric vehicles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often say "CMOS is better than CCD" without specifying *which ADAS function*. Always tie the trade-off to a concrete use-case to score full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define "disparity" in stereo vision. Why is disparity inversely proportional to depth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Disparity $d$ is the horizontal pixel offset $(x_L - x_R)$ between corresponding points in a rectified stereo image pair.

**Inverse-proportionality reasoning:**
- A **near object** subtends a *large* horizontal shift between the two views → large $d$ → small $Z$.
- A **far object** subtends a *small* horizontal shift → small $d$ → large $Z$.
- Mathematically, from the pinhole projection of two horizontally-offset cameras with baseline $B$:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} \;\Rightarrow\; Z \propto \frac{1}{d}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not state "disparity is distance between cameras." The disparity is between **image pixels**, not physical cameras. Examiners specifically dock marks for this confusion.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for depth $Z$ of a 3-D point from a rectified stereo pair in terms of focal length $f$, baseline $B$, and disparity $d$. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A forward-facing stereo camera has a baseline $B = 0.20$ m, focal length $f = 1000$ pixels, and image resolution $1280 \times 720$. Compute (i) the depth of a vehicle whose disparity is 25 pixels, and (ii) the disparity of a pedestrian at 8 m. Also explain why stereo depth accuracy degrades with distance. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Setup coordinate frames.** *(1 mark)*
Place the left camera at origin $(0,0,0)$. World point $P = (X, Y, Z)$. Right camera is at $(B, 0, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Apply pinhole projection to left camera.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply pinhole projection to right camera.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}, \quad y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute disparity.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\
&= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
&= \frac{fB}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for depth and interpret.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

**Conclusion:** Depth is inversely proportional to disparity; doubling the disparity halves the depth. The formula assumes rectified cameras with parallel optical axes.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b) — Numerical + Discussion (7 marks)

**(i) Depth at disparity = 25 pixels:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{25} \\
&= \frac{200}{25} = 8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Disparity at $Z = 8$ m:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{8} \\
&= \frac{200}{8} = 25 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Parts (i) and (ii) are *consistent by design* — the question is testing whether students apply the formula correctly in both directions.

**(iii) Why stereo depth accuracy degrades with distance:** *(3 marks)*

From the sensitivity derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB}
$$

- Error grows **quadratically** with $Z$.
- At long range, $d$ becomes a *fractional-pixel* quantity → quantisation noise dominates.
- Sub-pixel matching (census, SGM) helps but cannot beat physical limits.
- Hence long-range ADAS (highway L2+) fuses stereo with **radar** (constant range error in metres) for robustness.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete image-processing pipeline of an automotive front camera, from photon capture to lane-detection output. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the Canny edge-detection algorithm in detail. Why is Gaussian smoothing performed before gradient computation? State the role of hysteresis thresholding. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a) — Camera Pipeline (7 marks)

**Block diagram (text description — full Mermaid in Section 4.1):** *(2 marks)*

The pipeline proceeds through the following stages:

1. **Optical Front-End:** Lens assembly focuses incoming light onto the CMOS sensor.
2. **Sensor Read-Out:** Photodiodes convert photons to electrons; rolling or global shutter reads them out.
3. **ISP (Image Signal Processor):** Performs demosaicing, lens-shading correction, noise reduction, white-balance, gamma correction.
4. **Preprocessing:** Rectification, undistortion using intrinsic $\mathbf{K}$ and distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2)$.
5. **Feature / Deep Extraction:** Canny/Sobel edges **OR** CNN backbone (ResNet-50 / EfficientNet).
6. **Perception Output:** Lane polynomial fit, vehicle bounding boxes, sign classification.
7. **Decision:** Output to ADAS ECU → AEB / LKA / ACC actuator commands.

**Why each stage matters:** *(3 marks)*

- **Demosaicing** reconstructs full RGB from Bayer pattern (1 red, 2 green, 1 blue per 2×2 block).
- **Rectification** aligns stereo epipolar lines with image rows — otherwise disparity search becomes 2-D.
- **CNN backbone** provides translation-invariant features at multiple scales (FPN).

**Real-world latency budget:** *(2 marks)*

| Stage | Latency |
|---|---|
| Sensor exposure + read-out | 5–10 ms |
| ISP + preprocessing | 5–15 ms |
| CNN inference (10 W SoC) | 15–30 ms |
| Decision + actuation | 5–10 ms |
| **Total** | **30–65 ms** |

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b) — Canny Edge Detection (7 marks)

**Algorithm steps:** *(4 marks)*

1. **Gaussian smoothing** — convolve image with $G_\sigma$ to suppress noise.
2. **Gradient computation** — apply Sobel kernels $S_x$ and $S_y$, compute $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ and direction $\theta = \arctan2(I_y, I_x)$.
3. **Non-maximum suppression** — thin edges by retaining only local gradient maxima along $\theta$.
4. **Double thresholding** — classify pixels into strong ($I > T_H$), weak ($T_L < I < T_H$), or suppressed.
5. **Edge tracking by hysteresis** — retain weak pixels connected to strong ones via 8-connectivity.

**Why Gaussian before gradient?** *(2 marks)*

The gradient operator amplifies high-frequency noise. Differentiating a noisy signal is mathematically ill-posed (derivative of white noise is unbounded variance). Gaussian smoothing acts as a regulariser:

$$
G_\sigma \star I \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x} (G_\sigma \star I) = \left(\frac{\partial G_\sigma}{\partial x}\right) \star I
$$

This separates smoothing from differentiation via linearity, suppressing noise before the derivative is taken.

**Role of hysteresis:** *(1 mark)*

Hysteresis prevents **broken contours**. A single global threshold either loses faint-but-true edge segments or admits spurious noise. By retaining weak pixels only when connected to a strong edge, Canny produces **closed, contiguous boundaries** — critical for lane-curve polynomial fitting in ADAS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In **Q.A(b)**, marks are lost if students forget to **state units** (m, pixels) explicitly. KTU specifically deducts ½ mark for dimensional ambiguity.
> - In **Q.B(a)**, candidates often **omit the ISP block** and jump straight from sensor to feature extraction. This is treated as an *incomplete pipeline* — full 7 marks only if all 5 stages (sensor → ISP → preprocess → feature → decision) are present.
> - In **Q.B(b)**, writing "Canny is better than Sobel" without *mathematical justification* (hysteresis, NMS) is worth at most 4/7 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Camera Systems & Image Processing**

### 🔑 Core Geometric Concepts
- Pinhole projection: $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$.
- Intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ (5 DoF: $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, \gamma$).
- Extrinsic matrix $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ (6 DoF).
- Full camera matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$.
- Radial distortion: $x_d = x_u (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4)$.

### 🔑 Stereo Vision Essentials
- Disparity $d = x_L - x_R$.
- Depth $Z = fB/d$.
- Rectification aligns epipolar lines to image rows.
- Depth error $\propto Z^2 / (fB)$ — degrades quadratically.

### 🔑 Sensor & Optics Facts
- **CMOS** dominates automotive due to lower power, on-chip ADC, lower cost.
- **Global shutter** required for fast motion (avoid rolling-shutter skew).
- **Dynamic range > 120 dB** needed for tunnel-entry/exit scenarios.
- **FOV**: $\text{FOV} = 2 \arctan(w / 2f)$.

### 🔑 Image Processing Pipeline Order
1. Demosaic → 2. Denoise → 3. Colour convert (RGB→YUV) → 4. Rectify → 5. Detect edges/features → 6. Classify/Track.

### 🔑 Algorithm Mnemonics
- **Canny** = Gaussian → Gradient → NMS → Double-Threshold → Hysteresis.
- **Harris** = $\det(\mathbf{M}) - \alpha \cdot \text{trace}(\mathbf{M})^2$.
- **LK Optical Flow** = brightness constancy + spatial gradient.
- **Sobel kernels** = $S_x = [[-1,0,1],[-2,0,2],[-1,0,1]]$, $S_y = S_x^T$.

### 🔑 ADAS-Specific Applications
| Application | Algorithm Pair |
|---|---|
| Lane detection | Canny + Hough / UFLD (deep) |
| Vehicle detection | YOLO / Faster R-CNN |
| Pedestrian | HOG + SVM / SSD |
| Visual Odometry | ORB features + PnP + RANSAC |
| Free-space | v-disparity + U-disparity |
| Traffic Sign | Colour threshold + CNN |

### 🔑 Examiner's Golden Rules
1. Always **state assumptions** (rectified, no skew, principal point at centre).
2. Always **include units** in numerical answers.
3. Use **vector/matrix notation** consistently in derivations.
4. When asked to "compare," produce a **table** — it earns marks for structure.
5. When asked to "derive," show **every algebraic step** — partial credit is awarded line-by-line.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Camera Systems and Image Processing**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cameras Used in ADAS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Cameras Used in ADAS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Driving architectures, **cameras** are passive, electro-optical sensing devices that capture the **intensity, spectrum, and spatial distribution of reflected or emitted light** from the vehicle's surroundings. They convert photonic energy into a two-dimensional (2D) digital image via an image sensor (typically CMOS or CCD) composed of a photodiode array, and are the **primary modality** for object classification, lane detection, traffic sign recognition, and semantic scene understanding.

The "types" of ADAS cameras refer to the **optical, sensor, and geometric configurations** tailored to specific driving tasks — each engineered to handle distinct ranges, fields-of-view (FoV), lighting conditions, and computational pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Cameras are a cornerstone sensor in the **perception stack** of SAE Level 2+ vehicles. The classification by FoV, spectral response, and 3D-capability is a **frequently tested concept** in University exams.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Eye Paradigm

Imagine a driver with **specialized eyes**:
- A **forward-facing "eagle eye"** that spots traffic signs kilometres ahead (telephoto).
- Two **wide-angle "side eyes"** for blind-spot awareness (fisheye).
- **"Night-vision" eyes** that detect heat signatures of pedestrians in fog (thermal).
- A **"hyper-speed eye"** that captures only movement (event-based).

Each ADAS camera type is a **machine equivalent of one such specialized eye**, optimized for a sub-task. The fusion of these cameras recreates a **360°, robust, all-weather perception** of the environment — analogous to how binocular, peripheral, and color vision work together in human cognition.

## 1.3 The Core Classification Framework

ADAS cameras are typically classified along **four axes**:

| Axis | Options |
|------|---------|
| **Spectral Response** | Visible (RGB), Near-Infrared (NIR), Far-Infrared (FIR/Thermal), Polarized |
| **Geometry / FoV** | Narrow (Tele), Medium, Wide, Ultra-Wide (Fisheye) |
| **3D Capability** | Monocular, Stereo, Multi-View, ToF |
| **Temporal Response** | Frame-based, Event-based (Neuromorphic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Most exam questions ask students to *match* a camera type to a use-case (e.g., "Which camera detects pedestrians in fog?"). Memorizing the **spectral-FoV-task triad** is the fastest path to full marks.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in ADAS Camera Datasheets

The following industry-standard parameters are **bold-marked** because they routinely appear in KTU numerical and descriptive questions:

- **Focal Length (f):** measured in **millimetres (mm)**; typical automotive range: **2 mm – 50 mm**.
- **Field of View (FoV):** measured in **degrees (°)**; horizontal FoV = 30° (Narrow), 60° (Medium), 100° (Wide), 190° (Fisheye).
- **Aperture (f-number, N):** ratio f/D; typical automotive: **f/1.6 to f/2.8**.
- **Resolution:** measured in **megapixels (MP)**; modern ADAS: **1.2 MP to 8 MP**.
- **Frame Rate:** measured in **frames per second (fps)**; ADAS typical: **30 fps to 60 fps** (some systems: **120 fps**).
- **Dynamic Range:** measured in **decibels (dB)**; automotive: **≥ 120 dB**.
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 2 or higher).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship governing geometric FoV is given by:
>
> $$\text{FoV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$$
>
> where $d$ is the sensor diagonal (or dimension of interest) and $f$ is the focal length. This formula is **directly derivable** and frequently tested as a 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of ADAS Camera Types

### A. Monocular (Single-Lens) RGB Camera
- **Sensor:** Single CMOS sensor with **Bayer filter** (R, G, B mosaic).
- **Output:** 2D image; **no direct depth**.
- **Pros:** Lowest cost, mature pipeline (CNN-based), high resolution.
- **Cons:** Depth must be inferred (e.g., via known object sizes, monocular depth estimation networks).
- **Use Cases:** Lane detection, traffic sign recognition (TSR), traffic light recognition (TLR), forward collision warning (FCW).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mono + AI depth estimation** is the de-facto architecture in Tesla's HW3 Autopilot front camera and Mobileye EyeQ-series systems.

### B. Stereo Camera
- **Sensor:** Two synchronized cameras separated by a precisely known **baseline** $B$ (typically 120–350 mm).
- **Output:** Disparity map $\rightarrow$ depth map.
- **Pros:** True 3D reconstruction, robust for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking).
- **Cons:** Sensitive to calibration drift; struggles with low-texture scenes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disparity–Depth equation** (high-yield, KTU tested):
>
> $$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$
>
> where $Z$ is depth (m), $f$ is focal length (pixels), $B$ is baseline (m), and $d$ is disparity (pixels).

### C. Wide-Angle / Fisheye Camera
- **Sensor:** Lens with FoV up to **190°** (often 180°+).
- **Output:** Highly distorted 2D image.
- **Pros:** Covers blind-spots, surround-view, parking.
- **Cons:** Requires **geometric undistortion** (inverse equidistant or equisolid-angle models).

> [!NOTE]
> **Surround View Monitor (SVM)** typically uses **4 fisheye cameras** (front, rear, left, right) stitched into a bird's-eye-view.

### D. Near-Infrared (NIR) Camera
- **Sensor:** CMOS with **extended spectral response up to 1100 nm**; works with **active IR illuminator** (typically 850 nm or 940 nm).
- **Pros:** Robust in low-light, headlight-glare immunity.
- **Cons:** Monochrome only.

### E. Far-Infrared / Thermal Camera (LWIR)
- **Sensor:** Microbolometer array (uncooled) detecting **long-wave IR (8–14 µm)**.
- **Output:** Thermal image based on **blackbody radiation**.
- **Pros:** Pedestrian/animal detection in **complete darkness, fog, smoke**.
- **Cons:** Low spatial resolution, expensive, no color.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stefan–Boltzmann law** (foundational thermal physics — relevant for LWIR theory):
>
> $$P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$$
>
> where $P$ is radiated power, $\varepsilon$ is emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}~\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$, $A$ is area, $T$ is absolute temperature. Pedestrians ($\sim$307 K) radiate against backgrounds ($\sim$273 K), giving the **thermal contrast** exploited by ADAS night-vision systems.

### F. Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera
- **Sensor:** Emits modulated IR light; measures **phase shift** between emitted and reflected wave.
- **Output:** Per-pixel depth (typically 0.1–10 m).
- **Pros:** Independent of ambient light; real-time depth.
- **Cons:** Limited range, interference from sunlight, lower resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ToF depth equation:**
>
> $$d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{\text{mod}}}$$
>
> where $c$ is speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s), $\phi$ is measured phase shift, $f_{\text{mod}}$ is modulation frequency.

### G. Event-Based (Neuromorphic) Camera
- **Sensor:** Asynchronous pixels (e.g., **Dynamic Vision Sensor, DVS**); each pixel reports **only intensity changes** with microsecond latency.
- **Output:** Stream of events $(x, y, t, p)$ where $p$ is polarity.
- **Pros:** Ultra-low latency (< 1 ms), high dynamic range (>120 dB), no motion blur.
- **Cons:** Sparse output, immature toolchain, novel algorithms.

### H. Multi-Camera / Surround-View System
- **Configuration:** 4–6 fisheye + front telephoto + rear medium.
- **Pipeline:** Intrinsic + extrinsic calibration → image stitching → bird's-eye view (BEV) representation.
- **Critical for:** Automated parking, SAE Level 3+ redundancy.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables | Units | Use Case |
|---|----------|-----------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $\text{FoV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | $d$ = sensor dimension, $f$ = focal length | mm → ° | Any camera FoV calculation |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $Z$ = depth, $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity | m | Stereo depth |
| 3 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $A$ = area, $T$ = temp | W | Thermal IR theory |
| 4 | $d_{\text{ToF}} = \dfrac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{\text{mod}}}$ | $\phi$ = phase, $f_{\text{mod}}$ = modulation | m | ToF ranging |
| 5 | $\text{Pixel size} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor dim}}{\text{Resolution}}$ | – | µm/pixel | Resolution/FOV tradeoff |
| 6 | $\text{Ground Res.} = \dfrac{Z \cdot \text{Pixel size}}{f}$ | $Z$ = range | m/pixel | Detectability |

> [!WARNING]
> When using table syntax in your **own exam answers**, **never** use the vertical bar `|` for absolute value or conditional probability inside equations — use `\vert` or `\mid`. This prevents KTU answer-sheet markdown from breaking.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stereo + Mono fusion** powers Mercedes DRIVE PILOT Level 3 and Honda Sensing 360.
- **NIR + Mono + Fisheye** stacks are the basis of **Mobileye SuperVision™** (13 cameras).
- **Thermal cameras** are mandatory in certain EU NCAP 2025 protocols for pedestrian AEB at night.
- **Event-based cameras** are being researched by **Prophesee** and **iniVation** for high-speed obstacle anticipation (> 100 km/h).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View from Pinhole Camera Geometry

The pinhole camera model projects a 3D point onto a 2D image plane. The half-angle subtended by a sensor of dimension $d$ at the pinhole is obtained by elementary trigonometry.

**Step 1:** Consider a point at the **edge of the sensor**, at horizontal distance $f$ (focal length) from the pinhole. The point is offset by $d/2$ from the principal axis.

**Step 2:** The angle $\theta$ between the principal axis and the line connecting the pinhole to the sensor edge satisfies:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{d/2}{f}
$$

**Step 3:** The full FoV spans **both sides of the axis**, so we double the half-angle:

$$
\text{FoV} = 2\theta = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Validate with a sanity check. For a typical ADAS forward camera: $d = 6.4~\text{mm}$ (1/3″ sensor diagonal), $f = 6~\text{mm}$. Then:

$$
\text{FoV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{6.4}{2 \times 6}\right) = 2\arctan(0.5333) = 2 \times 28.07^{\circ} = 56.15^{\circ}
$$

> This matches the **typical 50–60° medium FoV** of front-facing ADAS cameras. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Depth from Disparity

**Step 1:** Consider two pinhole cameras with identical focal length $f$ and baseline $B$ (perpendicular separation).

**Step 2:** A 3D point $P$ projects to pixel $x_L$ in the left image and $x_R$ in the right image. The **disparity** is:

$$
d = x_L - x_R
$$

**Step 3:** Using similar triangles (the classical stereo geometry), the depth $Z$ is **inversely proportional** to disparity:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
$$

**Step 4:** Dimensional check: $f$ in pixels, $B$ in metres, $d$ in pixels → $Z$ in metres. ✓

**Step 5:** Numerical example — for a stereo rig with $f = 1000$ pixels, $B = 0.2$ m, observed disparity $d = 50$ pixels:

$$
Z = \frac{1000 \times 0.2}{50} = 4~\text{m}
$$

> This is the exact depth computed by a stereo camera for a 4 m distant object. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ToF Depth from Phase Shift

**Step 1:** A modulated IR wave of frequency $f_{\text{mod}}$ is emitted. The wave travels distance $d$ to the object and back (round-trip distance = $2d$).

**Step 2:** The round-trip time is $\Delta t = \dfrac{2d}{c}$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 3:** The corresponding phase shift is $\phi = 2\pi f_{\text{mod}} \cdot \Delta t$.

**Step 4:** Substituting $\Delta t$:

$$
\phi = 2\pi f_{\text{mod}} \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f_{\text{mod}} d}{c}
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $d$:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \phi}{4\pi f_{\text{mod}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ambiguity range:** $d$ is unique only if the round-trip phase stays within $[0, 2\pi)$, giving a **maximum unambiguous range**:
>
> $$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2f_{\text{mod}}}$$
>
> This is why automotive ToF systems use **multiple modulation frequencies** to disambiguate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Camera Type vs. Performance Attributes

| Attribute | Mono | Stereo | Fisheye | NIR | Thermal | ToF | Event-Based |
|-----------|------|--------|---------|-----|---------|-----|-------------|
| **Depth (direct)** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Color** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Low-light** | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ✅✅ | ✅✅ | ✅ | ✅✅ |
| **Fog/Rain robustness** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ |
