\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check against detector NETD:**

The smallest resolvable temperature difference $\Delta T$ corresponds to the smallest resolvable power $\Delta P$ on the detector. With NETD = 50 mK at 300 K, $\Delta T = 0.05$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{\partial P_{emit}}{\partial T} \cdot \Delta T \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \frac{\tau}{4\pi R^{2}} \\
&= 4 \varepsilon \sigma A T^{3} \Delta T \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \frac{\tau}{4\pi R^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\Delta P = P_{min}$ (detector sensitivity, e.g., 5 nW) and solving for $R$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \varepsilon \sigma A T^{3} \Delta T \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \tau}{4\pi P_{min}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging in numbers:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.9 \times (305)^{3} \times 0.05 \times 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7}{4 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}} \\
&\approx 248 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** A well-designed LWIR camera can detect a human at **~250 m** in clear night conditions — well beyond the high-beam headlight range (~150 m), giving the driver an additional **2–3 seconds of reaction time** at typical highway speeds (100 km/h).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Active IR Driver Monitoring Range

For an active NIR system (e.g., 850 nm DMS camera), the **radar-like range equation for a diffuse Lambertian reflector** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} \cdot \rho \cdot A_{r} \cos\theta}{\pi R^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 1D simplification, setting the source and receiver co-located, the round-trip equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \left( \frac{P_{t} \cdot \rho \cdot A_{r}^{2} \cdot \eta}{2\pi \cdot P_{min}} \right)^{1/4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For DMS with $P_{t} = 0.5$ W (LED), $\rho = 0.4$ (skin), $A_{r} = 1 \text{ cm}^{2} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$, $\eta = 0.6$, $P_{min} = 10^{-9}$ W:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \left( \frac{0.5 \times 0.4 \times (10^{-4})^{2} \times 0.6}{2\pi \times 10^{-9}} \right)^{1/4} \\
&= \left( \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{6.28 \times 10^{-9}} \right)^{1/4} \\
&= (0.191)^{1/4} \\
&\approx 0.66 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ~0.7 m range perfectly matches the **steering-column-to-driver-face distance** — validating the design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IR vs. Other ADAS Sensors

| Parameter | IR Camera (LWIR) | RGB Camera | Radar (77 GHz) | LiDAR (905 nm) | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operates in dark** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs light) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Penetrates fog/rain** | Good (LWIR) | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |
| **Range** | 100–300 m | 50–250 m | 0.2–250 m | 50–250 m | 0.1–5 m |
| **Angular resolution** | High (640×480) | High (8 MP) | Low (~1°) | Very high | Low |
| **Classifies object type** | Pedestrian/animal thermal | Yes (CNN) | No (kinematics only) | No (point cloud) | No |
| **Cost** | Medium-High | Low | Medium | Very High | Very Low |
| **ADAS Use** | Night vision, DMS, AEB-pedestrian (night) | Lane, signs, VRU | ACC, AEB, BSD | HD map, localization | Parking |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — IR Sensor in ADAS Perception Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Object - Pedestrian, Animal, Vehicle, Driver] --> B[IR Radiation Emission - 8 to 14 um LWIR]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Path - Fog, Rain, Humidity Attenuation]
    C --> D[IR Optics - Germanium or Chalcogenide Lens]
    D --> E[Microbolometer FPA - 640x480 or 1024x768 Pixels]
    E --> F[Readout ASIC - Row Column Multiplexing]
    F --> G[Analog Front End - Amplification and ADC]
    G --> H[Thermal Image Buffer - Raw Thermogram]
    H --> I{ADAS ECU Processing Branch}
    I --> J[Non Uniformity Correction NUC]
    J --> K[Noise Reduction and Super Resolution]
    K --> L[CNN Classifier - YOLOv5 Thermal or EfficientDet]
    L --> M[Object Track and Trajectory Predictor]
    M --> N[Sensor Fusion with Radar and RGB]
    N --> O[Decision - AEB Warning LKA HMI Alert]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This flowchart traces the IR signal from emission by a real-world object, through the atmosphere, into the camera's optics, across the microbolometer focal plane array, into the readout electronics, and finally through the ADAS perception and fusion pipeline that decides whether to brake, steer, or warn.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Active IR Driver Monitoring System (DMS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[850 nm or 940 nm IR LED Array] --> optA[IR Bandpass Filter]
    optA --> driverFace[Driver Face and Eyes]
    driverFace --> optB[Camera Lens f 2.0]
    optB --> sensorA[NIR CMOS Sensor - 1 MP Global Shutter]
    sensorA --> ispA[ISP Pipeline - HDR Defog]
    ispA --> eyeDet[Eye Landmark Detection CNN]
    eyeDet --> gazeEst[Gaze Vector and PERCLOS Estimator]
    gazeEst --> drowsyOut{Drowsiness Detected}
    drowsyOut -- Yes --> alertOut[Audible Haptic Steering Vibration Alert]
    drowsyOut -- No --> contMon[Continue Monitoring Loop]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The DMS uses a co-located IR LED illuminator and NIR camera. The illuminator floods the driver's face with 850 nm light, the camera captures the reflected NIR image, and the perception stack extracts eye landmarks, gaze vector, and PERCLOS (percentage of eye closure) to detect drowsiness.

---

### 4.3 Comparative State Diagram — IR Sensor Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOnSelfTest
    PowerOnSelfTest --> Calibration: FFC Shutter Calibration OK
    Calibration --> PassiveThermal: Default LWIR Mode
    PassiveThermal --> ActiveIllumination: Low Contrast Scene
    ActiveIllumination --> PedestrianDetect: IR Signature Above Threshold
    PedestrianDetect --> Classification: ROI Cropped
    Classification --> TrackAndFusion: CNN Class Confidence Greater 0.8
    TrackAndFusion --> ActuatorCommand: AEB or HMI Trigger
    ActuatorCommand --> PassiveThermal: Cycle Continues
    Calibration --> FaultMode: Sensor Fault Detected
    FaultMode --> LimpHome: Degraded Operation
    LimpHome --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "Infrared Sensor" and list any two ADAS applications where IR sensors are essential.**

**Model Answer:**

An Infrared (IR) sensor is an electronic device that detects and measures infrared radiation (electromagnetic waves in the 0.7 µm – 1000 µm wavelength range) emitted or reflected by objects, converting it into an electrical signal proportional to temperature or reflectance.

**Two essential ADAS applications:**
1. **Night Vision Assist (NVA)** — LWIR thermal cameras detect pedestrians and animals beyond headlight range in total darkness.
2. **Driver Monitoring System (DMS)** — Active 850 nm NIR cameras track eye gaze and PERCLOS for drowsiness detection irrespective of ambient light.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Always mention the *wavelength range* in the definition — students frequently lose the 1 mark allotted for band specification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**State Wien's Displacement Law. Using it, calculate the peak emission wavelength of a human body at 310 K. Take Wien's constant b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K.**

**Model Answer:**

**Wien's Displacement Law** states that the peak emission wavelength of a blackbody is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}$$

**Calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{310} \\
&= 9.35 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 9.35 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A human body at 310 K emits maximum IR radiation at **9.35 µm**, which lies within the **LWIR (8–14 µm) atmospheric window** — justifying the choice of LWIR cameras for automotive pedestrian detection.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Module-Level)

---

**Module 2 — Infrared Sensors and Their Role in ADAS** (Choose **ONE** of the following)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **microbolometer-based LWIR thermal camera** used in automotive night-vision ADAS. Clearly state the role of each block from optics to output thermogram.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A pedestrian (assumed cylindrical, area 0.9 m², emissivity 0.98, temperature 305 K) is observed by a thermal camera. The lens diameter is 5 cm, and the optical + atmospheric transmittance is 0.7. Estimate the **maximum range** at which the pedestrian can be detected if the camera's **NETD is 50 mK** and detector sensitivity $P_{min}$ = 5 nW. Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A microbolometer-based LWIR camera consists of the following blocks:

1. **Germanium (Ge) or Chalcogenide Lens** — focuses 8–14 µm LWIR radiation onto the sensor. (Visible-light glass is opaque to LWIR.)
2. **Athermalized Lens Housing** — maintains focus across -40 °C to +85 °C automotive temperature range.
3. **Microbolometer Focal Plane Array (FPA)** — 640×480 or 1024×768 pixels, each pixel is a suspended VOₓ or a-Si membrane whose resistance changes with absorbed IR power.
4. **Readout Integrated Circuit (ROIC)** — multiplexes pixel signals row-by-row, performs correlated double sampling.
5. **TEC-less Temperature Stabilization** — non-uniformity correction (NUC) compensates for self-heating drift.
6. **Analog Front End + ADC** — converts analog pixel voltages to 14-bit digital thermograms.
7. **Output Interface** — USB 3.0, CSI-2, or Ethernet, feeding the ADAS ECU.

**Working:** Each microbolometer pixel absorbs IR photons, its temperature rises by $\Delta T \propto P_{IR}$, its resistance changes by $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, and the readout ASIC measures this $\Delta R$ as a voltage. The 2D array of $\Delta R$ values forms the thermal image.

**Advantages for ADAS:** Uncooled (no cryocooler → low power, long life), fast (60 Hz), passive (no laser safety issues), and excellent thermal contrast for pedestrians.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Using the radiometric range equation derived in Section 3.2:

$$
R_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \varepsilon \sigma A T^{3} \Delta T \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \tau}{4\pi P_{min}}}
$$

**Compute numerator term by term:**

- $4 \varepsilon \sigma A T^{3} \Delta T = 4 \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.9 \times (305)^{3} \times 0.05$
- $(305)^{3} = 2.838 \times 10^{7}$
- Numerator = $4 \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.9 \times 2.838 \times 10^{7} \times 0.05$
- Numerator = $4 \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 0.9 \times 2.838 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-8+7}$
- Numerator = $4 \times 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 0.9 \times 0.1419 \times 10^{-1}$
- Numerator = $4 \times 0.98 \times 0.7237 \times 10^{-1}$
- Numerator = $2.837 \times 10^{-1} = 0.2837$ (approx)

**Compute denominator:**
- $4\pi P_{min} = 4\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 6.283 \times 10^{-8}$

**Include lens area** $A_{lens} = \pi (0.025)^{2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m² and $\tau = 0.7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{0.2837 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7}{6.283 \times 10^{-8}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3.898 \times 10^{-4}}{6.283 \times 10^{-8}}} \\
&= \sqrt{6203.5} \\
&\approx 78.8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** A full radiometric derivation including the F-number and the spectral integral of $\partial L/\partial T$ over the LWIR band is required for an exact NETD-based computation. The above simplification assumes broadband radiance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - Students commonly **forget the factor of 4** in the Stefan–Boltzmann derivative ($\partial T^{4}/\partial T = 4T^{3}$). This causes a 2-mark deduction.
> - Do not confuse **emissivity ε** with **reflectivity ρ** — they are NOT the same quantity.
> - Always state the **lens material** (Germanium/Chalcogenide) in part (a); common glass absorbs LWIR and blocks the signal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Passive IR (Thermal)** and **Active IR (NIR illuminator + camera)** sensors in terms of principle, wavelength band, ADAS applications, advantages, and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An automotive DMS uses an 850 nm IR LED illuminator (0.5 W, eye-safety limited) and a NIR CMOS camera with lens area $10^{-4}$ m². If the driver's skin has reflectivity 0.4, optical efficiency 0.6, and the camera's minimum detectable power is 1 nW, compute the **maximum driver-monitoring range** using the co-located active-IR range equation.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Passive IR (Thermal Camera) | Active IR (NIR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Detects natural thermal emission from objects | Emits IR light and detects reflection |
| **Wavelength** | 8–14 µm (LWIR) | 850 nm or 940 nm (NIR) |
| **Detector** | Microbolometer (VOₓ) | Silicon CMOS / InGaAs photodiode |
| **Illumination** | None — passive | Active IR LED / VCSEL array |
| **Range** | 100–300 m | 0.3–1.5 m (in-cabin DMS) |
| **ADAS Application** | Night vision, pedestrian AEB, animal detection | Driver monitoring, occupant detection |
| **Advantage** | Works in total darkness, no laser safety, weather penetration | Cheap, high resolution, works on faces/eyes with controlled illumination |
| **Limitation** | Expensive, cannot read text, slow at 9 Hz–60 Hz | Limited range, requires emitter, eye-safety concerns above Class 1 |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Co-located active-IR round-trip range equation:

$$
R = \left( \frac{P_{t} \cdot \rho \cdot A_{r}^{2} \cdot \eta}{2\pi \cdot P_{min}} \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Substitute values:**
- $P_{t} = 0.5$ W
- $\rho = 0.4$
- $A_{r} = 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\eta = 0.6$
- $P_{min} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ W

**Numerator:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{t} \cdot \rho \cdot A_{r}^{2} \cdot \eta &= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times (10^{-4})^{2} \times 0.6 \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.6 \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Denominator:**
$$
2\pi \cdot P_{min} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-9} = 6.283 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Range:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \left( \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{6.283 \times 10^{-9}} \right)^{1/4} \\
&= (0.191)^{1/4} \\
&\approx 0.66 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** ~0.66 m maximum range — well-matched to the steering-column-to-driver distance of 0.6–0.8 m, validating the DMS hardware design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> - Many students write the **radar range equation** ($R \propto P^{1/4}$) but **omit the factor of 2 in the denominator** when using the co-located active-IR form. This causes a 1-mark deduction.
> - Unit mismatch: ensure $A_r$ is in **m²** (not cm²) and $P_{min}$ is in **watts** before substitution.
> - In part (a), do NOT list 5+ points without organizing into a table — examiners reward **structured comparison** heavily.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IR spectrum in ADAS:** NIR (0.7–1.4 µm) for active DMS, LWIR (8–14 µm) for passive thermal night vision.
- **Human peak emission:** ~9.35 µm at 310 K → justifies LWIR camera choice (Wien's law).
- **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $j^{\ast} = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ — total emitted power scales with T⁴.
- **Microbolometer:** uncooled, TCR-based, dominates automotive LWIR sensors.
- **Active NIR uses 850 nm** (visible faint red glow) or 940 nm (fully covert).
- **Germanium/Chalcogenide lenses** are mandatory for LWIR; standard glass is opaque.
- **NETD** is the key figure of merit for thermal cameras — aim for < 50 mK.
- **Eye-safety** (IEC 60825 Class 1) is a hard regulatory constraint for active IR.
- **Range equation for active IR:** $R \propto (P_t \cdot \rho \cdot A_r^2 / P_{min})^{1/4}$.
- **Range equation for passive IR:** $R \propto (P_{emit} \cdot A_{lens} \cdot \tau / P_{min})^{1/2}$.
- **Pedestrian detection range:** ~250 m in clear night (LWIR) — adds 2–3 s reaction time at 100 km/h.
- **IR is complementary to RADAR and RGB** — IR provides *thermal/classification* signature, RADAR provides *kinematic range*, RGB provides *visual detail*.
- **Fog penetration:** LWIR > NIR > visible — the reason thermal cameras are preferred over RGB in adverse weather.
- **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)** at night relies heavily on IR + radar fusion — IR provides the object class, radar provides the closing rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Data Fusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Data Fusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor Data Fusion (SDF)** in the context of Autonomous Driving and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) is formally defined as the **multi-stage process of integrating, correlating, and combining data from multiple heterogeneous sensors** (cameras, RADAR, LiDAR, ultrasonic, GNSS/IMU) to produce a unified, accurate, timely, and complete representation of the vehicle's internal state and its external operating environment, thereby reducing uncertainty that would otherwise exist if each sensor were used independently.

According to the **Joint Directors of Laboratories (JDL) Data Fusion Model** — the canonical reference architecture adopted worldwide for military and automotive perception — sensor fusion is a hierarchical cognitive process with six levels:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JDL Fusion Levels (Adapted for ADAS):**
> - **Level 0 — Source Pre-Processing:** Raw signal conditioning (denoising, A/D conversion, calibration).
> - **Level 1 — Object Refinement (Detection/Tracking):** Spatio-temporal alignment, state estimation, classification.
> - **Level 2 — Situation Refinement:** Relationship between entities (e.g., vehicle-pedestrian proximity).
> - **Level 3 — Threat/Impact Assessment:** Risk evaluation (TTC, collision probability).
> - **Level 4 — Process Refinement:** Adaptive sensor/resource management.
> - **Level 5 — Cognitive Refinement:** Mission-level decision support.

For ADAS, the most intensely studied levels are **Levels 1, 2, and 3**, with Level 0 being a prerequisite.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a busy intersection with your **eyes closed but ears open**. You can roughly *hear* that a car is approaching, but you have **no idea** whether it is 5 meters or 50 meters away, whether it is a truck or a motorcycle, or which lane it occupies.

Now, **open your eyes** — you see the vehicle clearly. Your brain instantly **merges** the auditory cue (distance + presence) with the visual cue (type + lane + speed). The combined perception is **vastly superior** to either sense alone. This is exactly what **Sensor Data Fusion** does — it is the *brain* of the autonomous vehicle.

| Human Sense | Automotive Equivalent | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 👁️ Vision | Camera (Mono/Stereo) | Rich semantic info, lane markings, signs | Fails in fog/darkness |
| 👂 Hearing | Microphone / Ultrasonic | Close-range, robust | Poor range resolution |
| 🦇 Echolocation | Ultrasonic / Radar | Excellent range, all-weather | Low resolution |
| 🦅 3-D Mapping | LiDAR | Precise 3-D geometry | Expensive, weather-degraded |
| 🧭 Inner ear balance | IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) | High-rate ego-motion | Drifts over time |
| 🛰️ GPS | GNSS Receiver | Global absolute position | Low rate, multipath errors |

> [!NOTE]
> **No single sensor is sufficient for SAE Level 3+ autonomy.** Multi-modal fusion is therefore not optional — it is a *safety-critical necessity*.

---

## 1.3 Standard Automotive Performance Metrics

The following performance metrics are commonly evaluated for any sensor fusion stack and are **bold-highlighted** because examiners frequently test them:

- **Detection Range:** **250 m** for automotive forward RADAR, **200 m** for LiDAR.
- **Angular Resolution:** **0.1°–0.25°** for LiDAR, **~1°** for RADAR.
- **Update Rate:** Camera **30–60 Hz**, RADAR **20 Hz**, LiDAR **10–20 Hz**, IMU **100–1000 Hz**.
- **Time-to-Collision (TTC) Threshold:** **≤ 2.7 s** (Euro NCAP AEB standard).
- **Object-Level Position Accuracy:** **±0.1 m** (LiDAR-grade).
- **Velocity Accuracy:** **±0.1 m/s**.

---

## 1.4 Why Fusion? The Case for Combining Sensors

The motivations are non-negotiable:

1. **Complementary Coverage** — Cameras see *colour/shape*; RADAR sees *range/velocity*. Together: complete situational awareness.
2. **Redundancy (Fault Tolerance)** — If LiDAR fails in heavy rain, RADAR + camera maintain perception (**ASIL-D functional safety**).
3. **Reduced Uncertainty** — Combined noise variance is **lower** than any single sensor (formalized by Cramer-Rao bound).
4. **Increased Confidence (Probability)** — Bayesian fusion raises detection confidence above detection thresholds.
5. **Extended Operational Domain (ODD)** — 24/7 operation across day, night, snow, fog, tunnels.
6. **Lower Latency Decision-Making** — Cross-validated measurements accelerate object confirmation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensor Fusion

### A. By **Architectural Level** (Data Abstraction)

| Fusion Level | What is Fused? | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Early / Low-Level (Data-Level)** | Raw sensor data (pixels + point clouds) | Highest information retention | Requires precise calibration, large bandwidth |
| **Intermediate (Feature-Level)** | Extracted features (edges, clusters, bounding boxes) | Balanced trade-off | Information loss |
| **Late / High-Level (Decision-Level)** | Independent per-sensor object lists (tracks) | Modular, fault-tolerant | No cross-sensor synergy at low level |

### B. By **System Topology**

| Topology | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralized Fusion** | Raw data from all sensors sent to one central processor | High-bandwidth buses (Ethernet 1000BASE-T1) |
| **Decentralized (Peer-to-Peer)** | Each sensor has its own processor; only track-lists exchanged | CAN-FD automotive ECUs |
| **Distributed Fusion** | Sub-fusion nodes combine groups of sensors before global fusion | Modular AV stacks (Apollo, Autoware) |
| **Hierarchical Fusion** | Multi-layer: raw → local → global | Production ADAS |

### C. By **Algorithmic Paradigm**

- **Probabilistic:** Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, Unscented Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Bayesian Networks.
- **Evidential:** Dempster-Shafer Theory.
- **Optimization-Based:** Graph SLAM, Iterative Closest Point (ICP).
- **Learning-Based:** Deep Fusion Networks, PointPainting, TransFusion.
- **Hybrid:** KF + Neural Networks (e.g., Deep Kalman Filters).

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Prerequisites

Every fusion algorithm rests on three foundational concepts:

### I. Bayesian Inference

The cornerstone of probabilistic fusion. Given prior belief $P(H)$ about hypothesis $H$ and new evidence $E$, the updated belief is:

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading:** "The **posterior** equals the likelihood times the prior, divided by the evidence." This is the engine driving every recursive fusion update.

### II. Gaussian Distributions

Most closed-form fusion algorithms (KF family) assume measurements and noise are Gaussian:

$$x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

where $\mu$ is the **mean** and $\sigma^{2}$ is the **variance**.

### III. State-Space Representation

The vehicle's state at time $k$ is denoted:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z & \dot{x} & \dot{y} & \dot{z} & \psi & \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$$

This contains position $(x,y,z)$, velocity $(\dot{x},\dot{y},\dot{z})$, heading $\psi$, and yaw-rate $\dot{\psi}$.

---

## 2.3 The Kalman Filter — The Heart of Real-Time Fusion

The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is the optimal recursive estimator for linear-Gaussian systems. It runs in two recurring steps: **Predict** and **Update**.

### Prediction Step

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_{k}\,\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1} + \mathbf{B}_{k}\,\mathbf{u}_{k}$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}_{k}\,\mathbf{P}_{k-1\mid k-1}\,\mathbf{F}_{k}^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}_{k}$$

### Update (Measurement) Step

$$\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{k} = \mathbf{z}_{k} - \mathbf{H}_{k}\,\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}$$

$$\mathbf{S}_{k} = \mathbf{H}_{k}\,\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\,\mathbf{H}_{k}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_{k}$$

$$\mathbf{K}_{k} = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\,\mathbf{H}_{k}^{\top}\,\mathbf{S}_{k}^{-1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_{k}\,\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{k}$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_{k}\,\mathbf{H}_{k})\,\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{F}_{k}$ = State transition matrix
- $\mathbf{B}_{k}$ = Control-input matrix
- $\mathbf{u}_{k}$ = Control input (e.g., steering angle, throttle)
- $\mathbf{Q}_{k}$ = Process noise covariance
- $\mathbf{H}_{k}$ = Observation matrix
- $\mathbf{R}_{k}$ = Measurement noise covariance
- $\mathbf{P}$ = State estimate covariance (uncertainty)
- $\mathbf{K}_{k}$ = **Kalman Gain** (optimal blending factor)
- $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{k}$ = Innovation / measurement residual

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $\mathbf{K}_k$:** If $\mathbf{R}_k \to 0$ (perfect sensor), $\mathbf{K}_k \to 1$ (trust the measurement fully). If $\mathbf{Q}_k \to 0$ (perfect model), $\mathbf{K}_k \to 0$ (trust the prediction). The Kalman Gain is the *optimal honesty dial*.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Use / When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Probabilistic fusion of two hypotheses |
| 2 | Constant-Velocity State Transition | $\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Ego-vehicle motion model |
| 3 | Constant-Acceleration Transition | $\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t & \tfrac{\Delta t^{2}}{2} \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Lead-vehicle tracking |
| 4 | Kalman Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}\,\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\mid k-1}$ | Forward-propagate state |
| 5 | Kalman Covariance Prediction | $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{P}\,\mathbf{F}^{\top} + \mathbf{Q}$ | Uncertainty propagation |
| 6 | Kalman Gain | $\mathbf{K}_{k} = \mathbf{P}\,\mathbf{H}^{\top}(\mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{P}\,\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Optimal weighting |
| 7 | KF State Update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \hat{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\,\tilde{\mathbf{y}}$ | Correct prediction with measurement |
| 8 | KF Covariance Update | $\mathbf{P} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})\mathbf{P}$ | Reduce uncertainty after measurement |
| 9 | Innovation Covariance | $\mathbf{S}_{k} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}$ | Mahalanobis gating |
| 10 | Weighted Scalar Fusion | $\hat{x} = \dfrac{\sigma_{2}^{2}\,x_{1} + \sigma_{1}^{2}\,x_{2}}{\sigma_{1}^{2}+\sigma_{2}^{2}}$ | Two-sensor optimum under independence |
| 11 | Fused Variance | $\sigma^{2}_{fused} = \dfrac{\sigma_{1}^{2}\sigma_{2}^{2}}{\sigma_{1}^{2}+\sigma_{2}^{2}}$ | Two-sensor minimum-variance |
| 12 | Dempster-Shafer Combine | $(m_{1}\oplus m_{2})(C) = \dfrac{\sum_{A\cap B=C}m_{1}(A)\,m_{2}(B)}{1-K}$ | Conflicting evidence fusion |
| 13 | Conflict Mass $K$ | $K = \sum_{A\cap B=\varnothing}m_{1}(A)\,m_{2}(B)$ | DS normalization constant |
| 14 | EKF Jacobian | $\mathbf{F}_{jac} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\bigg\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$ | Nonlinear state model linearization |
| 15 | UKF Sigma Points | $\chi_{i} = \bar{x} \pm (\sqrt{(n+\lambda)\,\mathbf{P}})_{i}$ | Unscented transform for non-Gaussian |
| 16 | Cramer-Rao Lower Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{x}) \geq \dfrac{1}{\mathcal{I}(x)}$ | Theoretical minimum variance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KF validity:**
> (i) Linear system dynamics; (ii) Gaussian process & measurement noise; (iii) Zero-mean white noise. If any of these are violated → use **EKF**, **UKF**, or **Particle Filter**.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

In the automotive industry, these formulas underpin:

- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** KF estimates distance to leading vehicle using fused RADAR + camera.
- **Lane Centering Assist:** Combines lane-detection (camera) with yaw-rate (IMU) via complementary filter.
- **Automated Emergency Braking (AEB):** Tracks pedestrians via LiDAR-camera fusion; triggers brake if TTC < **2.7 s**.
- **Self-Localization (GNSS/IMU fusion):** Tightly-coupled ESKF in **Tesla FSD** and **Mercedes Drive Pilot**.
- **Occupancy Grid Mapping (OGM):** Bayesian fusion of LiDAR scans for static environment representation.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Two-Sensor Weighted Fusion

**Problem Setup:** Two independent sensors measure the same scalar quantity (e.g., vehicle speed). Sensor 1 reports $x_1$ with variance $\sigma_1^2$; Sensor 2 reports $x_2$ with variance $\sigma_2^2$. Derive the **minimum-variance unbiased estimate** $\hat{x}$.

**Step 1 — Assume Linear Estimator.**

Because the measurements are jointly Gaussian and unbiased, the optimal estimator is linear:

$$\hat{x} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2$$

**Step 2 — Unbiasedness Constraint.**

$$E[\hat{x}] = w_1 E[x_1] + w_2 E[x_2] = (w_1+w_2)\,x = x$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Minimize Total Variance.**

$$\sigma^{2}_{fused} = E\!\left[(\hat{x}-x)^{2}\right] = w_1^{2}\sigma_1^{2} + w_2^{2}\sigma_2^{2}$$

Substitute $w_2 = 1 - w_1$:

$$\sigma^{2}_{fused} = w_1^{2}\sigma_1^{2} + (1-w_1)^{2}\sigma_2^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and Set to Zero.**

$$\frac{d\sigma^{2}_{fused}}{dw_1} = 2w_1\sigma_1^{2} - 2(1-w_1)\sigma_2^{2} = 0$$

Solve:

$$w_1\sigma_1^{2} = (1-w_1)\sigma_2^{2}$$

$$w_1\sigma_1^{2} = \sigma_2^{2} - w_1\sigma_2^{2}$$

$$w_1(\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}) = \sigma_2^{2}$$

$$w_1 = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}, \qquad w_2 = \frac{\sigma_1^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Fused Estimate.**

$$\hat{x} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}\,x_1 + \frac{\sigma_1^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}\,x_2$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{x} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}\,x_1 + \sigma_1^{2}\,x_2}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Fused Variance.**

Substitute $w_1$ back:

$$\sigma_{fused}^{2} = w_1\sigma_1^{2} = \frac{\sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}\,\sigma_1^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{fused}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_1^{2}\sigma_2^{2}}{\sigma_1^{2}+\sigma_2^{2}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Significance:** The fused variance is *always* **smaller** than either $\sigma_1^2$ or $\sigma_2^2$. This is the **classical mathematical proof** that fusion reduces uncertainty — the central engineering motivation for sensor fusion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kalman Gain as Optimal Linear Combiner

**Step 1 — Re-state the Innovation.**

The state update is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}_{k}(\mathbf{z}_{k} - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1})$$

**Step 2 — Define Estimation Error.**

$$\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k}, \qquad \mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1} = \mathbf{x}_{k} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Measurement Model** $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1})$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{v}_k$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k} = \mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{v}_k$$

$$\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})\,\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{v}_k$$

**Step 4 — Form the Posterior Covariance.**

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = E\!\left[\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k}\,\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k}^{\top}\right]$$

Assuming uncorrelated $\mathbf{e}_{k\mid k-1}$ and $\mathbf{v}_k$:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})\,\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})^{\top} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{K}^{\top}$$

**Step 5 — Minimize the Trace of $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{K}$.**

Setting $\dfrac{\partial \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P})}{\partial \mathbf{K}} = 0$ yields:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top} - \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}) = 0$$

Solving for $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{K}_{k} = \mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\,\mathbf{H}^{\top}\,(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}\mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R})^{-1}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back for Final Covariance.**

Using the matrix inversion lemma, the Joseph form is:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})\,\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k-1}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}\mathbf{H})^{\top} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{K}^{\top}$$

This guarantees $\mathbf{P}_{k\mid k}$ remains **symmetric positive-definite**, a numerical-stability requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Update of Object Existence (Grid Cell)

For occupancy-grid mapping, let $p(m_i)$ denote the prior probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied. A sensor returns evidence $z$ with false-positive rate $\alpha$ and detection rate $\beta$. Apply log-odds form for numerical stability:

$$l_i = \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}$$

Recursive log-odds update:

$$l_{i,t} = l_{i,t-1} + \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t)} - l_{i,0}$$

With inverse sensor model:

$$p(m_i \mid z_t = \text{occupied}) = \beta, \qquad p(m_i \mid z_t = \text{free}) = 1 - \alpha$$

Therefore:

$$l_{i,t} = l_{i,t-1} + \begin{cases} \log\dfrac{\beta}{1-\beta}, & z_t = \text{occupied at cell } i \\[4pt] \log\dfrac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}, & z_t = \text{free at cell } i \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log-odds?** Probabilities multiply on each update; log-odds **add**. This converts Bayesian fusion into a computationally trivial operation suitable for millions of grid cells updated at LiDAR frame rate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fusion Algorithm Selection

| Algorithm | Linearity | Noise Assumption | Computational Cost | Accuracy | Automotive Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Average** | Linear | Gaussian | O(1) | Low | Backup sensor voting |
| **KF** | Linear | Gaussian | O(n³) | Optimal (linear) | RADAR + IMU |
| **EKF** | Mildly nonlinear | Gaussian | O(n³) | 1st-order approx | GNSS/INS |
| **UKF** | Nonlinear | Gaussian | O(n³) | 2nd-order, no Jacobians | LiDAR odometry |
| **Particle Filter** | Any | Any | O(N·n) | High (samples) | Lane-tracking in clutter |
| **Dempster-Shafer** | Any | Ignorance allowed | Exponential | Handles conflict | Threat/risk assessment |
| **Bayesian Net** | Any | Discrete/CPD | Inference-hard | Probabilistic reasoning | Driver-intent prediction |
| **Deep Fusion NN** | Learned | Data-driven | Inference O(layers) | SOTA accuracy | Camera-Radar object detection |

Where $n$ = state dimension, $N$ = number of particles.

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Recursive Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize x0 P0] --> S1[Time Update Predict x hat k given k-1]
    S1 --> S2[Propagate Covariance P k given k-1 F P F transpose plus Q]
    S2 --> S3[Receive Measurement z k]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Innovation y tilde z k minus H x hat]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Kalman Gain K P H transpose S inverse]
    S5 --> S6[Update State x hat k equals x hat plus K y tilde]
    S6 --> S7[Update Covariance P k I minus K H P k given k-1]
    S7 --> S8{More Measurements?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S1
    S8 -- No --> S9[Output x hat and P]
```

**Reading:** Two-phase recursive loop — **Predict** propagates the state and covariance forward using the motion model; **Update** injects the new sensor measurement, blending via the optimal Kalman Gain.

---

## 4.3 JDL Fusion Model for ADAS (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC0[Level 0 Source Pre-Processing] --> SRC1[Level 1 Object Refinement]
    SRC1 --> SRC2[Level 2 Situation Refinement]
    SRC2 --> SRC3[Level 3 Threat Assessment]
    SRC3 --> SRC4[Level 4 Process Refinement]
    SRC4 --> SRC5[Level 5 Cognitive Refinement]

    SRC4 -.feedback.-> SRC0
    SRC4 -.feedback.-> SRC1
    SRC5 -.mission.-> SRC3
```

**Reading:** Linear progression of abstraction levels with feedback from Level 4 (resource management) to lower levels, and mission context from Level 5 to Level 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

        LF2[Local Fusion Node B] --> GF
    end
```

**Reading:** Three architectural alternatives — Centralized (raw data to one processor), Decentralized (smart sensors exchange tracks only), and Distributed Hierarchical (group-level fusion feeds global fusion).
