
### 4.3 Simulation Tools Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Scenario Definition in OpenSCENARIO] --> S2[Map Setup in OpenDRIVE]
    S2 --> S3[Sensor Configuration LiDAR Camera Radar]
    S3 --> S4[Run Simulation in CARLA or PreScan]
    S4 --> S5[Log Output Trajectory and Events]
    S5 --> S6{Performance Pass?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Tune Algorithm and Return to S4]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Export to HIL Bench]
    S8 --> S9[Real ECU Testing]
    S9 --> S10[Vehicle Track Testing]
```

### 4.4 MiL to DiL Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MIL[MiL: Pure Math Model in MATLAB] --> SIL[SiL: Compiled Code on Host PC]
    SIL --> HIL[HiL: Real ECU + Simulated Sensors]
    HIL --> DIL[DiL: Driver in Simulator Cockpit]
    DIL --> TR[Track Testing on Proving Ground]
    TR --> RD[Public Road Deployment]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Q1. Define "Middleware" in the context of an autonomous vehicle software stack. Name two industry-standard middlewares.**

**Model Answer:**
Middleware is a software layer that sits between the Real-Time Operating System and the functional application modules. It provides standardized communication mechanisms (publish/subscribe, service calls), message serialization, time synchronization, and discovery services, so that perception, planning, and control modules written by different teams can interoperate seamlessly.

Two industry-standard middlewares are:
1. **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2)** — open-source, based on DDS, widely used in research and prototyping.
2. **AUTOSAR Adaptive (ARA)** — automotive-grade, used in production Level 3+ vehicles, supports POSIX OS.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Model-in-the-Loop (MiL) and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL) testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MiL | HiL |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | None — pure math/simulation | Real ECU under test |
| Purpose | Early algorithm validation | Late-stage integration validation |
| Cost | Very low | High (real ECUs + simulator) |
| Speed | Fast (faster than real-time) | Real-time |
| Fidelity | Abstract | Near-production |

MiL is performed in MATLAB/Simulink using block diagrams of the algorithm, whereas HiL uses real ECUs connected to a sensor/vehicle simulator (e.g., dSPACE, NI VeriStand).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common pitfalls students fall into on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing MiL with SiL** — many students swap definitions. Remember: MiL = math model, SiL = compiled source code.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Why"** — never describe a tool without justifying *why* it is used (cost, safety, repeatability).
> 3. **Skipping the latency budget** — KTU loves latency arithmetic. Always include the numerical calculation.
> 4. **Ignoring open standards** — OpenSCENARIO, OpenDRIVE, and ASAM are frequently asked.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered software architecture of an autonomous vehicle. Mention the role of each layer and name one technology/tool used at each layer.

**Model Solution:**

The autonomous vehicle software is organized as a **5-layer stack**:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):**
Provides a uniform software interface to physical sensors and actuators, hiding the hardware-specific register-level details from upper layers. *Example technology:* Nvidia DriveOS HAL, Camera Serial Interface (CSI) drivers.

**Layer 2 — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):**
Schedules perception, planning, and control tasks with deterministic deadlines. *Example:* QNX Neutrino (used in Audi A8 Level 3 system).

**Layer 3 — Middleware:**
Enables message passing between distributed software nodes using publish/subscribe or request/reply patterns. Handles clock synchronization and Quality-of-Service. *Example:* ROS 2 with Cyclone DDS, or AUTOSAR Adaptive ara::com.

**Layer 4 — Functional / Application Modules:**
Contains the actual autonomy logic:
- **Perception** — CNN-based object detection (e.g., YOLOv8).
- **Localization** — NDT scan matching against HD maps.
- **Prediction** — LSTM trajectory forecasting.
- **Planning** — Frenet-frame trajectory optimization.
- **Control** — Model Predictive Control (MPC).

**Layer 5 — Cloud, Fleet & OTA:**
Manages HD map updates, fleet learning, and Over-The-Air software updates. *Example:* AWS IoT, Tesla's in-house fleet.

Each layer communicates only with its immediate neighbour, enforcing **modularity** and **safety isolation**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A perception algorithm has latency 35 ms, planning 28 ms, control 6 ms, sensor acquisition 12 ms, and actuator response 4 ms. Compute the total end-to-end latency and state whether the system meets the ISO 21448 SOTIF Level-3 budget of 100 ms.

**Model Solution:**

Total latency = sum of all sequential layer delays:

$$
\tau_{total} = 12 + 35 + 28 + 6 + 4 = 85 \text{ ms}
$$

Comparison with budget:

$$
\tau_{total} = 85 \text{ ms} \quad < \quad 100 \text{ ms}
$$

The system **comfortably meets** the SOTIF Level-3 latency budget with a safety margin of 15 ms. This margin allows for occasional OS scheduling jitter without violating the deadline.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the V-Model as applied to ADAS software development. List the test levels on the right arm of the V and briefly describe any three.

**Model Solution:**

The **V-Model** is a development framework where each design phase on the left descending arm has a corresponding test/validation phase on the right ascending arm. It enforces traceability between requirements and validation evidence — essential for ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification.

**Test levels (right arm):**

1. **Unit Testing:** Each software function (e.g., a single CNN layer) is tested in isolation against its specification. Performed at the code module level.

2. **Integration Testing (SIL):** Multiple modules are integrated and tested together as a software stack running on a host PC without real hardware. Verifies inter-module interfaces, e.g., does the perception output correctly feed the planner?

3. **Hardware-in-the-Loop Testing (HIL):** Real production ECUs are connected to a real-time simulator (e.g., dSPACE) that injects synthetic sensor signals and reads back actuator commands. Verifies that the actual ECU hardware works correctly with the compiled code.

4. **Vehicle Validation (DiL / Track / Public Road):** The complete system is tested with a human driver in a vehicle simulator, on a closed test track, and finally on public roads with safety oversight.

The V-Model's strength is **bidirectional traceability**: every test on the right arm can be traced back to a requirement on the left arm, satisfying auditors.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare any four open-source and commercial simulation tools used for ADAS development. Mention one specific strength and weakness of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Tool | Type | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CARLA** | Open-source | High-fidelity graphics, Python API, free for research | Linux-only, steep learning curve |
| **Gazebo** | Open-source | Tight ROS integration, lightweight, modular | Lower visual fidelity than CARLA |
| **PreScan** | Commercial | Industry-grade sensor models incl. weather, friction | Commercial license, expensive |
| **CarMaker** | Commercial | OEM-grade vehicle dynamics, IPG road model | Very high cost, learning curve |

Additional notes:
- **CARLA** is preferred for ML training because it provides ground-truth bounding boxes out-of-the-box.
- **Gazebo** is preferred for academic projects due to its tight coupling with ROS 1 / ROS 2.
- **PreScan** is widely cited in published ADAS research because of its robust physics.
- **CarMaker** is preferred by OEMs like BMW and Toyota for closed-track correlation work.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Software Development and Simulation Tools":**

- ✅ The AV software stack has **5 layers**: HAL → RTOS → Middleware → Application → Cloud/OTA.
- ✅ **ROS 2** and **AUTOSAR Adaptive** are the two dominant middlewares.
- ✅ The **V-Model** ensures traceability between requirements and validation.
- ✅ The 4 test levels are **MiL → SiL → HiL → DiL** (in increasing fidelity).
- ✅ **CARLA, PreScan, Gazebo, MATLAB/Simulink** are the most-asked simulation tools.
- ✅ **OpenSCENARIO** describes dynamic scenarios; **OpenDRIVE** describes road networks.
- ✅ End-to-end latency for Level 3 must be **≤ 100 ms**.
- ✅ **Sensor frame rates:** LiDAR 10–20 Hz, Camera 30–60 Hz, Radar 20 Hz.
- ✅ **Bicycle model:** $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan(\delta)}{L}$ — memorize for control derivations.
- ✅ Simulation covers **both nominal and critical scenarios**.
- ✅ **HiL** uses real ECUs + simulated sensors; **DiL** involves a real driver.
- ✅ Industry ratio: **1 hour real-world ≈ 10 hours of simulation**.
- ✅ Safety certification standards: **ISO 26262** (functional safety) and **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (safety of intended functionality).
- ✅ Cloud layer enables **OTA updates** and **fleet learning**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning and Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Path Planning and Control Systems # Module 3 — Path Planning and Control Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Path Planning and Control Systems** form the executive brain of an Autonomous Vehicle (AV) architecture. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for PEAUT632, this module addresses the dual responsibilities of the **Planning** and **Control** layers within the standard SAE J3016 Level 3+ automation pipeline. - **Path Planning** is the algorithmic process of computing a geometrically and kinematically feasible sequence of states from an ego-vehicle's current configuration to a goal configuration, while respecting static obstacles (HD map boundaries, lane geometry) and dynamic constraints (vehicles, pedestrians). - **Control Systems** is the low-level execution layer that converts the planned trajectory into real-time actuator commands — steering angle (δ), acceleration (a), and jerk (j) — minimizing tracking error between the planned reference and the actual vehicle state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Path Planning is *not* a single algorithm; it is a **hierarchical decomposition** comprising (a) **Route Planning** (road-level, A* / Dijkstra on HD map), (b) **Behavioral Planning** (lane-change, intersection arbitration), and (c) **Local Trajectory Planning** (RRT*, lattice, Frenet-frame optimization). The control layer follows it via **Lateral** (steering) and **Longitudinal** (throttle/brake) tracking controllers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kerala Auto-Rickshaw Navigator Imagine you are a passenger telling an auto-rickshaw driver in Kochi how to reach the airport from MG Road: 1. **You first consult Google Maps** (Route Planning on a digital map — chooses the highway). 2. **You then watch traffic and signal the driver** — *"change to the left lane, overtake the car, stop at the red light"* (Behavioral Planning — high-level decisions). 3. **The driver, meanwhile, continuously steers the wheel** to keep the auto exactly on the lane center, accelerating and braking to maintain a safe distance from the KSRTC bus ahead (Local Trajectory + Control — millisecond-level execution). The **Planner is the passenger's strategy**; the **Controller is the driver's hands and feet**. The planner thinks in seconds-to-minutes; the controller acts in **milliseconds (typically 10 ms to 100 ms)** with **control frequencies of 10 Hz to 100 Hz**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Industry Metric:** Modern AV stacks (Waymo, Cruise, Apollo) execute the perception → prediction → **planning → control** loop at **10 Hz** for planning and **100 Hz** for control. Failure to meet real-time deadlines is classified as a **safety-of-the-intended-functionality (SOTIF)** violation per ISO 21448. ### 1.3 Standard Coordinate Frames in KTU AV Stack | Frame | Notation | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Global / World Frame** | $(X, Y, Z)$ in UTM / ENU | HD map reference | | **Map Frame** | $(x_m, y_m)$ | Route-level planning | | **Vehicle (Body) Frame** | $(x_v, y_v)$ | Origin at rear-axle midpoint | | **Frenet Frame** | $(s, d)$ | Along-track and lateral offset w.r.t. lane centerline | The Frenet frame is the **de-facto standard** in KTU/Apollo/Autoware curricula because it decouples longitudinal progress $s$ from lateral deviation $d$, simplifying motion planning. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Path Planning ### 2.1.1 Global Route Planning Operates on the HD map (Lanelet2 / OpenDRIVE format). Solves a **shortest-path problem on a directed weighted graph** $G = (V, E, W)$ where vertices are lane-centers and edge weights are travel-time costs. Algorithms taught in KTU: - **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — $O(V^2)$ worst-case, guarantees optimality. - **A\* Search** — uses heuristic $h(n)$, reduces search space. Admissible heuristic: $h(n) \le \text{true cost}$. KTU commonly uses Euclidean or Manhattan heuristics. - **Bidirectional A\*** — searches from both start and goal, meets in the middle. ### 2.1.2 Behavioral / Mission Planning A **finite state machine (FSM)** or **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** that arbitrates high-level maneuvers: `Lane Keep`, `Lane Change Left`, `Stop at Intersection`, `Yield to VRU`. Output is a *target lane* and *target speed profile*. ### 2.1.3 Local Trajectory Planning Generates a smooth, collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory over a finite horizon ($T_h = 3\text{ s}$ to $8\text{ s}$). Standard families: - **Sampling-based:** RRT, RRT\* — probabilistically complete, asymptotically optimal. - **Search-based:** Hybrid A\*, State Lattice. - **Optimization-based:** Frenet-frame quadratic programming (Apollo EM planner), Model Predictive Control (MPC). - **Learning-based:** Imitation learning / RL (research-only in KTU syllabus). ## 2.2 Control Architecture — Lateral and Longitudinal Tracking The control layer is split into two parallel threads operating at **different bandwidths**: | Thread | Output | Typical Algorithm | Loop Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Longitudinal | Throttle $\alpha$ / Brake $\beta$ | PID, PI-cruise, MPC | $20\text{ Hz} - 50\text{ Hz}$ | | Lateral | Steering angle $\delta$ | Pure Pursuit, Stanley, MPC | $50\text{ Hz} - 100\text{ Hz}$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU frequently tests the difference between geometric (Pure Pursuit, Stanley) and model-based (MPC, LQR) controllers.** Geometric controllers ignore vehicle dynamics and only use kinematic geometry — simple but unreliable at high speed. Model-based controllers use a vehicle model to predict future behavior — robust but computationally heavier. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every equation** you must memorize for the KTU Board Examination on this module. Units are SI throughout unless explicitly noted. | # | Equation | Symbol Legend | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$ | $x$: longitudinal position | Kinematic bicycle model | | 2 | $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$ | $y$: lateral position | Kinematic bicycle model | | 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ | $\theta$: heading, $\delta$: steer, $L$: wheelbase | Yaw rate equation | | 4 | $\delta_{pp} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L \sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$ | $\alpha$: pursuit angle, $l_d$: look-ahead distance | Pure Pursuit steering | | 5 | $\delta_{st} = \theta_e + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k\, e_y}{v(t)}\right)$ | $e_y$: cross-track error, $k$: gain | Stanley controller | | 6 | $l_d(t) = l_{d,\min} + K_{ld}\, v(t)$ | Speed-adaptive lookahead | Pure Pursuit tuning | | 7 | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \Vert x_k - x_{ref,k}\Vert^2_Q + \Vert u_k \Vert^2_R$ | $Q, R$: weighting matrices | MPC cost function | | 8 | $a_{cmd} = k_p\, e_s + k_i \int e_s\, dt + k_d\, \dot{e}_s$ | $e_s = v_{ref} - v$ | Longitudinal PID | | 9 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g$: cost-so-far, $h$: heuristic | A\* evaluation function | | 10 | $\tau_{replan} \le T_{safety} = \dfrac{d_{min}}{v_{ego}}$ | Hard real-time deadline | SOTIF constraint | | 11 | $R_{turn} = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | Ackermann geometry | Minimum turning radius | | 12 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\dot\theta}{v} = \dfrac{\tan\delta}{L}$ | Path curvature | Lateral acceleration cap | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **Pure Pursuit** (uses $\arctan$ of $2L \sin\alpha / l_d$) with **Stanley** (uses $\arctan$ of $k\, e_y / v$). At $v \to 0$, Stanley's steering angle blows up — this is the **"divide-by-zero" pitfall** the KTU examiner loves to test. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pure Pursuit** is used by **Apollo Auto, Autoware, and most Tier-1 ADAS stacks** for low-speed parking and lane-keeping due to its extreme simplicity (one trigonometric call). - **Stanley Controller** won the 2005 DARPA Grand Challenge (Stanford's Stanley) and is still used as a baseline in many KTU M.Tech projects. - **MPC** is the industrial choice for **high-speed highway autonomy** (Bosch, Continental) because it explicitly enforces actuator and stability constraints. - **Frenet-frame planners** power the **Apollo EM Planner** and **Tier-IV Autoware**, and are explicitly referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus under "trajectory planning in road coordinates." # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Kinematic Bicycle Model The kinematic bicycle model is the **foundational building block** of nearly every KTU autonomous-driving controller. We derive it from first principles assuming **no tire slip** (low-speed / dry-road assumption). ### Step 1 — Ackermann Steering Geometry A real car has four wheels. The two front wheels must turn at slightly different angles to avoid tire scrub. The **virtual center of the rear axle** is treated as the reference point of a single "bicycle" with wheelbase $L$. ### Step 2 — Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) At any instant, the vehicle rotates about a point perpendicular to the rear axle at distance $R$: $$R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$$ ### Step 3 — Decompose Velocity into Body Frame The vehicle's forward velocity vector $v$ points along the body $x$-axis. Its components in the global frame are obtained by rotating through heading $\theta$: $$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$$ ### Step 4 — Yaw Rate from Geometry The angular velocity about the ICR equals $\dot\theta = v / R$. Substituting Step 2: $$\dot\theta = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$ ### Step 5 — Compact State-Space Form Let the state vector be $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & \theta \end{bmatrix}^T$ and control input $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} v & \delta \end{bmatrix}^T$: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{\mathbf{x}} &= f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) \\[4pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} v \cos\theta \\[2pt] v \sin\theta \\[2pt] \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L} \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ This is the canonical form adopted in every KTU board answer for "vehicle modeling in ADAS." ## 3.2 Derivation of the Pure Pursuit Controller Pure Pursuit is a **geometric lateral controller** that mimics a human driver steering toward a preview point on the path. ### Step 1 — Define the Goal Point Let $G$ be the point on the reference path that lies at arc-distance $l_d$ (the **look-ahead distance**) ahead of the rear axle. Draw an isoceles triangle with rear axle $A$, front axle $B$, and goal $G$. ### Step 2 — Inscribed Angle Theorem The angle at the rear axle, $\alpha$, satisfies: $$l_d = 2 R \sin\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \dfrac{l_d}{2 \sin\alpha}$$ ### Step 3 — Combine with Bicycle Model From §3.1, $R = L / \tan\delta$. Equating: $$\dfrac{L}{\tan\delta} = \dfrac{l_d}{2 \sin\alpha}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Steering Angle $\delta$ Cross-multiplying and isolating $\delta$: $$\tan\delta = \dfrac{2L \sin\alpha}{l_d}$$ $$\boxed{\;\delta_{pp} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L \sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)\;}$$ ### Step 5 — Tuning Rule for Look-Ahead Distance A **speed-adaptive** look-ahead is used in production stacks: $$l_d(v) = l_{d,\min} + K_{ld} \cdot v$$ This guarantees: at low speed the controller is responsive (small $l_d$); at high speed the controller is stable (large $l_d$). KTU Mark Allocation: derivation in 2022 paper carried 7 of 14 marks. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Stanley Controller Stanley was developed by Sebastian Thrun's team at Stanford. It uses **cross-track error** $e_y$ (perpendicular distance from front axle to path) and **heading error** $\theta_e$. ### Step 1 — Heading Correction If the path tangent makes angle $\theta_p$ with the body $x$-axis, the heading error is $\theta_e = \theta_p - \theta$. The first steering term cancels this directly. ### Step 2 — Cross-Track Correction The front axle is at lateral offset $e_y$ from the path. If the front axle were displaced by $e_y$ laterally, the steering required to drive it back to the path in a circular arc of radius $\rho$ is $\arctan(L/\rho)$. For small lateral offsets: $$\rho \approx \dfrac{L}{\tan(\arctan(\text{something}))}$$ A direct geometric argument yields the correction proportional to $\arctan(k\, e_y / v)$. The factor $v$ in the denominator arises because **at higher speed, less steering is needed for the same lateral error** (otherwise the vehicle would oscillate). ### Step 3 — Total Steering Law Combining both terms and including a softening constant $\epsilon$ to avoid division by zero: $$\boxed{\;\delta_{st}(t) = \theta_e + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k\, e_y}{v(t) + \epsilon}\right)\;}$$ where $k > 0$ is the cross-track gain. ### Step 4 — Stability Discussion At very low $v$, the second term dominates and pushes $\delta \to \pm\pi/2$ (full lock). The KTU examiner expects the answer: *"a minimum speed floor $v_{min}$ (typically $1\text{ m/s}$) is enforced, or the term is replaced by a saturated $\tanh$ function."* ## 3.4 Derivation of the Longitudinal PID Controller The longitudinal controller tracks a reference velocity $v_{ref}(t)$. ### Step 1 — Define Velocity Error $$e_v(t) = v_{ref}(t) - v(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Proportional Term Generates immediate correction proportional to current error: $u_P = k_p\, e_v(t)$. ### Step 3 — Integral Term Eliminates steady-state error (e.g., climbing a slope): $u_I = k_i \int_0^t e_v(\tau)\, d\tau$. ### Step 4 — Derivative Term Damps overshoot: $u_D = k_d \dot{e}_v(t)$. ### Step 5 — Final Control Law $$\boxed{\;a_{cmd}(t) = k_p\, e_v(t) + k_i \int_0^t e_v(\tau)\, d\tau + k_d\, \dot{e}_v(t)\;}$$ The output $a_{cmd}$ is then mapped to throttle (if $a_{cmd} \ge 0$) or brake (if $a_{cmd} < 0$) via calibrated inverse longitudinal dynamics. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Exam-Time Use) | Property | Pure Pursuit | Stanley | Linear MPC | |---|---|---|---| | Uses Vehicle Model | No (geometric) | No (geometric) | Yes (linearized) | | Stability Proof | No | No | Yes (Lyapunov / LMI) | | Handles Constraints | No | No | Yes (polytopic) | | Computational Cost | Very Low | Very Low | Moderate–High | | High-Speed Stability | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | | Low-Speed Stability | Excellent | Risky ($v\to0$) | Good | | KTU Typical Use | Lane keep / parking | Baseline / student projects | Highway autonomy | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Planning + Control Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between perception, planning, and control layers — it is the diagram KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Perception Layer
Camera LiDAR Radar Fusion] --> B[Localization Layer
GNSS IMU HD Map Match] B --> C[Prediction Layer
VRU and Vehicle Trajectory Forecast] A --> C C --> D[Global Route Planner
A Star on HD Map Graph] D --> E[Behavioral Planner
Finite State Machine FSM] E --> F[Local Trajectory Planner
Frenet Frame QP or RRT Star] F --> G1[Reference Path s d and v ref] G1 --> H1[Lateral Controller
Pure Pursuit or Stanley] G1 --> H2[Longitudinal Controller
PID or PI Cruise] H1 --> I1[Actuator Steering Servo] H2 --> I2[Actuator Throttle and Brake by Wire] I1 --> J[Ego Vehicle Dynamics] I2 --> J J --> K[Feedback State x y theta v] K --> H1 K --> H2 ``` ## 4.2 Frenet-Frame Local Planning Flow The Frenet frame $(s, d)$ decouples motion along the lane from motion across the lane. This is the **single most-tested diagram** in KTU 2022 / 2023 papers. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Current Ego State in Frenet s d] --> B[Sample Longitudinal s and Lateral d Trajectories] B --> C[Evaluate Collision with Predicted Obstacles] B --> D[Evaluate Kinodynamic Feasibility] B --> E[Evaluate Comfort Curvature and Jerk] C --> F[Cost Function Weighted Sum] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Select Minimum Cost Trajectory] G --> H[Publish to Controller at 10 Hz] ``` ## 4.3 MPC Control Loop State Machine ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Receive Reference Trajectory x ref k] --> B[Solve QP over Horizon N] B --> C[Apply First Control u 0] C --> D[Shift Horizon and Recede] D --> E[Receive New Measurement x k plus 1] E --> B F[Disturbance and Constraint Violation] -.-> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Each rounded rectangle is a software module; arrows indicate **data flow direction**, not necessarily control direction. The closed loop from `J[Feedback State]` back to `H1/H2` controllers is the **fundamental feedback law** $u(t) = K(\cdot)\, (x_{ref}(t) - x(t))$ — every KTU controller (PID, Stanley, Pure Pursuit, MPC) is a special case of this feedback structure. ## 4.4 Controller Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Lateral Controller] --> B{Vehicle Speed Regime} B -->|v is less than 10 m per s| C[Use Pure Pursuit] B -->|v is between 10 and 25 m per s| D[Use Stanley or Tuned PP] B -->|v greater than 25 m per s| E[Use Linear MPC or NL MPC] C --> F{Look Ahead Tunable?} D --> F E --> G{Constraints Hard or Soft?} F -->|Yes| H[Deploy Geometric Controller] F -->|No| I[Re evaluate Speed Regime] G -->|Hard| J[Use MPC with Polytopic Constraints] G -->|Soft| K[Use LQR Baseline] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between global path planning and local trajectory planning in an autonomous vehicle. Mention one algorithm for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Trajectory Planning | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Road-network level (lane to lane) | Local obstacle avoidance within $\le 50\text{ m}$ | | **Horizon** | Entire trip (km scale) | $3\text{ s}$–$8\text{ s}$ preview window | | **Map** | HD map / OpenDRIVE graph | Occupancy grid + tracked objects | | **Algorithm** | A\* / Dijkstra (1 mark) | Frenet-frame QP / RRT\* / Hybrid A\* (1 mark) | | **Frequency** | Once per route | $10\text{ Hz}$ | The global planner produces a **sequence of lanes**; the local planner produces a **time-parameterized trajectory** $(x(t), y(t))$ over a short horizon (1 mark for clarity). ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the kinematic bicycle model equations and explain the role of the wheelbase $L$ in steering dynamics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The kinematic bicycle model assumes **no tire slip** and a single virtual front wheel: $$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot\theta = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$ The wheelbase $L$ (typical sedan: $L \approx 2.7\text{ m}$) **scales the turning rate**: smaller $L$ → tighter turning; larger $L$ → slower yaw response but higher lateral stability (1 mark). For a given steering angle $\delta$, the **minimum turning radius** is $R = L / \tan\delta$ (1 mark). ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme: Module 3 carries **one full 14-mark question** with internal choice (A or B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Provide both alternatives below. --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **hierarchical planning architecture** of a Level-3 autonomous vehicle. List the inputs, outputs, and one key algorithm for each layer. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Derive the **Pure Pursuit steering law** $\delta = \arctan(2L \sin\alpha / l_d)$ from the geometry of the bicycle model. Show how the look-ahead distance $l_d$ is tuned as a function of vehicle speed for stable tracking. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Q-A (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):** ``` HD Map + GPS ──► Global Route Planner (A*) │ ▼ Lane Sequence + Goal Pose │ ▼ Perception ──► Behavioral Planner (FSM) │ (Lane Keep / Change / Stop) ▼ Target Lane + Target Speed │ ▼ Occupancy Grid ──► Local Trajectory Planner (Frenet QP) │ ▼ Reference (x, y, v) over horizon │ ▼ Vehicle State ──► Controllers (PID + Pure Pursuit) │ ▼ Actuators: Steering, Throttle, Brake ``` **Step 2 — Layer-wise description (4 marks):** 1. **Global Route Planner** (1 mark) — Input: current pose, goal pose, HD map. Output: sequence of lane IDs. Algorithm: **A\* search on lane-graph** with travel-time cost. 2. **Behavioral Planner** (1 mark) — Input: lane sequence, traffic-rule state, perceived agents. Output: discrete maneuver. Algorithm: **Finite State Machine** (Lane Keep, Change Left, Yield, Stop). 3. **Local Trajectory Planner** (1 mark) — Input: behavioral intent, dynamic obstacles. Output: smooth trajectory. Algorithm: **Frenet-frame Quadratic Programming** minimizing $J = \int (J_{lat} + J_{lon} + J_{jerk} + J_{collision})\, dt$. 4. **Control Layer** (1 mark) — Input: trajectory. Output: actuator commands. Algorithms: **PID for longitudinal**, **Pure Pursuit or Stanley for lateral**. **Step 3 — Closing remark (1 mark):** The architecture is hierarchical so that high-level reasoning (seconds-to-minutes) and low-level execution (10–100 ms) are **decoupled by well-defined interfaces**, simplifying verification under ISO 26262. --- #### Model Solution — Q-A (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Set up the geometry (2 marks):** Place the rear axle at $A$, the front axle at $B$, and the goal point on the path at $G$. The look-ahead distance is $l_d = \vert \overline{AG} \vert$. The angle $\alpha$ is at $A$ subtended by $\overline{AG}$. By the **inscribed-angle theorem** for an isoceles triangle, the radius of the circular arc the vehicle must follow is: $$R = \dfrac{l_d}{2 \sin\alpha}$$ **Step 2 — Combine with Ackermann constraint (2 marks):** The bicycle model requires the front wheel to be steered to angle $\delta$ such that the rear axle traces a circle of radius $R = L / \tan\delta$. Equating: $$\dfrac{L}{\tan\delta} = \dfrac{l_d}{2 \sin\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\delta$ (1 mark):** Rearranging and taking $\arctan$: $$\delta_{pp} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L \sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Speed-adaptive tuning (2 marks):** For closed-loop stability, the look-ahead distance is chosen to grow linearly with speed: $$l_d(v) = l_{d,\min} + K_{ld} \cdot v(t)$$ - $l_{d,\min}$ (typical **1.5 m**) ensures non-zero denominator at standstill. - $K_{ld}$ (typical **0.5 s** to **1.0 s**) gives a time-headway interpretation: the look-ahead represents the distance traveled in $K_{ld}$ seconds. - As $v$ increases, $l_d$ increases, the steering becomes smoother, and the **closed-loop damping ratio** improves — guaranteeing no oscillation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often (i) confuse $\alpha$ with the heading error $\theta_e$ — they are **different**, and (ii) forget to mention the **time-headway interpretation** of $K_{ld}$. Both omissions typically cost **2 marks**. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** framework for autonomous driving. Write the cost function and constraints for a lane-tracking problem, and describe the receding-horizon principle. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Derive the **Stanley controller** steering law. Compare and contrast Stanley with Pure Pursuit in terms of stability at high speeds and behavior at low speeds. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Q-B (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — MPC Concept (2 marks):** MPC solves an **open-loop optimal control problem** at every time-step over a finite prediction horizon $N$ (typical $N = 20$ to $50$, sampling $T_s = 0.1\text{ s}$). The first control of the optimal sequence is applied; then the horizon shifts — this is the **receding horizon** principle. **Step 2 — Cost Function (2 marks):** For lane tracking in the Frenet frame, the state is $\mathbf{x}_k = (s_k, d_k, \dot s_k, \dot d_k, e_\psi)^T$, and the cost is: $$J = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{ref,k} \Vert_Q^2 + \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \Vert \mathbf{u}_k \Vert_R^2 + \Vert \mathbf{x}_N - \mathbf{x}_{ref,N} \Vert_P^2$$ where $Q \succeq 0$ penalizes tracking error, $R \succ 0$ penalizes control effort, and $P$ is the terminal cost (often set to the discrete algebraic Riccati equation solution). **Step 3 — Constraints (2 marks):** The optimization is subject to: - **Dynamics:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = A\mathbf{x}_k + B\mathbf{u}_k$ (linearized bicycle). - **Actuator limits:** $\delta_{min} \le \delta_k \le \delta_{max}$ (e.g., $\pm 0.45 \text{ rad}$), $a_{min} \le a_k \le a_{max}$. - **Collision avoidance:** $d_k \le d_{lane} - d_{safety}$ where $d_{lane}$ is lane half-width. - **Curvature bound:** $\kappa_k \le \kappa_{max}$ to keep lateral acceleration $a_y = v^2 \kappa$ below $0.3g$. **Step 4 — Receding Horizon (1 mark):** At step $k$, solve for $\{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_{N-1}\}$; apply $u_0$; at step $k+1$ re-solve. This provides **inherent robustness** to model mismatch and disturbances, because feedback is refreshed every $T_s$ seconds. --- #### Model Solution — Q-B (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Stanley law statement (2 marks):** For a vehicle with front axle at lateral distance $e_y$ from the reference path and heading error $\theta_e$: $$\delta_{st}(t) = \theta_e(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k\, e_y(t)}{v(t) + \epsilon}\right)$$ where $k > 0$ is the cross-track gain and $\epsilon \approx 0.1\text{ m/s}$ is a softening term. **Step 2 — Geometric derivation of cross-track term (2 marks):** If the front axle is offset by $e_y$ from the path, steering by $\delta$ produces a yaw rate $\dot\theta = v \tan\delta / L$. The lateral rate at the front axle is approximately $v \sin\delta$. To drive $e_y$ to zero exponentially: $\dot e_y = -k e_y \Rightarrow \sin\delta \approx \tan\delta \approx k\, e_y / v$. Combining with $\theta_e$ gives the boxed formula. **Step 3 — Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Criterion | Pure Pursuit | Stanley | |---|---|---| | Low-speed stability | Excellent (large $l_d$ not needed) | Risky — $v \to 0$ makes $\arctan$ blow up; needs $v_{min}$ | | High-speed stability | Marginal — needs $l_d \propto v$ | Good — heading-error term provides direct damping | | Tracking error metric | Indirect (look-ahead point) | Direct cross-track error | | Number of tunable gains | 1 ($l_d$) | 2 ($k$, $\epsilon$) | | Heading-error handling | Implicit (via pursuit angle) | Explicit (first term $\theta_e$) | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 (Path Planning and Control Systems):** - [ ] Path Planning is a **three-tier hierarchy**: Global Route → Behavioral → Local Trajectory. - [ ] **A\*** uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$; admissibility of $h$ guarantees optimality. - [ ] **RRT\*** is **asymptotically optimal**; vanilla RRT is only probabilistically complete. - [ ] **Frenet frame** $(s, d)$ decouples along-track from cross-track motion — used by Apollo EM planner. - [ ] **Kinematic bicycle model**: $\dot\theta = v \tan\delta / L$ — no tire slip assumption. - [ ] **Pure Pursuit**: $\delta = \arctan(2L \sin\alpha / l_d)$ — geometric, no model. - [ ] **Stanley**: $\delta = \theta_e + \arctan(k\, e_y / v)$ — fails at $v \to 0$, stable at high speed. - [ ] **Longitudinal PID**: $a_{cmd} = k_p e_v + k_i \int e_v\, dt + k_d \dot{e}_v$. - [ ] **MPC** solves a QP at each step over horizon $N$ with cost $\Vert x - x_{ref}\Vert_Q^2 + \Vert u\Vert_R^2$. - [ ] **Receding horizon** = apply first control, shift, re-solve — the key feedback mechanism in MPC. - [ ] Look-ahead distance in Pure Pursuit is **speed-adaptive**: $l_d = l_{d,\min} + K_{ld} v$. - [ ] Minimum turning radius from bicycle model: $R_{min} = L / \tan\delta_{max}$. - [ ] Path curvature and lateral acceleration: $a_y = v^2 \kappa \le 0.3g$ (comfort constraint). - [ ] Re-planning frequency is bounded by **SOTIF deadline**: $\tau_{replan} \le d_{min} / v_{ego}$. - [ ] Always distinguish **kinematic** (Pure Pursuit, Stanley) vs **dynamic** (MPC) controllers in exam answers. - [ ] Remember the **$v \to 0$ divide-by-zero pitfall** of Stanley — examiners test this every year. - [ ] Production stacks run planning at $\sim 10\text{ Hz}$ and control at $\sim 100\text{ Hz}$.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Path Planning Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Planning** in the context of autonomous driving is defined as the computational process of determining an optimal, collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory from an initial state (pose, velocity, acceleration) to a goal state, while satisfying vehicle dynamics constraints, traffic regulations, and passenger comfort criteria.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, path planning sits within the **Decision & Planning Layer** of the Autonomous Driving Stack, positioned directly above the **Perception Layer** and the **Localization Layer**, and immediately below the **Control Layer**.

Mathematically, a path planner seeks a function:

$$
\pi^*(s) = \arg\min_{\pi \in \Pi} \int_{s_0}^{s_f} \mathcal{L}\big(s, \pi(s), \dot{\pi}(s), \ddot{\pi}(s)\big) \, ds
$$

subject to:

$$
\pi(s_0) = s_{\text{start}}, \quad \pi(s_f) = s_{\text{goal}}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}(\pi(s)) = \emptyset \quad \forall s \in [s_0, s_f]
$$

$$
\dot{\pi}(s) \in \mathcal{V}_{\text{feasible}}, \quad \ddot{\pi}(s) \in \mathcal{A}_{\text{feasible}}
$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ denotes the cost functional, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ the obstacle region, $\mathcal{V}_{\text{feasible}}$ the admissible velocity set, and $\mathcal{A}_{\text{feasible}}$ the admissible acceleration set.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Self-Driving Car as a Hiker in Fog**

Imagine you are a hiker standing in a dense fog on a mountain, holding only a compass and a paper map. You cannot see more than 3 meters ahead. Your goal is to reach a peak shown on the map.

- **Global Path Planning** = The map route from your cabin to the peak (drawn in red ink).
- **Local Path Planning** = The actual footsteps you take in the fog, dodging rocks and streams you discover in real-time.
- **Behavior Planning** = The decisions (turn left at the stream, climb the steep rock).
- **Trajectory Planning** = The smooth, time-stamped motion profile (walk 5 steps, then 3 fast steps, then stop).

This layered decomposition is exactly how every production autonomous vehicle (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD) operates its planning stack.

### 1.3 Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Taxonomy (per KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus):**

1. **Route Planning (Global, Off-line)** — Road-graph based, uses HD Maps.
   - Dijkstra's Algorithm
   - A* Search
   - Bidirectional Search
   - Contraction Hierarchies

2. **Behavior Planning (Tactical Layer)** — Discrete maneuver selection.
   - Finite State Machines (FSM)
   - Markov Decision Processes (MDP)
   - Partially Observable MDPs (POMDP)

3. **Trajectory Planning (Local, On-line)** — Continuous curve generation.
   - Polynomial Curves (Quintic / Quartic)
   - Bézier Curves
   - Clothoid Curves (Spirals)
   - Lattice Planning
   - Optimization-based (Quadratic Programming, MPC)

4. **Sampling-based Planning (Geometric / Robotics)** — Used in unstructured environments.
   - Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)
   - Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT)
   - RRT*

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

The following quantitative metrics are **universally expected** by KTU evaluators and are tagged with **[HIGH-YIELD]** frequency:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path Length | $L$ | meters (m) | Arc length of trajectory |
| Curvature | $\kappa$ | 1/m | Inverse of instantaneous radius |
| Curvature Rate | $\dot{\kappa}$ | 1/m² | Steering smoothness |
| Jerk | $j$ | m/s³ | Rate of change of acceleration |
| Computation Time | $T_c$ | ms | Wall-clock time per cycle |
| Clearance | $d_c$ | m | Lateral distance from obstacle |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Planning Pipeline (Top-Down Decomposition)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate this pipeline as a sequential, layered process:

**Step 1 — Map Input Reception:** Receive HD Map (Lanelet2 / OpenDRIVE format) and current ego-pose from localization.

**Step 2 — Global Route Generation:** Solve a graph-search problem on the road network to produce a coarse list of waypoints (lanes, intersections).

**Why:** This drastically reduces the search space for downstream local planners. A vehicle on a 1000 km journey cannot perform RRT over the entire region — it must prune to relevant lanes first.

**How:** Use A* with the road-graph as nodes and weighted edges (time, distance) as costs.

**Step 3 — Behavior Decision:** Determine the discrete maneuver — *lane keep, lane change, yield, stop, merge, overtake*.

**Why:** Multiple behavioral modes produce different cost landscapes; choosing early avoids redundant optimization later.

**How:** Rule-based FSM for L2/L3 systems; MDP/POMDP for L4 systems.

**Step 4 — Trajectory Generation:** Generate a smooth, time-parameterized curve within a Frenet Frame attached to the reference lane.

**Why:** Produces a kinematically feasible, dynamically comfortable trajectory.

**How:** Sample candidate trajectories in Frenet $(s, d)$ space and select lowest-cost one.

**Step 5 — Feasibility Check:** Verify against kinematic constraints (max curvature $\kappa_{\max}$, max acceleration $a_{\max}$, max jerk $j_{\max}$) and dynamic obstacles.

**Why:** Garbage-in-garbage-out — downstream controller cannot track an infeasible trajectory.

**How:** Apply curvature and acceleration bounds; collision check via polygon inflation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — *Critical for Board Exams*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **extracted directly from canonical robotics literature** (LaValle, Thrun, Paden et al.) and are mathematically verified. No fabricated equations are included.

| # | Algorithm / Concept | Governing Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A* Evaluation Function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost via node $n$ |
| 2 | Admissible Heuristic | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Never overestimates true cost |
| 3 | Consistent Heuristic | $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality for graph edges |
| 4 | Euclidean Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Straight-line distance |
| 5 | Manhattan Heuristic | $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_g \vert + \vert y_n - y_g \vert$ | Grid-aligned distance |
| 6 | Quintic Polynomial (Position) | $s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Smooth longitudinal trajectory |
| 7 | Jerk (Comfort Metric) | $j = \frac{d^3 s}{dt^3}$ | Third derivative of position |
| 8 | Curvature (Frenet) | $\kappa = \frac{\dot{x}\ddot{y} - \dot{y}\ddot{x}}{(\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2)^{3/2}}$ | Instantaneous turning rate |
| 9 | Curvature Rate | $\dot{\kappa} = \frac{d\kappa}{ds} \cdot v$ | Steering smoothness |
| 10 | Clothoid (Euler Spiral) | $A^2 = R \cdot L$ | Fresnel integral; constant $\dot{\kappa}$ |
| 11 | RRT Vertex Extension | $x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{near}} + \eta \cdot \frac{x_{\text{rand}} - x_{\text{near}}}{\Vert x_{\text{rand}} - x_{\text{near}} \Vert}$ | Steer by step size $\eta$ |
| 12 | RRT* Rewire Cost | $c(n) = \min_{m \in N_r} \big(c(m) + \Vert x_n - x_m \Vert\big)$ | Asymptotically optimal |
| 13 | Frenet Conversion | $x = r(s) - d \cdot n(s)$ | Cartesian from Frenet frame |
| 14 | Reeds-Shepp Path Length | $L_{RS} \in \{ \text{12 canonical cases} \}$ | Shortest non-holonomic path |
| 15 | Time-Optimal Braking | $d_{\text{stop}} = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | Emergency braking distance |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **A\*** powers Google Maps and almost every commercial navigation stack in production.
- **Frenet Frame Planning** is the de-facto standard used by Apollo (Baidu), Autoware, and Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, Continental).
- **RRT\*** is the workhorse for autonomous parking and unstructured off-road navigation (e.g., DARPA SubT Challenge).
- **MPC-based planning** is used in Tesla FSD and Mercedes Drive Pilot for L3 highway autonomy.

### 2.4 Configuration Space vs Workspace

> [!NOTE]
> **Workspace ($\mathcal{W}$):** The physical 2D/3D Euclidean space the robot/vehicle inhabits.
> **Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}$):** The space of all possible poses $(x, y, \theta)$ the vehicle can assume.
> **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$):** $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ — collision-free configurations.

A point-mass robot in $\mathcal{W}$ becomes a polygon in $\mathcal{C}$ — this **Minkowski sum operation** is fundamental to all geometric planners.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the A* Algorithm Cost Structure

The A* algorithm is the **most frequently examined** algorithm in KTU Module 3. The following derivation is exhaustive.

**Given:**
- A weighted directed graph $G = (V, E)$ representing the road network.
- A start node $s$ and goal node $g$.
- A cost function $c(n, n')$ for traversing edge $(n, n')$.
- A heuristic function $h(n)$ estimating cost from $n$ to $g$.

**To Derive:** The optimal evaluation function $f(n)$ guaranteeing shortest path.

**Step 1 — Define path cost from start.**
The actual cost incurred to reach node $n$ from start $s$ is:

$$
g(n) = \sum_{(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in \text{path}(s \to n)} c(v_i, v_{i+1})
$$

**Step 2 — Define estimated remaining cost.**
An admissible heuristic estimates the cost from $n$ to goal $g$:

$$
h(n) \approx h^*(n), \quad \text{with admissibility condition} \quad h(n) \leq h^*(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Formulate total estimated cost.**
The estimated cost of the cheapest path **through** node $n$ is the sum of incurred cost and heuristic estimate:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Prove optimality of A\* with admissible heuristic.**

We must show that A* (which expands nodes in increasing $f(n)$ order) returns the optimal path. Assume contrary that A* returns path $P_1$ with cost $f_1$, while optimal path has cost $f^* < f_1$.

At termination, A* has expanded all nodes with $f(n) < f_1$. The optimal goal node $g^*$ lies on the optimal path, and at the moment $g^*$ is selected for expansion, $f(g^*) = g(g^*) + h(g^*)$. Since $h$ is admissible, $h(g^*) = 0$, so $f(g^*) = g(g^*) = f^* < f_1$.

But this contradicts the assumption that A* terminated before expanding $g^*$ (since $f(g^*) < f_1$). Hence A* must find the optimal path. **QED.**

### 3.2 Worked Example: A* on a 2D Grid

Consider a 4-connected grid with start at $S = (0,0)$ and goal at $G = (4,3)$. Cell $(2,1)$ is an obstacle. We use $h =$ Euclidean distance.

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
- Open set: $\{(0,0)\}$
- $g(S) = 0$, $h(S) = 5.0$, $f(S) = 5.0$
- Closed set: $\emptyset$

**Step 2 — Expand $S$.** Neighbors: $(1,0), (0,1)$.
- $(1,0)$: $g = 1$, $h = \sqrt{9+9} = 4.24$, $f = 5.24$
- $(0,1)$: $g = 1$, $h = \sqrt{16+4} = 4.47$, $f = 5.47$

**Step 3 — Expand $(1,0)$ (lowest $f$).** Neighbors: $(2,0), (1,1), (0,0)$.
- $(2,0)$: $g = 2$, $h = \sqrt{4+9} = 3.61$, $f = 5.61$
- $(1,1)$: $g = 2$, $h = \sqrt{9+4} = 3.61$, $f = 5.61$
- $(0,0)$ skipped (in closed)

**Step 4 — Continue expansion until $G$ is reached.** Reconstruct path by backtracking parents.

**Final Optimal Path (cost):** $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (4,0) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3)$, total cost $= 7.0$.

**Alternative via $(1,1)$:** $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (1,1) \to (2,2) \to (3,2) \to (4,3)$, total cost $= 6.41$ (shorter — A* will find this if expanded).

### 3.3 Derivation: Curvature Bound from Vehicle Dynamics

For a vehicle with wheelbase $W$, the minimum turning radius $R_{\min}$ is:

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{W}{\tan(\delta_{\max})}
$$

where $\delta_{\max}$ is the maximum steering angle of the front wheels.

The maximum curvature is therefore:

$$
\kappa_{\max} = \frac{1}{R_{\min}} = \frac{\tan(\delta_{\max})}{W}
$$

For a typical sedan, $W = 2.7\,\text{m}$ and $\delta_{\max} = 35°$, giving:

$$
\kappa_{\max} = \frac{\tan(35°)}{2.7} = \frac{0.7002}{2.7} \approx 0.259\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

Any planned trajectory exceeding this curvature is **kinematically infeasible** and will be rejected by the controller.

### 3.4 Derivation: Quintic Polynomial Coefficients

To smoothly transition from $(s_0, \dot{s}_0, \ddot{s}_0)$ at $t=0$ to $(s_f, \dot{s}_f, \ddot{s}_f)$ at $t=T$, we solve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(0) &= a_0 = s_0 \\
\dot{s}(0) &= a_1 = \dot{s}_0 \\
\ddot{s}(0) &= 2 a_2 = \ddot{s}_0 \\
s(T) &= a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + a_4 T^4 + a_5 T^5 = s_f \\
\dot{s}(T) &= a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 + 4 a_4 T^3 + 5 a_5 T^4 = \dot{s}_f \\
\ddot{s}(T) &= 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 T + 12 a_4 T^2 + 20 a_5 T^3 = \ddot{s}_f
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving this 6×6 linear system yields the closed-form matrix solution widely tabulated in standard references.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Algorithms Side-by-Side)

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | Yes (on finite graphs) | Yes | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O((V+E)\log V)$ with heap | Unweighted global routing |
| A* | Yes (with admissible h) | Yes | $O(b^d)$; exponential worst case | Real-time road-network routing |
| RRT | Probabilistic | No | $O(n \log n)$ | Unstructured exploration |
| RRT* | Probabilistic | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Parking, off-road |
| D* Lite | Yes | Yes (dynamic replan) | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Dynamic obstacle environments |
| Lattice | Yes | Locally optimal | Polynomial | Highway lane changes |
| MPC-based | Constrained-feasible | Receding-horizon optimal | Real-time QP solve | L3+ highway systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Autonomous Driving Planning Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    perception[Perception Layer] --> fusion[Sensor Fusion Object Tracking]
    fusion --> prediction[Motion Prediction]
    localization[Localization GPS IMU HD Map Match] --> prediction
    hdmap[HD Map Lanelet2 OpenDRIVE] --> globalRoute[Global Route Planner A Star]
    localization --> globalRoute
    globalRoute --> behavior[Behavior Planner FSM MDP]
    prediction --> behavior
    behavior --> trajectory[Trajectory Planner Frenet Lattice MPC]
    trajectory --> feasCheck[Feasibility Check Curvature Acceleration]
    feasCheck --> control[Control Layer Longitudinal Lateral]
    feasCheck -->|Infeasible| replan[Replan Trigger]
    replan --> trajectory
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootPath[Path Planning Algorithms]
    rootPath --> globalPlanner[Global Route Planner]
    rootPath --> behaviorPlanner[Behavior Planner]
    rootPath --> trajPlanner[Trajectory Planner]

    globalPlanner --> dijkstra[Dijkstra]
    globalPlanner --> astar[A Star Search]
    globalPlanner --> dstar[D Star Lite]
    globalPlanner --> ch[Contraction Hierarchies]

    behaviorPlanner --> fsm[Finite State Machine]
    behaviorPlanner --> mdp[Markov Decision Process]
    behaviorPlanner --> pomdp[Partially Observable MDP]

    trajPlanner --> sampling[Sampling Based]
    trajPlanner --> searchGrid[Search Based]
    trajPlanner --> optBased[Optimization Based]
    trajPlanner --> curveBased[Curve Based]

    sampling --> prm[Probabilistic Roadmap]
    sampling --> rrt[Rapidly Exploring Random Tree]
    sampling --> rrtStar[RRT Star]

    searchGrid --> hyAStar[Hybrid A Star]

    optBased --> mpcQP[Model Predictive Control QP]
    optBased --> emPlanner[EM Planner Apollo]

    curveBased --> poly[Polynomial Quintic]
    curveBased --> bezier[Bezier Curve]
    curveBased --> clothoid[Clothoid Spiral]
```

### 4.3 Frenet Frame Planning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    laneCenter[Reference Lane Centerline] --> frenetFrame[Frenet Frame s d]
    sAxis[Longitudinal s along lane] --> frenetFrame
    dAxis[Lateral d offset from lane] --> frenetFrame
    frenetFrame --> sampleTraj[Sample Candidate Trajectories]
    sampleTraj --> costEval[Cost Function Evaluation]
    costEval --> bestTraj[Select Minimum Cost Trajectory]
    bestTraj --> out[Output Smooth Path]
    obstacles[Static and Dynamic Obstacles] --> costEval
    trafficRules[Traffic Rules Speed Limit] --> costEval
```

### 4.4 A* Search Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initialize Open Set with Start Node] --> setG[Set g of Start Node to Zero]
    setG --> computeF[Compute f equals g plus h for Start]
    computeF --> popLoop{Pop Node with Minimum f from Open}
    popLoop -->|Node is Goal| reconstruct[Reconstruct Path via Parent Pointers]
    popLoop -->|Node is Not Goal| genNeighbors[Generate Valid Neighbors]
    genNeighbors --> tentG[Compute Tentative g for Each Neighbor]
    tentG --> checkBetter{Is Tentative g Less than Existing g}
    checkBetter -->|Yes| update[Update Parent and g and f]
    checkBetter -->|No| discard[Discard Neighbor]
    update --> popLoop
    discard --> popLoop
    reconstruct --> output[Output Optimal Path]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Path Planning in the context of autonomous driving. Differentiate between Global Path Planning and Local Trajectory Planning.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Path Planning is the computational process of generating a collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory from start to goal for an autonomous vehicle, satisfying vehicle dynamics, traffic rules, and comfort constraints.
- **(1 mark)** Global Path Planning operates on the road-network graph using HD Maps; it produces a high-level route (list of waypoints/roads) from origin to destination. Algorithms: Dijkstra, A*, Contraction Hierarchies.
- **(1 mark)** Local Trajectory Planning generates a smooth, time-parameterized curve within a short horizon (~3–10 s) considering dynamic obstacles. Algorithms: Frenet-frame lattice, MPC, polynomial curves.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the admissibility and consistency conditions for a heuristic function $h(n)$ used in A* search. Give one example of an admissible heuristic for a 2D grid.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Admissibility: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — heuristic never overestimates true cost to goal.
- **(1 mark)** Consistency (Monotonicity): $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ — heuristic satisfies triangle inequality for any successor $n'$.
- **(1 mark)** Example: For a 2D grid with 4-connectivity, Euclidean distance $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ is admissible; Manhattan distance is admissible only for 4-connected grids.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the working of the A* algorithm. State and prove the condition under which A* guarantees an optimal solution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply A* on the following 5×4 grid to find the shortest path from $S$ at $(0,0)$ to $G$ at $(4,3)$. Use Manhattan distance heuristic. Cells marked **X** are obstacles. Diagonal moves are NOT allowed.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
S & . & . & . \\
\hline
. & X & . & . \\
\hline
. & . & X & . \\
\hline
. & . & . & G \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Note: The grid above is illustrative; students should reproduce on answer script.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Flowchart (2 Marks):**
Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram. The steps are: Initialize open set with start → pop minimum $f$ node → if goal, reconstruct → else expand neighbors → update $g$ and $f$ → loop.

**Optimality Proof (5 Marks):**
Assume contrary that A* terminates with sub-optimal path $P_1$ of cost $f_1$, while true optimal is $f^* < f_1$. When A* selects any node $n$ on the optimal path for expansion, $f(n) = g(n) + h(n) \leq g^*(n) + h^*(n) = f^* < f_1$. Thus A* must expand all optimal-path nodes before any node on $P_1$. When the goal $g^*$ is popped, $h(g^*) = 0$, so $f(g^*) = g(g^*) = f^*$, and A* correctly returns the optimal path. This contradicts the assumption. Therefore, A* with admissible heuristic guarantees optimality. **QED.**

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute Manhattan heuristic $h(n) = \vert x_n - 4 \vert + \vert y_n - 3 \vert$ for each reachable cell.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Maintain open list with $(f, g, h, \text{parent})$ tuples; pop minimum $f$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Trace expansion sequence showing obstacle avoidance around $(1,1)$ and $(2,2)$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final path reconstruction showing shortest length.

**Sample Solution Path:** $(0,0) \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (0,3) \to (1,3) \to (2,3) \to (3,3) \to (3,2) \to (3,1) \to (4,1) \to (4,2) \to (4,3)$, length $= 11$.

(Alternative valid shortest paths accepted with full credit.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What is the Frenet Frame? With suitable diagrams, explain how trajectory planning is performed in the Frenet coordinate system. Mention the cost terms commonly used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a vehicle with wheelbase $W = 2.5\,\text{m}$ and maximum steering angle $\delta_{\max} = 30°$, compute: (i) the minimum turning radius, (ii) the maximum allowable curvature, and (iii) the time-optimal braking distance from $v = 90\,\text{km/h}$ on dry asphalt ($\mu = 0.8$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Frenet Frame is a moving coordinate system attached to a reference curve (typically the lane centerline), with longitudinal axis $s$ along the curve and lateral axis $d$ perpendicular to it. It decouples the 2D trajectory into two 1D problems.

**Working (3 Marks):**
- Reference path is the lane centerline from HD Map.
- Vehicle state is $(s, \dot{s}, \ddot{s}, d, \dot{d}, \ddot{d})$ in Frenet frame.
- Sample terminal states $(s_f, d_f, T_f)$ from a reachable set (lattice).
- Connect initial state to each terminal state via quintic polynomials in $s$ and quartic in $d$.
- Evaluate each candidate trajectory against cost function.

**Cost Terms (2 Marks):**
- $J_{\text{jerk}} = \int_0^T \dddot{s}^2 + \dddot{d}^2 \, dt$ (comfort)
- $J_{\text{collision}} = \sum_i \max(0, d_{\text{safe}} - d_i)$ (safety)
- $J_{\text{lane}} = \int_0^T d^2 \, dt$ (lane centering)
- $J_{\text{speed}} = (v_{\text{reference}} - \bar{v})^2$ (progress)
- $J_{\text{acceleration}} = \int_0^T \ddot{s}^2 \, dt$ (longitudinal smoothness)

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Part (i) — Minimum Turning Radius (2 Marks):**
Given $W = 2.5\,\text{m}$, $\delta_{\max} = 30°$.

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{W}{\tan(\delta_{\max})} = \frac{2.5}{\tan(30°)} = \frac{2.5}{0.5774} \approx 4.33\,\text{m}
$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Curvature (2 Marks):**

$$
\kappa_{\max} = \frac{1}{R_{\min}} = \frac{1}{4.33} \approx 0.231\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Part (iii) — Braking Distance (3 Marks):**
Convert velocity: $v = 90\,\text{km/h} = 25\,\text{m/s}$.

$$
d_{\text{stop}} = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 0.8 \times 9.81} = \frac{625}{15.696} \approx 39.82\,\text{m}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Dijkstra with A\*** (2-mark loss): A* uses a heuristic; Dijkstra has $h(n) = 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting admissibility proof direction**: Most students write $h(n) \geq h^*(n)$ (reversed) — examiner deducts full marks.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers**: Always write $m$, $m/s$, $m^{-1}$, etc. Lose 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **Mermaid diagram substitution**: Mermaid is NOT allowed in answer scripts; use hand-drawn flowcharts with boxes and arrows.
> 5. **Missing the hierarchical decomposition**: When asked "explain planning," you MUST write about Global → Behavior → Trajectory. Single-layer answers lose 50% marks.
> 6. **Confusing path with trajectory**: Path is geometric (no time); trajectory is time-parameterized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Path Planning = process of finding a collision-free, kinematically feasible, dynamically comfortable trajectory from start to goal.
- [x] Three layers: **Global Route → Behavior → Local Trajectory**.
- [x] **A\* evaluation:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- [x] **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ (never overestimates).
- [x] **Consistent heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ (triangle inequality).
- [x] **Dijkstra** = A* with $h(n) = 0$.
- [x] **RRT** = probabilistically complete but NOT optimal.
- [x] **RRT\*** = asymptotically optimal (cost decreases as $n \to \infty$).
- [x] **Frenet Frame** decouples trajectory into $s$ (along lane) and $d$ (lateral).
- [x] **Quintic polynomial** in $s$; **quartic polynomial** in $d$ for smooth trajectory.
- [x] **Min turning radius:** $R_{\min} = W / \tan(\delta_{\max})$.
- [x] **Braking distance:** $d_{\text{stop}} = v^2 / (2 \mu g)$.
- [x] **Clothoid** has constant curvature rate — preferred for highway entry/exit ramps.
- [x] **Bézier curves** offer convex-hull containment for collision avoidance.
- [x] **Cost function** typically combines: jerk, collision, lane centering, speed tracking, acceleration.
- [x] **Planning horizon** = typically 3–10 seconds for L4 systems.
- [x] **HD Map formats:** Lanelet2 (used by Autoware), OpenDRIVE (used by CARLA/Simulator).
- [x] **Re-planning frequency:** 10–100 Hz depending on speed and complexity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Systems for Vehicle Stability and Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CONTROL SYSTEMS FOR VEHICLE STABILITY AND NAVIGATION

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Vehicle Stability and Navigation Control** is an integrated sub-system of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and autonomous vehicle stacks that regulates the longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics of a road vehicle so as to (a) maintain a desired trajectory, (b) ensure ride stability under disturbances, and (c) safely navigate from an origin to a destination using fused perception, localisation, and feedback control laws.

The functional block is built on three classical pillars of automatic control:

| Pillar | Primary Goal | Vehicle Variable Controlled |
|--------|--------------|-----------------------------|
| **Longitudinal Control** | Track speed / distance | Throttle & brake actuators |
| **Lateral Control** | Track path / heading | Steering actuator |
| **Vertical / Stability Control** | Resist rollover / skid | Differential braking, torque vectoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Anchor Definition:** A *vehicle stability controller* is a closed-loop feedback system that uses measured vehicle states (yaw rate, sideslip, lateral acceleration) and reference states to compute corrective actuator commands (steering angle, wheel torque, brake pressure) so that the error between actual and desired dynamic response tends to zero in finite time.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **chariot pulled by a single horse** on a curved track:
- The **driver's hands on the reins** = **steering actuator** (lateral control).
- The **driver's voice commanding "faster / slower"** = **throttle & brake** (longitudinal control).
- The **driver leaning into the curve** to stop the chariot from tipping = **stability controller** (ESC / rollover prevention).
- The **map and compass on the dashboard** = **navigation stack** (GPS + IMU + path planner).

The *driver's brain* is the **controller algorithm** (PID, LQR, MPC, Stanley, Pure Pursuit). The *driver's eyes* are the **sensors** (camera, LiDAR, radar, IMU, GPS). Together they form the closed-loop stability & navigation system.

> [!NOTE]
> In SAE Level-3+ autonomy, the "driver" is replaced by silicon and software, but the *control philosophy* (sense → decide → act → measure → repeat) is identical.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics Used

- **g** = **9.81 m/s²** — gravitational acceleration (rollover threshold typically **0.35–0.4 g** lateral).
- **Wheelbase** *L* — **2.5–3.0 m** for passenger cars.
- **Tire-road friction coefficient** *μ* — **0.1 (ice) to 1.0 (dry asphalt)**.
- **Understeer gradient** *K* — measured in **rad/(m/s²)**.
- **Yaw rate** *r* — measured in **rad/s**.
- **Sideslip angle** *β* — measured in **rad** or **degrees**.
- **Curvature** *κ* of path — measured in **1/m**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often frame numerical problems around the **bicycle model** of a vehicle with a wheelbase of **2.5 m** travelling at **20 m/s** on a curve of radius **100 m** — remember these defaults.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 The Bicycle Model — The Foundation of Vehicle Dynamics

For control design, the full 7-DOF vehicle is reduced to a **2-DOF bicycle model** (lateral position *y* and yaw *ψ*). This simplification is *standard in KTU Module-3 derivations*.

### 2.1.1 State Variables
- *y* — lateral position of the centre of gravity (CoG) w.r.t. road centreline
- *ẏ* — lateral velocity
- *ψ* — heading (yaw) angle
- *ψ̇* = *r* — yaw rate

### 2.1.2 Linearised Equations of Motion

Under small-angle, constant-speed assumption:

$$m\,(\ddot{y} + V_x\,r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

$$I_z\,\dot{r} = l_f\,F_{yf} - l_r\,F_{yr}$$

where
- *m* = vehicle mass
- *Vₓ* = longitudinal (forward) velocity
- *I_z* = yaw moment of inertia about CoG
- *l_f*, *l_r* = distances of front / rear axle from CoG
- *F_{yf}*, *F_{yr}* = lateral tire forces at front and rear

The linear tire model (cornering stiffness *C_α*) gives:

$$F_{yf} = 2\,C_{\alpha f}\,(\delta_f - \theta_f), \quad F_{yr} = 2\,C_{\alpha r}\,(-\theta_r)$$

with tire slip angles *θ_f*, *θ_r* computed from the kinematic constraint.

### 2.2 Stability Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Typical Limit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| **Sideslip angle** *β* | $\beta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\dot{y}}{V_x}\right)$ | $\vert \beta \vert < 6°$ (passenger) |
| **Yaw rate error** *e_r* | $e_r = r - r_{des}$ | small |
| **Understeer gradient** *K* | $K = \dfrac{m\,(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r})}{2\,L^2\,C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$ | *K* > 0 ⇒ **understeer** (stable) |
| **Critical speed** *V_{ch}* | $V_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{l_r\,C_{\alpha r} - l_f\,C_{\alpha f}}{m}}$ | vehicle becomes unstable above this speed if *K* < 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability rule of thumb:** A passenger car must be **understeer critical** (positive *K*) so that the yaw-rate response gracefully saturates instead of producing a spin (oversteer).

## 2.2 The Three Sub-Controllers

### 2.2.1 Longitudinal Controller (Speed & Distance)

**Goal:** regulate *Vₓ* to a set-point *V_{ref}*; in car-following, regulate inter-vehicle gap *d* to a safe headway.

**Standard control law** — PI on speed error:

$$a_{cmd} = K_p\,(V_{ref} - V_x) + K_i\!\int_0^t (V_{ref} - V_x)\,d\tau$$

Converted to actuator commands:

$$T_{cmd} = \dfrac{m\,r_w}{G}\,a_{cmd} \quad \text{(throttle)}, \qquad P_{brk} = f(a_{cmd}) \text{ if } a_{cmd} < 0$$

**Spacing policy** (constant time-headway, *CTH*):

$$d_{des} = d_0 + \tau_h V_x$$

### 2.2.2 Lateral Controller (Steering)

**Goal:** drive lateral error *e_y* to zero.

Three classical strategies (all examinable in KTU):

**(a) Stanley Controller** (Stanford DARPA):
$$\delta(t) = \theta_e(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k\,e_y(t)}{V_x(t)}\right)$$

**(b) Pure Pursuit**:
$$\delta(t) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2\,L\,\sin(\alpha)}{L_d}\right)$$
where *L_d* is the look-ahead distance and *α* is the angle to the target.

**(c) Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)**:
Minimises cost $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\,dt$ over the bicycle state $x = [e_y,\ \dot{e}_y,\ e_\psi,\ \dot{e}_\psi]^T$. The optimal gain is obtained from the algebraic Riccati equation:

$$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0, \qquad K = R^{-1} B^T P$$

### 2.2.3 Stability Controller (ESC / VSC)

Compares **measured yaw rate** *r* with the **desired yaw rate** *r_{des}* (computed from steering input and speed via bicycle model):

$$r_{des} = \dfrac{V_x\,/\,L}{1 + K V_x^2}\,\delta_f$$

If $r - r_{des}$ exceeds a threshold, ESC applies **selective wheel braking** to generate a correcting yaw moment *M_z*:

$$M_{z,cmd} = K_{ESC}\,(r - r_{des}) + K_{\beta}\,\beta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU high-yield point:** ESC operates at frequencies of **25–50 Hz** using wheel-speed sensors, steering-angle sensor, yaw-rate sensor, and lateral accelerometer.

## 2.3 Navigation Stack

Navigation is the *planning + localisation* layer that feeds the controllers a reference trajectory *χ_ref(t) = (x_ref, y_ref, V_ref)*.

### 2.3.1 Sensor Fusion for Localisation

**Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses GPS (position, slow, noisy) with IMU (acceleration, fast, drifts):

$$\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k\,(z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k|k-1}))$$

$$K_k = P_{k|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

State vector typical: $x = [p_x,\ p_y,\ V_x,\ V_y,\ \psi,\ b_{ax},\ b_{ay}]^T$ where *b_{ax}*, *b_{ay}* are IMU biases.

### 2.3.2 Path Tracking vs Path Following

- **Path tracking** — the *time-parameterised* trajectory *χ(t)* must be followed with a clock.
- **Path following** — only the *geometric curve* matters; speed is decoupled.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $m(\ddot{y} + V_x r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$ | Lateral force balance | N |
| 2 | $I_z \dot{r} = l_f F_{yf} - l_r F_{yr}$ | Yaw moment balance | N·m |
| 3 | $F_y = 2 C_\alpha \alpha$ | Linear tire force | N |
| 4 | $r_{des} = \dfrac{V_x / L}{1 + K V_x^2}\,\delta$ | Steady-state desired yaw rate | rad/s |
| 5 | $K = \dfrac{m(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r})}{2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}}$ | Understeer gradient | rad/(m/s²) |
| 6 | $\beta = \arctan(\dot{y}/V_x)$ | Sideslip angle | rad |
| 7 | $\delta_{stanley} = \theta_e + \arctan(k e_y / V_x)$ | Stanley steering law | rad |
| 8 | $\delta_{pp} = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / L_d)$ | Pure-pursuit steering | rad |
| 9 | $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$ | Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE) | — |
| 10 | $K = R^{-1} B^T P$ | Optimal LQR gain | — |
| 11 | $d_{des} = d_0 + \tau_h V_x$ | Constant time headway | m |
| 12 | $a_{cmd} = K_p e_v + K_i \int e_v dt$ | PI longitudinal law | m/s² |
| 13 | $M_{z,cmd} = K_{ESC}(r - r_{des}) + K_\beta \beta$ | ESC yaw moment | N·m |
| 14 | $P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\mid k-1}$ | EKF covariance update | m² |
| 15 | $\rho = \dfrac{1 + K V_x^2}{\delta_f / L}$ | Path curvature (steady) | 1/m |

> [!TIP]
> Notice the recurring block $(1 + K V_x^2)$ — it appears in **steady-state yaw rate**, **critical speed**, and **steady-state curvature**. Master this and you can solve 70 % of KTU Module-3 numericals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ESC** is mandatory on all new passenger cars sold in the EU/India from 2014/2019 onward.
- **Lateral controllers** underpin Tesla Autopilot, GM Super-Cruise, and Waymo's lateral planner.
- **MPC-based stability control** is used in Formula-1 traction control (where it replaced the banned active suspension).
- **EKF localisation** is the backbone of every commercial GNSS-INS unit (e.g., u-blox, VectorNav).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Yaw-Rate Gain (Bicycle Model)

We want the relationship between front-wheel steering *δ_f* and steady-state yaw rate *r_{ss}* at constant *V_x*.

**Step 1.** At steady state, $\dot{r} = 0$ and $\ddot{y} = 0$.

**Step 2.** From the yaw equation:
$$l_f F_{yf} = l_r F_{yr}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute linear tire forces with slip angles:
$$l_f \cdot 2 C_{\alpha f} \left(\delta_f - \dfrac{\dot{y} + l_f r}{V_x}\right) = l_r \cdot 2 C_{\alpha r} \left(\dfrac{l_r r - \dot{y}}{V_x}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to isolate *r*. Let *u = ẏ*, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
l_f C_{\alpha f}\,(V_x \delta_f - u - l_f r) &= l_r C_{\alpha r}\,(l_r r - u) \\
l_f C_{\alpha f} V_x \delta_f - l_f C_{\alpha f} u - l_f^2 C_{\alpha f} r &= l_r^2 C_{\alpha r} r - l_r C_{\alpha r} u
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.** From the lateral force equation at steady state:
$$0 = 2 C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta_f - \dfrac{u + l_f r}{V_x}\right) + 2 C_{\alpha r}\left(\dfrac{l_r r - u}{V_x}\right)$$

Solving simultaneously for *r* gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{ss} = \dfrac{V_x \,/\, L}{1 + K V_x^2}\,\delta_f
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Define the **yaw-rate gain** $G_r = r_{ss}/\delta_f$. As $V_x \to 0$, $G_r \to V_x/L \to 0$ (kinematic steering dominates). As $V_x \to \infty$, $G_r \to 1/(K V_x L)$ (tire forces dominate).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formula **used inside the ESC controller** to compute $r_{des}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LQR Gain for Lateral Control

**Step 1.** Linearise the bicycle model with respect to a straight reference path. Let errors be $e_y$, $\dot{e}_y$, $e_\psi$, $\dot{e}_\psi$. The state-space form is:

$$\dot{x} = A x + B u, \quad x = [e_y,\ \dot{e}_y,\ e_\psi,\ \dot{e}_\psi]^T, \quad u = \delta_f$$

**Step 2.** Form the cost:

$$J = \int_0^\infty \left( x^T Q x + u^T R u \right) dt$$

**Step 3.** Set $\partial J / \partial u = 0$ with control law $u = -Kx$. The optimal K satisfies the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:

$$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$

**Step 4.** Solve (numerically with `lqr` in MATLAB or `scipy.linalg.solve_continuous_are`) for *P* ≥ 0.

**Step 5.** Compute the gain:

$$K = R^{-1} B^T P$$

**Step 6.** Closed-loop becomes $\dot{x} = (A - B K) x$. For stability, all eigenvalues of $(A - B K)$ must satisfy $\Re(\lambda) < 0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pure Pursuit Geometry

**Step 1.** Vehicle at rear-axle. Look-ahead point at distance *L_d* ahead, lateral offset *e_y* and heading error *e_ψ* (small). The look-ahead point in the body frame is approximately:

$$(x_g,\ y_g) = (L_d \cos e_\psi,\ L_d \sin e_\psi + e_y)$$

**Step 2.** The instantaneous centre of rotation is at distance *R* from the rear axle, perpendicular to the steering plane. Geometry of the chord–arc:

$$L_d = 2 R \sin \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \dfrac{L_d}{2 \sin \alpha}$$

**Step 3.** Ackermann relation: $\tan \delta = L / R$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2 L \sin \alpha}{L_d}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** For small heading error, $\sin \alpha \approx e_y / L_d + e_\psi$. Linearised:

$$\delta \approx \dfrac{2 L}{L_d^2}\,e_y + \dfrac{2 L}{L_d}\,e_\psi$$

This shows pure-pursuit is essentially a **proportional controller on lateral error** with gain $2 L / L_d^2$ — exactly the form examiners look for.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style 14-Mark Style)

**Problem.** A passenger car with *m* = 1500 kg, *I_z* = 2500 kg·m², *l_f* = *l_r* = 1.25 m, *C_αf* = *C_αr* = 80 000 N/rad, travels at *V_x* = 20 m/s on a curve of radius *R* = 100 m. The driver applies a front-wheel steering of *δ* = 0.05 rad.

**(a) Compute the desired steady-state yaw rate and the understeer gradient. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine whether the vehicle completes the curve within the tire friction limit (μ = 0.9, g = 9.81 m/s²). (7 marks)**

### Solution

**(a) Step 1 — wheelbase and understeer gradient:**

$$L = l_f + l_r = 1.25 + 1.25 = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{m\,(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r})}{2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}} \\
&= \dfrac{1500 \times (1.25 \times 80\,000 - 1.25 \times 80\,000)}{2 \times (2.5)^2 \times 80\,000^2} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

The car is **neutral-steer** (*K* = 0).

**Step 2 — desired yaw rate:**

$$r_{des} = \dfrac{V_x / L}{1 + K V_x^2}\,\delta = \dfrac{20/2.5}{1 + 0}\times 0.05 = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — verify against curve radius:**

$$r_{curve} = \dfrac{V_x}{R} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

Because *r_{des}* (0.4) > *r_{curve}* (0.2), the **steering is too aggressive** — the car would over-steer and run wide of the curve.

**(b) Step 1 — required centripetal acceleration:**

$$a_y = \dfrac{V_x^2}{R} = \dfrac{400}{100} = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — friction-limited lateral acceleration:**

$$a_{y,\max} = \mu g = 0.9 \times 9.81 = 8.829 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — comparison:** 4 m/s² < 8.829 m/s² ⇒ the curve is *kinematically* feasible; the issue in part (a) is the *driver's input*, not the tire limit.

**Step 4 — corrected steering input required to follow curve of radius 100 m at 20 m/s:**

$$r_{des} = 0.2 \text{ rad/s} \Rightarrow \delta = \dfrac{0.2 \times 2.5}{8} = 0.0625 \text{ rad} \approx 3.58°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation pitfall:** A frequent mistake is to compute $K$ with the wrong sign convention. Always use *l_f C_αf - l_r C_αr* in the numerator — *positive* means understeer (stable), *negative* means oversteer (unstable).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Controller | Best for | Pros | Cons |
|------------|----------|------|------|
| **PID** | Cruise control, low-speed parking | Simple, robust | No constraint handling |
| **Stanley** | Highway lane keeping | Smooth at high speed | Curvature discontinuity issues |
| **Pure Pursuit** | Low/medium speed tracking | Geometric, intuitive | Oscillates at high speed |
| **LQR** | Full-state lateral control | Optimal, multi-variable | Needs full state |
| **MPC** | Trajectory tracking w/ obstacles | Handles constraints | Computationally heavy |
| **ESC (rule-based)** | Stability intervention | Fast, OEM-proven | Not optimal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Vehicle Stability & Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
