```mermaid
    A[Reference Trajectory Generator] --> B[Path Planner]
    B --> C[Trajectory Smoother]
    C --> D[Longitudinal Controller]
    C --> E[Lateral Controller]
    D --> F[Throttle and Brake Actuator]
    E --> G[Steering Actuator]
    H[GPS Receiver] --> I[Sensor Fusion EKF]
    J[IMU] --> I
    K[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> I
    L[Camera and LiDAR] --> I
    I --> M[State Estimator]
    M --> D
    M --> E
    F --> N[Vehicle Dynamics]
    G --> N
    N --> O[Yaw Rate Sensor]
    N --> P[Lateral Accelerometer]
    O --> M
    P --> M
    N --> Q[Stability Controller ESC]
    M --> Q
    Q --> R[Differential Braking]
    R --> N
```

## 4.2 Bicycle Model — Force & Angle Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT_AXLE
        A1[Front Tire Lateral Force Fyf] --> A2[Sum of Forces in y]
        A3[Distance lf from CoG] --> A4[Yaw Moment Computation]
    end
    subgraph REAR_AXLE
        B1[Rear Tire Lateral Force Fyr] --> A2
        B2[Distance lr from CoG] --> A4
    end
    A2 --> C1[Lateral Acceleration Eqn]
    A4 --> C2[Yaw Rate Eqn]
    C1 --> D[Linearised State Space Form]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Controller Design LQR or PID]
    E --> F[Steering Angle Output]
```

## 4.3 Control Hierarchy for Autonomous Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Mission Planner Route Level] --> L2[Behaviour Layer Overtake Stop Yield]
    L2 --> L3[Motion Planner Trajectory]
    L3 --> L4[Feedback Controller Longitudinal Lateral Stability]
    L4 --> L5[Vehicle Actuators Throttle Brake Steering Differential Brake]
    L5 --> L6[Vehicle Body]
    L6 --> L7[Sensors GPS IMU Camera LiDAR Radar]
    L7 --> L3
    L7 --> L4
```

## 4.4 ESC Decision Flow (Logical Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Read Sensors Steering Yaw Rate Wheel Speed Lateral Accel] --> S2[Compute Desired Yaw Rate r_des]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Error e = r minus r_des]
    S3 --> S4{Is abs e greater than Threshold}
    S4 -- No --> S5[No Intervention]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Identify Oversteer or Understeer]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Corrective Yaw Moment Mz]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Selective Wheel Braking]
    S8 --> S9[Re-evaluate Next Cycle 20 ms]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU answer-writing tip:** Always draw a labelled block diagram of the closed-loop *before* writing equations. Examiners give 2–3 marks just for a clear, well-labelled figure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define "understeer gradient" and explain its role in vehicle stability. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The understeer gradient *K* is defined as the rate of change of the front-wheel steering angle with respect to lateral acceleration, expressed in rad/(m/s²). It is computed as $K = m(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r}) / (2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r})$.
- If *K* > 0 ⇒ **understeer** (stable — yaw-rate gain decreases with speed).
- If *K* < 0 ⇒ **oversteer** (unstable — yaw-rate gain increases with speed, may lead to spin).
- If *K* = 0 ⇒ **neutral steer** (yaw-rate gain constant).
A passenger car is designed to be slightly understeer-critical to give a graceful, intuitive limit behaviour.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Stanley and Pure Pursuit lateral controllers. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pure Pursuit | Stanley |
|---------|--------------|---------|
| Origin of geometry | Geometric (chord-arc) | Heading + cross-track error |
| Main input | Look-ahead point | Heading error + lateral error |
| Steering law | $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / L_d)$ | $\delta = \theta_e + \arctan(k e_y / V_x)$ |
| Speed dependence | Indirect (via *L_d*) | Explicit (denominator *V_x*) |
| Behaviour at low speed | Stable | Divides by zero risk |
| Best use | Low–medium speed | Highway lane keeping |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the necessary free-body diagram, derive the linearised 2-DOF bicycle model equations of motion. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A car has *m* = 1200 kg, *L* = 2.4 m, *l_f* = 1.1 m, *l_r* = 1.3 m, *C_αf* = 70 000 N/rad, *C_αr* = 65 000 N/rad. It travels at *V_x* = 25 m/s. Compute the understeer gradient, the critical speed, and the steady-state yaw-rate gain at 25 m/s for *δ* = 0.04 rad. Comment on stability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Bicycle Model**

*Assumptions:* (i) small steering and slip angles, (ii) constant forward speed, (iii) symmetric vehicle, (iv) lumped front/rear axle, (v) linear tire behaviour.

Free-body yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
m(\ddot{y} + V_x \dot{\psi}) &= 2 C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta_f - \dfrac{\dot{y} + l_f \dot{\psi}}{V_x}\right) + 2 C_{\alpha r}\left(\dfrac{l_r \dot{\psi} - \dot{y}}{V_x}\right) \\
I_z \ddot{\psi} &= 2 l_f C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta_f - \dfrac{\dot{y} + l_f \dot{\psi}}{V_x}\right) - 2 l_r C_{\alpha r}\left(\dfrac{l_r \dot{\psi} - \dot{y}}{V_x}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

(Diagrammatic sketch of CoG, *l_f*, *l_r*, *F_yf*, *F_yr*, *δ_f*, *β* to be drawn for full marks.)

**(b) Numerical**

**Step 1 — understeer gradient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{m(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r})}{2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}} \\
&= \dfrac{1200(1.1 \times 70\,000 - 1.3 \times 65\,000)}{2 \times 2.4^2 \times 70\,000 \times 65\,000} \\
&= \dfrac{1200(77\,000 - 84\,500)}{2 \times 5.76 \times 4.55 \times 10^9} \\
&= \dfrac{1200 \times (-7\,500)}{5.242 \times 10^{10}} \\
&= -1.72 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/(m/s²)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — critical speed:**

$$V_{ch} = \sqrt{\dfrac{l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f}}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{84\,500 - 77\,000}{1200}} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — yaw-rate gain at 25 m/s:**

$$G_r = \dfrac{V_x / L}{1 + K V_x^2} = \dfrac{25/2.4}{1 + (-1.72 \times 10^{-4})(625)} = \dfrac{10.417}{1 - 0.1075} = \dfrac{10.417}{0.8925} = 11.67 \text{ (rad/s)/rad}$$

**Step 4 — yaw rate at δ = 0.04 rad:**

$$r_{ss} = 11.67 \times 0.04 = 0.467 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — comment on stability:**
*K* < 0 ⇒ **oversteer**; the car is unstable above 2.5 m/s. At 25 m/s, *G_r* is *amplified* by ≈12 % relative to a neutral-steer vehicle — the driver would experience a snappy, twitchy response. Real OEMs would compensate by increasing *C_αf* (larger front tires) or reducing *l_f* CoG placement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation pitfall — sign of *K*.** A negative *K* is *not* "no understeer" — it is **active oversteer**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write "vehicle is stable" without computing *V_{ch}* and comparing to *V_x*.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Extended Kalman Filter algorithm for GPS/INS sensor fusion used in autonomous vehicle navigation. State the prediction and update equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an LQR-based lateral controller for a vehicle moving at 25 m/s. Given: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a_{22} & a_{23} & a_{24} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & a_{42} & a_{43} & a_{44} \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ b_2 \\ 0 \\ b_4 \end{bmatrix}$, with $Q = \text{diag}(10, 1, 5, 1)$ and *R* = 1. Compute the optimal gain *K* using the ARE and state the closed-loop stability condition. *(Numerical values may be assumed for $a_{ij}$, $b_i$ as $a_{22} = -5$, $a_{23} = 4$, $a_{24} = 0$, $a_{42} = -3$, $a_{43} = -2$, $a_{44} = -4$, $b_2 = 2$, $b_4 = 1$.)* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) EKF for GPS/INS Fusion**

*Process model* (non-linear, IMU integration):
$$x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_{k-1}) + w_{k-1}, \quad w \sim \mathcal{N}(0,Q)$$

State: $x = [p_x,\ p_y,\ V_x,\ V_y,\ \psi,\ b_a]^T$. Input: IMU acceleration *a* and yaw rate *ψ̇*.

*Measurement model* (GPS position + velocity):
$$z_k = h(x_k) + v_k, \quad v \sim \mathcal{N}(0,R)$$

**Prediction step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{k|k-1} &= f(\hat{x}_{k-1|k-1}, u_{k-1}) \\
P_{k|k-1} &= F_k P_{k-1|k-1} F_k^T + Q_k
\end{aligned}$$

where $F_k = \partial f / \partial x \vert_{\hat{x}_{k-1}}$ is the **Jacobian** (linearisation at each step — the "Extended" in EKF).

**Update step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_k &= P_{k|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1} \\
\hat{x}_{k|k} &= \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k|k-1})) \\
P_{k|k} &= (I - K_k H_k) P_{k|k-1}
\end{aligned}$$

where $H_k = \partial h / \partial x \vert_{\hat{x}_{k|k-1}}$.

The output is a high-rate (~200 Hz) pose estimate with bounded covariance, even if GPS is intermittently lost.

**(b) LQR Design**

**Step 1 — substitute numerical values:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 & -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \text{diag}(10, 1, 5, 1), \quad R = 1$$

**Step 2 — solve ARE $A^T P + P A - P B B^T P + Q = 0$:**

Using standard solver (MATLAB `lqr(A,B,Q,R)`), one obtains:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 4.21 & 1.05 & 3.78 & 0.46 \\ 1.05 & 0.31 & 1.02 & 0.14 \\ 3.78 & 1.02 & 5.13 & 0.62 \\ 0.46 & 0.14 & 0.62 & 0.13 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — compute gain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= R^{-1} B^T P = B^T P \\
K &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} P \\
K &= \begin{bmatrix} 1.05\times 2 + 0.46 & 0.31\times 2 + 0.14 & 1.02\times 2 + 0.62 & 0.14\times 2 + 0.13 \end{bmatrix} \\
K &= \begin{bmatrix} 2.56 & 0.76 & 2.66 & 0.41 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — control law:**

$$u = -Kx = -2.56\,e_y - 0.76\,\dot{e}_y - 2.66\,e_\psi - 0.41\,\dot{e}_\psi$$

**Step 5 — stability check:** Eigenvalues of $(A - B K)$:

$$\lambda(A - B K) = \{-2.1 \pm 1.4 j,\ -3.0 \pm 1.1 j\}$$

All $\Re(\lambda) < 0$ ⇒ **asymptotically stable** closed loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation pitfall — solvability of ARE.** Students often forget to check **(A,B) is stabilisable** and **(A, Q^{1/2}) is detectable** before applying LQR. If *R* = 0 or singular, ARE may have no positive-semidefinite solution. Examiners deduct marks for ignoring this precondition.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Bicycle model** is the workhorse: state = $[y, \dot{y}, \psi, r]^T$, inputs = $[\delta_f, V_x]$.
- **Understeer gradient** $K = m(l_f C_{\alpha f} - l_r C_{\alpha r}) / (2 L^2 C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r})$; passenger cars must have *K* > 0.
- **Steady-state yaw rate** $r_{ss} = \dfrac{V_x/L}{1 + K V_x^2}\,\delta_f$ — used in ESC.
- **Sideslip** $\beta = \arctan(\dot{y}/V_x)$ must remain small (< 6°); large *β* ⇒ loss of control.
- **PID** for longitudinal, **Stanley / Pure Pursuit / LQR** for lateral, **ESC** for stability.
- **Pure Pursuit** gain = $2L / L_d^2$ on lateral error — proportional controller in disguise.
- **Stanley** adds a *heading* term, making it smooth at high speed.
- **LQR** solves ARE: $A^T P + PA - PBR^{-1}B^T P + Q = 0$, gain $K = R^{-1} B^T P$.
- **MPC** adds constraint handling; widely used in research and F1.
- **EKF** fuses GPS (low rate, accurate) with IMU (high rate, drifts) — prediction (Jacobian of *f*) + update (Jacobian of *h*).
- **Navigation** = (Localisation via EKF) + (Path planning via A* / RRT) + (Path tracking via feedback).
- **ESC sampling rate** ≈ 25–50 Hz; operates on yaw-rate error and sideslip.
- **Look-ahead distance** $L_d$ trades off *stability margin* vs *tracking accuracy* in pure pursuit.
- Always **draw a labelled block diagram** before any derivation — earns 2–3 marks.
- **Critical speed** $V_{ch} = \sqrt{(l_r C_{\alpha r} - l_f C_{\alpha f})/m}$ must exceed max operating speed if *K* < 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU golden rule:** Master the block $(1 + K V_x^2)$ and the three classical lateral controllers, and you will comfortably score **14/14** on any Module-3 question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Decision-Making and Obstacle Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Decision-Making and Obstacle Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time Decision-Making in Autonomous Driving** is the algorithmic process by which an autonomous vehicle (AV) selects and executes driving maneuvers within bounded computational time, given a perceived representation of the environment, vehicle state, and mission goals. The decision module must guarantee **temporal determinism** — the time from perception update to actuation command must remain below the system's **control cycle latency budget**, typically **100 ms** for L3+ highway pilots and **50 ms** for L4 urban robots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Obstacle Avoidance** is the closed-loop capability of an AV to modify its nominal trajectory in response to static or dynamic obstacles while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, safety, and legal constraints. It encompasses three inter-locked sub-functions: **(i) Perception** (detection), **(ii) Prediction** (intent/forecast), and **(iii) Planning** (maneuver synthesis).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Driver Cognitive Loop"

Imagine a human driver approaching a roundabout. The driver's brain, every ~200 ms, performs an OODA-like cycle:

- **Observe:** Eyes + ears feed raw data ("a silver car is coming from the left").
- **Orient:** Brain contextualizes ("that car is moving fast, I have right-of-way confusion").
- **Decide:** Motor cortex commits ("I will slow down and let the silver car pass").
- **Act:** Foot moves to brake pedal.

The autonomous stack mirrors this with **deterministic** software:

- **Observe →** Sensors (Camera, LiDAR, Radar) + Sensor Fusion
- **Orient →** Object Tracking + Intent Prediction Network
- **Decide →** Behavior Planner (Finite State Machine / MDP / POMDP)
- **Act →** Trajectory Planner + Longitudinal/Lateral Controllers

> [!TIP]
> The crucial difference: a human driver can "improvise" with non-deterministic reaction times, but an AV must **mathematically prove** (or at least probabilistically guarantee) that a chosen maneuver is collision-free — hence the heavy use of **reachable sets**, **safety envelopes**, and **time-to-collision (TTC)** metrics.

## 1.3 Core Quantitative Metrics Used Throughout the Module

The following metrics are *industry standards* referenced in every KTU question on this topic:

| Metric | Standard Notation | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time-to-Collision** | $TTC$ | $\geq 2.0\ \text{s}$ | Time until ego-vehicle contacts a lead obstacle assuming constant velocity |
| **Time Headway** | $TH$ | $\geq 1.5\ \text{s}$ | Temporal gap between ego and lead vehicle |
| **Minimum Risk Maneuver (MRM)** | $t_{MRM}$ | $\leq 10\ \text{s}$ | Maximum time budget for a safe stop |
| **Latency Budget** | $\tau_{loop}$ | $\leq 100\ \text{ms}$ | Total perception→actuation delay |
| **Safety Distance** | $d_{safe}$ | $d_{min} + v \cdot TTC$ | Minimum bumper-to-bumper gap |

> [!NOTE]
> All decisions in real-time AV stacks are **time-bounded** — they are not about *finding the optimum* but about *finding a safe and comfortable solution before the next control tick expires*. This is the central philosophical distinction from classical offline path planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decision Architecture (3-Layer Stack)

Modern autonomous driving stacks (Apollo, Autoware, Waymo's ChauffeurNet, NVIDIA DRIVE) decompose real-time decision-making into a strict **hierarchical pipeline**:

### Layer 1 — Mission / Route Planner (Top)

- Operates on a **road network graph** $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ are lane-center waypoints and edges $E$ are road segments weighted by travel time.
- Algorithm: Classical **Dijkstra** or **A\*** with a heuristic $h(n) =$ Euclidean distance to goal.
- Time horizon: $\sim$ **minutes to hours**; refresh rate: $\sim 1\ \text{Hz}$.

### Layer 2 — Behavior Planner (Middle)

- Selects **driving mode** (lane keep, lane change, intersection negotiation, yield, stop).
- Implementations:
  - **Finite State Machine (FSM)** — deterministic, interpretable, L2/L3.
  - **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** — stochastic, policy-based, L3+.
  - **Partially Observable MDP (POMDP)** — handles sensor uncertainty, L4.
- Time horizon: $\sim 5\text{–}10\ \text{s}$; refresh rate: $\sim 10\ \text{Hz}$.

### Layer 3 — Motion / Trajectory Planner (Bottom)

- Synthesizes a **continuous spatio-temporal trajectory** $\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{y}(t), \psi(t)$ for the next $\sim 3\text{–}8$ seconds.
- Algorithms: **Lattice Planner**, **Frenet-frame Polynomial Planner**, **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**, **RRT\***, **Hybrid A\***.
- Time horizon: $\sim 3\text{–}8\ \text{s}$; refresh rate: $\geq 20\ \text{Hz}$.

## 2.2 The Obstacle Avoidance Pipeline

For the lower two layers, obstacle avoidance proceeds in four phases:

- **Phase A — Detection & Tracking:** Multi-object trackers (SORT, DeepSORT, IMM-Kalman) maintain a list of obstacles $O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_n\}$ with state vectors $[\hat{p}_i, \hat{v}_i, \hat{a}_i, \hat{\sigma}_i]$.
- **Phase B — Prediction:** Each obstacle's future path is rolled out using **Constant Velocity (CV)**, **Constant Turn Rate and Velocity (CTRV)**, or **learned motion models** (RNN, Social-LSTM).
- **Phase C — Conflict Detection:** Compute the **ego-obstacle overlap region** in $(s, t)$ Frenet space.
- **Phase D — Maneuver Synthesis:** Replan trajectory around the predicted occupancy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — at least one of these equations will appear in every KTU 2024 ESE Part-B question on this module.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-to-Collision** (longitudinal) | $$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} \quad \text{when } v_{rel} > 0$$ | $d_{rel}$ = relative distance; $v_{rel}$ = relative closing speed | $\text{m},\ \text{m/s},\ \text{s}$ |
| 2 | **Time Headway** | $$TH = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{ego}}$$ | $v_{ego}$ = ego vehicle speed | $\text{s}$ |
| 3 | **Safe Following Distance** (kinematic) | $$d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\cdot \tau_h + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}$$ | $d_{min}$ = bumper gap at standstill; $\tau_h$ = headway time; $a_{max,brake}$ = max deceleration | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | **Reachable Set (1-D, constant accel)** | $$x(t) = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$$ | Standard kinematic reach | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | **Velocity Obstacle (VO) Conic Half-Angle** | $$\theta_{VO} = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_A + r_B}{d_{AB}}\right)$$ | $r_A, r_B$ = radii; $d_{AB}$ = center-to-center distance | $\text{rad}$ |
| 6 | **Frenet Conversion** | $$s = s_0 + \int_0^t \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}\,d\tau,\quad d = \text{signed lateral offset}$$ | $(s, d)$ = arc-length / lateral | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | **Quintic Polynomial Trajectory** | $$d(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$ | $a_i$ solved from 6 boundary conditions | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | **MPC Cost (quadratic)** | $$J = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\big\Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{ref,k}\big\Vert_Q^2 + \Vert u_k\Vert_R^2 + \Vert \mathbf{x}_N - \mathbf{x}_{ref,N}\Vert_P^2$$ | $Q, R, P$ = positive-definite weight matrices | — |
| 9 | **Stochastic Safety (Gaussian)** | $$P(\text{collision}) = 1 - \prod_i \left(1 - p_i\right)$$ | $p_i$ = per-obstacle collision probability | — |
| 10 | **RRT\* Asymptotic Cost** | $$c_{best} \le c^*\cdot\left(1 + \frac{\log n}{n}\right)^{1/d}$$ | $n$ = samples; $d$ = dimension; $c^*$ = optimal | — |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answers, **always state units** and **always define variables the first time** you introduce them. A common 2-mark deduction is missing unit annotation on physical quantities.

## 2.4 Why a Three-Layer Architecture?

The hierarchical decomposition is driven by a fundamental computer-science principle: **separation of temporal abstractions**.

- A monolithic planner that simultaneously routes the trip and plans the 0.5 s lateral nudge around a pothole would have a search space dimensionality in the millions — it would never finish within 100 ms.
- Splitting the problem by **time horizon** reduces each sub-problem to a tractable size:
  - Routing is rare and global → solved once per 30 s.
  - Behavior choice is medium-horizon → solved at 10 Hz.
  - Trajectory is short-horizon → solved at 50 Hz with explicit constraints.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Trade-Offs

| Approach | Strength | Weakness | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-based FSM** | Interpretable, easy to certify | Brittle, edge cases explode | Mobileye, Tesla classic stack |
| **End-to-End Learning (CNN→steering)** | Handles unstructured scenes | Black box, no safety guarantee | NVIDIA PilotNet, comma.ai |
| **Imitation / Behavior Cloning** | Easy to train | Distribution-shift failure | Waymo ChauffeurNet |
| **Optimization (MPC)** | Constraint-satisfying by construction | Computational cost scales with horizon | Bosch, Continental L3 |
| **Sampling (RRT\*)** | Probabilistic completeness | Jerky, not time-optimal | Research / DARPA-style |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** The official syllabus explicitly names "Reactive vs. Deliberative Planning" and "Safety Frameworks" — so the next section derives these mathematically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safe Following Distance from Kinematic First Principles

The classic KTU Part-B 14-mark question. We derive $d_{safe}$ starting from Newton's laws.

**Step 1 — Set up the scenario.** Let the ego vehicle travel at $v_{ego}$ and a lead vehicle at $v_{lead}$ with bumper gap $d_{rel}$. The ego applies maximum braking deceleration $a_{max,brake}$ (negative); the lead vehicle brakes at $a_{lead}$.

**Step 2 — Distance traveled by ego during braking (kinematic equation).**

$$
d_{ego} = v_{ego}\, t_{stop} + \frac{1}{2}\, a_{max,brake}\, t_{stop}^2
$$

Here $t_{stop}$ is the time for ego to reach zero velocity. From $v_f = v_i + a t$, with $v_f = 0$:

$$
0 = v_{ego} + a_{max,brake}\, t_{stop} \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_{stop} = -\frac{v_{ego}}{a_{max,brake}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $t_{stop}$ into $d_{ego}$.**

$$
d_{ego} = v_{ego}\!\left(-\frac{v_{ego}}{a_{max,brake}}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\, a_{max,brake}\!\left(\frac{v_{ego}^2}{a_{max,brake}^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
d_{ego} = -\frac{v_{ego}^2}{a_{max,brake}} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{v_{ego}^2}{a_{max,brake}} = -\frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the same to the lead vehicle.**

$$
d_{lead} = -\frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply conservation of separation.** Initially, the gap is $d_{rel}$. After both vehicles stop, the gap must be $\geq d_{min}$. The total distance the ego must "consume" to come to rest safely is:

$$
d_{rel} + d_{lead} - d_{ego} \geq d_{min}
$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to solve for $d_{rel} = d_{safe}$.**

$$
d_{safe} = d_{min} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}} + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}}
$$

**Step 8 — Add the time-headway component for comfort/reaction.**

$$
d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\cdot \tau_h + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}
$$

This is the canonical **kinematic safe-distance equation** used by ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) and AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) systems. It appears in **every** KTU AV textbook on obstacle avoidance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $v_{lead} = v_{ego}$ and both decelerate at the same rate, the equation collapses to $d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\cdot \tau_h$, which is the classic "1-second rule" of human driving. $\checkmark$

## 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Collision from Position History

A sensor reports the bumper-to-bumper distance every $100\ \text{ms}$. Two successive measurements are $d_1$ and $d_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute relative velocity from finite differences.**

$$
v_{rel} \approx \frac{d_1 - d_2}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time-to-collision.**

$$
TTC = \frac{d_1}{v_{rel}}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision rule (typical AEB logic).**

$$
\text{If } TTC \leq TTC_{warn} \rightarrow \text{visual/auditory warning}
$$
$$
\text{If } TTC \leq TTC_{brake} \rightarrow \text{apply full braking}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU standard values: $TTC_{warn} = 2.6\ \text{s}$, $TTC_{brake} = 1.4\ \text{s}$ (Euro NCAP 2018 protocol).

## 3.3 Derivation: Quintic Polynomial for Smooth Lane Change

To plan a smooth lateral lane change from $d_0 = 0$ to $d_f = 3.5\ \text{m}$ in $T = 4\ \text{s}$ with zero initial and final lateral velocity and acceleration:

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions (six conditions, six unknowns).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(0) = d_0, \quad & d(T) = d_f \\
\dot d(0) = 0, \quad & \dot d(T) = 0 \\
\ddot d(0) = 0, \quad & \ddot d(T) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to $d(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(0) &= a_0 = d_0 \\
\dot d(t) &= a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + 4 a_4 t^3 + 5 a_5 t^4 \\
\ddot d(t) &= 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $t=0$.**

$$
\dot d(0) = a_1 = 0, \qquad \ddot d(0) = 2 a_2 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t=T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(T) &= d_0 + a_3 T^3 + a_4 T^4 + a_5 T^5 = d_f \\
\dot d(T) &= 3 a_3 T^2 + 4 a_4 T^3 + 5 a_5 T^4 = 0 \\
\ddot d(T) &= 6 a_3 T + 12 a_4 T^2 + 20 a_5 T^3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the 3×3 linear system in $[a_3, a_4, a_5]^T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_3 T^3 + a_4 T^4 + a_5 T^5 &= d_f - d_0 \\
3 a_3 T^2 + 4 a_4 T^3 + 5 a_5 T^4 &= 0 \\
6 a_3 T + 12 a_4 T^2 + 20 a_5 T^3 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For $T = 4\ \text{s}$, $d_0 = 0$, $d_f = 3.5\ \text{m}$, dividing by $T^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\, a_3 + 16\, a_4 + 64\, a_5 &= 0.546875 \\
3\, a_3 + 12\, a_4 + 60\, a_5 &= 0 \\
6\, a_3 + 48\, a_4 + 480\, a_5 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving yields the unique trajectory coefficients; the lateral jerk profile is therefore $C^2$-continuous — i.e., **comfortable for passengers** (this is why quintic is preferred over cubic).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reactive vs. Deliberative vs. Hybrid

| Property | Reactive (Subsumption) | Deliberative (Planner) | Hybrid (3-Layer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision time** | $\leq 1\ \text{ms}$ | $\geq 100\ \text{ms}$ | $\leq 100\ \text{ms}$ |
| **Optimality** | Locally greedy | Globally optimal | Sub-optimal but feasible |
| **Robustness to sensor noise** | Very high | Low | High |
| **Memory footprint** | Small | Large (map) | Medium |
| **Failure mode** | Oscillation / local minima | Timeout / infeasible | Smooth handover to safe stop |
| **Use case** | L1/L2 / AEB | L4 research | L3 / L4 production |
| **Pioneer** | Brooks (1986) | Latombe (1991) | Petti et al., 1997; Dickmanns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects students to know *one* weakness of each approach in 3-mark questions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: TTC Computation for a Sensor Dropout

A radar reports distances every $50\ \text{ms}$: $d_1 = 25\ \text{m}$, $d_2 = 24.4\ \text{m}$, $d_3 = 23.7\ \text{m}$. Ego velocity is $v_{ego} = 20\ \text{m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Average relative velocity from the three samples.**

$$
v_{rel} = \frac{d_1 - d_3}{2\Delta t} = \frac{25 - 23.7}{2(0.05)} = \frac{1.3}{0.1} = 13\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lead vehicle's true velocity.**

$$
v_{lead} = v_{ego} - v_{rel} = 20 - 13 = 7\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TTC.**

$$
TTC = \frac{d_1}{v_{rel}} = \frac{25}{13} \approx 1.92\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.** Since $TTC < TTC_{warn} = 2.6\ \text{s}$, the planner should issue a **forward collision warning**; since $TTC > TTC_{brake} = 1.4\ \text{s}$, no emergency braking yet.

**Step 5 — Required deceleration to avoid collision if lead brakes hard ($a_{lead} = -6\ \text{m/s}^2$):**

$$
a_{ego,req} = \frac{v_{ego}^2 - v_{lead}^2}{2\, d_{rel}} = \frac{400 - 49}{50} = 7.02\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

Since $7.02 < a_{max,brake} = 8\ \text{m/s}^2$, the maneuver is **physically feasible**.

> [!TIP]
> This numerical example is graded in Section 5 as a 7-mark Part-B sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Real-Time Decision Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end data flow** from raw sensor bytes to steering/acceleration commands. It is the canonical answer figure for a 14-mark question on "Explain the real-time decision-making pipeline in an autonomous vehicle."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Camera Raw Frames] --> F1[Perception Fusion]
    A2[LiDAR Point Clouds] --> F1
    A3[Radar Detections] --> F1
    A4[HD Map Query] --> F1
    F1 --> B1[Object Detection & Tracking]
    B1 --> B2[Occupancy Grid & Semantic Map]
    B2 --> C1[Prediction Module]
    C1 --> C2[Predicted Trajectories of Agents]
    C2 --> D1[Behavior Planner FSM or POMDP]
    D1 --> D2[Discrete Maneuver Choice]
    D2 --> E1[Motion Planner Lattice or MPC]
    E1 --> E2[Smooth Trajectory xy psi]
    E2 --> F2[Controller PID or LQR]
    F2 --> G1[Vehicle CAN Bus Commands]
    G1 --> H1[Brake Throttle Steer Actuators]
    H1 --> I1[Vehicle Dynamics]
    I1 -. feedback .-> B1
    style A1 fill:#cce5ff
    style A2 fill:#cce5ff
    style A3 fill:#cce5ff
    style F1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style B1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style C1 fill:#fff2cc
    style D1 fill:#d5e8d4
    style E1 fill:#d5e8d4
    style F2 fill:#f8cecc
    style G1 fill:#f8cecc
    style H1 fill:#e1d5e7
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- Blue boxes = raw sensor input
- Orange boxes = perception
- Yellow boxes = prediction
- Green boxes = planning (the "decision core")
- Red boxes = control
- Purple boxes = physical actuation
- The dashed feedback line represents **closed-loop re-perception** at the next control tick.

## 4.2 Obstacle Avoidance State Machine (Lane-Keep vs. Lane-Change vs. Brake)

The decision core is a **Finite State Machine (FSM)**. The Mermaid below models the three primary behavioral states plus transition conditions. KTU expects this exact diagram in 7-mark questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LaneKeep
    LaneKeep --> LaneChangeLeft: lead_TTC less than 2.6 AND left_lane_clear AND gap_more_than 3s
    LaneKeep --> EmergencyBrake: TTC less than 1.4 s
    LaneChangeLeft --> LaneKeep: lane_change_complete AND no_lane_change_needed
    LaneChangeLeft --> EmergencyBrake: obstacle_in_target_lane
    EmergencyBrake --> MinimalRiskManeuver: standstill_reached
    EmergencyBrake --> LaneKeep: obstacle_cleared
    MinimalRiskManeuver --> [*]: hazard_lights_on
    note right of LaneChangeLeft: Trigger: Lateral gap greater than 3.5 m
    note right of EmergencyBrake: Trigger: AEB threshold breached
    note right of MinimalRiskManeuver: ISO 23150 L3 fallback
```

> [!NOTE]
> The notation `lead_TTC less than 2.6` is a placeholder for a Boolean predicate — in actual C++/ROS 2 code this becomes something like `if (lead.TTC() < 2.6 && left_lane.free() && left_gap > 3.0)`.

## 4.3 Algorithm Flowchart: A* Search in Frenet Frame for Obstacle Avoidance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Ego pose and obstacle list] --> S1[Initialize open set with start node]
    S1 --> S2[Pop node with lowest f cost]
    S2 --> S3{Has goal been reached?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Expand to 6 neighbor lattice nodes]
    S4 --> S5[For each neighbor check collision]
    S5 --> S6{Is neighbor in collision?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Discard neighbor]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Compute g cost and heuristic h]
    S8 --> S9[Push neighbor into open set]
    S9 --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> S10[Reconstruct and smooth path]
    S10 --> S11[Output trajectory to controller]
    S11 --> S12[End]
    style S0 fill:#cce5ff
    style S5 fill:#fff2cc
    style S6 fill:#f8cecc
    style S12 fill:#d5e8d4
```

**Why this matters for the exam:** A\* in Frenet $(s, d)$ coordinates is the workhorse planner of Baidu Apollo, the most widely-deployed open-source AV stack. KTU examiners can ask you to **trace** the A\* algorithm for a 3-node grid and compute $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Reactive Safety Layer + Deliberative Layer)

Because physical drawings of obstacle geometry are not Mermaid-friendly, the following block diagram conveys the **dual-process safety architecture** that is now standard in ISO 21448 (SOTIF) and ISO 23150 (L3 fallback):

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Deliberative["Deliberative Layer - Non-Real-Time"]
        DL1[HD Map and Route]
        DL2[Behavior Planner]
        DL3[Motion Planner]
    end
    subgraph Reactive["Reactive Safety Layer - Hard Real-Time"]
        RL1[Collision Detector]
        RL2[TTC Calculator]
        RL3[Safety State Machine]
        RL4[Override Actuator]
    end
    DL3 --> R1[Nominal Trajectory]
    R1 --> RL1
    R1 --> RL2
    RL2 --> RL3
    RL1 --> RL3
    RL3 --> RL4
    RL4 --> R2[Final Commands to Vehicle]
    R2 -. monitored .-> RL3
    style DL1 fill:#fff2cc
    style DL2 fill:#fff2cc
    style DL3 fill:#fff2cc
    style RL1 fill:#f8cecc
    style RL2 fill:#f8cecc
    style RL3 fill:#f8cecc
    style RL4 fill:#e1d5e7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key property: **the Reactive layer is independently certified** (typically to ASIL-D under ISO 26262). It runs at $100\ \text{Hz}$ on a dedicated ECU and can override the deliberative layer at any time, regardless of whether the deliberative layer is healthy or in fault state. This is the architectural answer to the question, *"How does an L3 vehicle guarantee safety if the AI planner fails?"*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Time-to-Collision (TTC) and state the standard warning and braking thresholds used in modern Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TTC is defined as the time remaining before a collision, assuming both ego and target continue at their current relative velocity. Mathematically,

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} \quad \text{where } v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{lead}$$

The standard Euro NCAP / ISO thresholds are:

- $TTC_{warn} = 2.6\ \text{s}$ — visual and auditory forward-collision warning.
- $TTC_{brake} = 1.4\ \text{s}$ — autonomous full braking intervention.

TTC computation is **latency-critical**: it must be updated every $\leq 50\ \text{ms}$ to remain valid for emergency decisions. *(Full marks for stating the formula, units, and both thresholds with a one-sentence justification.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Compare and contrast reactive and deliberative planning paradigms in autonomous driving. Give one representative algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reactive planning responds directly to the current sensor stimulus without an internal world model. It is fast ($\leq 1\ \text{ms}$ per cycle), robust to noise, but locally greedy and prone to oscillation. Representative algorithm: the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** or the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)**.

Deliberative planning uses a pre-built map and a global search to compute an (approximately) optimal path. It is more globally optimal but slow ($\geq 100\ \text{ms}$ per cycle) and brittle under map uncertainty. Representative algorithm: **A\***, **D\***, or **RRT\***.

The KTU-mandated modern compromise is the **hybrid 3-layer architecture** (Apollo / Autoware), which uses a deliberative global route, a behavior FSM, and a reactive local trajectory planner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark loss:** Writing "reactive is fast and deliberative is slow" without naming a specific algorithm. The 2024 rubric explicitly allocates 1 mark for an algorithm example.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the hierarchical three-layer decision-making architecture of a Level-3 autonomous vehicle. Identify the algorithm used at each layer and the typical operating frequency.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The three-layer architecture decomposes real-time decision-making by **time horizon** and **abstraction level**:

- **Layer 1 — Global Route Planner (1 mark)**
  - Computes the corridor from origin to destination on a road-graph.
  - Algorithm: **A\*** with Euclidean heuristic.
  - Frequency: $\sim 1\ \text{Hz}$, horizon: minutes to hours.

- **Layer 2 — Behavior Planner (1.5 marks)**
  - Selects discrete maneuver (lane keep, lane change, yield, stop).
  - Algorithm: **Finite State Machine** (deterministic) or **POMDP** (uncertainty-aware).
  - Frequency: $\sim 10\ \text{Hz}$, horizon: $5\text{–}10\ \text{s}$.

- **Layer 3 — Motion / Trajectory Planner (1.5 marks)**
  - Synthesizes continuous, kinematically feasible trajectory.
  - Algorithm: **Frenet-frame quintic polynomial** or **Lattice planner** or **MPC**.
  - Frequency: $\geq 20\ \text{Hz}$, horizon: $3\text{–}8\ \text{s}$.

- **Safety Layer (1 mark)**
  - Independent reactive TTC monitor running at $100\ \text{Hz}$ on a separate ASIL-D ECU; can override all three layers.

- **Block Diagram (1 mark)** — see Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Mermaid block diagram.
- **Closed-loop justification (1 mark)** — feedback arrow from vehicle dynamics to perception re-runs each tick; this guarantees that any planning error is corrected within one control cycle.

### Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**An autonomous ego vehicle travelling at $v_{ego} = 25\ \text{m/s}$ detects a stationary lead vehicle at a radar-measured bumper-to-bumper distance of $d_{rel} = 60\ \text{m}$. The maximum achievable braking deceleration is $a_{max,brake} = 7\ \text{m/s}^2$. The driver's reaction-time analog is fixed at $\tau_h = 0\ \text{s}$ (autonomous stack). Compute:**

- **(i) the Time-to-Collision,**
- **(ii) the minimum braking distance required to avoid the collision,**
- **(iii) the safe stopping distance using the full kinematic equation assuming the lead vehicle remains stationary ($a_{lead} = 0$), and**
- **(iv) comment on whether the AEB system should brake, warn, or coast.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Time-to-Collision (1.5 marks)**

The lead vehicle is stationary, so $v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{lead} = 25 - 0 = 25\ \text{m/s}$.

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \frac{60}{25} = 2.4\ \text{s}$$

**(ii) Minimum braking distance (1.5 marks)**

From kinematics, $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 a d$. With $v_f = 0$, $a = -7\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$0 = (25)^2 - 2(7) d_{brake} \quad\Rightarrow\quad d_{brake} = \frac{625}{14} \approx 44.64\ \text{m}$$

**(iii) Safe stopping distance (2 marks)**

$$
d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\cdot \tau_h + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}
$$

With $d_{min} = 2\ \text{m}$, $\tau_h = 0$, $v_{lead} = 0$:

$$
d_{safe} = 2 + 0 + \frac{625}{14} - 0 \approx 46.64\ \text{m}
$$

**(iv) AEB Decision (2 marks)**

- Available margin: $60 - 46.64 = 13.36\ \text{m}$.
- Since $TTC = 2.4\ \text{s} < TTC_{warn} = 2.6\ \text{s}$, a **forward collision warning** is appropriate.
- Since $TTC = 2.4\ \text{s} > TTC_{brake} = 1.4\ \text{s}$, **no emergency braking** is required yet, but the planner should pre-charge the brake booster and begin a comfort brake of $\sim 3\ \text{m/s}^2$.
- The system should also **not coast** because the safety margin of 13.36 m is not large enough to absorb a sudden cut-in from the adjacent lane.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on A(b):**
> 1. Forgetting to convert units ($25\ \text{m/s} = 90\ \text{km/h}$ — students often write km/h and skip unit conversion, losing 0.5 mark).
> 2. Confusing $d_{rel}$ (radar-measured bumper gap) with $d_{brake}$ (kinematic stopping distance) — these are different quantities.
> 3. Skipping the verbal justification in part (iv); the KTU rubric gives 2 marks specifically for the *decision comment*, not just the numbers.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3 Internal Choice]**

### Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the safe-following-distance equation for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system from first principles, clearly stating all assumptions. Explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Constant ego deceleration $a_{max,brake}$ (negative).
2. Constant lead-vehicle deceleration $a_{lead}$ (negative).
3. Initial bumper gap $d_{rel}$.
4. Minimum standstill gap $d_{min}$ (typically 2 m).
5. Headway time $\tau_h$ represents the time the ego would need to perceive and react; in autonomous mode it is a *comfort* parameter.

**Derivation (4 marks — see Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive line-by-line derivation):**

Starting from $d_{rel} + d_{lead} - d_{ego} \geq d_{min}$ and substituting $d_{ego} = -\dfrac{v_{ego}^2}{2 a_{max,brake}}$, $d_{lead} = -\dfrac{v_{lead}^2}{2 a_{lead}}$, and adding the time-headway term $v_{ego} \tau_h$:

$$
d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\, \tau_h + \frac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}} - \frac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term (2 marks):**
- $d_{min}$ — geometric standstill safety margin (a pedestrian could walk between the cars).
- $v_{ego}\, \tau_h$ — distance travelled by ego during the perception-to-actuation loop, ensuring the system has *time* to react.
- $\dfrac{v_{ego}^2}{2\,a_{max,brake}}$ — kinematic stopping distance of ego (braking energy dissipation).
- $-\dfrac{v_{lead}^2}{2\,a_{lead}}$ — credit for the fact that the lead vehicle is also decelerating; this term reduces the required gap.

### Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Velocity Obstacle (VO) approach for dynamic obstacle avoidance. Draw the geometric construction, derive the half-angle of the collision cone, and discuss its limitation in human-robot interaction scenarios.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The Velocity Obstacle (VO), introduced by Fiorini and Shiller (1998), is the set of all ego velocities $v_{ego}$ that would cause a collision with an obstacle moving at $v_{obs}$ within a given time horizon. It is a **Minkowski-summed** cone in velocity space.

**Geometric construction (1 mark):**

The collision cone is constructed in ego-centric velocity space:

- Translate obstacle velocity by $-v_{obs}$ to make it stationary.
- Draw a circle of radius $r_A + r_B$ around the origin (ego), where $r_A, r_B$ are the inflated radii of the two agents.
- Draw a tangent line from the origin to the circle. The collision cone lies between the two tangent lines.

**Derivation of half-angle (2.5 marks):**

The half-angle $\theta_{VO}$ subtended at the origin by the tangent point is given by:

$$
\sin \theta_{VO} = \frac{r_A + r_B}{d_{AB}}
$$

where $d_{AB}$ is the center-to-center distance between ego and obstacle. Therefore:

$$
\theta_{VO} = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_A + r_B}{d_{AB}}\right)
$$

Any ego velocity vector whose tip falls **inside** the cone is guaranteed to cause a collision; the planner must select a velocity **outside** the cone (preferably on the boundary) to avoid collision while maintaining progress.

**Limitation in HRI scenarios (1.5 marks):**
The classical VO is **reciprocal** — it assumes the obstacle can take any action, which is unrealistic for human-driven vehicles (humans follow road rules and social conventions). The **Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO)** and **Optimal Reciprocal Collision Avoidance (ORCA)** methods (van den Berg et al., 2008) address this by assuming both agents share collision-avoidance responsibility, producing smoother, more socially-compliant trajectories. The exam marks are awarded for: (a) stating the limitation clearly, (b) naming RVO or ORCA as the corrective, and (c) one sentence on why smoothness matters (passenger comfort + avoiding the "freezing robot problem").

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on B(b):**
> 1. Forgetting to inflate the obstacle radius (Minkowski sum). A common 1-mark loss.
> 2. Writing $\arcsin(r/d)$ without defining $r$ and $d$ — the rubric deducts 0.5 mark for undefined variables.
> 3. Stating "VO is perfect for AVs" — this is wrong; the 1.5-mark limitation sub-part explicitly tests the student's awareness of RVO/ORCA.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 10 high-yield facts to memorize before the KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] **TTC threshold table:** $2.6\ \text{s}$ (warn), $1.4\ \text{s}$ (brake), $1.0\ \text{s}$ (MRM) — *memorize the values, not just the concept*.
- [x] **Three-layer architecture:** Route (A\*, 1 Hz) → Behavior (FSM, 10 Hz) → Motion (Lattice/MPC, 20–50 Hz).
- [x] **Safe-distance formula:** $d_{safe} = d_{min} + v_{ego}\tau_h + \dfrac{v_{ego}^2}{2 a_{max,brake}} - \dfrac{v_{lead}^2}{2 a_{lead}}$ — be ready to derive it cold.
- [x] **Frenet frame $(s, d)$:** the **standard coordinate system** for trajectory planning; $s$ = arc-length, $d$ = lateral offset.
- [x] **Quintic polynomial:** gives $C^2$-continuous (smooth jerk) lane changes; six coefficients, six boundary conditions.
- [x] **Reactive vs. Deliberative:** reactive = $< 1\ \text{ms}$, deliberative = $> 100\ \text{ms}$; modern AVs use a hybrid.
- [x] **Velocity Obstacle:** $\theta_{VO} = \arcsin((r_A + r_B)/d_{AB})$; the limitation is non-reciprocity, fixed by RVO/ORCA.
- [x] **Latency budget:** end-to-end perception→actuation must be $\leq 100\ \text{ms}$ for L3+ highway; $\leq 50\ \text{ms}$ for L4 urban.
- [x] **Safety Layer is independent and ASIL-D certified**; it can override the deliberative AI planner at any time (ISO 23150).
- [x] **Standard simulation/metrics:** Euro NCAP AEB protocol, ISO 21448 (SOTIF), NHTSA TTC framework — cite these in 14-mark answers to earn the "depth" mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three things to AVOID writing in a KTU 2024 exam answer:**
> 1. Never claim a real AV system uses "pure end-to-end deep learning for *all* decisions" — this is wrong for any production L3+ system.
> 2. Never say "the planner computes the *optimal* path" — it computes a *feasible* path under time constraint.
> 3. Never omit **units** on physical quantities — 0.5 to 1 mark deduction per instance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing and Edge Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Edge Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Computing in Autonomous Driving

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Cloud Computing for Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) refers to the on-demand delivery of computing, storage, and AI services over a network (typically cellular/V2X), enabling vehicles to offload non-time-critical tasks such as HD map generation, fleet learning, fleet analytics, and software (OTA) updates from local in-vehicle hardware to large-scale, elastic, centralized data centers.*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the cloud is the **"brain outside the car"** — a virtually infinite pool of GPUs, TPUs, storage, and trained models that the vehicle talks to over the internet.

### 🔎 Intuitive Analogy — The Car as a Mobile Phone

Imagine your smartphone without the internet. It can still take photos and play music, but it cannot fetch a new WhatsApp message, get live traffic from Google Maps, or push a software patch. An autonomous vehicle without the cloud is exactly the same: it can drive locally using on-board sensors, but it cannot:
- Refresh its HD map,
- Learn from billions of kilometers driven by other cars,
- Receive a critical safety recall patch,
- Find a newly opened charging station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** Cloud is **NOT** used for the real-time control loop of the vehicle (steering, braking, acceleration). It is used for **non-real-time, heavy, fleet-wide** operations. Real-time tasks are pushed to the **edge**.

---

## 1.2 Edge Computing in Autonomous Driving

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Edge Computing in ADAS/AV refers to the paradigm where computation, data processing, and inference are performed close to the data source (i.e., inside the vehicle ECU, ADAS domain controller, or at a Roadside Unit / MEC server), reducing latency, bandwidth consumption, and dependence on the central cloud.*

The "edge" in vehicular systems exists at **three hierarchical tiers** — and KTU examiners frequently test this layering:

| Tier | Location | Typical Latency | Typical Use Case |
|------|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Vehicle Edge** | Inside ECU / Domain Controller | **< 10 ms** | Object detection, sensor fusion, AEB |
| **Infrastructure Edge (MEC)** | Roadside Unit / 5G base station | **< 20 ms** | Cooperative perception, intersection management |
| **Cloud** | Hyperscaler data center | **50 – 200 ms** | HD map generation, ML training, OTA |

### 🔎 Intuitive Analogy — The Reflex vs. The Thought

- **Edge = Reflex**: When you touch a hot stove, your hand pulls back **before** your brain consciously registers the pain. This is the edge — sub-10 ms reaction. If you had to send the temperature reading to a server 1,000 km away, wait for its reply, and then act, your hand would be burned.
- **Cloud = Thought**: After the burn, you think about why stoves are dangerous, search Google, and update your knowledge. This is the cloud — slower, but capable of deep reasoning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latency Threshold for Safety-Critical ADAS:** Industry consensus (SAE J3016 + ISO 26262 ASIL-D) requires a **deterministic end-to-end latency < 100 ms** for collision avoidance. This is **physically impossible** over a long-haul cloud round trip, which is why edge processing is non-negotiable.

---

## 1.3 Why Both? The Need for a Hybrid Architecture

An autonomous car needs **both**. The cloud handles what the edge cannot (heavy training, global map updates), and the edge handles what the cloud cannot (real-time safety decisions). This duality is called **Cloud–Edge Continuum** in KTU Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "CEL" :**
> - **C**loud → **C**ompute-heavy, **C**entral, **C**old (latency-tolerant)
> - **E**dge → **E**ssential, **E**mb edded, **E**vent-critical (low-latency)
> - **L**ink between them → **V2X** (DSRC, C-V2X, 5G NR)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cloud Computing Service Models for AVs (SPaaS Stack)

KTU expects you to map the standard Cloud stack onto automotive use cases:

1. **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** — Virtual ECUs, test fleets on AWS RoboMaker, simulation farms.
2. **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** — Kubernetes-managed ROS 2 / Autoware pipelines, ML Ops for perception model CI/CD.
3. **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** — Fleet management dashboards, predictive maintenance apps for OEM service centers.
4. **FaaS / Serverless** — Event-driven functions like "trigger retraining when 1 million km of new corner cases are uploaded".

## 2.2 Edge Computing Hierarchy (KTU Standard 3-Tier Model)

The vehicular edge architecture follows the **ETSI MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** reference framework adapted by 3GPP for **C-V2X**:

$$
\text{Cloud} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{MEC (RSU/Base Station)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{On-board ECU}
$$

### Why distribute? — The Engineering Drivers

- **Latency bound**: Newtonian physics + ISO 26262 dictate reaction time.
- **Bandwidth bound**: A Level-4 AV generates **~ 4 TB / day** of raw sensor data. Streaming all of it to the cloud is economically and physically infeasible.
- **Reliability bound**: Cellular coverage has dead zones; the car **must** drive safely offline.
- **Privacy bound**: GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023, and global data-sovereignty laws forbid raw cabin/face data leaving the vehicle.

## 2.3 Communication Technologies (V2X)

| Tech | Standard | Latency | Range | KTU Exam Note |
|------|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| **DSRC** | IEEE 802.11p | ~ 4 ms | 300 m | Deprecated in favour of C-V2X |
| **C-V2X (PC5 sidelink)** | 3GPP Rel. 14 | ~ 5 ms | 300 m | Direct device-to-device, no network |
| **C-V2X (Uu via 5G)** | 3GPP Rel. 16/17 | ~ 10–20 ms | 1 km+ | Network-scheduled, handover needed |
| **5G NR URLLC** | 3GPP Rel. 15+ | **< 1 ms** (target) | 1 km+ | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency |

## 2.4 Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates

OTA is the canonical cloud-to-vehicle pipeline. It typically uses a **dual-bank A/B partitioning** scheme on the ECU flash to ensure rollback safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OTA Standard for KTU:** SOTA (Software-Over-The-Air) for infotainment / maps, FOTA (Firmware-Over-The-Air) for ECU / ADAS binaries. FOTA requires ISO 26262-compliant secure boot and signed images.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $t_{\text{RTT}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{c} + t_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end latency | Cloud: 50–200 ms; Edge: < 20 ms |
| 2 | $D_{\text{daily}} = N_{\text{cam}} \cdot R_{\text{fps}} \cdot t_{\text{op}} \cdot B_{\text{frame}}$ | Data generated per day | L4 AV: **~ 4 TB/day** |
| 3 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{fuse}} + T_{\text{plan}} + T_{\text{control}}$ | Sense–Plan–Act cycle | Must be ≤ 100 ms (ASIL-D) |
| 4 | $R_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{veh}} \cdot \bar{B}_{\text{veh}}}{\eta_{\text{cell}} \cdot A_{\text{cell}}}$ | Bandwidth demand in a cell | Used to size MEC uplink |
| 5 | $S_{\text{SLA}} = 1 - P(\,t_{\text{RTT}} > t_{\text{max}}\,)$ | Service Level Agreement | 99.999 % for safety |
| 6 | $B_{\text{frame}} = W \cdot H \cdot C \cdot b$ | Raw frame size (bits) | 1920×1080×3×8 ≈ **49.8 Mbit** |
| 7 | $C_{\text{comp}} = N_{\text{FLOPs}} \cdot f_{\text{clock}}^{-1}$ | Compute capacity needed | Edge GPU: ~ 30 TOPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule Reminder:** All absolute values use `\vert` inside formulas. In the table above the divider is `$\vert$`-free by design.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tesla Dojo / Fleet Learning**: Cars upload *compressed corner-case snippets* to the cloud; Dojo retrains the perception network; the updated weights are pushed via OTA to the entire fleet.
- **Waymo Driver**: HD maps are cloud-built from survey vehicles, then distributed as **vector tiles** for in-car localization.
- **Bosch / Continental MEC**: Roadside Units at intersections run **cooperative perception** — fusing V2X messages with their own cameras to detect occluded pedestrians.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Daily Data Volume of a Sensor Stack

A typical **Level-3 autonomous test vehicle** is equipped with:
- 8 cameras at 30 fps, 1920 × 1080 resolution, 3 colour channels, 8 bits/channel
- 1 front LiDAR @ 10 Hz producing ~ 2 million points/sec, each point 24 bytes
- 5 radars @ 20 Hz, 128 bytes/packet

**Step 1 — Per-frame size of one camera**

A single uncompressed RGB frame:

$$
B_{\text{frame}} = W \cdot H \cdot C \cdot b_{\text{depth}}
$$

Substitute $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $C = 3$, $b_{\text{depth}} = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{frame}} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \times 8 \; \text{bits} \\[4pt]
&= 6,\!220,\!800 \times 8 \; \text{bits} \\[4pt]
&= 49,\!766,\!400 \; \text{bits} \\[4pt]
&= 6.22 \; \text{MB} \quad (\text{since } 1\;\text{MB} = 8 \times 10^{6}\;\text{bits})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Data rate of 8 cameras at 30 fps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{cams}} &= 8 \times 30 \times B_{\text{frame}} \\[4pt]
&= 240 \times 6.22\;\text{MB/s} \\[4pt]
&= 1492.8 \;\text{MB/s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 11.65 \; \text{Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — LiDAR contribution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{lidar}} &= N_{\text{pts}} \cdot S_{\text{pt}} \cdot f_{\text{lidar}} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \times 10^{6} \times 24 \times 10 \;\text{bytes/s} \\[4pt]
&= 480 \times 10^{6} \;\text{bytes/s} \\[4pt]
&= 3.84 \; \text{Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total instantaneous data rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= R_{\text{cams}} + R_{\text{lidar}} + R_{\text{radars}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 11.65 + 3.84 + 0.10 \;\text{Gbit/s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 15.59 \; \text{Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily data volume (assuming 2 hours of active driving)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{daily}} &= R_{\text{total}} \cdot t_{\text{drive}} \\[4pt]
&= 15.59 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times 3600 \;\text{bits} \\[4pt]
&= 1.122 \times 10^{14} \; \text{bits} \\[4pt]
&= 14.03 \; \text{TB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Sending 14 TB/day over a typical 4G uplink (≈ 50 Mbit/s sustained) would require **> 600 hours** of transmission time. This mathematically proves why **raw data stays at the edge** and only *features / corner cases* are sent to the cloud.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Edge Latency Budget for AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)

Assume a vehicle at $v = 90\;\text{km/h} = 25\;\text{m/s}$ must brake before hitting a stationary object.

**Step 1 — Total reaction distance allowed**

For a maximum stopping distance of $d_{\max} = 40\;\text{m}$ (typical KTU textbook value for highway AEB test):

$$
d_{\max} = v \cdot t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}
$$

where $\mu = 0.9$ (dry asphalt), $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the braking term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{brake}} &= \dfrac{(25)^{2}}{2 \times 0.9 \times 9.81} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{625}{17.658} \\[4pt]
&\approx 35.4 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_{\text{react}}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{react}} &= \dfrac{d_{\max} - d_{\text{brake}}}{v} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{40 - 35.4}{25} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4.6}{25} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.184 \; \text{s} \;\; = \; 184 \; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract sensing and actuation overhead**

Typical breakdown:
- Camera + radar sensing: 50 ms
- Sensor fusion + planning: 30 ms
- Brake actuator mechanical delay: 20 ms
- **Available budget for network/compute decision: 184 − 100 = 84 ms**

$$
t_{\text{decision}}^{\max} \approx 84 \; \text{ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Any cloud round-trip above ~ 84 ms causes an AEB failure at 90 km/h. Even 5G URLLC cannot guarantee this without **MEC (edge) pre-processing**. This is the mathematical justification for the cloud–edge split taught in KTU Module 3.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Cloud vs. Edge

| Decision Criterion | Choose Cloud | Choose Edge |
|--------------------|--------------|-------------|
| Latency requirement | > 100 ms | < 100 ms |
| Compute intensity (FLOPs) | > 100 TFLOPs/task | < 50 TOPS |
| Data volume per inference | > 1 GB | < 100 MB |
| Connectivity dependency | Tolerant to loss | Must work offline |
| Regulatory data class | Non-personal, anonymized | Personal / safety-critical |
| Update frequency | Periodic (days/weeks) | Real-time (ms) |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always justify *both* sides of this matrix when arguing for a hybrid design. Examiners award the "design justification" mark only if you explain the trade-off, not just state it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud–Edge Continuum for Autonomous Driving (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle Sensors: Cam / LiDAR / Radar / GPS / IMU] --> B[On-board ECU Domain Controller]
    B --> C{Time-Critical? less than 100 ms}
    C -- Yes --> D[Edge Inference Engine: Object Detection, Sensor Fusion]
    D --> E[Drive-by-wire Actuators: Steer, Brake, Throttle]
    C -- No --> F[Feature Extractor: Snippets, Embeddings]
    F --> G[V2X / 5G Uplink]
    G --> H[MEC Server at Roadside Unit]
    H --> I[Cooperative Perception: V2X Message Broker]
    H --> J[Cloud Data Lake: AWS S3, Azure Blob]
    J --> K[GPU Training Farm: Dojo, DGX]
    K --> L[Updated HD Maps, ML Weights, OTA Bundles]
    L --> G
    H --> M[Fleet Analytics and Predictive Maintenance]
    M --> N[OEM Dashboard SaaS]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU exam tip):**
- The left half is **inside** the vehicle (edge).
- The right half is **outside** the vehicle (cloud + MEC).
- Arrows crossing the V2X boundary imply **cellular/Wi-Fi dependency**.
- The feedback loop `L → G` is the **OTA / fleet-learning cycle**.

---

## 4.2 OTA Update Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[OEM DevOps Git Push] --> S2[Build Farm: Yocto, Bazel]
    S2 --> S3[Signing Service: HSM, RSA-4096]
    S3 --> S4[Cloud CDN: AWS CloudFront]
    S4 --> S5[Vehicle Telematics Unit TCU]
    S5 --> S6{Verify Signature and Manifest}
    S6 -- Invalid --> S7[Reject and Alert SOC]
    S6 -- Valid --> S8[Download to Partition B]
    S8 --> S9{Health Checks Pass}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Rollback to Partition A]
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[Swap A/B and Reboot]
    S11 --> S12[Report Telemetry to Cloud]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two partitions?** If the new firmware bricks the ECU, the previous known-good firmware (Partition A) is untouched and can be reverted. This is the **A/B atomic update** strategy — a guaranteed KTU exam favourite.

---

## 4.3 V2X Data Flow (Vehicle ↔ Infrastructure ↔ Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[V2V: Car A sends BSM] --> N1[PC5 Sidelink: Direct D2D]
    V2[V2I: RSU sends SPaT Signal] --> N2[Uu Interface via 5G gNB]
    V3[V2P: Phone sends VRU Alert] --> N2
    N1 --> M1[MEC Server: Cooperative Awareness]
    N2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Edge AI: Intersection Collision Prediction]
    M2 -- Warning --> V4[Actuator Display / Haptic Seat]
    M1 --> C1[Cloud Aggregator]
    C1 --> C2[Big-Data Analytics]
    C2 --> C3[City Traffic Mgmt SaaS]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for V2X:** "V2X = Vehicle to **Everything**": V2V (Vehicle), V2I (Infrastructure), V2P (Pedestrian), V2N (Network/Cloud), V2G (Grid, for EV charging).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between Cloud Computing and Edge Computing in the context of ADAS. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example):**

| Aspect | Cloud Computing | Edge Computing |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Location | Centralized hyperscaler data center | Inside vehicle ECU or RSU (MEC) |
| Latency | 50 – 200 ms | < 20 ms |
| Compute power | Virtually unlimited (GPU farms) | Bounded by in-vehicle thermal envelope |
| Connectivity | Required | Can operate offline |
| **Example (ADAS)** | HD map generation from fleet data, ML model retraining | Real-time AEB object detection on camera frames |

> **Marking Insight:** Award 1 mark for clearly distinguishing **latency**; 1 mark for a *correct ADAS-specific example*; 1 mark for any valid additional difference (bandwidth, reliability, or privacy).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Over-the-Air (OTA) update? List its two main types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

OTA update is the wireless delivery of new software, firmware, maps, or ML model weights to a vehicle over a cellular or Wi-Fi connection, **without requiring a service-centre visit**.

Two types:
1. **SOTA (Software-Over-The-Air)** — Infotainment apps, navigation maps, voice assistant models. (1.5 marks)
2. **FOTA (Firmware-Over-The-Air)** — ECU firmware, ADAS perception stack, braking controller binaries. Must be ISO 26262 safe, uses A/B partitioning and cryptographic signing. (1.5 marks)

> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention **secure boot + signed images** for FOTA — examiners explicitly test this in 2024 scheme.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture** of Cloud–MEC–Vehicle Edge computing for autonomous driving. Highlight the latency and reliability trade-offs at each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Level-4 autonomous vehicle generates **8 cameras × 30 fps × 1920 × 1080 × 8-bit** RGB data, plus a LiDAR at **2 × 10⁶ points/sec × 24 bytes/point × 10 Hz**. Compute:
1. The instantaneous data rate in **Gbit/s**.
2. The **daily volume in TB** assuming **3 hours** of driving.
3. Justify why **streaming this raw data to the cloud is infeasible**, and propose a tier-wise edge pre-processing strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The three-tier architecture splits the autonomous-driving compute stack into *Vehicle Edge*, *Infrastructure Edge (MEC)*, and *Cloud* layers, each balancing latency, bandwidth, and compute intensity.

**Tier 1 — Vehicle Edge (2 marks):**
- Located inside the ECU or ADAS domain controller.
- Latency: **< 10 ms**, deterministic.
- Runs safety-critical tasks: camera perception, radar tracking, AEB logic, lane-keeping.
- Must work even with no network.

**Tier 2 — MEC / Infrastructure Edge (2 marks):**
- Located at 5G base station or Roadside Unit (RSU).
- Latency: **< 20 ms**, near-real-time.
- Runs cooperative perception (e.g., intersection collision prediction), traffic-light signal phase timing (SPaT), and dynamic map deltas.
- Acts as a low-latency bridge between the vehicle and the cloud.

**Tier 3 — Cloud (2 marks):**
- Located in hyperscaler data centers.
- Latency: **50 – 200 ms**, non-deterministic.
- Runs ML training, HD map generation, fleet analytics, OTA packaging.
- Provides global intelligence but cannot intervene in real-time safety loops.

**Conclusion:** The trade-off is **latency vs. compute depth** — closer to the sensor for safety, further away for intelligence.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Camera data rate (2 marks):**

From Section 3.1 derivation:

$$
B_{\text{frame}} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \times 8 = 49,\!766,\!400 \;\text{bits} \approx 6.22 \; \text{MB}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{cams}} &= 8 \times 30 \times 49.77 \; \text{Mbit/s} \\[4pt]
&= 11.94 \; \text{Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LiDAR data rate (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{lidar}} &= 2 \times 10^{6} \times 24 \times 10 \times 8 \;\text{bits/s} \\[4pt]
&= 3.84 \; \text{Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total and daily volume (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= 11.94 + 3.84 = 15.78 \; \text{Gbit/s} \\[4pt]
D_{\text{daily}} &= 15.78 \times 10^{9} \times 3 \times 3600 / 8 \;\text{bytes} \\[4pt]
&= 2.13 \times 10^{13} \; \text{bytes} \approx 21.3 \; \text{TB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification + edge strategy (2 marks):**
- 21.3 TB/day exceeds typical 4G LTE monthly cap of 30 GB in minutes.
- Tier-wise strategy:
  - **Vehicle Edge:** Run YOLOv8 / BEVFormer inference locally; extract only **bounding boxes + embeddings**.
  - **MEC:** Aggregate embeddings from 50 cars at an intersection to build a **cooperative perception map**.
  - **Cloud:** Receive only compressed corner-case snippets (≈ 100 MB/event) for **fleet-wide retraining**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **OTA update workflow** for an ADAS ECU with a labelled block diagram. Discuss the role of **A/B partitioning**, **secure boot**, and **rollback** in functional safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous vehicle is travelling at **100 km/h** on a highway. The maximum allowed stopping distance is **45 m**. The road-tire friction coefficient is **μ = 0.85**. The sensing + actuation overhead is **120 ms**.
1. Compute the **maximum permissible decision latency** for the AEB system.
2. State whether the cloud can be used for this decision, justifying with the derived budget. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**OTA Workflow (3 marks):**
1. OEM developer pushes code → CI/CD build farm compiles → Signing service (HSM) attaches RSA-4096 signature.
2. Signed artifact uploaded to **Cloud CDN**.
3. Telematics Control Unit (TCU) downloads the package via 4G/5G.
4. ECU verifies signature against OEM root certificate stored in TPM.
5. Image is written to **inactive Partition B**; on success, boot flags swap → Partition B becomes active.
6. Health-checks run post-boot; if stable, old Partition A is repurposed.

**A/B Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Two identical flash partitions allow atomic updates.
- During the update, the vehicle always has a known-good fallback.
- Critical for ISO 26262 ASIL-B/D certification.

**Secure Boot + Rollback (2 marks):**
- Secure boot ensures only OEM-signed binaries execute.
- On boot failure (3 failed watchdog resets), bootloader automatically rolls back to Partition A.
- Telemetry of the rollback is reported to the OEM Security Operations Centre (SOC).

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert units (1 mark):**
$v = 100 \;\text{km/h} = 27.78 \; \text{m/s}$, $d_{\max} = 45 \; \text{m}$, $\mu = 0.85$.

**Step 2 — Braking distance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{brake}} &= \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{(27.78)^{2}}{2 \times 0.85 \times 9.81} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{771.6}{16.677} \\[4pt]
&\approx 46.27 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical observation (1 mark):**

Since $d_{\text{brake}} = 46.27 \; \text{m} > d_{\max} = 45 \; \text{m}$, the vehicle **cannot stop in time using braking alone**. Therefore AEB must act **before** the brake phase even starts.

**Step 4 — Maximum permissible decision latency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{react}}^{\max} &= \dfrac{d_{\max} - d_{\text{brake}}}{v} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{45 - 46.27}{27.78} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{-1.27}{27.78} \\[4pt]
&\approx -0.046 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **negative** reaction budget implies that **even zero-latency braking is insufficient** — the system must additionally issue **pre-emptive warnings and brake-by-wire pre-charge** while in normal driving mode.

**Step 5 — Can the cloud be used? (1 mark):**
**No.** Even with the most generous 5G URLLC budget (~ 5 ms), the negative headroom leaves no margin for cloud round-trip. The decision **must** be made at the **vehicle edge**, with cloud used only for *post-incident forensics*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** between Mbit/s and MB/s (factor of 8) is the single most common error in data-volume calculations. Always convert bits ↔ bytes explicitly.
> 2. **Negative latency budget** problems often trick students into writing "0 ms" instead of explaining the **physical impossibility** and proposing **pre-emptive actuation**.
> 3. **OTA answers without cryptography** lose 2 marks. Always mention **signed images + secure boot + TPM**.
> 4. **V2X answers without 3GPP references** are considered incomplete in 2024 scheme. Mention C-V2X Rel. 14/16/17.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ☁️ **Cloud** = global brain: HD map generation, ML training, OTA packaging, fleet analytics. Latency-tolerant.
- ⚡ **Edge** = local reflex: AEB, lane-keep, sensor fusion, cooperative perception. Sub-100 ms, deterministic.
- 🛰️ **MEC** sits between cloud and edge, used for V2X cooperative awareness and intersection management.
- 📡 **V2X** = V2V + V2I + V2P + V2N + V2G; backed by **DSRC** or **C-V2X** over 5G.
- 🔁 **OTA** has two flavours — **SOTA** (apps) and **FOTA** (firmware) — both demand cryptographic signing and A/B partitioning.
- 📊 **Data volume** of a single L4 AV ≈ **4 TB/day** of raw sensor data; only **features and corner cases** go to cloud.
- 🧮 **AEB latency budget** at 90 km/h is ≈ **184 ms** total; sensing + actuation already consume 100 ms, leaving ≈ **84 ms** for the decision.
- 🛡️ **Functional safety** mandates **ISO 26262** ASIL-D for edge ECUs; cloud is **never** in the safety loop.
- 🔐 **Privacy** (GDPR / DPDP Act 2023) restricts biometric / cabin data — must be processed at the edge or anonymized before upload.
- 📈 **Standard pattern for KTU 14-mark answers:** *diagram (2) + tier description (3) + numerical justification (2) + counter-argument (2) + conclusion (1) + safety/standard citation (1) + diagram (3)*.
- 🧠 **Mnemonic — "CEL":** Cloud-Central-Cold, Edge-Essential-Embedded, Link = V2X.
- 🚗 **OTA rollback** is achieved via **A/B partitions + watchdog timeout + signed manifests**.
- 🏎️ **Negative reaction budget** = system needs **pre-emptive actuation**, not "faster cloud".

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Cloud Computing and Edge Processing.** These notes are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, **NEP 2020**, and **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels (L1–L6) expected in the End-Semester Evaluation (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Cloud Computing in Autonomous Vehicle Ecosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cloud Computing in the Autonomous Vehicle (AV) Ecosystem** refers to the on-demand, networked delivery of computing resources — including storage, processing, analytics, and machine-learning services — over the internet to support the perception, planning, and actuation lifecycle of self-driving vehicles. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the cloud functions as the **off-board "collective intelligence" layer** that complements the **on-board Electronic Control Units (ECUs)**, providing scalable compute, big-data ingestion, software-defined updates, and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) coordination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Approved Wording)**
> *Cloud computing in autonomous driving is a distributed, service-oriented architecture wherein a fleet of connected vehicles continuously offloads sensor data, requests HD map updates, downloads AI model weights, and synchronizes with the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) backend to enable real-time, large-scale, and continuously improving self-driving capability.*

The deployment models relevant to AVs are:
- **Public Cloud** (AWS, Azure, GCP)
- **Private Cloud** (OEM-owned data centers)
- **Hybrid Cloud** (combined edge + cloud)
- **Vehicular Cloud / Vehicular Fog** (V2V/V2I pooled resources)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "External Brain & Memory Vault"

Imagine a self-driving car as a brilliant but short-term-memory-limited human driver.

| Human Driver | Autonomous Vehicle |
|---|---|
| Brain (immediate decisions) | On-board ECUs, GPUs, NPUs |
| Long-term memory (lifetime experience) | **Cloud Data Center** |
| Learning from other drivers' mistakes | **Fleet Learning / Federated AI** |
| Reading updated road maps | **HD Map Cloud Service** |
| Software update at garage | **OTA (Over-the-Air) Update** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The cloud is *not* a replacement for in-vehicle compute. It is the **"external hippocampus"** — a high-latency, high-capacity companion that the vehicle consults for non-real-time cognition, global learning, and software lifecycle management.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Industry-Standard)

The following metrics govern cloud design for AVs and appear frequently in KTU numerical problems:

- **End-to-End Latency:** **< 100 ms** for safety-critical V2X (5G URLLC target: **1 ms**)
- **Bandwidth per Vehicle:** **~4 TB/day** (Waymo-reported, L4 fleet)
- **HD Map Refresh Cycle:** **< 1 hour** for Level-4 zones
- **OTA Payload Size:** **~1–10 GB** per major release
- **Cloud Availability SLA:** **99.99% (Four-Nines)** — roughly **52.6 minutes** of annual downtime

> [!NOTE]
> Standard latency tiering used in cloud-AV design:
> - **Real-time loop:** **< 10 ms** (on-board only)
> - **Near-real-time loop:** **10–100 ms** (MEC / edge)
> - **Non-real-time loop:** **> 100 ms** (central cloud)
