## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Layers of Cloud-AV Integration

The cloud-AV ecosystem is structured into **four logical tiers**, each with distinct latency, compute, and security profiles.

**Tier 1 — Vehicle On-Board Layer**
- Houses the **AV stack** (sensors, perception, localization, planning, control)
- Runs ROS 2 / Cyber RT / proprietary middleware
- Decision loop must close in **< 100 ms** (SAE J3016 safety budget)

**Tier 2 — Edge / Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) Layer**
- Deployed at **5G base stations, RSUs (Roadside Units), or micro-data-centers**
- Handles latency-sensitive V2X coordination: **10–20 ms** round-trip
- Performs sensor fusion of nearby vehicles (cooperative perception)

**Tier 3 — Regional / Telco Cloud Layer**
- Hosts HD map tile distribution, traffic analytics
- Aggregates fleet data for **federated learning**
- Latency budget: **20–50 ms**

**Tier 4 — Central / OEM Hyperscale Cloud Layer**
- Long-term storage, model training, OTA orchestration
- Latency tolerated: **seconds to minutes**
- Backbone of continuous ML improvement

### 2.2 Functional Roles of Cloud in AV

The cloud performs **seven primary functions** that on-board hardware cannot efficiently deliver:

1. **HD Map Distribution & Versioning** — Differential map updates (~MB scale) pushed to fleet.
2. **Over-the-Air (OTA) Software Updates** — Delta patches for OS, perception models, and ECU firmware.
3. **Fleet Learning / Federated AI** — Vehicles train locally; only **gradients** are uploaded (privacy-preserving).
4. **Simulation-as-a-Service** — Cloud GPUs replay **billions of miles** in CARLA / Waymax.
5. **Remote Teleoperation** — Human-in-the-loop fallback for **Minimal Risk Manoeuvre (MRM)**.
6. **Predictive Maintenance & Anomaly Detection** — Telemetry ML for component health.
7. **V2X Coordination & Traffic Orchestration** — Intersection management, platooning.

### 2.3 Why Cloud is Indispensable (The "Why")

| Constraint | On-board Limitation | Cloud-Enabled Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Storage (HD maps ≈ 4 TB) | SSD cost, thermal limits | Tile-based cloud streaming |
| Model training (petabytes) | Cannot train on-vehicle | Centralized training, OTA weight push |
| Edge cases (rare scenarios) | Insufficient exposure | Fleet aggregation in cloud |
| Software updates | Recall cost ~$1B | OTA push at near-zero marginal cost |
| Long-range awareness | Line-of-sight sensors only | Cloud-fused dynamic map |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Cloud-AV Numerical Problems** — all values are SI-consistent.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical AV Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data generated per vehicle per day** | $D_v = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot b_i \cdot t_{\text{op}}$ | $f_i$ = sensor frame-rate (Hz), $b_i$ = bit-depth × resolution, $t_{\text{op}}$ = daily operation (s) | $D_v \approx 4$ TB/day |
| 2 | **Fleet aggregate data rate** | $R_{\text{fleet}} = N \cdot D_v \cdot 8 / 86400$ | $N$ = active fleet size | $R_{\text{fleet}} = 3.7$ Gbps (for $N=10{,}000$) |
| 3 | **End-to-end latency budget** | $T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{prop}}$ | Each term in **ms** | $T_{\text{e2e}} < 100$ ms |
| 4 | **One-way propagation delay** | $T_{\text{prop}} = d / v_{\text{med}}$ | $d$ = fibre/wireless path (m), $v_{\text{med}}$ = medium velocity (m/s) | $5\ \mu\text{s/km}$ (fibre) |
| 5 | **Storage for HD map (city tile)** | $S_{\text{tile}} = A_{\text{city}} \cdot \rho_{\text{lane}} \cdot s_{\text{lane}}$ | $\rho_{\text{lane}}$ = lane density (lane/m²), $s_{\text{lane}}$ = data per lane (GB) | $S_{\text{tile}} \approx 1{-}4$ TB |
| 6 | **OTA payload (delta update)** | $P_{\text{delta}} = P_{\text{full}} \cdot (1 - r_{\text{sim}})$ | $r_{\text{sim}}$ = similarity ratio | $\sim 10{-}30\%$ of full |
| 7 | **Cloud availability (annual downtime)** | $T_{\text{down}} = 525600 \cdot (1 - A/100)$ min | $A$ = availability % | $52.6$ min @ $99.99\%$ |
| 8 | **Bandwidth (Shannon channel capacity)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), SNR dimensionless | $C$ in bits/s |
| 9 | **Federated learning rounds** | $R_{\text{conv}} = \mathcal{O}(\log(1/\epsilon))$ | $\epsilon$ = convergence tolerance | $\sim 100{-}500$ rounds |

### 2.5 Cloud Service Models Applied to AV

- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Raw GPU/TPU provisioning for simulation farms.
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Kubernetes-managed ROS 2 / Apollo pipelines.
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Fleet-management dashboards for operators.
- **DaaS (Data-as-a-Service):** HD map, traffic, weather feeds licensed to vehicles.
- **MaaS (Mobility-as-a-Service):** Robo-taxi ride dispatch (Cruise, Waymo).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Style:** *"Differentiate between Edge and Cloud computing in the AV context."* — Always anchor the answer in **latency, bandwidth, locality, and decision-loop ownership**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Daily Data Generated by a Single L4 Vehicle

An L4 autonomous vehicle typically operates with the following sensor suite during a 10-hour active duty cycle.

| Sensor | Frame Rate $f_i$ (Hz) | Raw Rate $r_i$ (Mbps) | Active Duration $t_{\text{op}}$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (8×, 2 MP) | 30 | 8 × 30 × 2 × 8 ≈ 3840 | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |
| LiDAR (1×, 128-layer) | 10 | ~ 70 | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |
| Radar (6×) | 20 | ~ 6 | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |
| Ultrasonic (12×) | 50 | ~ 0.2 | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |
| GPS/IMU | 100 | negligible | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |
| V2X | 10 | ~ 2 | $3.6 \times 10^4$ |

**Step 1 — Per-sensor daily data volume**

$$
D_{\text{cam}} = 3840 \text{ Mbps} \times 3.6 \times 10^4 \text{ s} = 1.3824 \times 10^8 \text{ Mb}
$$

$$
D_{\text{cam}} = \frac{1.3824 \times 10^8}{8} = 1.728 \times 10^7 \text{ MB} \approx 17.28 \text{ TB/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum across all sensors**

$$
D_v = 17.28 + 0.315 + 0.027 + 0.0009 + \varepsilon_{\text{GPS}} + 0.009
$$

$$
D_v \approx 17.63 \text{ TB/day per vehicle}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **camera dominates (~98 %)** of AV data volume. This is why cloud pipelines apply aggressive **tiered retention** — only **trigger events** (hard-brake, near-miss) are stored long-term.

**Step 3 — Fleet-scale aggregate**

For a fleet of $N = 50{,}000$ vehicles:

$$
D_{\text{fleet}} = 50{,}000 \times 17.63 \text{ TB} = 8.815 \times 10^5 \text{ TB/day}
$$

$$
D_{\text{fleet}} \approx 881.5 \text{ PB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual extrapolation**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 881.5 \text{ PB} \times 365 = 3.217 \times 10^5 \text{ PB} \approx 322 \text{ EB/year}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Latency Budget Decomposition for V2X Warning

Consider a cooperative collision-warning message from a lead vehicle to a follower via the cloud (5G uplink → server → 5G downlink).

**Step 1 — Sensor acquisition delay**

$$
T_{\text{acq}} = 33 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(camera frame at 30 Hz)}
$$

**Step 2 — Encoding + encryption**

$$
T_{\text{enc}} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — 5G uplink (Shannon-limited, $B=20$ MHz, SNR $= 15$ dB $\approx 31.6$)**

$$
C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(32.6) = 20 \times 10^6 \times 5.02 = 1.004 \times 10^8 \text{ bps}
$$

For a **512-byte V2X message**:

$$
T_{\text{uplink}} = \frac{512 \times 8}{1.004 \times 10^8} \approx 40.8 \text{ \mu s} \approx 0.04 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Cloud processing (negligible server compute)**

$$
T_{\text{proc}} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — 5G downlink + edge forwarding**

$$
T_{\text{downlink}} = 0.04 \text{ ms} + T_{\text{MEC}} = 0.04 + 3 = 3.04 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — End-to-end aggregation**

$$
T_{\text{e2e}} = 33 + 5 + 0.04 + 8 + 3.04 = 49.08 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dominant term is the **sensor frame period (33 ms)**, not the network. This is a frequent KTU exam insight: *"Network latency is rarely the bottleneck; perception and planning dominate."*

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge vs Cloud vs On-board Trade-off

| Parameter | On-board | Edge (MEC) | Central Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | **< 10 ms** | **10–20 ms** | **50–500 ms** |
| Compute | Limited (few hundred TOPS) | Medium (kTOPS shared) | Effectively unlimited |
| Privacy | High (data stays local) | Medium | Low (data leaves vehicle) |
| Cost per inference | High (BOM amortized) | Low (shared) | Very low (hyperscale) |
| Offline capability | Full | Partial | None |
| Use case | Reactive control | Cooperative perception | Fleet learning, OTA |

### 3.4 Derivation — HD Map Differential Update Size

A full city HD map is **$P_{\text{full}} = 4$ TB**. Differential updates use **Mandelbrot-set inspired quadtree deltas** with similarity ratio $r_{\text{sim}} = 0.85$.

$$
P_{\text{delta}} = 4 \text{ TB} \times (1 - 0.85) = 0.6 \text{ TB} = 600 \text{ GB}
$$

At 4G LTE average of $R = 25$ Mbps:

$$
T_{\text{download}} = \frac{600 \times 8 \times 1024}{25 \times 10^6} \times 10^3 = 196{,}608 \text{ s} \approx 54.6 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert **GB → Gb** (multiply by 8) and **Mbps → bps** (multiply by $10^6$) before division. This is the **#1 numerical-error trap** in cloud-AV problems.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud-AV End-to-End Functional Architecture

The following block-level diagram maps the full data flow from a single autonomous vehicle through edge, telco, and OEM cloud tiers, highlighting the **decision-loop ownership** at each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VehOnboard[AV Onboard Stack\nSensors, Perception, Planning, Control]
    V2XUplink[V2X Uplink Radio\n5G NR C-V2X PC5 Interface]
    MECProc[MEC Edge Processor\nRSU and Base Station]
    TelcoCloud[Telco Regional Cloud\nHD Map Tile Cache, V2X Broker]
    OEMCentral[OEM Hyperscale Cloud\nAWS Azure GCP Backend]
    FleetLearn[Fleet Learning Service\nFederated Aggregation Engine]
    SimFarm[Simulation Farm\nCARLA Waymax Replay Cluster]
    OTAOrig[OTA Orchestrator\nDelta Patch Generation]
    Teleop[Remote Teleoperation Hub\nHuman Fallback MRM]
    HDMapStore[HD Map Versioned Storage\nQuad Tree Delta Repository]
    PredMaint[Predictive Maintenance\nTelemetry Anomaly ML]

    VehOnboard -- Cooperative Awareness Message --> V2XUplink
    V2XUplink -- Low Latency Forwarding --> MECProc
    MECProc -- Fused Dynamic Map --> TelcoCloud
    VehOnboard -- Model Gradients and Logs --> OEMCentral
    VehOnboard -- OTA Request --> OTAOrig
    OEMCentral --> FleetLearn
    OEMCentral --> SimFarm
    OEMCentral --> OTAOrig
    OTAOrig -- Delta Patches --> VehOnboard
    TelcoCloud --> HDMapStore
    HDMapStore -- Map Tiles --> VehOnboard
    TelcoCloud --> Teleop
    Teleop -- Remote Command --> VehOnboard
    OEMCentral --> PredMaint
    PredMaint -- Service Alert --> VehOnboard
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Lifecycle — From Sensor to Cloud and Back

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Raw Data\n4 TB per day] --> B{On-board Filter\nTrigger Detection}
    B -- Routine Frames --> C[Discard After 7 Days\nLocal SSD]
    B -- Trigger Events --> D[Compression and\nPII Redaction]
    D --> E[Secure Upload via TLS\nto OEM Cloud]
    E --> F[Object Store S3 Compatible\nTiered Cold Storage]
    F --> G[Auto Labeling Pipeline\nFoundation Model Assisted]
    G --> H[Retraining Dataset]
    H --> I[Model Training Cluster\nGPU TPU Farm]
    I --> J[Model Registry and\nValidation Suite]
    J --> K[Staged OTA Rollout\nCanary 1 percent]
    K --> L[Fleet Wide Deployment]
    L --> A
```

### 4.3 Federated Learning Loop in the AV Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GModel[Global Model\nin OEM Cloud]
    V1[Vehicle 1\nLocal Training]
    V2[Vehicle 2\nLocal Training]
    V3[Vehicle N\nLocal Training]
    Enc1[Encrypted Gradient Upload]
    Enc2[Encrypted Gradient Upload]
    EncN[Encrypted Gradient Upload]
    Agg[Secure Aggregation\nFedAvg Server]
    Eval[Validation and\nShadow Mode Test]
    NewG[Updated Global Model]

    GModel --> V1
    GModel --> V2
    GModel --> V3
    V1 --> Enc1
    V2 --> Enc2
    V3 --> EncN
    Enc1 --> Agg
    Enc2 --> Agg
    EncN --> Agg
    Agg --> Eval
    Eval --> NewG
    NewG -.-> GModel
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Note how the **decision loop** is closed twice — once in the **fast loop** (V2X, 10–50 ms, edge-owned) and once in the **slow loop** (model improvement, hours-days, cloud-owned). This duality is the **conceptual core** of cloud-AV systems and is a recurring KTU long-answer theme.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Cloud Architecture & Data Pipeline*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-tier cloud-AV architecture** (On-board → MEC → Telco → OEM Cloud). For each tier, specify the typical **latency budget, primary function, and one example workload**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An L4 autonomous vehicle is equipped with:
- 8 cameras (2 MP, 30 Hz, 8-bit, YUV422)
- 1 LiDAR (128-layer, 10 Hz, ~70 Mbps)
- 6 Radars (20 Hz, ~1 Mbps each)

Calculate the **total raw data generated per vehicle per 10-hour duty cycle** in TB. If a fleet of **20,000 such vehicles** uploads **only 5 %** of this data to the cloud daily, find the **daily cloud storage requirement** in PB. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Tier | Latency Budget (1 Mark) | Primary Function (½ Mark) | Example Workload (½ Mark) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1: On-board** | **< 10 ms** | Real-time perception & control | Lane-keeping, AEB actuation |
| **Tier 2: MEC (Edge)** | **10–20 ms** | V2X message brokering, sensor fusion | Cooperative intersection perception |
| **Tier 3: Telco Cloud** | **20–50 ms** | HD map tile caching, traffic analytics | Real-time dynamic map refresh |
| **Tier 4: OEM Hyperscale** | **seconds–minutes** | OTA, fleet learning, simulation | Federated training of perception model |

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Use the diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes — student should draw the four blocks vertically with bidirectional arrows, labelling one example workload per tier.

**Connecting narrative (1 Mark):** State that the **lower the tier, the tighter the latency budget** and the **higher the data locality**; the upper tiers trade latency for **scale, persistence, and global intelligence**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Camera data rate per camera (1 Mark)**

$$
r_{\text{cam,1}} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ px} \times 30 \text{ Hz} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 9.6 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} = 960 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — All 8 cameras (1 Mark)**

$$
R_{\text{cam}} = 8 \times 960 = 7680 \text{ Mbps} = 7.68 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily camera data over 10 hours (1 Mark)**

$$
D_{\text{cam}} = 7.68 \text{ Gbps} \times 36000 \text{ s} = 2.7648 \times 10^5 \text{ Gb}
$$

$$
D_{\text{cam}} = \frac{2.7648 \times 10^5}{8} = 34{,}560 \text{ GB} = 34.56 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4 — LiDAR data (1 Mark)**

$$
D_{\text{lidar}} = 70 \text{ Mbps} \times 36000 \text{ s} / 8 = 3.15 \times 10^5 \text{ GB} = 0.315 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5 — Radar data (1 Mark)**

$$
D_{\text{radar}} = 6 \times 1 \text{ Mbps} \times 36000 \text{ s} / 8 = 27 \text{ GB} = 0.027 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6 — Total per vehicle (½ Mark)**

$$
D_v = 34.56 + 0.315 + 0.027 = 34.902 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 7 — Fleet daily upload at 5 % (1 Mark)**

$$
D_{\text{fleet}} = 20{,}000 \times 34.902 \times 0.05 = 34{,}902 \text{ TB} = 34.902 \text{ PB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $D_v \approx 34.9$ TB/vehicle/day; $D_{\text{fleet}} \approx 34.9$ PB/day.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *OTA, Federated Learning & Security*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **Over-the-Air (OTA) update architecture** for autonomous vehicles. With a labelled diagram, describe the **four stages** of an OTA campaign: (i) packaging, (ii) staging, (iii) canary rollout, (iv) fleet-wide deployment. Mention **two security mechanisms** essential to OTA integrity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a **federated learning** setup, **1,000 AVs** each train a perception model locally on their own driving data. After each round, the **FedAvg** algorithm aggregates the model weights. Suppose:
- Each vehicle holds a local dataset of $n_i$ samples; total $N = \sum n_i = 2 \times 10^6$.
- The model has $M = 5 \times 10^7$ trainable parameters.
- Each parameter is a 32-bit float (4 bytes).

Calculate the **total bandwidth consumed by gradient upload per round**, in GB. If the average uplink is **$R = 50$ Mbps**, find the **time taken to upload gradients from all 1,000 vehicles in parallel** (in seconds). State **one privacy benefit** and **one limitation** of federated learning in AVs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**OTA Architecture Description (3 Marks):**

1. **Packaging** — OEM compiles signed delta packages (BSDiff or Courgette) containing firmware, OS kernel, perception model weights, and HD map diffs. Cryptographic signing with **ECDSA P-256**.
2. **Staging** — Packages uploaded to a **staging cloud region**; CI/CD pipeline runs HIL (Hardware-in-Loop) and SIL (Software-in-Loop) regression tests.
3. **Canary Rollout** — Push to **1–5 %** of the fleet ("canary cohort") with **shadow-mode comparison** against the old model.
4. **Fleet-Wide Deployment** — Phased rollout in 10 % → 50 % → 100 % waves, gated by **anomaly KPIs**.

**Labelled Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw four sequential boxes (Package → Stage → Canary → Fleet), with arrow back to vehicle, and indicate **rollback path** on failure.

**Security Mechanisms (2 Marks, 1 each):**
- **Code signing** with hardware-rooted trust (TPM 2.0 / HSM).
- **Secure boot + encrypted transport (TLS 1.3)** and **rollback protection** to prevent version downgrade attacks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Per-vehicle gradient size (1 Mark)**

$$
G_{\text{per\_veh}} = M \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 5 \times 10^7 \times 4 = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} = 200 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Total upload across 1,000 vehicles (1 Mark)**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = 1000 \times 200 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MB} = 200 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-vehicle upload time at 50 Mbps (1 Mark)**

$$
T_{\text{veh}} = \frac{200 \text{ MB} \times 8}{50 \text{ Mbps}} = \frac{1600}{50} = 32 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Parallel upload time (1 Mark)**

Since all 1,000 vehicles upload **in parallel** and the link is **per-vehicle**:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = 32 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Federated learning privacy benefit (1 Mark):**
Raw driving data (which may contain license plates, faces, geolocation) **never leaves the vehicle** — only model gradients are shared, satisfying **GDPR / DPDP Act compliance**.

**Step 6 — Limitation (1 Mark):**
**Gradient leakage attacks** (e.g., DLG – Deep Leakage from Gradients) can reconstruct training samples, so additional **secure aggregation + differential privacy noise** is mandatory.

**Final Answer:** $G_{\text{total}} = 200$ GB per round; $T = 32$ s parallel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Always convert Mbps → bps (×$10^6$) and MB → Mb (×8) before dividing. **Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Confusing "parallel" vs "sequential"** — In federated learning, vehicle uploads happen *in parallel*; never sum their times.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in Part B (a)** — Even a rough 4-box sketch earns 2 marks; **no diagram = full 2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Forgetting security in OTA** — OTA without code-signing/secure-boot is a **partial answer**; the board awards security its own line item.
> 5. **Missing the "Why"** — Pure definitions without explaining *why* the cloud is needed (storage, scale, training) score lower; always include the **engineering rationale**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 1 hour before the exam.**

- ☐ Cloud is the **off-board intelligence layer**; on-board ECUs own the **< 100 ms safety loop**.
- ☐ **Four tiers:** On-board → MEC/Edge → Telco Cloud → OEM Hyperscale; latency grows bottom-up, scale grows top-down.
- ☐ **Seven cloud functions** in AV: HD maps, OTA, federated learning, simulation, teleop, predictive maintenance, V2X orchestration.
- ☐ **OTA** is staged (Package → Stage → Canary → Fleet) and **must** use code-signing + secure boot.
- ☐ **Federated Learning** preserves privacy — only gradients, not raw data, leave the vehicle.
- ☐ **Camera dominates** AV data volume (~98 %); use **trigger-based** retention, not blanket recording.
- ☐ **Edge = low latency, low scale; Cloud = high latency, infinite scale.** Pick by decision-loop ownership.
- ☐ **Key numerical constants to memorise:** 4 TB/day/vehicle, 100 ms safety loop, 1 ms 5G URLLC target, 99.99 % cloud SLA.
- ☐ **Master these formulas:** $D_v = \sum f_i b_i t_{\text{op}}$, $T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{prop}}$, $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- ☐ **Always state privacy + security** in any cloud-AV answer — boards reward GDPR/DPDP awareness.
- ☐ **Memorise the FedAvg bandwidth equation** — gradients = $M \times 4$ bytes per vehicle.
- ☐ **SAE J3016** safety budget ties the on-board loop to the cloud through **MRM (Minimal Risk Manoeuvre)** fallback.
- ☐ **5G NR C-V2X PC5** is the sidelink standard for V2V/V2I; **Uu** is the uplink to base station.
- ☐ **Differential HD map updates** use **quadtree deltas** — only changed tiles are pushed, not the full map.
- ☐ **Draw a labelled diagram** in every 14-mark answer; a missing diagram costs 2 marks even with perfect text.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge Processing for Real-Time Data Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Edge Processing for Real-Time Data Handling # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Edge Processing** refers to the paradigm of performing computational tasks—sensing, perception, sensor fusion, path planning, and actuation control—physically on or near the autonomous vehicle's embedded hardware, rather than transmitting raw sensor streams to a remote cloud data center for analysis. In the context of Autonomous Driving and ADAS, "the edge" denotes the in-vehicle Electronic Control Unit (ECU) domain, including domain controllers, zonal processors, and dedicated AI accelerators. **Real-Time Data Handling** is the disciplined engineering of data acquisition, buffering, preprocessing, and inference pipelines such that the end-to-end latency from physical event capture (e.g., a pedestrian stepping into the road) to actuator command issuance remains bounded below a deterministic worst-case deadline. KTU 2024 categorizes such systems into: - **Hard Real-Time Systems**: Missing a deadline constitutes a system failure (e.g., automatic emergency braking). - **Soft Real-Time Systems**: Missing a deadline degrades quality but does not cause catastrophic failure (e.g., infotainment rendering). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In an L3+ autonomous vehicle, the perception-to-actuation loop must close within **< 100 ms** (often **< 30 ms** at L4/L5). This hard physical constraint is the singular reason edge processing exists in modern ADAS stacks. Cloud round-trips typically exhibit **200–800 ms** latency over 4G/5G networks, which is *catastrophically* inadequate for collision avoidance. ## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Reflexive Chef vs. The Delivery Driver** > > Imagine you are hosting a dinner party and a guest suddenly chokes. > > - **Cloud Processing (The Delivery Driver)**: You call a hospital, describe symptoms, wait for an ambulance to drive across town, and receive instructions. Total time: **15 minutes**. By then, the guest is in serious trouble. > - **Edge Processing (The Reflexive Chef)**: A trained first-aider standing *right there* performs the Heimlich maneuver instantly. Total time: **< 10 seconds**. > > Your autonomous vehicle's braking system is the first-aider. The pedestrian appearing in front of your camera is the choking guest. Cloud round-trip is the ambulance. **Edge processing saves lives because it removes the network from the critical path.** ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following metrics are *universally* adopted in KTU evaluation for real-time automotive systems: - **End-to-End Latency Bound (Deadline)**: **100 ms** (SAE Level 3 standard), **30 ms** (Level 4/5 target) - **Sensor Sampling Rates**: Camera **30–60 Hz**, LiDAR **10–20 Hz**, Radar **20–50 Hz**, CAN Bus **500 Hz–1 MHz** - **GPU Memory Bandwidth (Modern Edge AI)**: **~204 GB/s** (e.g., NVIDIA Orin) - **FPGA Deterministic Jitter**: **< 10 ns** - **In-Vehicle Network Bandwidth**: CAN-FD **2–5 Mbps**, Automotive Ethernet **100 Mbps–1 Gbps**, TSN **up to 10 Gbps** > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for Exam**: Remember the **"Three Latency Budgets"** — Perception **30 ms**, Planning **40 ms**, Control **10 ms**, summing to a total of **80 ms** with **20 ms safety margin**. This 80/20 rule is a frequent KTU question stem. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Real-Time Data Pipeline (Operational Theory) The in-vehicle edge data flow can be decomposed into **six deterministic stages**. Each stage has a bounded Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) and a deterministic scheduling policy. ### Stage 1: Sensor Acquisition - **Function**: Sample physical phenomena via Camera, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic, IMU, GNSS. - **Constraint**: Triggered by hardware interrupt with bounded jitter. - **Why**: Sensors are *asynchronous* to the CPU clock; they must be polled or DMA-driven to prevent data loss. ### Stage 2: Preprocessing & Synchronization - **Function**: ISP (Image Signal Processor) tone mapping, LiDAR point-cloud registration, time-stamping. - **Why**: Raw sensor data is noisy and temporally misaligned across sensors (e.g., camera at t=0 ms, LiDAR at t=3 ms). Edge processors reconcile these timestamps via hardware PTP (Precision Time Protocol) clocks. ### Stage 3: Perception (AI Inference) - **Function**: Object detection, semantic segmentation, lane detection, occupancy grid generation. - **Hardware**: GPU or NPU executing CNN/Transformer models (e.g., YOLO, PointPillars, BEVFormer). - **Why**: Neural network inference is *embarrassingly parallel* and benefits from local tensor cores, avoiding multi-hop network jitter. ### Stage 4: Sensor Fusion & Localization - **Function**: Kalman Filter / Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) / Particle Filter to fuse heterogeneous sensor outputs. - **Why**: Redundancy overcomes single-sensor failure modes (e.g., camera glare, LiDAR snow occlusion). ### Stage 5: Prediction & Planning - **Function**: Behavior prediction of other agents, trajectory generation (lattice, Frenet-frame, MPC). - **Why**: Must run on deterministic CPUs because planning algorithms are *branchy* and unsuitable for GPU parallelism. ### Stage 6: Control & Actuation - **Function**: Convert planned trajectory into steering, throttle, brake commands over CAN/Ethernet. - **Why**: Final link to physical world; runs at **1 kHz** on safety-certified MCUs (e.g., Infineon AURIX). ## 2.2 Scheduling Theory on the Edge Real-time edge systems employ one of three classical scheduling policies: 1. **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**: Static priority = inverse of period. Optimal for *preemptive* fixed-priority systems. 2. **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**: Dynamic priority based on imminent deadline. Optimal in the *dynamic* class. 3. **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)**: Hardware-level traffic shaping with time-aware shapers for in-vehicle Ethernet. **Liu & Layland's Schedulability Test** (1969, still exam-relevant): For a set of *n* periodic tasks with worst-case execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$, RMS guarantees schedulability *if*: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ For large $n$, the bound approaches **0.693** (Liu-Layland utilization bound). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU Part B numerical questions. Memorize the units column. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning | Unit | |---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|------| | 1 | End-to-End Latency | $L_{total} = L_{sens} + L_{pre} + L_{perc} + L_{plan} + L_{ctrl}$ | Sum of all pipeline stage delays | ms | | 2 | Sensor Throughput | $R = f_s \times N_{bits} \times N_{ch}$ | Data rate from sensor to ECU | Mbps | | 3 | Utilization Factor | $U_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of CPU consumed by task $i$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Liu-Layland Bound | $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Maximum safe CPU utilization (RMS) | dimensionless | | 5 | Response Time Test | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time of task $i$ | ms | | 6 | Memory Bandwidth | $B = N_{cores} \times f_{clock} \times W_{bus} \times 8$ | Max data transfer rate | GB/s | | 7 | Deadline Miss Probability | $P_{miss} = 1 - P(\text{WCET} \leq D)$ | Probability of system failure | dimensionless | | 8 | Frame Rate to Period | $T = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Conversion between FPS and period | s | | 9 | CPU Load (Multi-core) | $L = \frac{\sum_{i} C_i}{\sum_{i} T_i}$ | Aggregate utilization across cores | dimensionless | | 10 | SNR for Sensor Fusion | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Sensor quality metric | dB | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Table Warning**: Note that absolute value bars $\lvert x \rvert$ are written with `\lvert \rvert` macros, **not** the pipe character `|`, to prevent markdown table parsing errors. KTU examiners *will* deduct marks for broken formatting in submitted digital answer sheets. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Edge processing is *not* merely an academic exercise. It directly determines: - **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)**: ASIL-D rated systems (highest safety integrity) demand deterministic edge computing because cloud links cannot be certified. - **Regulatory Compliance**: UN Regulation No. 157 (Automated Lane Keeping) requires the perception stack to function **without network connectivity**. - **Cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434)**: Local processing reduces the attack surface by eliminating the cloud link that could be hijacked. - **Cost Economics**: A 1 Gbps cellular link consumes **~15 W** of continuous transmission power; on-board edge AI consumes **~30 W** total while *eliminating* recurring data costs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency Budget Calculation **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** An autonomous vehicle uses a Camera (30 Hz), LiDAR (10 Hz), and Radar (20 Hz). Compute the worst-case sensor-to-decision latency if the perception stage processes one LiDAR frame every 100 ms, the planning stage runs at 50 ms intervals, and the control loop runs at 1 ms intervals. Assume deterministic scheduling with no interrupt jitter. ### Step 1: Identify Sensor Sampling Periods Convert sampling frequencies to periods using $T = 1/f_s$. $$T_{camera} = \frac{1}{30 \text{ Hz}} = 33.33 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{lidar} = \frac{1}{10 \text{ Hz}} = 100 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{radar} = \frac{1}{20 \text{ Hz}} = 50 \text{ ms}$$ **Explanation:** The slowest sensor in the pipeline dictates the upper bound on wait time for synchronized fusion. Here, LiDAR at 100 ms is the bottleneck. ### Step 2: Compute Worst-Case Sensor Wait Time In a synchronized pipeline, the perception stage must wait until the slowest sensor's data is ready. The worst-case wait is the LiDAR period itself. $$L_{sens} = T_{lidar} = 100 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 3: Sum the Pipeline Stage Latencies $$L_{total} = L_{sens} + L_{perc} + L_{plan} + L_{ctrl}$$ $$L_{total} = 100 + 50 + 50 + 1$$ $$L_{total} = 201 \text{ ms}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: The end-to-end latency is **201 ms**, which exceeds the SAE Level 3 hard deadline of **100 ms**. The system must either upgrade to a faster LiDAR (e.g., 20 Hz reducing wait to 50 ms) or implement *out-of-order* fusion with bounded staleness compensation. This is a classic KTU numerical answer where students lose marks by forgetting to add control loop latency. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Liu-Layland Schedulability Test **Problem Statement:** A domain controller runs three periodic tasks: - Task $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 2$ ms, $T_1 = 10$ ms (Perception, highest priority) - Task $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 3$ ms, $T_2 = 20$ ms (Planning, medium priority) - Task $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 4$ ms, $T_3 = 40$ ms (Logging, lowest priority) Verify if the task set is schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling on a single-core CPU. ### Step 1: Compute Individual Utilization Factors $$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$ $$U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15$$ $$U_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{4}{40} = 0.10$$ ### Step 2: Compute Aggregate Utilization $$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 0.45$$ ### Step 3: Compute Liu-Layland Upper Bound For $n = 3$ tasks: $$U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = 3 \times \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)$$ $$2^{1/3} = 1.2599$$ $$U_{bound} = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$ ### Step 4: Compare and Conclude $$U_{total} = 0.45 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7798$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: The task set is **schedulable** under RMS. *However*, the bound is sufficient but not necessary — we should also run a **Response Time Analysis** for exact verification. Students who stop at the Liu-Layland test get full marks; examiners reward those who note the limitation. ### Step 5: Exact Response Time Analysis (Bonus) For $\tau_2$ (preempted only by $\tau_1$): $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 3$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 = 3 + 0 \times 2 = 3$$ Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 3$ ms $\leq T_2 = 20$ ms. ✓ Schedulable. For $\tau_3$ (preempted by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$): $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{20} \right\rceil \times 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{20} \right\rceil \times 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9$$ Fixed point reached: $R_3 = 9$ ms $\leq T_3 = 40$ ms. ✓ Schedulable. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensor Throughput and Bus Bandwidth **Problem Statement:** A 5-megapixel automotive camera (RGB, 8 bits/channel) runs at 60 Hz. Compute (a) raw sensor throughput, (b) compressed throughput assuming 10:1 H.265 compression, and (c) determine if Automotive Ethernet (1 Gbps) is sufficient without compression. ### Step 1: Compute Raw Throughput Resolution = $2592 \times 1944$ pixels $\approx 5.04$ MP. $$N_{bits} = 2592 \times 1944 \times 8 \times 3 = 120.96 \text{ Mbits/frame}$$ $$R_{raw} = 120.96 \times 60 = 7257.6 \text{ Mbps} \approx 7.26 \text{ Gbps}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Compressed Throughput $$R_{compressed} = \frac{R_{raw}}{10} = \frac{7.26}{10} = 0.726 \text{ Gbps} = 726 \text{ Mbps}$$ ### Step 3: Compare to Available Bandwidth $$R_{compressed} = 726 \text{ Mbps} \leq 1000 \text{ Mbps (Automotive Ethernet)}$$ $$R_{raw} = 7258 \text{ Mbps} \gg 1000 \text{ Mbps (Automotive Ethernet)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: Raw 5MP@60fps data **cannot** be streamed over standard Automotive Ethernet. Either compression, reduced frame rate, or higher-bandwidth TSN (multi-Gbps) is mandatory. This is why edge processing exists: to compress/decompress intelligently *on-board* rather than waste transmission bandwidth. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Edge Processing Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical in-vehicle edge computing stack used in modern ADAS platforms (NVIDIA DRIVE, Mobileye EyeQ, Tesla HW4). ```mermaid flowchart LR sensIn[Sensor Array Layer
Camera, LiDAR, Radar, IMU, GNSS] --> dmaIn[DMA and PTP Time Sync
Hardware Timestamping] dmaIn --> preProc[Preprocessing Layer
ISP, Noise Filter, ROI Crop] preProc --> percEng[Perception Engine
CNN, BEV, Transformer] preProc --> fusionEng[Sensor Fusion Engine
EKF, Particle Filter] percEng --> fusionEng fusionEng --> predMod[Prediction Module
Trajectory Forecasting] predMod --> planMod[Planning Module
MPC, Lattice Planner] planMod --> ctrlMod[Control Module
PID, LQR on AURIX MCU] ctrlMod --> actOut[Actuator Layer
Steer, Throttle, Brake via CAN-FD] ctrlMod --> hmiOut[HMI and Logging
Display, Telemetry] classDef hw fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000 classDef sw fill:#e2ece9,stroke:#3a5a40,color:#000 classDef crt fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#000 class sensIn,actOut,hmiOut hw class dmaIn,preProc,percEng,fusionEng,predMod,planMod sw class ctrlMod crt ``` ## 4.2 Real-Time Scheduling Timeline This Gantt-style timeline illustrates the precedence and deadline constraints for three tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic priority on a single edge processor core. ```mermaid gantt title Real-Time Task Schedule on Edge ECU (Hyperperiod = 40 ms) dateFormat X axisFormat %S section High Priority tau1_perception (C=2ms, T=10ms) :active, t1_0, 0, 2 tau1_perception (C=2ms, T=10ms) :active, t1_1, 10, 12 tau1_perception (C=2ms, T=10ms) :active, t1_2, 20, 22 tau1_perception (C=2ms, T=10ms) :active, t1_3, 30, 32 section Medium Priority tau2_planning (C=3ms, T=20ms) :active, t2_0, 2, 5 tau2_planning (C=3ms, T=20ms) :active, t2_1, 22, 25 section Low Priority tau3_logging (C=4ms, T=40ms) :active, t3_0, 5, 9 ``` ## 4.3 Edge vs. Cloud Processing Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startNew([New Sensor Event Arrives]) --> q1{Latency Requirement
less than 100 ms?} q1 -- Yes --> edgeProc[Process on Edge ECU
GPU, NPU, or FPGA] q1 -- No --> q2{Bandwidth Available
greater than 100 Mbps?} q2 -- Yes --> cloudProc[Offload to Cloud
5G MEC or Data Center] q2 -- No --> buffer[Local Buffer and
Store-and-Forward] edgeProc --> safetyCheck{ASIL Compliance
and Determinism OK?} safetyCheck -- Yes --> actuate[Issue Actuator Command] safetyCheck -- No --> failSafe[Enter Fail-Safe Mode
Minimum Risk Maneuver] cloudProc --> analytics[Aggregate Analytics
and Model Training] analytics -.Updates.-> modelOTA[OTA Model Deployment
to Edge Fleet] modelOTA -.Push.-> edgeProc ``` ## 4.4 Hardware Layering Within an Edge Domain Controller ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DC["Domain Controller (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ6)"] safetyMCU["Safety MCU Layer
AURIX TC397, ASIL-D
1 kHz Control Loop"] rtCPU["Real-Time CPU Layer
ARM Cortex-R52, AUTOSAR Classic
Planning and Decision"] aiAccel["AI Accelerator Layer
GPU Tensor Cores, NPU
Perception Inference"] sharedMem["Shared LPDDR5 Memory
204 GB/s Bandwidth"] end safetyMCU --- sharedMem rtCPU --- sharedMem aiAccel --- sharedMem sensorBus["Sensor Bus Layer
GMSL, FPD-Link, Automotive Ethernet"] --> DC DC --> vehicleBus["Vehicle Bus Layer
CAN-FD, FlexRay, TSN"] classDef asild fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000 classDef rt fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 classDef ai fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2b8a3e,color:#000 classDef mem fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000 class safetyMCU asild class rtCPU rt class aiAccel ai class sharedMem mem ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define *Edge Processing* in the context of autonomous vehicles. Why is it preferred over cloud-based processing for safety-critical functions like Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Edge processing refers to performing computation—sensing, perception, decision-making, and actuation—on-board the vehicle using embedded ECUs, rather than transmitting raw sensor data to remote cloud servers. It is preferred over cloud processing for AEB because: 1. **Latency Determinism**: AEB requires end-to-end latency below **100 ms**, whereas cloud round-trips over 4G/5G introduce **200–800 ms** of variable network delay. 2. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)**: Cloud links cannot be certified to ASIL-D; edge ECUs can be. 3. **Connectivity Independence**: Tunnels, parking garages, and rural areas lack reliable network coverage; AEB must function *offline*. 4. **Cybersecurity**: Eliminating the network link reduces the remote-attack surface. *(Award 1 mark per correct point, maximum 3.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **Hard Real-Time** and **Soft Real-Time** systems. Give one automotive example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Attribute | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time | |-----------|----------------|----------------| | Deadline Consequence | Missing a deadline = **system failure** (catastrophic) | Missing a deadline = **degraded quality** (recoverable) | | Response Guarantee | Deterministic, bounded WCET required | Statistical, best-effort | | Typical Latency | **< 10–100 ms** | **100 ms – seconds** | **Automotive Examples:** - **Hard Real-Time**: Electronic Stability Control (ESC) — control loop runs at 200 Hz; missing a cycle can cause loss of vehicle control. - **Soft Real-Time**: Infotainment touchscreen rendering — a dropped frame causes minor visual stutter but does not endanger occupants. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > For all Part B questions on edge processing, students **commonly lose marks** at the following four points: > > 1. **Forgetting units**: Writing "latency = 100" without "ms". Always include units in every numerical answer. > 2. **Mixing up utilization and load**: $U_i = C_i/T_i$ is *per-task*; aggregate load is the *sum*. Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion. > 3. **Ignoring the control loop latency**: Students often compute only perception + planning and forget the 1 kHz control loop (~1 ms). Always include it. > 4. **Confusing WCET with average execution time**: WCET is the *worst case* — using average values in schedulability tests is a **2-mark penalty** under KTU valuation. > 5. **Not stating assumptions**: Every derivation must begin with "Assume deterministic scheduling, no interrupt jitter, single-core execution" unless otherwise stated. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an in-vehicle edge computing platform for an L3 autonomous vehicle. Identify the role of each subsystem and the typical latency budget allocated to each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Three real-time tasks run on an edge ECU under Rate Monotonic Scheduling: - $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$ ms, $T_1 = 5$ ms - $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 10$ ms - $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 5$ ms, $T_3 = 25$ ms Using the **Response Time Analysis**, determine if the task set is schedulable. Show all iterations. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Architecture (4 marks) and Latency Budget (3 marks):** The in-vehicle edge computing platform comprises the following functional blocks: 1. **Sensor Array Layer**: Cameras (8 MP, 30 Hz), LiDARs (128-channel, 10 Hz), Radars (77 GHz, 20 Hz), IMU, GNSS receivers. 2. **Preprocessing Layer**: Hardware ISP for camera demosaicing, LiDAR motion compensation, timestamp synchronization via PTP. 3. **Perception Engine**: GPU/NPU running deep neural networks (YOLO, BEVFormer) for object detection, semantic segmentation, lane detection. 4. **Sensor Fusion Module**: Extended Kalman Filter on CPU fuses perception outputs with odometry and map data. 5. **Planning Module**: Behavior prediction and trajectory generation (Frenet-frame, lattice, or MPC). 6. **Control Module**: Safety MCU (AURIX) executes 1 kHz PID/LQR controllers. 7. **Actuation Layer**: Steer-by-wire, throttle-by-wire, brake-by-wire over CAN-FD or Automotive Ethernet. **Typical Latency Budget (aligned with the 100 ms SAE L3 standard):** | Stage | Budget | |-------|--------| | Sensor acquisition + preprocessing | **20 ms** | | Perception (AI inference) | **30 ms** | | Sensor fusion + localization | **15 ms** | | Prediction + planning | **25 ms** | | Control + actuation | **10 ms** | | **Total** | **100 ms** | *Examiner Note: Award full marks if the student draws a coherent block diagram with correct arrows and labels the latency allocations within ±20% of the values above.* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Step 1: Order tasks by period (Rate Monotonic = shorter period = higher priority).** Priority order: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$. **Step 2: Compute response time for $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no preemption):** $$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \leq T_1 = 5 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 3: Compute response time for $\tau_2$ (preempted only by $\tau_1$):** Iteration 0: $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}$$ Iteration 1: $$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ Iteration 2: $$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 3 \text{ ms} \leq T_2 = 10 \text{ ms}$ ✓ **Step 4: Compute response time for $\tau_3$ (preempted by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):** Iteration 0: $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5 \text{ ms}$$ Iteration 1: $$R_3^{(1)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 = 5 + 1 + 2 = 8 \text{ ms}$$ Iteration 2: $$R_3^{(2)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 = 5 + 2 + 2 = 9 \text{ ms}$$ Iteration 3: $$R_3^{(3)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{5} \right\rceil \times 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \times 2 = 5 + 2 + 2 = 9 \text{ ms}$$ Fixed point reached: $R_3 = 9 \text{ ms} \leq T_3 = 25 \text{ ms}$ ✓ **Conclusion**: All three tasks meet their deadlines. The system is **schedulable** under RMS. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **Liu-Layland schedulability test** for Rate Monotonic Scheduling. A domain controller must run three tasks with the following parameters. Verify schedulability using the test: - $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 3$ ms, $T_1 = 20$ ms - $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$ ms, $T_2 = 10$ ms - $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 1$ ms, $T_3 = 5$ ms **(7 marks)** **(b)** An autonomous vehicle's perception pipeline includes a Camera at 30 fps, a LiDAR at 10 fps, and a Radar at 20 fps. If the fusion node uses *out-of-order* processing with bounded staleness of 50 ms, compute the worst-case effective latency. Justify whether the system meets the SAE Level 3 deadline of 100 ms. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Liu-Layland Test (3 marks):** For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling on a single processor, the task set is guaranteed to be schedulable if the total CPU utilization $U$ satisfies: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ The right-hand side is the **Liu-Layland bound**, which equals **$\ln(2) \approx 0.693$** for large $n$. **Numerical Application (4 marks):** Note: Under RMS, priorities are assigned inversely to periods. So $\tau_3$ (period 5 ms) has highest priority; $\tau_2$ (10 ms) medium; $\tau_1$ (20 ms) lowest. $$U_1 = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15$$ $$U_2 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$ $$U_3 = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$ $$U_{total} = 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.55$$ Liu-Layland bound for $n=3$: $$U_{bound} = 3 \times \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$ **Comparison**: $$U_{total} = 0.55 \leq 0.7798 \quad \checkmark$$ **Conclusion**: The task set is schedulable under RMS with a comfortable **22.9% utilization headroom** available for additional low-priority tasks (e.g., logging, OTA telemetry). --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Step 1: Compute Sensor Sampling Periods:** $$T_{cam} = \frac{1000}{30} = 33.33 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{lidar} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{radar} = \frac{1000}{20} = 50 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2: Compute Effective Fusion Latency:** In *out-of-order* fusion with bounded staleness, the fusion node does **not** wait for the slowest sensor. Instead, it processes whichever sensor data is fresh within a 50 ms staleness budget. The slowest sensor period is 100 ms, but with staleness compensation, the perception output is at most 50 ms stale. $$L_{eff} = T_{slowest} - L_{staleness} = 100 - 50 = 50 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3: Add Down-Stream Pipeline Latencies:** $$L_{total} = L_{eff} + L_{plan} + L_{ctrl}$$ $$L_{total} = 50 + 40 + 10 = 100 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 4: Compare to SAE L3 Deadline:** $$L_{total} = 100 \text{ ms} \leq 100 \text{ ms (deadline)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: The system *just meets* the SAE L3 deadline **exactly at the boundary**. This is **not a safe engineering margin**. In real deployment, the architects would either (a) upgrade the LiDAR to 20 Hz to reduce $T_{lidar}$ to 50 ms, bringing total latency to **50 ms**, or (b) reduce staleness budget to **30 ms**, bringing total latency to **80 ms** with a 20 ms safety margin. *Examiners award full marks to students who flag this boundary risk explicitly.* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Read 5 minutes before exam.** - [x] **Edge processing** = on-vehicle computation; eliminates cloud latency. - [x] **Hard real-time** = missing deadline = catastrophic failure (AEB, ESC). - [x] **Soft real-time** = missing deadline = degraded UX (infotainment). - [x] **SAE L3 deadline** = 100 ms; **L4/L5 target** = 30 ms. - [x] **Typical pipeline** = Sensor → Preprocess → Perception → Fusion → Planning → Control → Actuator. - [x] **Latency budget** = 30 + 30 + 25 + 10 + 5 = **100 ms** (perception-dominant). - [x] **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** = static priority ∝ 1/period. - [x] **EDF** = dynamic priority based on closest deadline. - [x] **Liu-Layland bound** for $n$ tasks: $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$; limit = 0.693. - [x] **Response time** of task $i$: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$. - [x] **Hardware layers**: Safety MCU (ASIL-D, AURIX) → RT CPU (Cortex-R, AUTOSAR) → AI Accelerator (GPU/NPU). - [x] **Sensor bus**: Camera = GMSL/FPD-Link; LiDAR = Automotive Ethernet; Radar = CAN-FD. - [x] **Vehicle bus**: CAN-FD (2–5 Mbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps–1 Gbps), TSN (10 Gbps). - [x] **Throughput formula**: $R = f_s \times N_{pixels} \times N_{bits/channel} \times N_{channels}$. - [x] **5MP@60fps raw** ≈ 7.26 Gbps — exceeds Automotive Ethernet; requires compression or TSN. - [x] **Determinism sources**: Pre-emptive RTOS (QNX, VxWorks, AUTOSAR), hardware PTP clocks, DMA-driven I/O. - [x] **Standards**: ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO/SAE 21434 (cybersecurity), AUTOSAR (architecture). - [x] **Pitfall**: Always add the control loop latency (~1 ms) in any end-to-end calculation. - [x] **Pitfall**: WCET ≠ average execution time — always use WCET in schedulability tests. - [x] **Pitfall**: Out-of-order fusion improves latency but introduces staleness — bound it explicitly. - [x] **Pitfall**: Absolute value in formulas: use $\lvert x \rvert$ macros, never bare pipe characters. - [x] **Pitfall**: At 100 ms exactly, you have *zero* safety margin — flag this in any answer. - [x] **Engineering mantra**: *“Latency is not a metric — it is a safety boundary.”*

topics.title: Examples of Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platforms**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cloud-Based Autonomous Driving Platform** is a distributed, on-demand computing and data management ecosystem that resides on remote hyperscale data centers and is consumed by autonomous vehicles (AVs), Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), and Tier-1 suppliers for the purposes of **fleet learning, HD map curation, large-scale simulation, Over-The-Air (OTA) deployment, and big-data analytics**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, these platforms are characterized by the orchestration of **petabyte-scale storage, GPU/TPU-accelerated training, and low-latency V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication** between in-vehicle edge ECUs and the cloud backend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A cloud-based AD platform = Cloud (Storage + AI Training) ⇄ Edge (Vehicle ECUs + Sensors) ⇄ Fleet (Connected Cars).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Brain Hospital for Cars"**
> 
> Imagine every autonomous car on the road as a **medical intern doctor**. Each intern can diagnose common diseases (basic object detection, lane keeping) using knowledge loaded into their brain. But when they encounter a **rare, complex case** (an unusual accident scenario, a strange construction zone), they don't rely on their own memory — they **call a "Brain Hospital" in the cloud**. The hospital has thousands of super-expert consultants (NVIDIA H100 GPUs, TPUs), massive medical libraries (petabytes of driving data), and even a virtual reality simulator where the intern can practice 10,000 variations of the rare case **in 10 minutes** before going back to the road. The "Brain Hospital" is the **cloud platform**. Once the consultant solves the case, the **updated prescription is broadcast back to ALL interns in the fleet** through OTA updates — meaning the entire fleet gets smarter from a single encounter. This is the essence of *fleet learning* enabled by cloud platforms.

### 1.3 Industry-Leading Examples (The "Big Four" Cloud AD Platforms)

| # | Platform | Parent Company | Primary Use Case |
|---|----------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **NVIDIA DGX Cloud + DRIVE Cloud** | NVIDIA | AI model training, DRIVE Sim simulation, mapping |
| 2 | **Google Cloud (Waymo, Wayve)** | Alphabet | HD maps, ML pipelines, Waymo Open Dataset |
| 3 | **AWS (IoT, RoboMaker, S3)** | Amazon | Fleet management, data lake, virtual simulation |
| 4 | **Microsoft Azure (AVM)** | Microsoft | SCANeR Studio, Azure Maps, AD validation |
| 5 | **Baidu Apollo Cloud** | Baidu | Apollo open-source stack, maps (China-centric) |
| 6 | **Tesla Dojo + Autonomy Day Cloud** | Tesla | Vision-only training, FSD beta fleet learning |
| 7 | **Mobileye Road Experience Management (REM™)** | Intel/Mobileye | Crowdsourced HD map harvesting |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in the Cloud AD Domain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Bandwidth per AV:** $\approx$ **1–4 TB/day** (raw sensor log upload).
> - **Latency budget for V2X:** **< 100 ms** (safety-critical) / **< 1 s** (infotainment).
> - **HD Map size (single city):** $\approx$ **10–100 GB**.
> - **Training compute:** **ExaFLOP** scale (e.g., $10^{18}$ FLOPs per training run).
> - **Global AV fleet data growth:** projected at **> 500 PB/day** by 2030.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Functional Pillars of Every Cloud AD Platform

Every cloud-based AD platform (whether NVIDIA, Google, AWS, or Baidu) is decomposed into **three functional pillars** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### Pillar 1: **Data Ingestion & Storage Layer** (The "Memory")
- **Function:** Ingests petabytes of raw sensor data (camera, LiDAR, RADAR, CAN bus, GNSS) uploaded from the vehicle fleet.
- **KTU Key Components:**
  - **Object storage** (e.g., AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage) — cost-optimized for unstructured data.
  - **Data lake architecture** — schema-on-read (Parquet, ORC) vs. schema-on-write.
  - **Tiered storage** — Hot (NVMe SSD, queried daily) → Warm (HDD, monthly) → Cold (Glacier/Archive).
- **Why it matters:** A single L4 robotaxi can generate **~30 TB/day** of raw data. Without a robust storage layer, data is lost or becomes unsearchable.

#### Pillar 2: **AI Training & Simulation Layer** (The "Reasoning")
- **Function:** Trains deep neural networks (perception, prediction, planning) and runs massive-scale *regression testing* in synthetic worlds.
- **KTU Key Components:**
  - **GPU/TPU clusters** (NVIDIA H100, Google TPU v5, AMD MI300).
  - **Distributed training frameworks** — Horovod, PyTorch DDP, Megatron-LM.
  - **Closed-loop simulation** — CARLA, NVIDIA DRIVE Sim, LGSVL, Waymax.
- **Why it matters:** You cannot validate a Level-4 system by road-testing alone; you need **billions of virtual kilometers** in simulation. Cloud GPUs make this economically feasible.

#### Pillar 3: **Deployment & Fleet Learning Layer** (The "Voice")
- **Function:** Pushes trained models, HD map deltas, and OTA software back to every vehicle in the fleet. Also aggregates "edge case" discoveries from the fleet back to the cloud (the *closed learning loop*).
- **KTU Key Components:**
  - **OTA update orchestrators** (e.g., Tesla OTA, NVIDIA DRIVE OTA).
  - **Shadow-mode deployment** — new model runs in parallel with production model; differences are logged for safety validation.
  - **V2X edge gateways** — 5G/C-V2X modems bridge the cloud to the vehicle.

### 2.2 The Closed-Loop Fleet Learning Cycle (KTU High-Yield)

The defining feature of a *cloud-based* platform is the **closed-loop cycle**, which edge-only systems cannot perform:

$$ \text{Edge} \xrightarrow{\text{Raw data, edge cases}} \text{Cloud} \xrightarrow{\text{Trained model, HD map delta}} \text{Edge} \rightarrow \cdots $$

This loop is repeated continuously, so the **N-th vehicle to encounter a rare scenario benefits from the learning gained by the (N-1) previous vehicles**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Unit | KTU Relevance |
|--------|----------|----------------------|--------------|---------------|
| $D_{\text{AV}}$ | Daily data generated per AV | $D_{\text{AV}} = N_c \cdot R_c \cdot t + N_l \cdot R_l \cdot t$ | TB/day | Storage sizing |
| $B_{\text{uplink}}$ | Required uplink bandwidth | $B_{\text{uplink}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{AV}}}{T_{\text{upload}} \cdot \eta}$ | Gbps | Telematics design |
| $T_{\text{cloud}}$ | End-to-end cloud latency | $T_{\text{cloud}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{tx}}$ | ms | V2X safety |
| $F_{\text{train}}$ | Training compute | $F_{\text{train}} = N_{\text{params}} \cdot 6 \cdot D_{\text{tokens}}$ | FLOP | GPU procurement |
| $C_{\text{sim}}$ | Cost of simulation | $C_{\text{sim}} = N_{\text{scenarios}} \cdot C_{\text{GPU\text{-}hr}} \cdot \bar{t}_{\text{scene}}$ | USD | Cloud budget |
| $M_{\text{map}}$ | HD map size per region | $M_{\text{map}} = A_{\text{region}} \cdot \rho_{\text{features}}$ | GB | Map tile strategy |
| $S_{\text{OTA}}$ | OTA rollout time | $S_{\text{OTA}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{vehicles}} \cdot \bar{S}_{\text{pkg}}}{B_{\text{cell}} \cdot \eta}$ | hours | Fleet management |

**Where:**
- $N_c, N_l$ = number of cameras/LiDARs, $R_c, R_l$ = their data rates (Mbps), $t$ = hours/day of operation.
- $\eta$ = protocol efficiency ($\approx 0.7$–$0.85$).
- $\rho_{\text{features}}$ = feature density of HD map (lane lines, signs, etc.) in MB/km$^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The data-rate formula $D_{\text{AV}} = N_c \cdot R_c \cdot t + N_l \cdot R_l \cdot t$ is the **first thing** a Tier-1 supplier calculates when designing the **telematics control unit (TCU)** of a connected AV. For example, a vehicle with **8 cameras @ 8 MP × 30 fps** and **1 LiDAR @ 128 channels @ 20 Hz** generates roughly $D_{\text{AV}} \approx 2.5\text{ TB/day}$, which is why **5G uplink and edge-compression** are non-negotiable for cloud platforms.

### 2.4 Why Cloud Platforms Are Engineering-Essential

1. **Compute Asymmetry:** Training an L4 perception model requires $\sim 10^{25}$ FLOPs, but the in-vehicle SoC (e.g., NVIDIA Orin) delivers only $\sim 10^{12}$ FLOPs. The cloud provides the missing $10^{13}$× factor.
2. **Storage Asymmetry:** A 1-million-vehicle fleet generates exabytes/year — impossible to retain on-board.
3. **Knowledge Symmetry:** A single vehicle sees $\sim 10^6$ km/year. A 1M-fleet sees $10^{12}$ km/year — orders of magnitude more diverse data, only achievable via cloud aggregation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Daily Data Volume per AV (Storage Sizing)

**Problem Setup:** A Level-3 autonomous sedan is equipped with:
- $N_c = 6$ surround cameras, each $R_c = 50$ MB/s (compressed H.265 at 4K/30fps)
- $N_l = 1$ roof-mounted LiDAR, $R_l = 100$ MB/s
- $N_r = 5$ RADARs, $R_r = 0.5$ MB/s each
- $N_{\text{CAN}} = 1$ CAN-bus logger, $R_{\text{CAN}} = 0.01$ MB/s
- The vehicle operates for $t = 8$ hours/day in "log mode."

**Goal:** Compute total daily data $D_{\text{AV}}$ in TB.

**Step 1 — Camera contribution:**

$$D_c = N_c \cdot R_c \cdot t = 6 \times 50\,\text{MB/s} \times (8 \times 3600)\,\text{s}$$

$$D_c = 6 \times 50 \times 28800 = 8\,640\,000\,\text{MB} = 8.64\,\text{TB}$$

**Step 2 — LiDAR contribution:**

$$D_l = N_l \cdot R_l \cdot t = 1 \times 100\,\text{MB/s} \times 28800\,\text{s} = 2\,880\,000\,\text{MB} = 2.88\,\text{TB}$$

**Step 3 — RADAR contribution:**

$$D_r = N_r \cdot R_r \cdot t = 5 \times 0.5 \times 28800 = 72\,000\,\text{MB} = 0.072\,\text{TB}$$

**Step 4 — CAN-bus contribution:**

$$D_{\text{CAN}} = 1 \times 0.01 \times 28800 = 288\,\text{MB} \approx 2.88 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{TB}$$

**Step 5 — Total daily data per AV:**

$$D_{\text{AV}} = D_c + D_l + D_r + D_{\text{CAN}} = 8.64 + 2.88 + 0.072 + 0.000288$$

$$D_{\text{AV}} \approx 11.59\,\text{TB/day} \approx 12\,\text{TB/day}$$

**Step 6 — Fleet-level aggregation (1 million AVs):**

$$D_{\text{fleet}} = 10^6 \times 12\,\text{TB/day} = 12\,\text{EB/day (Exabytes/day)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, and Azure Blob** use **erasure coding and tiered pricing** — 12 EB/day from a single OEM fleet is larger than the entire daily internet traffic of many countries.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Uplink Bandwidth Requirement

**Problem Setup:** The 12 TB/day must be uploaded during off-peak hours, e.g., $T_{\text{upload}} = 4$ hours (overnight charging), with protocol efficiency $\eta = 0.75$.

**Step 1 — Total bits to upload:**

$$D_{\text{bits}} = 12\,\text{TB} \times 8 \times 10^{12}\,\text{bit/TB} = 9.6 \times 10^{13}\,\text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Raw bandwidth:**

$$B_{\text{raw}} = \frac{D_{\text{bits}}}{T_{\text{upload}}} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{13}}{4 \times 3600} = 6.67 \times 10^{9}\,\text{bps} \approx 6.67\,\text{Gbps}$$

**Step 3 — Apply protocol efficiency:**

$$B_{\text{uplink}} = \frac{B_{\text{raw}}}{\eta} = \frac{6.67}{0.75} \approx 8.89\,\text{Gbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single AV needs **~9 Gbps sustained uplink** to fully offload — this is why **5G mmWave (peak 10 Gbps) and edge-compression codecs (e.g., HEVC, learned compression)** are critical enablers of cloud AD platforms.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Latency Budget for Safety-Critical V2X

**Problem Setup:** An AV receives a hazard warning from the cloud (e.g., black ice 2 km ahead). Compute the maximum end-to-end latency allowed under a 100 ms safety budget.

$$T_{\text{cloud}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{tx}}$$

**Step 1 — Assign realistic sub-budgets:**

- $T_{\text{prop}}$ (5G one-way radio propagation) $= 5$ ms
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ (edge gateway buffering) $= 10$ ms
- $T_{\text{process}}$ (cloud inference) $= 30$ ms
- $T_{\text{tx}}$ (downlink transmission) $= 5$ ms

**Step 2 — Sum:**

$$T_{\text{cloud}} = 5 + 10 + 30 + 5 = 50\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Margin to safety budget:**

$$\text{Margin} = 100 - 50 = 50\,\text{ms (50% safety margin)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume the cloud "must" respond in < 10 ms. In reality, the 100 ms V2X safety budget is a **deliberate trade-off** — sub-10 ms response is reserved for **on-board** emergency braking (AEB). Cloud platforms handle *predictive, non-imminent* hazards.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Big-Four Cloud AD Platforms

| Feature | NVIDIA DGX Cloud + DRIVE | Google Cloud (Waymo) | AWS (RoboMaker + IoT) | Microsoft Azure (AVM) |
|---------|--------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Primary Strength** | GPU training + DRIVE Sim | HD maps (Vector Tiles) | Elastic data lake | SCANeR simulator |
| **AI Chips** | H100 / GH200 | TPU v5e / v5p | Trainium / Inferentia | NVIDIA H100 (Azure ND) |
| **Simulation Tool** | DRIVE Sim (Omniverse) | Waymax, CARLA via GCP | RoboMaker + CARLA | SCANeR Studio |
| **Map Service** | DRIVE Map (Crowd-sourced) | Google Maps / Waymo HD | HERE via AWS partnership | Azure Maps |
| **OTA** | DRIVE OTA | Android-like OTA | AWS IoT Jobs | Azure IoT Hub |
| **Open-Source** | Partial (Omniverse) | Waymo Open Dataset | CARLA, DeepRacer | AirSim |
| **Best For** | OEMs needing full-stack | Robotaxi companies | Cost-sensitive fleets | Academic research |

### 3.5 Detailed Example: NVIDIA DGX Cloud Architecture

NVIDIA's DGX Cloud is offered as a **bare-metal-as-a-service** with H100 clusters. Its AD-specific stack is layered as follows:

1. **Layer 1 — Infrastructure:** 8× H100 per DGX node, NVLink, InfiniBand HDR.
2. **Layer 2 — Frameworks:** CUDA, cuDNN, TensorRT, NeMo, TAO Toolkit.
3. **Layer 3 — AD-specific tools:**
   - **DRIVE Sim** (built on Omniverse) — photorealistic, sensor-accurate simulation.
   - **DRIVE Map** — multi-modal HD map with lane geometry, signage, drivable areas.
   - **DRIVE OS** — safety-certified QNX-based vehicle OS.
4. **Layer 4 — OTA + Fleet:** DRIVE Cloud SDK for model deployment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Cloud AD Platform Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VehFleet["VEHICLE FLEET (EDGE LAYER)"]
        V1["AV01 Cam LiDAR RADAR"]
        V2["AV02 Cam LiDAR RADAR"]
        V3["AV_n Cam LiDAR RADAR"]
    end

    subgraph Telematics["TELEMATICS GATEWAY"]
        TCU["5G V2X TCU Modem"]
        EdgeComp["Edge Encoder HEVC Compression"]
    end

    subgraph Ingest["CLOUD - INGESTION LAYER"]
        MQTT["MQTT 5G Broker"]
        S3["Object Storage Data Lake S3 GCS AzureBlob"]
        Kafka["Stream Processor Kafka Kinesis"]
    end

    subgraph AILayer["CLOUD - AI TRAINING AND SIMULATION LAYER"]
        GPUCluster["GPU Cluster H100 TPU v5"]
        SimEnv["Simulation DRIVE Sim CARLA Waymax"]
        Train["Training Pipeline Perception Prediction Planning"]
    end

    subgraph Deploy["CLOUD - DEPLOYMENT LAYER"]
        Registry["Model Registry MLflow"]
        OTA["OTA Orchestrator"]
        MapSvc["HD Map Service"]
    end

    V1 --> TCU
    V2 --> TCU
    V3 --> TCU
    TCU --> EdgeComp
    EdgeComp --> MQTT
    MQTT --> Kafka
    Kafka --> S3
    S3 --> Train
    S3 --> SimEnv
    Train --> GPUCluster
    SimEnv --> GPUCluster
    Train --> Registry
    SimEnv --> MapSvc
    Registry --> OTA
    MapSvc --> OTA
    OTA --> TCU
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow Sequence (Closed-Loop Fleet Learning)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AV as Autonomous Vehicle
    participant GW as 5G Telematics Gateway
    participant CL as Cloud Ingestion
    participant AI as AI Training Cluster
    participant SIM as Simulation Farm
    participant REG as Model Registry
    participant OTA as OTA Push Service

    AV->>GW: Raw sensor log (12 TB/day)
    GW->>GW: Compress + deduplicate
    GW->>CL: Upload via 5G uplink
    CL->>AI: Trigger labelling pipeline
    AI->>AI: Train perception model
    AI->>SIM: Regression test in 10^6 scenarios
    SIM-->>AI: Pass/Fail report
    AI->>REG: Register new model v_n+1
    REG->>OTA: Sign + stage artifact
    OTA->>GW: Delta OTA push (~500 MB)
    GW->>AV: Flash ECU + reboot
    AV->>AV: Run new model in shadow mode
```

### 4.3 Storage Tiering Strategy in Cloud AD

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Hot["HOT TIER - NVMe SSD - Last 7 days - Used for active training"]
    Warm["WARM TIER - HDD - 7 to 90 days - Used for re-labelling"]
    Cold["COLD TIER - Object Archive - 90 days to 7 years - Compliance and insurance"]
    Newest["Newest Telemetry Ingested"] --> Hot
    Hot -->|7 days elapsed| Warm
    Warm -->|90 days elapsed| Cold
```

### 4.4 Edge-Cloud Workload Split (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Edge["ON-VEHICLE EDGE - Real Time Sub 50 ms"]
        Perc["Perception Object Detection Semantic Segmentation"]
        Pred["Prediction Trajectory Forecasting"]
        PlanLocal["Local Planner Emergency AEB LKA"]
    end

    subgraph Cloud["CLOUD - Non Real Time Seconds to Hours"]
        HDMap["HD Map Generation and Update"]
        SimVal["Simulation Validation Billions of km"]
        FLEarn["Fleet Learning Aggregate Edge Cases"]
        Analytics["Fleet Analytics Utilisation Driver Behaviour"]
    end

    Edge -.uploads edge cases.-> Cloud
    Cloud -.OTA model updates HD map deltas.-> Edge
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three examples of cloud-based autonomous driving platforms and state one key strength of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **NVIDIA DGX Cloud + DRIVE Cloud** — Strength: GPU-accelerated training of perception models and DRIVE Sim for photorealistic closed-loop simulation. *(1 mark)*
2. **Google Cloud (Waymo)** — Strength: Industry-leading HD map service with centimeter-accurate lane geometry and crowdsourced updates. *(1 mark)*
3. **AWS for Autonomous Driving (RoboMaker + S3)** — Strength: Elastic, cost-optimized petabyte-scale data lake with on-demand GPU spot fleets. *(1 mark)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "fleet learning" in the context of cloud-based ADAS. Why is it impossible without a cloud backend?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fleet learning is the continuous, closed-loop process in which **every vehicle in a connected fleet uploads driving data and edge-case scenarios to a central cloud**, where aggregated models are retrained and **pushed back to the entire fleet** via OTA updates. Each new vehicle inherits the collective experience of all previous vehicles.

**Why cloud is essential (1 mark):** On-board storage is limited to a few TB, and on-board compute is $\sim 10^{12}$ FLOPs — insufficient to retrain billion-parameter networks on exabytes of data. Only a hyperscale cloud (with $10^{17}$–$10^{18}$ FLOPs and EB-scale storage) can support the retraining and broadcast step of the loop.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a typical cloud-based autonomous driving platform**. Label the three main layers and explain the data flow between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of NVIDIA DGX Cloud and Google Cloud** in enabling Level-3/Level-4 autonomous driving. Highlight the unique tools offered by each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
A typical cloud-based AD platform consists of three vertically-stacked layers:
1. **Ingestion Layer (2 marks):** Receives raw sensor data (camera, LiDAR, RADAR, CAN) from the fleet via 5G/V2X. Components: MQTT/Kafka brokers, object storage (S3/GCS), data lake with schema-on-read Parquet files.
2. **AI Training & Simulation Layer (3 marks):** GPU/TPU clusters train perception (CNN/Transformer), prediction (RNN/GNN), and planning (RL/optimization) models. Closed-loop simulation in DRIVE Sim / CARLA / Waymax validates the trained model against billions of virtual km. Labelling pipelines use auto-labellers (e.g., NeMo, SAM-based segmentation).
3. **Deployment & OTA Layer (2 marks):** A signed model artifact is registered in MLflow, then staged for OTA push via a delta-update protocol. The vehicle downloads only the changed weights (~500 MB), verifies the cryptographic signature, and switches to the new model in *shadow mode* before full activation.

> The closed-loop ensures the N-th vehicle benefits from the learnings of the previous N-1 vehicles — a feature unique to cloud-based (vs. edge-only) architectures.

**(b) NVIDIA DGX Cloud + Google Cloud (7 marks):**

| Aspect | NVIDIA DGX Cloud | Google Cloud |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| **Hardware** | 8× H100 GPUs per node, NVLink + InfiniBand *(1.5 marks)* | TPU v5e/v5p pods, custom ASIC *(1 mark)* |
| **AD Software Stack** | DRIVE OS, DRIVE Sim (Omniverse), DRIVE Map, TAO Toolkit *(2 marks)* | Waymo Driver stack, Vector Map tiles, Waymax simulator, Waymo Open Dataset *(1.5 marks)* |
| **Use Case** | OEMs building L2+/L3 vehicles (Mercedes, Volvo, Jaguar) *(0.5 mark)* | Robotaxi operators (Waymo) and ML research teams *(0.5 mark)* |

Both platforms offer **managed Kubernetes for distributed training**, **petabyte-scale object storage**, and **zero-trust security for OTA delivery** — making them the de-facto choices for AD development in 2024.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **complete data pipeline** of a cloud-based autonomous driving platform — from in-vehicle data capture to model deployment. Include a numerical example for daily data volume per vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **cloud-based vs. edge-only** processing for autonomous vehicles. Under what scenarios is each preferred? Justify with latency and compute arguments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Complete Data Pipeline (7 marks):**

The data pipeline consists of **5 sequential stages**:

1. **Capture (1 mark):** 6 cameras @ 50 MB/s, 1 LiDAR @ 100 MB/s, 5 RADARs @ 0.5 MB/s each, 1 CAN-bus @ 0.01 MB/s, for 8 hours/day.
2. **Edge Pre-processing (1 mark):** HEVC/H.265 compression, temporal deduplication, event-triggered recording (only save when novelty score > threshold).
3. **Upload (1 mark):** Via 5G TCU during off-peak hours (~9 Gbps uplink needed for full 12 TB/day raw).
4. **Cloud Storage + Labelling (2 marks):** Object storage (S3/GCS) → auto-labelling pipeline (NeMo, SAM) → human-in-the-loop review for low-confidence samples.
5. **Training + Sim + OTA (2 marks):** Distributed training on H100 cluster → DRIVE Sim regression → signed model in MLflow → delta OTA to fleet.

**Numerical example (covered in Section 3.1):** Total $\approx 12$ TB/day per AV.

**(b) Cloud vs. Edge Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Edge-Only (On-Vehicle) | Cloud-Based |
|-----------|-------------------------|-------------|
| **Latency** | < 10 ms (deterministic) | 50–500 ms (variable) |
| **Compute** | $\sim 10^{12}$ FLOPs (NVIDIA Orin) | $\sim 10^{17}$ FLOPs (H100 cluster) |
| **Storage** | 1–10 TB (NVMe) | EB-scale (S3) |
| **Connectivity Dependence** | None | 5G/V2X required |
| **Use Case** | AEB, LKA, ACC (safety-critical, real-time) | HD map updates, fleet learning, large-scale sim |
| **Failure Mode** | Graceful degradation | Susceptible to network outage |

**Preferred scenarios (2 marks):** Edge-only is preferred for **safety-critical functions under 50 ms** (AEB, collision avoidance). Cloud-based is preferred for **non-real-time, knowledge-intensive tasks** (map updates, long-horizon prediction, model retraining). The ideal architecture is a **hybrid** — safety on edge, intelligence on cloud.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students frequently write "the cloud is faster" — this is **WRONG**. Cloud has higher latency than edge. The correct statement is: "Cloud has *more* compute and *more* storage, but *higher* latency."
> - Students often confuse **5G *peak* bandwidth (10 Gbps)** with **sustained bandwidth (~1 Gbps in real deployments)**. Use realistic values in numericals.
> - When asked "list three platforms," do **not** list three services from the *same* company (e.g., S3 + EC2 + Lambda from AWS). Examiners expect *three different platforms from different vendors* or *three distinct tools* with clear differentiation.
> - **Mark-loss zone (0.5–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention the **OTA + cryptographic signing** step in Part B answers on architecture. Always close the loop.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must memorize before KTU ESE):**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Cloud AD Platform = remote data center + GPU cluster + storage + OTA stack, accessed by AV fleet.
> 
> ✅ **Three Layers:** Ingestion → AI Training/Sim → Deployment/OTA.
> 
> ✅ **Closed-Loop Fleet Learning:** Edge → Cloud → Edge (the "Brain Hospital" analogy).
> 
> ✅ **Data per AV per day:** $\approx 12$ TB raw (6 cam + 1 LiDAR + 5 RADAR @ 8 hrs).
> 
> ✅ **Fleet data:** $\approx 12$ EB/day for 1M vehicles.
> 
> ✅ **Uplink bandwidth needed:** $\approx 9$ Gbps per AV (sustained).
> 
> ✅ **Latency:** Edge < 10 ms (safety); Cloud 50–500 ms (non-safety).
> 
> ✅ **Big Four Platforms:** NVIDIA DGX Cloud, Google Cloud (Waymo), AWS, Microsoft Azure.
> 
> ✅ **Specialized Tools:** DRIVE Sim, DRIVE Map, Waymo Open Dataset, CARLA, Waymax, SCANeR.
> 
> ✅ **Why cloud is essential:** Asymmetry in compute ($10^{13}\times$), storage, and knowledge (fleet scale).
> 
> ✅ **Key Risk:** Network outage → degraded operation → must design edge fallback.
> 
> ✅ **2024 Trend:** Foundation models for driving (e.g., Wayve LINGO, NVIDIA DRIVE Foundation Model) trained on **cloud-scale multimodal data**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Integration and Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Integration and Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Integration and Calibration** in the context of Autonomous Driving and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) is the disciplined engineering process of unifying heterogeneous hardware (sensors, ECUs, actuators) and software modules (perception, planning, control) into a single, deterministic, real-time capable cyber-physical system, followed by the precise tuning of every internal parameter—spatial, temporal, dynamic, and control—so that the vehicle performs at design specification across all Operational Design Domain (ODD) conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Examiner Favourite):**
> *"System Integration is the systematic combination of verified sub-systems into a complete vehicle system, while Calibration is the precise adjustment of system parameters (geometric, dynamic, electronic) to ensure conformity with design intent and functional safety standards such as ISO 26262 and ISO/SAE 21434."*

The procedure is typically partitioned into four sub-domains:

1. **Sensor Integration and Calibration** – mounting geometry, intrinsic parameters, extrinsic transforms, and time base alignment.
