2. **Software Integration** – middleware (e.g., AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive, ROS 2), data buses (CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1, FlexRay), and functional modules.
3. **Vehicle Dynamics Calibration** – identification of cornering stiffness, tire slip behavior, mass distribution, and powertrain response.
4. **Control System Tuning** – gain scheduling, longitudinal/lateral controller parameter optimization, and longitudinal jerk/acceleration profiling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Precision Swiss Watch**
> Imagine you are assembling a luxury Swiss chronograph. You start with **100+ individual gears, springs, jewels, and hands**. Each component is machined to microns, but until they are placed on the *same axis*, *lubricated identically*, and *synchronized to the escapement*, the watch is just a pile of metal.
>
> - **Integration** is the act of placing every gear on its correct shaft and meshing them.
> - **Calibration** is setting the watch so that at 12:00:00 the second hand is exactly at 60, the mainspring tension matches the rated power reserve, and the balance wheel oscillates at exactly **28,800 vph (vibrations per hour) ± 2 seconds/day**.
>
> An autonomous vehicle is the same: a **camera, LiDAR, radar, and IMU** are individually accurate, but they are useless until their **coordinate frames are aligned (extrinsic calibration)**, their **clocks share the same microsecond (temporal sync)**, and their **outputs drive the steering/throttle/brake actuators with mathematically validated gain values (control calibration)**.

### 1.3 Core Quantitative Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extrinsic Translation Accuracy** | $\varepsilon_t$ | **± 1 cm to ± 2 cm** | Sensor-to-vehicle frame alignment |
| **Extrinsic Rotation Accuracy** | $\varepsilon_R$ | **± 0.05° to ± 0.1°** | Sensor orientation alignment |
| **Time Synchronization Skew** | $\Delta t$ | **≤ 1 ms (ASIL-D)**, **≤ 10 ms (ASIL-B)** | Cross-sensor data fusion validity |
| **Camera Reprojection Error** | $e_{rep}$ | **< 0.3 pixel RMS** | Intrinsic calibration quality |
| **Vehicle Heading Accuracy** | $\sigma_\psi$ | **≤ 0.1° (DGPS+RTK)** | Localization fusion |
| **Longitudinal Speed Error** | $\delta v_x$ | **≤ 0.1 m/s** | Ego-motion compensation |
| **Steering Angle Resolution** | $\Delta\delta$ | **≤ 0.1°** | Lateral control fidelity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Calibration is non-negotiable in ADAS:** A **1°** misalignment in a forward-facing camera can cause a pedestrian detected at **100 m** to be laterally mis-localized by **≈ 1.74 m**—potentially outside the drivable corridor. Hence, every parameter must be calibrated against a **ground-truth reference (checkerboard, AprilTag, total station, RTK-GNSS base station)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the System Integration Pipeline

The KTU 2024 module structures integration as a **five-layer architectural stack**. Each layer must be verified before ascending to the next—exactly like a **V-model testing philosophy**.

#### Layer 1 — Hardware Integration (Physical Layer)

- **Sensor mounting on calibrated brackets** (laser-aligned jig, accuracy **± 0.5 mm**).
- **Wiring harness routing** with **twisted-pair shielded cables** for CAN-FD (characteristic impedance **120 Ω ± 10 %**).
- **Power supply decoupling** with EMI filters and **TVS diodes** for transient suppression (per ISO 7637-2).
- **Thermal management** ensuring sensors operate within spec range (Camera: **−40 °C to +85 °C**, LiDAR: **−20 °C to +60 °C**).

#### Layer 2 — Sensor Calibration (Geometric & Temporal)

- **Intrinsic Calibration** — focal length $(f_x, f_y)$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, lens distortion coefficients $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$.
- **Extrinsic Calibration** — 6-DoF rigid transformation $[R \mid t]$ between sensor frames (e.g., LiDAR ↔ Camera ↔ Vehicle).
- **Temporal Calibration** — time offset $\Delta t$ and skew correction using PTP (IEEE 1588 v2) or hardware-triggered timestamping.

#### Layer 3 — Middleware & Bus Integration (Communication Layer)

- **Signal-to-Service translation** (Signal → PDU → Service in AUTOSAR Adaptive).
- **Bus arbitration and gateway configuration** (CAN-FD at **2 Mbps**, 100BASE-T1 Ethernet at **100 Mbps**).
- **Diagnostic protocol stack** — UDS over CAN (ISO 14229), DoIP over Ethernet.

#### Layer 4 — Functional Software Integration (Algorithm Layer)

- **Perception-Planning-Control handshake** via deterministic scheduling (e.g., **preempt-RT Linux** with kernel latency **< 50 μs**).
- **Sensor fusion** (EKF/UKF for state estimation, occupancy grid for environment).
- **Behavior arbitration** (rule-based FSM or POMDP for decision making).

#### Layer 5 — Vehicle Dynamics Calibration & Control Tuning (Actuation Layer)

- **Steering rack ratio identification** via sweep test.
- **Throttle map calibration** across RPM-load surface.
- **Brake pressure-to-deceleration curve** fitting.
- **PID / MPC / LQR gain tuning** for longitudinal and lateral controllers.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-relevant. Memorize the units, the boundary conditions, and the symbols.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Domain | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rigid Body Transformation** | $P_{target} = R \cdot P_{source} + t$ | Extrinsic Calibration | $R \in SO(3)$, $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ |
| 2 | **Homogeneous Matrix** | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 6-DoF Transform | $T \in SE(3)$ |
| 3 | **Camera Projection (Pinhole)** | $s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K [R \vert t] \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Camera-LiDAR Fusion | $K$ = intrinsic, $s$ = scale |
| 4 | **Camera Intrinsic Matrix** | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic Calibration | $f_x, f_y$ in **pixels** |
| 5 | **Radial Distortion** | $x_d = x (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | Lens Model | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ |
| 6 | **Bicycle Model Yaw Rate** | $r = \frac{v_x \tan(\delta_f)}{L}$ | Vehicle Dynamics | $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta_f$ = steer angle |
| 7 | **Sideslip Angle (Bicycle)** | $\beta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{l_r \tan(\delta_f)}{L}\right)$ | Vehicle Dynamics | $l_r$ = CoG to rear axle |
| 8 | **Ackermann Condition** | $\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{w}{L}$ | Steering Geometry | $w$ = track width |
| 9 | **PID Controller** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Control Tuning | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = gains |
| 10 | **Ziegler-Nichols (PI)** | $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = 0.8 T_u$ | Tuning Heuristic | $K_u$ = ultimate gain |
| 11 | **Longitudinal Decel** | $a_x = \frac{T_{brk} - F_{drag} - F_{roll}}{m}$ | Brake Cal | $T_{brk}$ = brake torque, $m$ = mass |
| 12 | **Cornering Stiffness (Linear Tire)** | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | Tire Model | $C_\alpha$ in **N/rad** |
| 13 | **EKF Prediction** | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_{k-1})$ | State Estimation | $f$ = non-linear process model |
| 14 | **EKF Update (Kalman Gain)** | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Sensor Fusion | $H$ = measurement Jacobian |
| 15 | **PTP Time Sync Offset** | $\theta = \frac{(t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3)}{2}$ | Temporal Calibration | $t_1$ to $t_4$ = timestamps |
| 16 | **Coordinate Frame Chain** | $P_{world} = T_{wb} \cdot T_{bs} \cdot P_{sensor}$ | Multi-Sensor Fusion | $b$ = body, $s$ = sensor |
| 17 | **Cost Function (Calib. Optimization)** | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_i \vert\vert p_i^{proj} - q_i^{obs} \vert\vert^2$ | Non-linear Least Squares | $p$ predicted, $q$ observed |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Calibration Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Highway Pilot (L2+)** | Lane centring, AEB | Steering angle must be accurate to **± 0.5°** for lane-keeping within **± 20 cm**. |
| **Automated Valet Parking (L4)** | Multi-camera surround fusion | Camera extrinsics must be **< 0.1°** to merge 4 fish-eye images seamlessly. |
| **Robotaxi (L4)** | LiDAR + Camera + Radar fusion | Time skew **> 5 ms** causes an 80 km/h vehicle to be mis-localized by **> 11 cm** between frames. |
| **Heavy-Truck Platooning** | Longitudinal control | Brake pressure-decel mapping must be **< 5 % error** to maintain **3 m** gap at **90 km/h**. |
| **Off-Road Autonomy** | IMU + GPS fusion | IMU bias drift of **0.01 °/s** accumulates to **36 arc-seconds per hour** = **174 m error** without GPS. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Camera-LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration via PnP (Perspective-n-Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most derivation-heavy topic in KTU 2024 Module 4. Master it.**

We want to find the rigid transformation $T_{LC} = [R_{LC} \mid t_{LC}] \in SE(3)$ that maps a 3-D point in the LiDAR frame $P^L$ to the Camera frame $P^C$.

**Step 1: Establish the rigid body transformation.**

By definition, any point in the LiDAR frame relates to the camera frame as:

$$P^C = R_{LC} \cdot P^L + t_{LC}$$

**Step 2: Convert to homogeneous form for compact algebra.**

Using the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} P^C \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{LC} & t_{LC} \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P^L \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Project the transformed point onto the image plane using the pinhole camera model.**

The pinhole projection is:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \cdot P^C$$

Substituting $P^C = R_{LC} P^L + t_{LC}$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R_{LC} & t_{LC} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P^L \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Use checkerboard correspondences to set up equations.**

Suppose we capture a planar calibration target observed by both sensors. Let the target contain $N$ known corner points with known 3-D coordinates in the LiDAR frame $\{P_i^L\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and corresponding 2-D image points $\{q_i = (u_i, v_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

For each correspondence:

$$s_i \begin{bmatrix} u_i \\ v_i \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R_{LC} & t_{LC} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P_i^L \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Linearize via Direct Linear Transform (DLT) as initial guess.**

Eliminate the unknown scale $s_i$ using cross product:

$$q_i \times (K [R_{LC} \mid t_{LC}] \tilde{P}_i^L) = 0$$

This yields **two independent linear equations per correspondence**. With $N \geq 6$ points, the system is solvable for the 12 unknowns of $[R_{LC} \mid t_{LC}]$ up to scale.

**Step 6: Refine via non-linear least squares (Levenberg-Marquardt).**

Minimize the sum of squared reprojection errors:

$$\mathcal{L}(R_{LC}, t_{LC}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \pi(K [R_{LC} \mid t_{LC}] \tilde{P}_i^L) - q_i \right\|^2$$

Where $\pi(\cdot)$ denotes the dehomogenization:

$$\pi\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} X/Z \\ Y/Z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7: Project to $SE(3)$ manifold to enforce rigid-body constraint.**

Since $R$ must satisfy $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = 1$, the solution is projected back via **SVD**:

$$R = U V^T \quad \text{where} \quad R_{init} = U \Sigma V^T$$

If $\det(R) < 0$, flip the sign of the last column of $U$.

**Step 8: Final transformation matrix is ready for fusion.**

$$T_{LC} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{LC} & t_{LC} \\ 0_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(3)$$

**Step 9: Convert $SE(3)$ to 6-DoF pose for storage (Euler + Translation or Quaternion + Translation).**

Using ZYX Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$:

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

And the equivalent unit quaternion representation:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_w \\ q_x \\ q_y \\ q_z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{with} \quad \vert q \vert = 1$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Vehicle Bicycle Model for Lateral Control Calibration

The **kinematic bicycle model** is the foundation of lateral control (lane keeping, lane change). We derive it here exhaustively.

**Step 1: Define coordinate frames.**

- Inertial frame $\{X, Y\}$: fixed to ground.
- Vehicle body frame $\{x, y\}$: origin at Centre of Gravity (CoG).

**Step 2: Relate the velocities of front and rear axles.**

Rear wheel velocity along its heading:

$$v_r = \dot{x} \cos(\psi) + \dot{y} \sin(\psi)$$

Front wheel velocity (steered by angle $\delta_f$):

$$v_f = (\dot{x} \cos(\psi) + \dot{y} \sin(\psi)) \cos(\delta_f) + (-\dot{x} \sin(\psi) + \dot{y} \cos(\psi)) \sin(\delta_f)$$

**Step 3: Apply the no-side-slip assumption at the rear wheel.**

In the bicycle model, the rear wheel does not slip laterally:

$$\dot{x} \sin(\psi) - \dot{y} \cos(\psi) = 0$$

Solving for $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ in terms of $v_r$ and $\psi$:

$$\dot{x} = v_r \cos(\psi), \quad \dot{y} = v_r \sin(\psi)$$

**Step 4: Relate front and rear velocities through the geometry of instantaneous rotation.**

The yaw rate $\dot{\psi} = r$ is the same for both wheels about the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR):

$$r = \frac{v_f \sin(\delta_f) - 0}{l_f} = \frac{0 - (-v_r \sin(\beta))}{l_r}$$

Where $\beta$ is the sideslip angle at the CoG.

**Step 5: Derive the kinematic yaw rate equation.**

Setting the two expressions for $r$ equal:

$$\frac{v_f \sin(\delta_f)}{l_f} = \frac{v_r \sin(\beta)}{l_r}$$

Using $v_r = v_x \cos(\beta)$ and $v_f \cos(\delta_f) = v_r$ (assuming small angles):

$$r = \frac{v_x \tan(\delta_f)}{L}$$

Where $L = l_f + l_r$ is the wheelbase. This is the **canonical KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{r = \frac{v_x \tan(\delta_f)}{L}}$$

**Step 6: Sideslip angle derivation.**

The CoG sideslip is:

$$\beta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{l_r \tan(\delta_f)}{L}\right)$$

This is small for typical highway driving (δ ≤ 5°, β ≤ 2°).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Calibration Error Propagation

When a sensor is mis-calibrated by $(\delta R, \delta t)$, the resulting localization error at distance $d$ is:

$$\varepsilon(d) = d \cdot \tan(\theta_{err}) \approx d \cdot \theta_{err} \quad \text{for small } \theta_{err}$$

| Error Type | Magnitude | Object at 30 m | Object at 100 m |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotation 0.1° (1.7 mrad) | Misalignment | **0.05 m (5 cm)** | **0.17 m (17 cm)** |
| Rotation 0.5° (8.7 mrad) | Misalignment | **0.26 m (26 cm)** | **0.87 m (87 cm)** |
| Translation 2 cm | Offset | **2 cm** | **2 cm** (depth-invariant) |
| Time skew 10 ms @ 100 km/h | Lateral shift | **0.28 m (28 cm)** | **0.28 m (28 cm)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight (KTU 2024):** Note that **rotation error scales with distance**, while **translation error does not**. This is why long-range radar or long-range camera calibration prioritizes **angular accuracy**, while short-range ultrasonic favors **translation accuracy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: ADAS System Integration Architecture (5-Layer Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph physical["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
        camF["Front Camera 8MP"]
        camS["Surround Camera x4"]
        lidR["Roof LiDAR 128-Ch"]
        radL["Long Range Radar"]
        radS["Corner Radar x4"]
        imu1["IMU 6-Axis 200Hz"]
        gnss1["GNSS RTK Receiver"]
        uss1["Ultrasonic x12"]
    end

    subgraph comms["LAYER 2: COMMUNICATION BUS"]
        busCAN["CAN-FD 2 Mbps"]
        busETH["100BASE-T1 Ethernet"]
        busFLEX["FlexRay 10 Mbps"]
        gw1["Central Gateway ECU"]
    end

    subgraph middle["LAYER 3: MIDDLEWARE AUTOSAR"]
        mwr1["RTE Runtime Env"]
        mwr2["Arai Adaptive Svc"]
        mwr3["Time Sync PTP"]
    end

    subgraph algo["LAYER 4: ALGORITHMIC MODULES"]
        algo1["Perception CNN"]
        algo2["Sensor Fusion EKF"]
        algo3["Behavior Planner FSM"]
        algo4["Trajectory Optim MPC"]
        algo5["Longitudinal Ctrl PID"]
        algo6["Lateral Ctrl Stanley"]
    end

    subgraph act["LAYER 5: ACTUATION"]
        actS["EPS Steering"]
        actT["Throttle By Wire"]
        actB["Brake By Wire"]
        actG["Gear Selector"]
    end

    camF --> busETH
    camS --> busETH
    lidR --> busETH
    radL --> busCAN
    radS --> busCAN
    imu1 --> busETH
    gnss1 --> busETH
    uss1 --> busCAN
    busCAN --> gw1
    busETH --> gw1
    busFLEX --> gw1
    gw1 --> mwr1
    mwr1 --> mwr2
    mwr2 --> mwr3
    mwr3 --> algo1
    mwr3 --> algo2
    algo1 --> algo2
    algo2 --> algo3
    algo3 --> algo4
    algo4 --> algo5
    algo4 --> algo6
    algo5 --> actT
    algo5 --> actB
    algo6 --> actS
    algo3 --> actG
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Vehicle on Calib Rig]) --> s1["Mechanical Mounting Check"]
    s1 --> s2["Camera Intrinsic Calib Checkerboard"]
    s2 --> s3["LiDAR Intrinsic Range Validation"]
    s3 --> s4["LiDAR Camera Extrinsic Calib Target"]
    s4 --> s5["Radar Extrinsic Reflector Sweep"]
    s5 --> s6["IMU Gyro Accel Bias Estimation"]
    s6 --> s7["GPS Antenna Lever Arm Calibration"]
    s7 --> s8["Time Synchronization PTP"]
    s8 --> s9["Vehicle Mass Inertia ID"]
    s9 --> s10["Steering Ratio Mapping"]
    s10 --> s11["Throttle Map Calibration"]
    s11 --> s12["Brake Pressure Decel Curve"]
    s12 --> s13["Controller PID Tuning"]
    s13 --> s14["HIL Hardware in Loop Test"]
    s14 --> s15["VIL Vehicle in Loop Test"]
    s15 --> check{All KPIs Met}
    check -->|No| s13
    check -->|Yes| endprod([Production Sign Off])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HIL Testing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sim["REAL TIME SIMULATOR dSPACE or NI"]
        simA["Vehicle Dynamics Model"]
        simB["Tire and Road Model"]
        simC["Sensor Models Camera LiDAR Radar"]
        simD["Traffic and Environment"]
    end

    subgraph io["I/O INTERFACE PANEL"]
        io1["CAN-FD Bus Emulator"]
        io2["Ethernet 100BASE-T1"]

    end

    subgraph dut["DEVICE UNDER TEST ECU"]
        dut1["Perception SoC"]
        dut2["Planning SoC"]

    end

    subgraph hmi["ENGINEER HMI"]
        hmi1["Automation Desk GUI"]
        hmi2["Failure Injection Panel"]
        hmi3["Real Time Plotter"]
    end

    simA --> io1
    simB --> io1
    simC --> io4
    simD --> io3
    io1 --> dut1
    io2 --> dut2
    io3 --> dut3
    dut1 --> io1
    dut2 --> io2
    dut3 --> io3
    io1 --> hmi1
    io2 --> hmi2
    io3 --> hmi3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Sensor Time Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gps["GNSS PPS Pulse Per Second"] --> ptp["PTP Grandmaster Clock"]
    ptp --> t1["Camera Timestamp 100 ms"]
    ptp --> t2["LiDAR Timestamp 100 ms"]
    ptp --> t3["Radar Timestamp 50 ms"]
    ptp --> t4["IMU Timestamp 200 Hz"]
    t1 --> buffer["Timestamp Aligner Buffer"]
    t2 --> buffer
    t3 --> buffer
    t4 --> buffer
    buffer --> sync["Synchronized Data Stream Skew under 1 ms"]
    sync --> fus["Sensor Fusion EKF Input"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Differentiate between **intrinsic calibration** and **extrinsic calibration** of a camera used in an ADAS perception module. Give one numerical performance target for each.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Intrinsic Calibration | Extrinsic Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Determines the internal optical parameters of the camera (focal length, principal point, lens distortion). | Determines the 6-DoF pose $[R \vert t]$ of the camera relative to another frame (vehicle, LiDAR, world). |
| **Parameters Estimated** | $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$ | Rotation matrix $R$, translation vector $t$ |
| **Reference Target** | Checkerboard or ChArUco board with known square size | Calibration board with both LiDAR-visible and camera-visible features |
| **Performance Target** | Reprojection error **< 0.3 pixel RMS** | Translation **± 1–2 cm**, rotation **± 0.05°–0.1°** |
| **Frequency** | Performed once per camera model (factory) | Performed per vehicle assembly (after mounting) |

**Mark Distribution (1 mark each):**
- (i) Clear definition of intrinsic — **1 mark**
- (ii) Clear definition of extrinsic — **1 mark**
- (iii) Numerical performance target for either — **1 mark**

---

**Q2.** Why is **time synchronization** between heterogeneous sensors (Camera, LiDAR, Radar, IMU) critical in an ADAS sensor-fusion pipeline? What is the typical acceptable skew for ASIL-D systems?

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Time synchronization ensures that data samples from different sensors with different native clock rates are temporally aligned before fusion. Without it, the fusion algorithm will associate objects from one time instant with another, causing:

1. **Ghost objects** appearing and disappearing at random in the occupancy grid.
2. **Latent collisions** at high speed (e.g., at **120 km/h = 33.3 m/s**, a **10 ms** skew causes a **0.33 m** position error).
3. **State estimator divergence** in EKF because measurements are no longer in the predicted time window.

**Typical Acceptable Skew Values:**
- **ASIL-D** (highest safety integrity): **≤ 1 ms**
- **ASIL-B** (medium integrity): **≤ 10 ms**
- **QM** (Quality Managed): **≤ 50 ms** acceptable

**Methods Used:** PTP (IEEE 1588 v2), GPS PPS, hardware-triggered timestamping, ROS 2 `use_sim_time` with `/clock` topic.

**Mark Distribution:**
- (i) Reason for synchronization — **1 mark**
- (ii) Consequence of poor sync with numerical example — **1 mark**
- (iii) ASIL-D value **≤ 1 ms** — **1 mark**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern: Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of the following two questions.**

---

## ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Camera-LiDAR extrinsic calibration** procedure. Derive the projection equation used to map LiDAR points onto the image plane and explain how the 6-DoF transformation matrix $T_{LC}$ is obtained. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** testing methodology for an ADAS ECU. List the major components of an HIL rig and discuss the role of fault injection and regression testing. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Camera-LiDAR Extrinsic Calibration (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
A Mermaid block diagram (as shown in Section 4) is drawn showing the calibration target (checkerboard with circular cut-outs), the LiDAR emitting points onto the target, the camera capturing the 2-D image, and the optimizer computing $[R \mid t]$.

**Projection Equation Derivation (3 marks):**

The 3-D point in the LiDAR frame is $P^L = (X, Y, Z)^T$. The same point in the camera frame is given by the rigid transform:

$$P^C = R_{LC} P^L + t_{LC}$$

Using the pinhole model with intrinsic matrix $K$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R_{LC} & t_{LC} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Obtaining the 6-DoF Transform (2 marks):**

- Collect $N \geq 6$ correspondences $(P_i^L, q_i)$ where $q_i = (u_i, v_i)$ is the detected image point.
- Solve the linear DLT system to get an initial $R_{LC}, t_{LC}$.
- Refine via Levenberg-Marquardt non-linear least squares to minimize reprojection error:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \pi(K [R_{LC} \mid t_{LC}] \tilde{P}_i^L) - q_i \right\|^2$$

- Project $R$ back to $SO(3)$ via SVD: $R = UV^T$.

---

### Part (b) — HIL Testing Methodology (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Hardware-in-the-Loop is a real-time simulation technique where the actual ADAS ECU (the "device under test") is connected to a real-time simulator that emulates sensors, vehicle dynamics, and environment, allowing closed-loop testing without a real vehicle.

**Major Components (3 marks):**
1. **Real-Time Simulator** (dSPACE SCALEXIO, NI VeriStand, Vector vTESTstudio) — runs the vehicle and environment models at deterministic step size (typically **1 ms**).
2. **I/O Interface Panel** — CAN-FD, Ethernet, analog/digital I/O, video injection.
3. **Host PC with HMI** — AutomationDesk, ControlDesk for scripting and visualization.
4. **Fault Injection Unit (FIU)** — can short, open, or inject erroneous signals on bus lines.
5. **Load Box** — simulates electrical loads of the real vehicle.

**Role of Fault Injection (2 marks):**
Fault injection simulates sensor failures, bus errors (e.g., CAN CRC error, dropped frames, signal stuck-at-value), and actuator malfunctions to verify the ECU's diagnostic and fail-safe behaviour (compliant with **ISO 26262**).

**Role of Regression Testing (1 mark):**
After every software change, the entire test suite (often **> 10,000 test cases**) is re-executed automatically to ensure no previously verified functionality has regressed. CI/CD pipelines (e.g., Jenkins + GitLab) integrate with HIL to enable nightly builds.

---

## ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **sensor fusion** for an autonomous vehicle. Derive the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) update step** and discuss its role in fusing Camera and Radar tracks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **vehicle dynamics calibration** procedure for a longitudinal control system. Derive the relationship between **brake pressure** and **deceleration**, and explain how the PID controller gains are tuned for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Sensor Fusion Architecture & EKF (7 Marks)

**Architecture (2 marks):**
A **centralized (top-level) fusion** architecture consists of:
- **Front-end (raw)**: Per-signal processing (object detection CNN for camera, CFAR for radar, segmentation for LiDAR).
- **Track-level fusion**: EKF/UKF that maintains a state vector $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, v_x, v_y, a_x, a_y)^T$ for each tracked object.
- **Occupancy grid fusion**: Probabilistic grid (e.g., 0.2 m × 0.2 m cells) for static environment.

**EKF Update Derivation (3 marks):**

The state is predicted using the constant-turn-rate motion model:

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_{k-1})$$

The measurement model is:

$$z_k = h(\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}) + v_k$$

The Kalman gain is:

$$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$

The updated state:

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}))$$

The updated covariance:

$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\mid k-1}$$

**Camera-Radar Fusion (2 marks):**
- Camera provides classification (pedestrian vs. vehicle) via CNN — **low update rate (10 Hz)** but rich semantics.
- Radar provides range, range-rate, and azimuth — **high update rate (20 Hz)** but no classification.
- EKF fuses both: the **measurement vector** $z$ stacks camera-detected (x, y, class) with radar-detected (ρ, ρ̇, θ), giving **better than either sensor alone** (object position accuracy **< 0.1 m**, velocity accuracy **< 0.5 m/s**).

---

### Part (b) — Vehicle Dynamics Calibration for Longitudinal Control (7 Marks)

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Conduct straight-line coast-down tests to identify rolling resistance and drag coefficients.
2. Conduct brake-sweep tests at multiple speeds (30, 60, 90 km/h) to map brake master-cylinder pressure → deceleration.
3. Conduct step-throttle tests to map accelerator pedal position → engine torque → longitudinal acceleration.

**Brake Pressure to Deceleration (2 marks):**

Applying Newton's second law along the longitudinal axis:

$$m a_x = F_{brk} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$$

$$F_{brk} = k_{brk} \cdot P_{brk}$$

$$a_x = \frac{k_{brk} P_{brk} - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 - m g (C_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)}{m}$$

For level road ($\theta = 0$):

$$a_x = \frac{k_{brk}}{m} P_{brk} - \frac{\rho C_d A v^2}{2m} - g C_r$$

This is **affine in $P_{brk}$** and **quadratic in $v$**, hence the calibration surface is a 2-D lookup table indexed by $(P_{brk}, v)$.

**PID Tuning for ACC (3 marks):**

A cascade architecture is typical:
- **Outer loop (spacing control)**: PID on inter-vehicle distance error $e_d = d_{desired} - d_{actual}$, outputs desired acceleration $a_{des}$.
- **Inner loop (acceleration control)**: PI on acceleration error $e_a = a_{des} - a_{actual}$, outputs throttle or brake command.

**Ziegler-Nichols tuning (closed-loop method):**
1. Set $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation of period $T_u$.
2. Record the ultimate gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$.
3. Apply: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.

**Performance Targets:** Settling time $t_s \leq 1.5$ s, overshoot $M_p \leq 5\%$, steady-state error $e_{ss} \leq 0.2$ m.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Compiled from Past KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing intrinsic vs. extrinsic** — Students often write "extrinsic calibration is the focal length of the camera" — this is **intrinsic**. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping units in formulas** — Writing $r = v \tan\delta / L$ without units loses the **dimensional consistency mark**. Always state: $r$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $\delta$ in rad, $L$ in m.
> 3. **Forgetting the homogeneous coordinate** — When writing the projection equation, the 4th row "1" is often omitted. This loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **HIL vs. SIL vs. VIL confusion** — HIL uses real ECUs with simulated plant; SIL uses software-only; VIL uses a real vehicle. Examiners *love* asking this distinction.
> 5. **Writing "PTP is for time sync" without mentioning IEEE 1588 v2** — the standard number is expected.
> 6. **Missing the safety standard (ISO 26262)** in any ADAS answer — always mention it for full marks.
> 7. **Not providing numerical values** in calibration answers (e.g., saying "high accuracy" instead of "± 0.05°") — Vague answers cap at **60–70 %** of the allocated marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **System Integration = Physical + Communication + Software + Functional + Actuation** — five layers, V-model verification.
- [ ] **Intrinsic Calibration** → internal camera optics ($f$, $c$, $k$, $p$); target accuracy **< 0.3 px**.
- [ ] **Extrinsic Calibration** → 6-DoF pose $[R \mid t]$ between sensors; target accuracy **± 0.05°** rotation, **± 1 cm** translation.
- [ ] **Rigid Body Transform:** $P^C = R_{LC} P^L + t_{LC}$; **Homogeneous:** $T \in SE(3)$.
- [ ] **Pinhole Projection:** $s [u, v, 1]^T = K [R \mid t] [X, Y, Z, 1]^T$.
- [ ] **Time Sync Methods:** GPS PPS, PTP (IEEE 1588 v2), hardware timestamping. **ASIL-D skew ≤ 1 ms**.
- [ ] **PTP Offset Equation:** $\theta = ((t_2 - t_1) - (t_4 - t_3))/2$.
- [ ] **Bicycle Model Yaw Rate:** $r = v_x \tan(\delta_f) / L$ — the **single most important formula** of this module.
- [ ] **Sideslip Angle:** $\beta = \arctan(l_r \tan(\delta_f) / L)$ — small angle approximation valid.
- [ ] **Ackermann Geometry:** $\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = w/L$ — ensures no tire scrub during turns.
- [ ] **Tire Linear Model:** $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ — valid for $\vert \alpha \vert \leq 5°$.
- [ ] **PID Controller:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \, de/dt$ — three terms, three roles (P=present, I=past, D=future).
- [ ] **Ziegler-Nichols:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- [ ] **EKF Update:** $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$; **Prediction:** $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1}, u_{k-1})$.
- [ ] **HIL = real ECU + simulated plant; SIL = software only; VIL = real vehicle + simulated environment**.
- [ ] **Bus Technologies:** CAN-FD (**2 Mbps**), Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1 (**100 Mbps**), FlexRay (**10 Mbps**), LIN (**19.2 kbps**).
- [ ] **Standards to mention in every answer:** ISO 26262 (Functional Safety), ISO/SAE 21434 (Cybersecurity), ISO 14229 (UDS), ISO 11898 (CAN).
- [ ] **Calibration workflow sequence:** Mounting → Intrinsic → Extrinsic → Temporal → Dynamics → Controller → HIL → VIL → Sign-off.
- [ ] **Key Insight:** Rotation error scales with distance, translation error does not — choose metric accordingly.
- [ ] **Cascade ACC architecture:** Outer (distance) PID → Inner (acceleration) PI → Throttle/Brake actuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of ADAS Components into Vehicle Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration of ADAS Components into Vehicle Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Integration of ADAS Components into Vehicle Systems** is the holistic engineering discipline of unifying heterogeneous sensors, embedded electronic control units (ECUs), high-performance compute platforms, communication networks, and mechatronic actuators into a single, deterministic, fail-operational cyber-physical system that is functionally compliant with **ISO 26262 (Functional Safety)**, **ISO 21434 (Cybersecurity)**, and **ISO/SAE 21448 (SOTIF – Safety of the Intended Functionality)**.

The integration encompasses:
- **Electrical/Electronic (E/E) Architecture** topology design
- **Sensor data fusion** and time-synchronisation
- **Domain consolidation** into High-Performance Compute (HPC) units
- **Middleware and service-oriented communication**
- **Actuator-by-wire integration** (brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, throttle-by-wire)
- **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** integration
- **Over-the-Air (OTA)** software lifecycle management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** "Integration is the systematic, model-based composition of perception, planning, and actuation subsystems into a vehicle-wide software-defined architecture that guarantees real-time behaviour, ASIL-rated safety, and seamless driver interaction."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Orchestra" View

Think of a modern ADAS-equipped car as a **symphony orchestra performing a complex musical piece at 100 km/h**:

| Orchestra Element | Vehicle ADAS Equivalent |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Conductor (raises baton) | Central compute / domain controller |
| Section leaders (1st Violin) | Sensor ECUs (camera, radar, lidar) |
| Musicians (cellos, horns) | Actuators (brake, steering, throttle) |
| Sheet music & cues | Communication bus protocols (CAN, Ethernet) |
| Tuning and rehearsal | HIL/SIL validation & calibration |
| Concert hall acoustics | Vehicle HMI (display, audio, haptic) |
| Emergency exit plan | Fail-operational redundancy & ASIL-D paths |

Just as the orchestra is only as good as its **conductor's ability to synchronise the instruments in real-time**, the vehicle is only as safe as its ability to synchronise sensors, fuse data, and actuate within a strict **latency budget** — typically **under 100 ms** for an emergency braking intervention.

## 1.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Integration

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Integration Metrics (must memorise for KTU ESE):**
> - **End-to-End Latency:** $\le \mathbf{100\ ms}$ for AEB, $\le \mathbf{10\ ms}$ for stability control
> - **Bandwidth per sensor:** Camera = **3–8 Gbps**, Lidar = **1–3 Gbps**, Radar = **< 100 Mbps**
> - **ASIL Decomposition Limit:** Sum of ASILs of decomposed paths $\le$ original ASIL
> - **Time Synchronisation Jitter:** $\le \mathbf{1\ \mu s}$ across sensors (PTP / gPTP)
> - **Fail-Operational Time:** $\ge \mathbf{500\ ms}$ after first fault (Level 3+)

## 1.4 Why "Integration" is a Dedicated Engineering Discipline

Modern vehicles contain **150+ ECUs** running **100 million+ lines of code**. Without rigorous integration methodology:
- Software regressions cascade across domains
- Real-time deadlines are missed
- Safety paths become unverifiable
- Cybersecurity attack surface explodes

Hence, ADAS integration is governed by the **V-Model (ISO 26262 Part 6)**, where each subsystem is developed and validated at every layer of decomposition, then re-validated at the integrated vehicle level through **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop)** and **vehicle-level proving-ground tests**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Integration Model

The integration architecture is conventionally decomposed into **five abstraction layers**, each with its own protocols, hardware, and verification strategy.

### Layer 1 – Physical / Sensor Layer
- Cameras (mono, stereo, surround-view)
- Radars (24 GHz SRR, 77 GHz MRR/LRR)
- Lidars (mechanical, MEMS, FMCW, OPA)
- Ultrasonic, GNSS+IMU, V2X (C-V2X, DSRC)

### Layer 2 – Communication / Network Layer
- **CAN / CAN-FD** – legacy, 1 Mbps / 5 Mbps
- **LIN** – low-speed body, 19.2 kbps
- **FlexRay** – deterministic, 10 Mbps
- **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1)** – backbone, up to **10 Gbps** (Multi-Gig)
- **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** – IEEE 802.1Qbv, 802.1AS

### Layer 3 – Compute / Domain Layer
- Distributed ECUs (legacy)
- **Domain Controllers** (ADAS, Cockpit, Body, Powertrain, Chassis)
- **Centralised HPC** (zonal architecture)
- Heterogeneous SoCs: **CPU + GPU + NPU (DSP/FPGA)**

### Layer 4 – Middleware / Service Layer
- **AUTOSAR Classic** (CP) – OSEK/VDX-based, real-time
- **AUTOSAR Adaptive (AP)** – POSIX, C++14/17, service-oriented
- **DDS (OMG DDS / ROS 2)** – publish-subscribe, used for autonomy stacks
- **SOME/IP** – service discovery over Ethernet

### Layer 5 – Application / Function Layer
- Sensor fusion algorithms (KF, EKF, UKF, particle filter)
- Perception CNNs (YOLO, BEVFormer, PointPillars)
- Planning (A*, RRT*, MPC, behaviour trees)
- Control (PID, LQR, MPC)
- HMI rendering (Kanzi, Qt, Android Automotive)

## 2.2 Functional Safety – ASIL Decomposition

**ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** ranges from **A (lowest)** to **D (highest)**. When a safety function is split across multiple channels, the ASIL can be **decomposed** according to ISO 26262-9.

The decomposition rules:
- **D** can be decomposed into **D + A**
- **D** can be decomposed into **C + C**
- **C** can be decomposed into **C + A** or **B + B**
- **B** can be decomposed into **B + A**
- **A** cannot be decomposed (must remain A)

The **Probabilistic Metric for random hardware failures (PMHF)** must satisfy:
$$
PMHF_{total} \le PMHF_{target}
$$
For an ASIL-D system:
$$
PMHF_{target} \lt 10^{-8}\ /\ hour
$$

## 2.3 Real-Time Scheduling & Latency Budget

For a periodic ADAS task set scheduled by **Rate Monotonic (RM)** or **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**, Liu & Layland's bound applies:
$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$
where $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time (WCET) of task $i$, and $T_i$ is its period.

For $n \rightarrow \infty$, the bound asymptotically approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

## 2.4 Sensor Fusion – Kalman Filter (Linear Discrete)

The discrete-time **Kalman filter** is the workhorse of ADAS fusion:
$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A\,\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B\,u_k \quad \text{(Predict State)}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = A\,P_{k-1\mid k-1}\,A^T + Q \quad \text{(Predict Covariance)}
$$

$$
K_k = P_{k\mid k-1}\,H^T \left(H\,P_{k\mid k-1}\,H^T + R\right)^{-1} \quad \text{(Kalman Gain)}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k\left(z_k - H\,\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}\right) \quad \text{(Update State)}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k\,H)\,P_{k\mid k-1} \quad \text{(Update Covariance)}
$$

where $Q$ and $R$ are the process and measurement noise covariances.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipes `|` are replaced with `\vert` to prevent Markdown table breakage.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Liu-Layland RM Bound | $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $\le 0.693$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | End-to-End AEB Latency | $T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{fuse} + T_{plan} + T_{act}$ | $\le 100$ | ms |
| 3 | ASIL-D PMHF Target | $PMHF \lt 10^{-8}$ | target | $1/\text{h}$ |
| 4 | ASIL Decomposition D | $D = C + C$ or $D + A$ | — | — |
| 5 | Vehicle Dynamics Cycle | $T_{cycle} = 10$ | default CAN sampling | ms |
| 6 | Lidar Point Rate | $R_{lidar} = N_{lines} \times f_{rot}$ | $\approx 2$–$10$ | Mpts/s |
| 7 | Camera Data Rate | $R_{cam} = W \times H \times fps \times bpp$ | $\approx 3$–$8$ | Gbps |
| 8 | CAN-FD Payload | max $\vert 64 \vert$ | 64 | bytes/frame |
| 9 | Ethernet MAC Delay | $\le 10$ | per switch | $\mu s$ |
| 10 | Kalman Innovation | $\nu_k = z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}$ | 3-sigma gate | — |
| 11 | Brake Pressure Build-up | $\Delta P / \Delta t$ | $\ge 50$ | bar/s |
| 12 | ISO 26262 Coverage | $\ge 99$ % | ASIL-D SPFM | % |
| 13 | Latent Fault Metric | $LFM \ge 90$ % | ASIL-D | % |
| 14 | TSN Time-Sync (gPTP) | $\le 1$ | jitter across ECU | $\mu s$ |
| 15 | OTA Delta Update | $\le 100$ | MB per ECU | MB |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Integration Theory |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| OEM Vehicle Programs | Defines E/E architecture roadmap (e.g., VW E³ 2.0, Tesla HW4) |
| Tier-1 Suppliers | Guides product portfolio (Bosch, Continental, ZF, Aptiv) |
| ADAS Validation | HIL bench sizing, sensor calibration, regression testing |
| Autonomous Trucking | Platooning V2X integration with chassis-by-wire |
| Aftermarket ADAS | Retrofit OBD-II gateways, telematics-based ADAS |
| Functional Safety Audit | TÜV/SGS sign-off of ASIL claims |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — End-to-End Latency Budget for an AEB System

**Problem Setup:** A typical Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) pipeline. Compute the **end-to-end latency** and verify it is within the ISO 22179 / Euro NCAP mandated **100 ms** budget.

**Step 1 — Identify the pipeline stages and their WCETs:**

| Stage | Description | WCET $T_i$ |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| Sensor sampling (radar + camera) | Hardware-triggered read | $T_{sense} = 10\ ms$ |
| Sensor preprocessing (ISP, FFT) | ISP + radar CFAR | $T_{pre} = 15\ ms$ |
| Object detection (CNN inference) | YOLO-style on NPU | $T_{detect} = 25\ ms$ |
| Tracking & fusion (EKF) | Multi-object tracker | $T_{fuse} = 10\ ms$ |
| Path planning (TTC estimator) | Time-To-Collision | $T_{plan} = 8\ ms$ |
| Actuation (brake-by-wire command) | Hydraulic build-up | $T_{act} = 20\ ms$ |

**Step 2 — Sum the WCETs along the critical path:**

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{sense} + T_{pre} + T_{detect} + T_{fuse} + T_{plan} + T_{act}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
T_{e2e} = 10 + 15 + 25 + 10 + 8 + 20
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the addition explicitly:**

$$
T_{e2e} = 25 + 25 + 10 + 8 + 20
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 50 + 10 + 8 + 20
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 60 + 8 + 20
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 68 + 20 = 88\ ms
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the regulatory limit:**

$$
T_{e2e} = 88\ ms \le 100\ ms \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Safety margin calculation:**

$$
M_{safety} = \frac{100 - 88}{100} \times 100\ \% = 12\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 12 % margin is acceptable for nominal operation, but under thermal throttling or CPU contention, the inference stage ($T_{detect}$) can spike to 35 ms. Hence, designers allocate a **20 %** safety buffer when sizing the compute SoC.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — ASIL Decomposition Math for a Steer-by-Wire System

**Problem:** A Level 3 lane-keeping function is rated **ASIL-D**. The system has two redundant ECUs (primary + secondary) and a fail-operational actuator. Decompose the ASIL and verify the PMHF constraint.

**Step 1 — Apply the ISO 26262-9 decomposition rule:**

ASIL-D can be decomposed into:
$$
D \rightarrow C + C
$$
or
$$
D \rightarrow D + A
$$

We choose the second option to keep one channel highly capable (primary) and the other simple (watchdog).

**Step 2 — Decompose into $D + A$:**

$$
ASIL_{primary} = D, \quad ASIL_{secondary} = A
$$

**Step 3 — PMHF constraint check:**

Each channel's failure rate $\lambda$ must satisfy:

$$
\lambda_{primary} \lt 10^{-8}\ /\ h, \quad \lambda_{secondary} \lt 10^{-6}\ /\ h
$$

**Step 4 — Use the redundant system PMHF model:**

For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted system with identical channels, the combined failure rate (assuming independence) is:

$$
PMHF_{combined} = \lambda_{primary} \cdot \lambda_{secondary} \cdot T_{diagnostic}
$$

Substituting:

$$
PMHF_{combined} = 10^{-8} \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 365
$$

$$
PMHF_{combined} = 10^{-14} \times 8760
$$

$$
PMHF_{combined} = 8.76 \times 10^{-11}\ /\ h
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the ASIL-D PMHF target:**

$$
8.76 \times 10^{-11}\ /\ h \ll 10^{-8}\ /\ h \quad \checkmark
$$

The decomposition satisfies the safety target with a margin of three orders of magnitude.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Liu–Layland Utilisation Bound for ADAS Task Set

**Problem:** Verify schedulability of an ADAS task set on a single-core ECU (e.g., an NXP S32K3 running AUTOSAR CP).

| Task | $T_i$ (Period, ms) | $C_i$ (WCET, ms) | Type |
|------|--------------------|------------------|------|
| $\tau_1$ Radar sampling | 10 | 1 | Periodic |
| $\tau_2$ Camera ISP | 33 | 5 | Periodic |
| $\tau_3$ Fusion EKF | 20 | 2 | Periodic |
| $\tau_4$ HMI render | 50 | 4 | Periodic |
| $\tau_5$ Diagnostic | 100 | 3 | Periodic |

**Step 1 — Compute the per-task utilisation:**

$$
u_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

$$
u_1 = \frac{1}{10} = 0.10, \quad u_2 = \frac{5}{33} \approx 0.1515
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{2}{20} = 0.10, \quad u_4 = \frac{4}{50} = 0.08
$$

$$
u_5 = \frac{3}{100} = 0.03
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the utilisations:**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{5} u_i = 0.10 + 0.1515 + 0.10 + 0.08 + 0.03 = 0.4615
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Liu–Layland bound for $n = 5$:**

$$
U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1) = 5(2^{0.2} - 1) = 5(1.1487 - 1) = 5(0.1487) = 0.7434
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$
U = 0.4615 \le 0.7434 \quad \checkmark
$$

The task set is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).

**Step 5 — Compute headroom (CPU idle time):**

$$
Idle = 1 - U = 1 - 0.4615 = 0.5385 \quad \text{or}\quad 53.85\ \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** 53 % headroom is sufficient for non-critical background tasks (logging, diagnostics, OTA), but if a new perception task is added, the bound must be re-verified.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Sensor Data Rate Calculation (Camera + Lidar)

**Camera (8 MP, 30 fps, YUV 4:2:0):**

Bits per pixel = 12 bpp.
$$
R_{cam} = 3840 \times 2160 \times 30 \times 12
$$
$$
R_{cam} = 8,294,400 \times 30 \times 12 = 248,832,000 \times 12 = 2,985,984,000\ \text{bps}
$$
$$
R_{cam} \approx 2.99\ \text{Gbps}
$$

**Lidar (128 lines, 20 Hz rotation, 1.5M points/sec):**

Assuming each point = 48 bytes (x, y, z, intensity, time, ring):
$$
R_{lidar} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 48 \times 8 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 384 = 576 \times 10^6\ \text{bps}
$$
$$
R_{lidar} = 576\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Total aggregator bandwidth at the domain controller:**

$$
R_{total} = R_{cam} + R_{lidar} + R_{radar} + \ldots
$$

$$
R_{total} \approx 3 + 0.576 + 0.1 + 0.05 \approx 3.7\ \text{Gbps}
$$

This mandates **2.5G / 5G / 10G Automotive Ethernet** (1000BASE-T1 or Multi-Gig) — **CAN-FD is insufficient**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Integration Trade-offs

| Design Decision | Alternative A | Alternative B | Engineering Trade-off |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Architecture | Distributed ECUs | Centralised HPC | A: more resilient, B: lower harness cost |
| Bus | CAN-FD | Automotive Ethernet | A: cheap, slow; B: fast, EMI-sensitive |
| Operating System | AUTOSAR CP | Linux + ROS 2 | A: hard real-time, B: rich ecosystem |
| Fusion | EKF (centralised) | Track-to-track (distributed) | A: optimal, B: scalable |
| Steering | EPS (assisted) | Steer-by-Wire | A: fail-passive, B: fail-operational |
| Compute SoC | Single SoC | Dual SoC lockstep | A: cost, B: ASIL-D capable |
| Redundancy | Cold standby | Hot standby | A: power saving, B: zero switchover latency |
| HMI | Cluster only | Cluster + AR-HUD | A: low distraction, B: better situational awareness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define the term "Integration of ADAS Components" and list four key challenges faced during vehicle-level integration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Integration of ADAS components is the systematic process of combining heterogeneous sensors, ECUs, software stacks, communication buses, and actuators into a single, deterministic, functionally safe vehicle system that satisfies ISO 26262 and SOTIF requirements. *(1 mark)*

The four key challenges are:
1. **Heterogeneous timing requirements** — mixing hard real-time chassis control (10 ms) with soft real-time perception (100 ms). *(1 mark)*
2. **ASIL decomposition and safety verification** — proving that decomposed paths still meet the original ASIL target. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Bandwidth limitation of legacy buses** — CAN-FD cannot carry 8 MP camera streams, mandating Ethernet migration. *(0.5 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **What is the role of AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (AP) in ADAS integration? Differentiate it briefly from AUTOSAR Classic (CP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of AUTOSAR AP:** AUTOSAR AP is the service-oriented middleware used in High-Performance Compute (HPC) domain controllers. It enables POSIX-based, dynamic deployment of C++ applications, supports **SOME/IP** and **DDS** for service-oriented communication, and is the de-facto standard for ADAS perception and planning stacks. *(1.5 marks)*

**Differentiation Table:**

| Parameter | AUTOSAR Classic (CP) | AUTOSAR Adaptive (AP) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| OS Basis | OSEK/VDX, static | POSIX, dynamic |
| Language | C | C++14/17 |
| Use Case | Body, chassis, powertrain ECUs | ADAS, autonomous, HPC |
| Update | Offline flashing | OTA, runtime |
| Communication | CAN, FlexRay, LIN | Ethernet, SOME/IP, DDS |

*(1.5 marks for the table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU ESE July 2024 Pattern]

> **(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **layered architecture** of an ADAS-integrated vehicle system. Discuss the function of **Automotive Ethernet and TSN** in meeting real-time and bandwidth requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw and explain the layered architecture (4 marks):**

The integrated ADAS vehicle architecture is organised into five layers:

1. **Sensor Layer:** Cameras, Radars, Lidars, Ultrasonic, GNSS/IMU.
2. **Network Layer:** CAN-FD, FlexRay, LIN, Automotive Ethernet, TSN.
3. **Compute Layer:** ADAS domain controller (SoC with CPU+GPU+NPU), chassis domain, cockpit domain.
4. **Middleware Layer:** AUTOSAR CP/AP, DDS, SOME/IP, ROS 2.
5. **Application Layer:** Perception, Sensor Fusion, Planning, Control, HMI rendering.

**Step 2 — Role of Automotive Ethernet (1.5 marks):**

Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1) provides **up to 10 Gbps** using a single twisted pair. It enables the transport of high-bandwidth camera and lidar streams that are impossible over legacy CAN-FD (max 5 Mbps). The 1000BASE-T1 standard supports **MACsec** for hardware-level cybersecurity.

**Step 3 — Role of TSN (1.5 marks):**

Time-Sensitive Networking (IEEE 802.1Q series) provides:
- **Time synchronisation (802.1AS / gPTP)** with $\le 1\ \mu s$ jitter.
- **Scheduled traffic (802.1Qbv / TAS)** guaranteeing latency for critical streams.
- **Frame preemption (802.1Qbu)** allowing best-effort and critical frames to coexist.
- **Stream reservation (802.1Qat / SRP)** for QoS guarantees.

Hence, TSN transforms standard Ethernet into a **deterministic, real-time capable** backbone — a prerequisite for fail-operational ADAS.

---

> **(b)** Compute the **end-to-end latency** of an AEB pipeline with the following stage WCETs: $T_{sense}=12\ ms$, $T_{pre}=18\ ms$, $T_{detect}=28\ ms$, $T_{fuse}=11\ ms$, $T_{plan}=9\ ms$, $T_{act}=22\ ms$. Comment on whether the system meets the **100 ms ISO 22179** requirement, and compute the safety margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum the WCETs:**

$$
T_{e2e} = 12 + 18 + 28 + 11 + 9 + 22
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 30 + 28 + 11 + 9 + 22 = 58 + 11 + 9 + 22
$$

$$
T_{e2e} = 69 + 9 + 22 = 78 + 22 = 100\ ms
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with ISO 22179 limit:**

$$
T_{e2e} = 100\ ms \le 100\ ms \quad \text{(Marginal compliance)}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety margin:**

$$
M = \frac{100 - 100}{100} \times 100\ \% = 0\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment (2 marks):**

The system is **marginally compliant** with zero safety margin. In real operation, thermal throttling, OS jitter, and CNN inference variability can push the latency beyond 100 ms. **Recommendation:** optimise the perception stage (use INT8 quantisation, model pruning) to reduce $T_{detect}$ by at least 10 ms, achieving a 10 % safety margin.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **ASIL Decomposition** as per ISO 26262-9. An AEB system is rated **ASIL-D**. Show mathematically how decomposing it into **C + C** channels satisfies the **PMHF** target of $10^{-8}\ /\ h$. Assume the diagnostic test interval is 24 h and each channel has an independent failure rate of $10^{-7}\ /\ h$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of ASIL Decomposition (2 marks):**

ASIL Decomposition (ISO 26262-9) allows a safety requirement rated at an ASIL to be split into redundant requirements assigned to different elements at lower ASILs, **provided the original safety goal is still met**. The rules are:
- $D \rightarrow C + C$ or $D + A$
- $C \rightarrow C + A$ or $B + B$
- $B \rightarrow B + A$
- $A$ is non-decomposable.

**Step 2 — Apply C + C decomposition (1 mark):**

We split the ASIL-D AEB requirement into two independent channels, each rated **ASIL-C**.

**Step 3 — PMHF calculation (3 marks):**

For independent channels with failure rate $\lambda$ and diagnostic interval $T_{diag}$:
$$
PMHF_{combined} = \lambda^2 \cdot T_{diag}
$$

Substitute:
$$
PMHF_{combined} = (10^{-7})^2 \times 24
$$

$$
PMHF_{combined} = 10^{-14} \times 24 = 2.4 \times 10^{-13}\ /\ h
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ASIL-D target (1 mark):**

$$
2.4 \times 10^{-13}\ /\ h \ll 10^{-8}\ /\ h \quad \checkmark
$$

The decomposition satisfies the safety target with a margin of five orders of magnitude.

---

> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **V-Model** as applied to ADAS integration. List any **four** test levels used in the corresponding right-hand branch of the V-Model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — V-Model explanation (3 marks):**

The V-Model (per ISO 26262 Part 6) maps each development phase (left arm) to a corresponding test phase (right arm), ensuring traceability. The left arm is decomposition: vehicle requirements → system → subsystem → component. The right arm is integration and validation in the **reverse order**: component test → subsystem integration → system integration (HIL) → vehicle validation.

**Step 2 — Diagram (2 marks):**

A textual representation:

```
     Vehicle Requirements
            |
     System Design              Vehicle Validation
            |                          ^
     Subsystem Design        System Integration Test
            |                          ^
     Component Design    Subsystem Integration Test
            |                          ^
     Implementation         Component Unit Test
```

**Step 3 — Four test levels (2 marks):**

1. **Component / Unit Test** — software module testing (MISRA-C, unit tests).
2. **Subsystem Integration Test** — multiple modules on an Evaluation Board.
3. **System Integration Test (HIL)** — real ECUs + simulated plant.
4. **Vehicle-Level Validation** — proving-ground tests, Euro NCAP, durability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diagnostic interval ($T_{diag}$)** in PMHF calculations — examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Confusing ASIL-D with "D + D"** — D cannot be decomposed into D + D; it is either C + C or D + A.
> 3. **Using `|` (pipe) inside Markdown tables for absolute value** — examiners expect the *symbolic* form $\vert x \vert$. In handwritten exams, write the bracket clearly.
> 4. **Omitting the "Engineering Comment"** in latency problems — KTU awards 2 marks for design recommendation.
> 5. **Drawing the V-Model left-to-right** instead of as a proper "V" shape — partial marks lost.
> 6. **Wrong direction of Liu-Layland** — the bound is an *upper* limit; the system is schedulable only if $U \le U_{bound}$.
> 7. **Forgetting units** (ms vs $\mu$s) — at least 0.5 mark deducted per missing unit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Integration of ADAS Components):**
> - **Integration** = unification of sensors, compute, network, actuators into a safe, deterministic, real-time system.
> - **Five Layers:** Sensor → Network → Compute → Middleware → Application.
> - **Buses:** CAN-FD (5 Mbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps to 10 Gbps), TSN for determinism.
> - **ASIL-D PMHF target:** $\lt 10^{-8}\ /\ h$.
> - **ASIL Decomposition rules:** $D \rightarrow C+C$ or $D+A$.
> - **Liu-Layland bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
> - **AEB Latency budget:** $\le 100$ ms (ISO 22179, Euro NCAP).
> - **Time Sync (gPTP) jitter:** $\le 1\ \mu s$.
> - **Kalman Filter** is the canonical sensor-fusion algorithm (predict–update cycle).
> - **V-Model** is the standard ISO 26262 development framework.
> - **AUTOSAR CP** → body/chassis, **AUTOSAR AP** → ADAS/autonomous.
> - **Middleware choices:** SOME/IP, DDS, ROS 2 RMW.
> - **Fail-operational** is mandatory for Level 3+ (vs fail-passive for Level 1–2).
> - **HIL/SIL** validation is required before any vehicle-level proving-ground test.
> - **Cybersecurity** is governed by **ISO 21434**, SOTIF by **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)**.
> - **OTA updates** are a key enabler of post-production ADAS improvement.
> - **Centralised HPC + zonal architecture** is the current industry trend (replacing distributed ECUs).
> - **Sensor data rate:** 8 MP camera ≈ **3 Gbps**, 128-line lidar ≈ **576 Mbps**, 4D radar ≈ **100 Mbps**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration Techniques for Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calibration Techniques for Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the systematic process of comparing an instrument's (sensor or actuator) output against a **traceable reference standard** of known accuracy, and adjusting (or characterising) the device so that its output lies within specified tolerances under defined operating conditions.

In the context of **Autonomous Driving and ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)**, calibration is the foundational step that ensures every sensor perceives the world in the *same geometric and temporal reference frame* as the vehicle's control algorithms, and that every actuator responds to commands with **known, deterministic, and repeatable behaviour**. Without calibration, an ADAS stack is mathematically inconsistent: a LiDAR point, a camera pixel, and a radar return that all refer to the *same physical object* would otherwise be projected into *different places*, causing phantom obstacles, missed detections, or unsafe control actions.

### 1.1 Why Calibration is Non-Negotiable in ADAS
A modern SAE Level 3+ vehicle carries heterogeneous sensors — each subject to:
1. **Manufacturing tolerances** (every camera leaves the factory with slightly different focal length).
2. **Mounting variances** (a LiDAR bolted ±0.5° off-axis rotates the entire world-map).
3. **Thermal drift** (IMU bias can vary by tens of µg per °C).
4. **Mechanical vibration and aging** (radar antenna phase shifts over the vehicle lifetime).
5. **Time-misalignment** (camera at 30 Hz, LiDAR at 10 Hz, radar at 20 Hz — all must be co-registered).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Orchestra Tuning Analogy** 🎻
> Think of an ADAS-equipped vehicle as an **orchestra**. The camera is the *violin*, the LiDAR is the *flute*, the radar is the *trumpet*, and the IMU is the *timpani*. The autonomous-driving CPU is the **conductor**. Before the concert (driving begins), every musician must be **tuned to the same A=440 Hz reference** — that tuning process *is calibration*. Even a perfect instrument, played off-pitch, ruins the symphony. Likewise, even a sensor with megapixel resolution, if uncalibrated, destroys the vehicle's perception of reality.

### 1.3 Key Reference Standards and Metrics
- **Reprojection Error (RPE):** Acceptable range typically **< 0.3 pixels** for camera intrinsic calibration.
- **Static Heading Accuracy (IMU):** Automotive-grade < **0.5°**.
- **LiDAR Range Accuracy:** ±**3 cm** at 100 m for premium units.
- **Actuator Linearity Error:** < **±2%** of full-scale command for throttle/brake.
- **Time Synchronization Skew:** < **1 ms** across the sensor cluster (ideally < 100 µs with PTP/IEEE 1588).

### 1.4 Classification of Calibration
| Class | Description | When Performed |
|---|---|---|
| **Factory Calibration** | Performed by OEM/ supplier with precision jigs | Once during manufacturing |
| **Workshop / Dealer Calibration** | After windshield replacement, suspension work, accident repair | After mechanical disturbance |
| **Online / Continuous Calibration** | Self-calibrates during driving using SLAM, lane markers, stationary landmarks | Throughout vehicle life |
| **Dynamic / Operational Calibration** | Performed while vehicle is moving on a calibrated route | Periodically / on demand |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Module 4 explicitly highlights the role of sensor and actuator calibration in maintaining the functional safety of perception and control loops in autonomous vehicles.*

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Calibration, fundamentally, is a **parameter estimation problem**. Given a mathematical model $f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ that describes how a sensor maps physical reality to digital measurements, calibration finds the parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimises an error metric $\mathcal{L}$ between predicted and observed measurements.

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of ADAS Calibration
1. **Intrinsic Calibration** — Characterises the sensor *itself* (e.g., focal length, IMU bias).
2. **Extrinsic Calibration** — Characterises the sensor's *pose* relative to the vehicle body frame.
3. **Temporal Calibration** — Characterises the sensor's *clock offset and jitter* relative to the vehicle master clock.

### 2.2 Camera Intrinsic Calibration — The Pinhole Model

A 3D world point $\mathbf{P}_w = [X, Y, Z]^T$ projects to image pixel $\mathbf{p} = [u, v]^T$ via:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $s$ is the projective scale factor.
- $\mathbf{K}$ is the **3×3 intrinsic matrix**.
- $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **3×3 rotation + 3×1 translation** describing the camera-to-world transform.
- $\mathbf{K}$ takes the form:

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

with $f_x, f_y$ being **focal lengths in pixels**, $(c_x, c_y)$ the **principal point**, and $\gamma$ the **skew coefficient** (≈ 0 for modern CMOS).

### 2.3 Lens Distortion Models

Real lenses introduce **radial** and **tangential** distortion. The corrected coordinates $(\tilde{u}, \tilde{v})$ before applying $\mathbf{K}$ are:

$$
\tilde{u} = u_d \left(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6\right) + 2 p_1 u_d v_d + p_2 \left(r^2 + 2 u_d^2\right)
$$

$$
\tilde{v} = v_d \left(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6\right) + p_1 \left(r^2 + 2 v_d^2\right) + 2 p_2 u_d v_d
$$

where $r^2 = u_d^2 + v_d^2$ and the distortion coefficients are $k_1, k_2, k_3$ (radial) and $p_1, p_2$ (tangential).

### 2.4 LiDAR-to-Camera Extrinsic Calibration

The 6-DOF rigid transform between a LiDAR frame $\{L\}$ and a camera frame $\{C\}$ is $\mathbf{T}_{L}^{C} \in SE(3)$:

$$
\mathbf{T}_{L}^{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{L}^{C} & \mathbf{t}_{L}^{C} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{R}_{L}^{C} \in SO(3), \quad \mathbf{t}_{L}^{C} \in \mathbb{R}^3
$$

A point $\mathbf{P}_L$ in the LiDAR frame projects to image pixel via:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{L}^{C} \mid \mathbf{t}_{L}^{C} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{P}_L
$$

### 2.5 IMU Calibration — Bias and Scale Factor

The accelerometer measurement model is:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{a}} = \mathbf{S}_a \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{n}_a
$$

where $\mathbf{S}_a$ is the **scale-factor + cross-coupling matrix** (3×3), $\mathbf{b}_a$ is the **bias vector** (m/s²), and $\mathbf{n}_a$ is **white noise**.

The gyroscope model mirrors this:

$$
\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \mathbf{S}_g \boldsymbol{\omega} + \mathbf{b}_g + \mathbf{n}_g
$$

### 2.6 Actuator Calibration — Drive-by-Wire

For a steer-by-wire system, the commanded steering wheel angle $\delta_{cmd}$ is mapped to the road-wheel angle $\delta_{road}$ via:

$$
\delta_{road} = K_{steer} \cdot \delta_{cmd} + \delta_{offset}
$$

Calibration determines the **steering ratio** $K_{steer}$ and **mechanical offset** $\delta_{offset}$ to ensure the vehicle tracks the desired path with sub-decimetre lateral error.

For an electronic throttle, the pedal-position sensor voltage $V_{pedal}$ is mapped to throttle-plate angle $\theta_{thr}$:

$$
\theta_{thr} = \alpha V_{pedal} + \beta
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are calibrated at the factory against a dynamometer.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Domain | Formula | Parameters | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinhole projection | $s[u, v, 1]^T = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{P}_w$ | $\mathbf{K}$ intrinsics, $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ extrinsics | pixels |
| Radial distortion | $1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6$ | $k_1, k_2, k_3$ | unitless |
| Tangential distortion | $2 p_1 u_d v_d + p_2(r^2+2 u_d^2)$ | $p_1, p_2$ | unitless |
| Reprojection error | $\text{RPE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert \mathbf{p}_i^{obs} - \mathbf{p}_i^{proj}\Vert_2$ | observed vs projected | pixels (target < 0.3) |
| LiDAR–Camera transform | $\mathbf{T}_L^C \in SE(3)$ | 3 rot + 3 trans DOF | rad, m |
| IMU accelerometer | $\tilde{\mathbf{a}} = \mathbf{S}_a \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{n}_a$ | scale, bias, noise | m/s² |
| IMU gyroscope | $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}} = \mathbf{S}_g \boldsymbol{\omega} + \mathbf{b}_g + \mathbf{n}_g$ | scale, bias, noise | rad/s |
| Allan variance | $\sigma^2(\tau) = \tfrac{1}{2}\langle (\bar{\Omega}_{k+1} - \bar{\Omega}_k)^2\rangle$ | cluster time $\tau$ | (°/h)² |
| Steering ratio | $\delta_{road} = K_{steer} \cdot \delta_{cmd} + \delta_{offset}$ | $K_{steer} \approx 14\!-\!18$ | deg/deg |
| Throttle linearisation | $\theta_{thr} = \alpha V_{pedal} + \beta$ | slope, offset | deg, V |
| Time skew | $\Delta t = t_{sensor} - t_{master}$ | sensor timestamp offset | ms / µs |
| ROS message header | $Header.stamp$ | Unix epoch seconds+nsec | s, ns |

### 2.8 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

> [!NOTE]
> In production ADAS stacks (Mobileye, NVIDIA DriveWorks, Apollo, Autoware), calibration is a **gated, version-controlled artifact**. A calibration file ($\texttt{camera\_intrinsics.yaml}$, $\texttt{lidar\_to\_camera.yaml}$, $\texttt{imu\_params.yaml}$) is generated, signed, and stored per-VIN. The vehicle will **refuse to enable Level 3 autonomy** if the calibration checksum is stale, suspect, or has not been refreshed after a major mechanical event (e.g., windscreen replacement moves the forward camera by ~2 cm).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the foundational mathematics that *every* KTU examiner expects a Module-4 answer to demonstrate: (i) the geometric derivation of the pinhole projection, (ii) the closed-form derivation of the **reprojection error** used as the calibration cost function, and (iii) the derivation of the **Allan-variance noise model** used to characterise IMU calibration.

### 3.1 Derivation: Pinhole Camera Projection from Similar Triangles

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider an ideal pinhole aperture at the origin of a camera frame $\{C\}$ with principal axis along $+Z_c$. A 3D point $\mathbf{P}_w = [X, Y, Z]^T$ (in the camera frame) lies at distance $Z$ in front of the pinhole.

**Step 2 — Similar triangles.** Light from $\mathbf{P}_w$ passes through the pinhole and intersects the image plane located at $z = f$ (focal length) on the other side. By similar triangles, the intersection point is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X' &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\
Y' &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Image-plane inversion.** Geometrically, the image is inverted through the pinhole. To remove this inversion, we place the *virtual* image plane at $z = f$ in front of the camera (this is the convention in computer vision). The signs drop and we get the same expression.

**Step 4 — Convert to pixel coordinates.** Pixel coordinates differ from image-plane metric units by a scale $k$ (pixels per metre) and an offset $(c_x, c_y)$ (the principal point). Let $f_x = k f$ and $f_y = k f / \text{aspect}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\
v &= f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Homogeneous form.** Stacking into matrix form yields the celebrated pinhole equation:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}, \quad s = Z
$$

**Step 6 — World-to-camera extrinsic prepend.** If $\mathbf{P}_w$ lives in a *world* frame, we apply the rigid transform first:

$$
\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s\begin{bmatrix} u\\v\\1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t})
$$

This derivation is the foundation for the **Direct Linear Transform (DLT)** used in Zhang's checkerboard calibration method.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reprojection Error Cost Function

**Step 1 — Observations.** Capturing $N$ known 3D world points $\mathbf{P}_w^{(i)}$ (e.g., corners of a checkerboard with known grid spacing), the corresponding observed pixel coordinates are $\mathbf{p}_{obs}^{(i)}$.

**Step 2 — Predicted projections.** Given a candidate parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}, k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2\}$, the predicted pixel is:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{proj}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \pi(\mathbf{P}_w^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

where $\pi(\cdot)$ denotes the full projection pipeline (extrinsic → intrinsic → distortion correction).

**Step 3 — Per-point residual.** The reprojection residual for point $i$ is:

$$
\mathbf{r}_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{p}_{obs}^{(i)} - \mathbf{p}_{proj}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \in \mathbb{R}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Non-linear least squares.** The optimal $\boldsymbol{\theta}^\star$ minimises the sum of squared residuals (L2 norm):

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^\star = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{r}_i(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \Vert_2^2
$$

**Step 5 — Closed-form linear initialisation (DLT).** For the *intrinsic* part with zero distortion, an algebraic linear solution exists. Homogenising the pinhole equation as $\mathbf{p} \times (\mathbf{K} \mathbf{P}) = \mathbf{0}$ gives 2 linear equations per point in the 9 unknown entries of $\mathbf{K}$ (up to scale). With $\geq 6$ points, the system $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{k} = \mathbf{0}$ is solved via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of $\mathbf{A}$: take the right-singular vector corresponding to the smallest singular value.

**Step 6 — Non-linear refinement.** Once initialised, **Levenberg–Marquardt** (or Gauss–Newton) iteratively updates $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using the Jacobian $\mathbf{J} = \partial \mathbf{r}/\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k - (\mathbf{J}^\top \mathbf{J} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{J}^\top \mathbf{r}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the damping factor. Convergence is declared when $\Vert \boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_k \Vert < 10^{-6}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Allan Variance for IMU Calibration

**Step 1 — Data collection.** A stationary IMU records angular rate $\bar{\Omega}_k$ averaged over clusters of duration $\tau$.

**Step 2 — Allan variance definition.** For cluster time $\tau$:

$$
\sigma_A^2(\tau) = \frac{1}{2 (M-1)} \sum_{k=1}^{M-1} \left( \bar{\Omega}_{k+1} - \bar{\Omega}_k \right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Read-off of noise parameters.** The **log-log plot** of $\sigma_A(\tau)$ vs $\tau$ reveals characteristic slopes:
- Slope **−1/2** → **Angle Random Walk (ARW)**.
- Slope **0** → **Bias Instability** (read at the minimum).
- Slope **+1/2** → **Rate Random Walk**.

These directly characterise the IMU's noise floor and feed into the Kalman-filter process-noise covariance $\mathbf{Q}$ used in sensor fusion.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Calibration Method to Use?

| Sensor / Actuator | Recommended Method | Reference Target | Expected Accuracy | Workshop Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Camera (Mono) | Zhang checkerboard, 10+ poses | Printed checkerboard | RPE < 0.2 px | 15 min |
| Forward Camera (Stereo) | Stereo calibration + rectification | Checkerboard + range stick | Baseline error < 0.5 mm | 25 min |
| Surround-View (4× fish-eye) | Multi-camera rig calibration | Custom calibration plate | RPE < 0.4 px | 45 min |
| LiDAR–Camera | Targetless (edge alignment) **or** checkerboard with retroreflective strips | AprilTag + LiDAR return panel | Rotation < 0.05°, translation < 2 cm | 30 min |
| Radar–Camera | Range-azimuth boresight + 1-D triangulation | Corner reflector at 5 m, 10 m, 20 m | Range < 0.1 m, azimuth < 0.2° | 20 min |
| IMU (6-axis) | 6-position tumble + Allan variance | Gravity vector (stationary) | Bias < 50 µg, ARW < 0.05 °/√h | 60 min |
| GNSS | Survey-in / RTK with fixed base | Known geodetic point | Position < 2 cm (RTK) | 5 min |
| Steer-by-Wire | Steering-angle sensor teach-in + on-centre routine | Wheel-alignment machine | Lateral error < 5 cm @ 30 m | 10 min |
| Electronic Throttle | Pedal–plausibility + idle/full learn | Dyno, fixed load cell | Linearity < ±2% | 8 min |
| Brake-by-Wire | Pressure-sensor trim + stroke learn | Hydraulic pressure transducer | Pressure < ±0.5 bar | 12 min |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Pinhole Projection)

**Problem.** A camera has $f = 50$ mm, pixel size $k = 100$ pixels/mm, principal point at the image centre, and a 3D point at world coordinates $\mathbf{P}_w = [0.2, 0.1, 5.0]^T$ m. Assuming zero extrinsic rotation/translation, compute the pixel coordinates.

**Solution.**

Step 1 — Focal length in pixels:
$f_x = f_y = 50 \times 100 = 5000$ pixels.

Step 2 — Apply projection:

$$
u = f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} = 5000 \cdot \frac{0.2}{5.0} = 5000 \cdot 0.04 = 200
$$

$$
v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} = 5000 \cdot \frac{0.1}{5.0} = 5000 \cdot 0.02 = 100
$$

Step 3 — Add principal point $(c_x, c_y) = (640, 360)$ (assuming 1280×720 sensor):

$$
u = 200 + 640 = 840 \text{ px}, \qquad v = 100 + 360 = 460 \text{ px}
$$

**Result:** The point projects to pixel **(840, 460)** on the image sensor.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact calculation done *every frame* by an ADAS perception pipeline — except with distortion correction, motion compensation, and rolling-shutter modelling layered on top.
