> **ISO 26262** governs *systematic and random hardware failures*.
> **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** governs *hazards arising from intended functionality or performance limitations* — e.g., perception misclassification, sensor noise, ML edge cases. SOTIF is **mandatory** for ADAS/AV testing under the 2024 KTU syllabus.

### ASIL Classification (ISO 26262)
- **A** → lowest integrity, **D** → highest integrity.
- ASIL D (e.g., AEB, Lane Keeping) requires ≥ 99 % single-point fault coverage and a Probabilistic Metric for random Hardware Failures (PMHF) ≤ 10⁻⁸ /h.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following formulae are **board-favourite** for 14-mark derivation problems.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | Required sample size for proportion (e.g., disengagement rate) at confidence $Z$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{\text{SOTIF}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{known-safe}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | SOTIF scenario coverage ratio | ratio |
| 3 | $\text{FDC} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU, undetected}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Fault Detection Coverage (ISO 26262) | ratio |
| 4 | $\text{MTTF}_{\text{D}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}$ | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | hours |
| 5 | $\text{PMHF} = \sum \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ | Probabilistic Metric for random Hardware Failures | FIT (10⁻⁹ /h) |
| 6 | $\text{MPD} = \dfrac{\text{Total Miles Driven}}{\text{Disengagements}}$ | Miles per Disengagement | miles |
| 7 | $\text{Req. Miles} = \dfrac{\text{Fatality Rate}_{\text{human}}}{\text{Target Rate}_{\text{AV}}} \cdot N_{\text{sim}}$ | RAND safety equivalence miles | miles |
| 8 | $P_{\text{acceptance}} = 1 - \beta^{n}$ | Bayesian reliability growth: probability system is accepted after $n$ successful runs | ratio |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEMs (Toyota, Bosch, Continental)**: Use HIL for 80 % of validation, road tests for 20 %.
- **L4 Robotaxi Companies**: 99 % simulation, 1 % road — but road data is fed back into simulation to close the loop ("scenario mining").
- **Type-Approval Authorities** (UNECE R157 ALKS, AIS-184): Mandate specific test cases (e.g., cut-in at 130 km/h) before market release.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: A modern ADAS programme runs roughly **10¹¹ km of simulated driving per week** at hyperscalers (Waymo, Cruise). Public-road testing alone is statistically and economically infeasible — hence simulation-dominant methodology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Miles for Statistical Safety Equivalence (RAND Equation)

**Problem Statement:** A human driver in the United States has a fatality rate of **1.09 fatalities per 10⁸ miles**. An AV must be at least 2× safer. If we run a fleet of 100 vehicles, each driving 24/7 at 25 mph average, how many *real-world* miles are needed to demonstrate the AV is statistically safer than a human at 95 % confidence?

**Given:**
- Human fatality rate: $r_h = 1.09 \times 10^{-8}$ per mile
- Target AV rate: $r_a = 0.5 \times r_h = 5.45 \times 10^{-9}$ per mile
- Confidence level: $Z = 1.96$ (95 %)
- Margin of error: $e = 20\%$ of $r_a$

**Step 1 — Write the general sample-size formula for a rare-event Poisson process:**

$$
n = \frac{Z^2}{r_a \cdot e_{\text{abs}}^2}
$$

where $e_{\text{abs}}$ is the absolute allowed error in failure-rate estimate.

**Step 2 — Compute absolute allowed error:**

$$
e_{\text{abs}} = 0.20 \times r_a = 0.20 \times 5.45 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
e_{\text{abs}} = 1.09 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per mile}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into formula:**

$$
n = \frac{(1.96)^2}{(5.45 \times 10^{-9})(1.09 \times 10^{-9})^{-1} \cdot \text{?}}
$$

Reformulating for **zero-failure** (Poisson) testing where we need at least 1 failure-free sample size with probability $p$:

$$
n = \frac{-\ln(1 - p)}{r_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for $p = 0.95$:**

$$
n = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{5.45 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{2.996}{5.45 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 5.49 \times 10^{8} \text{ miles}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to fleet-time:**

Fleet speed: $v = 25 \text{ mph}$, fleet size $N = 100$, hours/day $h = 24$:

$$
t_{\text{days}} = \frac{n}{N \cdot v \cdot h} = \frac{5.49 \times 10^{8}}{100 \times 25 \times 24}
$$

$$
t_{\text{days}} = \frac{5.49 \times 10^{8}}{60{,}000} \approx 9{,}150 \text{ days} \approx 25 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** 25 years of real-world driving is impossible — hence the **simulation-dominant methodology**. RAND's 2016 report showed ~1.1 × 10⁸ miles (≈ 400 years for 1 car). This single derivation is worth **7 marks** in any KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fault Detection Coverage (FDC) and ASIL Compliance

**Given:** An ECU has total failure rate $\lambda_{\text{total}} = 1000$ FIT, with detected dangerous failures $\lambda_{\text{DU,det}} = 990$ FIT and undetected dangerous failures $\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}} = 10$ FIT.

**Step 1 — Apply FDC formula:**

$$
\text{FDC} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{FDC} = 1 - \frac{10}{1000} = 0.99 = 99\%
$$

**Step 3 — Check ASIL compliance:**

- ASIL B requires FDC ≥ 90 % ✓
- ASIL C requires FDC ≥ 97 % ✓
- ASIL D requires FDC ≥ 99 % ✓ (exactly meets)

**Step 4 — PMHF calculation:**

$$
\text{PMHF} = \lambda_{\text{DU,undet}} = 10 \text{ FIT}
$$

ASIL D budget: $\leq 10$ FIT ✓ (at limit).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Acceptance Probability (Beta-Binomial)

**Scenario:** A regression suite has historically produced 0 failures over $k$ runs. What is the probability that the next $n$ runs will be failure-free (system accepted)?

**Prior:** Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ with $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$ (uniform).

**Posterior after $k$ successes:** Beta$(1+k, 1)$.

**Predictive probability of failure in a single run:**

$$
P(f) = \frac{\beta}{(\alpha + \beta) + k} = \frac{1}{2 + k}
$$

**Predictive probability of success in $n$ runs:**

$$
P_{\text{acceptance}} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2+k}\right)^n = \left(\frac{1+k}{2+k}\right)^n
$$

**Numerical Example:** $k = 99$ historical successful runs, need $n = 1000$ more:

$$
P_{\text{acceptance}} = \left(\frac{100}{101}\right)^{1000} = e^{1000 \cdot \ln(0.9901)} = e^{-9.95} \approx 4.77 \times 10^{-5}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner trap**: Students frequently substitute $P(f) = 1/k$ instead of the Laplace-rule-of-succession $1/(k+2)$. Always use the **+2 correction** for Bayesian estimates.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Methodology | Real-time? | Cost (₹ per test) | Reproducibility | Used For | KTU Marks Weight |
|-------------|------------|--------------------|-----------------|----------|------------------|
| **MIL** | 10,000× | < 1 | Perfect | Algorithm logic | 2 |
| **SIL** | 100× | ~ 5 | Perfect | Code regression | 2 |
| **HIL** | 1× | ~ 5,000 | Perfect | ECU firmware, fault injection | 4 |
| **DIL** | 1× | ~ 50,000 | High | HMI / takeover | 2 |
| **VIL** | 1× | ~ 1,00,000 | Medium | Actuator limits | 2 |
| **Track** | 1× | ~ 10,00,000 | Low (real env.) | Type approval | 1 |
| **Road** | 1× | Very high | None | Statistical validation | 1 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Differentiate between MIL, SIL, and HIL testing methodologies used in ADAS development. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**
- **MIL (Model-in-the-Loop):** The algorithm is represented as a high-level mathematical model (MATLAB/Simulink) and tested against a simulated environment. No production code is generated. It is the earliest verification stage.
- **SIL (Software-in-the-Loop):** Production-intent C/C++ code (or ROS 2 nodes) is compiled and run on a host PC against a virtual sensor/plant model. Catches integration and compiler-related defects.
- **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop):** The actual production ECU hardware, running production firmware, is stimulated in real-time by a hardware simulator (dSPACE, NI) that emulates sensors (cameras, radar) and actuators (steering, brake). Validates hardware-driver interaction and deterministic timing.
- **Key Difference:** Increasing levels of *realism* — from pure mathematical model (MIL) → compiled code (SIL) → real target silicon (HIL). **[3 Marks]**

### **Q2.** What is SOTIF (ISO 21448) and why is it critical for ADAS testing? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**
SOTIF stands for **Safety Of The Intended Functionality** (ISO 21448). It addresses hazards arising not from systematic/random hardware failures (covered by ISO 26262) but from **performance limitations** of the intended function or **foreseeable misuse** — e.g., a perception algorithm misclassifying a pedestrian in low light, or an AEB failing on a curved road.

It is critical for ADAS because ADAS relies heavily on **machine-learning perception** and **edge-case decision-making**, where functional correctness (ISO 26262) is insufficient — the system may be *fault-free* yet *unsafe*. SOTIF mandates a structured process of:
1. Identifying triggering conditions
2. Reducing Area 3 (unknown-unsafe) through additional testing/data
3. Validating residual risk acceptance

Without SOTIF compliance, type approval under UNECE R157 (ALKS) is impossible. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **AV Test Pyramid** with its six layers. For each layer, state the toolchain, test purpose, and a representative scenario. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An autonomous emergency braking (AEB) system must achieve ASIL D compliance. The ECU has total failure rate $\lambda_{\text{total}} = 1500$ FIT and 1450 FIT of dangerous failures are detected. Compute (i) FDC, (ii) PMHF, (iii) MTTF$_\text{D}$, and (iv) state whether the system meets ASIL D requirements (FDC ≥ 99 %, PMHF ≤ 10 FIT). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) AV Test Pyramid (7 Marks):**

| Layer | Method | Toolchain | Purpose | Representative Scenario |
|-------|--------|-----------|---------|--------------------------|
| L1 (bottom) | **MIL** | MATLAB/Simulink, CarMaker | Validate control algorithm logic | Lane-keeping PID controller tuning |
| L2 | **SIL** | ROS 2 + Gazebo, Apollo | Test production code against virtual sensors | Object-fusion pipeline regression |
| L3 | **HIL** | dSPACE, Vector VT, NI | Validate ECU firmware on target silicon | Inject CAN bus faults |
| L4 | **DIL** | Static driving simulator with motion platform | Validate HMI / takeover | Driver takes back control at T-3 s |
| L5 | **Track (Closed Course)** | ARAI, IDIADA proving ground | Type approval / Euro NCAP | AEB on stationary target at 50 km/h |
| L6 (top) | **Road (Public)** | Instrumented fleet / shadow mode | Statistical safety validation | Cut-in scenarios in dense traffic |

> The pyramid is traversed **bottom-up** during development and **top-down** during regression of defects found in higher layers.

**(b) ASIL D Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda_{\text{total}} = 1500$ FIT, $\lambda_{\text{DU,det}} = 1450$ FIT.

**Step 1 — Compute $\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}}$:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}} = \lambda_{\text{total}} - \lambda_{\text{DU,det}} = 1500 - 1450 = 50 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 2 — Fault Detection Coverage (FDC):**

$$
\text{FDC} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \frac{50}{1500} = 1 - 0.0333 = 0.9667 = 96.67\%
$$

**Step 3 — Probabilistic Metric for random Hardware Failures (PMHF):**

$$
\text{PMHF} = \lambda_{\text{DU,undet}} = 50 \text{ FIT} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean Time To Dangerous Failure:**

$$
\text{MTTF}_{\text{D}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ h}
$$

**Step 5 — ASIL D Compliance Check:**

| Requirement | ASIL D Limit | Computed | Status |
|-------------|--------------|----------|--------|
| FDC | ≥ 99 % | 96.67 % | ✗ FAIL |
| PMHF | ≤ 10 FIT | 50 FIT | ✗ FAIL |

**Conclusion:** The ECU **does NOT** meet ASIL D requirements. **Recommendations:** Add diagnostic mechanisms (e.g., dual-core lockstep, watchdog) to detect at least 15 more FIT of dangerous failures; redesign with a more reliable sensor interface. **[7 Marks — Complete]**

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)

**(a)** Explain **V&V (Verification and Validation)** in the context of ISO 26262 and ISO 21448 (SOTIF). With a neat sketch, describe the **V-Model** for ADAS development. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robotaxi operates at a target fatality rate of $r_a = 5.5 \times 10^{-9}$ per mile. Human fatality rate is $r_h = 1.1 \times 10^{-8}$ per mile. A single test vehicle averages 30 mph, 24 h/day. Using the Poisson zero-failure formula with 95 % confidence, calculate the required test miles and the corresponding calendar duration. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) V&V and V-Model (7 Marks):**

**Verification (ISO 26262):** Confirms the system is built *correctly* via reviews, walkthroughs, MIL/SIL/HIL tests. Each design step on the left of the V has a corresponding test on the right.

**Validation (SOTIF – ISO 21448):** Confirms the *right system* is built — system behaves as intended under all ODD conditions including edge cases, with no unknown-unsafe scenarios.

**V-Model Description (5 layers):**
- Left arm: ODD definition → Operational Analysis → System Architecture → Component Architecture
- Right arm: Unit Test (MIL/SIL) → Integration Test (HIL) → System Test (Track/VIL) → Acceptance Test (Road)
- The connecting horizontal lines show the **correspondence** (each design phase has a matching test phase).

**(b) Required Test Miles Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $r_a = 5.5 \times 10^{-9}$ per mile, $r_h = 1.1 \times 10^{-8}$ per mile, $p = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Apply Poisson zero-failure sample size:**

$$
n = \frac{-\ln(1 - p)}{r_a} = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{5.5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{2.996}{5.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.447 \times 10^{8} \text{ miles}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute calendar time:**

Daily miles per vehicle: $30 \times 24 = 720$ miles/day.

$$
t_{\text{days}} = \frac{5.447 \times 10^{8}}{720} = 7.56 \times 10^{5} \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_{\text{years}} = \frac{7.56 \times 10^{5}}{365} \approx 2073 \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** 2073 years of single-vehicle real-world testing is infeasible. The industry therefore shifts to **simulation-augmented testing** with statistically equivalent synthetic mileage. **[7 Marks — Complete]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing MIL with SIL:** Students often write "MIL tests compiled code" — wrong. MIL tests the *model*, not compiled code.
> 2. **Wrong FDC formula:** Never use $\lambda_{\text{DU,det}}/\lambda_{\text{total}}$ as FDC; the correct numerator is **undetected** dangerous failures.
> 3. **Forgetting Poisson "−ln"** in the RAND miles derivation — many students use a plain proportion formula and lose 3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the V-Model diagram** in 14-mark Q&A on V&V — examiners expect a labelled sketch worth at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the ODD** explicitly when describing scenarios — without ODD, any test result is meaningless.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AV Test Pyramid**: MIL → SIL → HIL → DIL → VIL → Track → Road (bottom-up).
- **V-Model pairs**: ODD ↔ Acceptance Test; Operational Analysis ↔ System Test; Component Arch ↔ Unit Test.
- **ISO 26262** = systematic + random HW failures (ASIL A–D).
- **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** = performance limitations + ML edge cases.
- **ASIL D limits**: FDC ≥ 99 %, PMHF ≤ 10 FIT.
- **FDC formula**: $\text{FDC} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,undet}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$.
- **RAND Miles formula**: $n = -\ln(1-p)/r_a$ for zero-failure acceptance.
- **Bayesian Laplace rule**: $P(f) = 1/(k+2)$ — **not** $1/k$.
- **Scenario tools**: ASAM OpenSCENARIO (scenarios), ASAM OpenDRIVE (maps).
- **Type-approval regulations**: UNECE R157 (ALKS L3), AIS-184 (India L2 ADAS).
- **Disengagement metric**: MPD (Miles per Disengagement) reported to CA DMV.
- **OEDR**: Object & Event Detection & Response — core ODD capability.
- **Key insight for exam**: Always argue *why* simulation cannot replace road tests **entirely** (residual unknown-unsafe scenarios require real-world coverage).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation Environments and Test Tracks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulation Environments and Test Tracks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Simulation Environment** in the context of Autonomous Driving (AD) and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) is a software-based, mathematically-modelled virtual ecosystem that emulates the kinematic, dynamic, and stochastic behaviour of a host vehicle, surrounding traffic agents, sensors, and the operational environment (road topology, weather, lighting). It is used as a **non-destructive, reproducible, and scalable** substitute for physical driving to validate perception, planning, and control algorithms before on-road deployment.

A **Test Track** (or **Proving Ground**) is a physically controlled, geographically bounded real-world facility equipped with calibrated infrastructure (lane markings, traffic signals, dummy vehicles, pedestrian mannequins, weather simulators) designed to execute **standardized, repeatable, and safety-critical** test manoeuvres for ADAS/AD homologation and certification.

Together, they form the **V-Model verification and validation (V&V) backbone** of the ISO 26262 and ISO 21448 (SOTIF) development lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to differentiate between **Model-in-the-Loop (MIL)**, **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)**, **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)**, and **Vehicle-in-the-Loop (VIL)**, and to explain the role of test tracks in the homologation chain (Euro NCAP, NHTSA, AIS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an autonomous vehicle as a **medical student learning surgery**. Before the student is allowed to operate on a real patient, they must:
1. Read textbooks and study anatomy (≡ Software-in-the-Loop on synthetic data).
2. Practice on a virtual-reality mannequin that simulates real tissue feedback (≡ Hardware-in-the-Loop with a steering robot and a mock dashboard).
3. Assist a senior surgeon on a real patient under supervision (≡ Proving Ground / Test Track with a safety driver).
4. Finally, operate independently (≡ Public-road deployment with ODD restrictions).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a surgeon-in-training is **never allowed to skip the mannequin stage** and go straight to the operating table, a self-driving algorithm is **never allowed to skip simulation and skip directly to public roads** because the combinatorial explosion of edge cases cannot be exhaustively discovered on real streets within a reasonable budget and timeframe.

### 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (bolded for emphasis)

- **Disengagement Rate (DR):** number of safety-driver takeovers per **1000 miles (or 1 million km)**.
- **Mean Distance Between Failures (MDBF):** average autonomous miles before any system fault.
- **Scenario Coverage (C):** the percentage of the operational design domain (ODD) parameter space exercised.
- **Sim-to-Real Gap (Δ):** the measurable performance deviation between a virtual sensor and its physical counterpart.
- **Time-To-Collision (TTC):** the time remaining before a collision if the current trajectory is held.
- **Euro NCAP Rating:** the 5-star consumer safety score derived from track tests.
- **ISO 21448 (SOTIF):** the standard governing the **Safety Of The Intended Functionality** of ADAS/AD systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The V-Model of ADAS/AD Validation

The development of safety-critical vehicle functions follows the **V-Model** defined in **ISO 26262**. The left descending arm represents decomposition (requirements → design → implementation), and the right ascending arm represents integration & validation (unit test → integration test → vehicle test → acceptance test).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the V-Model?** Because automotive systems demand a **traceable, bidirectional, audit-friendly** process for regulatory bodies. Each design level on the left must be matched by an equivalent test level on the right.

The testing pyramid for AD validation is structured as:

1. **Unit / Model-in-the-Loop (MIL)** — pure software control models executed on a desktop PC. Used in early development and CI/CD pipelines. Fastest iteration time.
2. **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)** — auto-generated C/C++ code from the same models is compiled and run on the host machine. Validates that the production code is functionally equivalent to the model (back-to-back testing).
3. **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** — the real production ECU is connected to a real-time simulator (e.g., dSPACE, NI VeriStand, Vector VT System) that emulates the rest of the vehicle and environment. Tests I/O, bus loads, and timing.
4. **Driver-in-the-Loop / Vehicle-in-the-Loop (DIL/VIL)** — the real vehicle is driven in a controlled environment, but a virtual world is injected into the perception stack (e.g., via screen projections or augmented reality). Tests the human–machine interaction and the full chain.
5. **Proving Ground / Test Track** — physical facility with real objects, real sensors, real weather.
6. **Public-Road Pilot / Shadow Mode** — the system runs silently in consumer vehicles, logging performance without taking action.

### 2.2 Sensor Simulation Models

A simulation environment must reproduce the **noise, bias, dropout, and failure modes** of every sensor. The standard sensor models are:

| Sensor | Physical Phenomenon | Key Model Output | Standard Library |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera | Pinhole projection + radiometry | RGB + depth + semantic masks | Carla, AirSim, NVIDIA DRIVE Sim |
| LiDAR | Time-of-flight (ToF) ray casting | 3D point cloud + intensity + ring index | Carla, Velodyne, Hesai |
| Radar | Doppler shift + FMCW chirp | Range, velocity, RCS (radar cross-section) | MATLAB Radar Toolbox, Prescan |
| IMU / GPS | Strap-down integration + inertial drift | 6-DoF pose + satellite pseudoranges | CARLA, ROS Gazebo |
| Ultrasonic | Acoustic pulse-echo | Distance 0.1 m – 5 m | MATLAB Driving Scenario |

### 2.3 Sim-to-Real Gap

The **Reality Gap (Δ_real)** is the divergence between simulated sensor statistics and real sensor statistics. It is computed as:

$$\Delta_{real} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\vert \mu_{sim}(i) - \mu_{real}(i) \vert}{\mu_{real}(i)} \times 100\%$$

where $\mu(i)$ is the i-th moment (mean, variance, kurtosis) of the sensor reading distribution. A gap below **5 %** is considered **synchronised** (industry benchmark for camera-only stacks).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for Module 4. Memorise the units, boundary conditions, and limit cases (v → 0, μ → 1, etc.).

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Domain & Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disengagement Rate | $DR = \dfrac{N_{d}}{D_{km}}$ | $N_{d}$ = number of disengagements, $D_{km}$ = total autonomous km | $DR \le 1$ per 1000 km for L4 maturity |
| 2 | Time-To-Collision | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ | $d_{rel}$ in m, $v_{rel}$ in m/s | $v_{rel} > 0$, $TTC < 4$ s ⇒ emergency brake |
| 3 | Stopping Distance | $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | $v$ in m/s, $t_{r}$ reaction time ≈ 1.0 s, $\mu$ friction coeff., $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$ | Dry asphalt $\mu \approx 0.7$, ice $\mu \approx 0.15$ |
| 4 | Scenario Coverage | $C = \dfrac{N_{exec}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{exec}$ = executed, $N_{total}$ = catalogued | $C \ge 95\%$ for type approval |
| 5 | Reality Gap | $\Delta_{real} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \dfrac{\vert \mu_{sim} - \mu_{real} \vert}{\mu_{real}}$ | % units | $\Delta_{real} < 5\%$ for L3 readiness |
| 6 | Radar Range (max) | $R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}}}$ | $P_{t}$ transmit power, $G$ antenna gain, $\lambda$ wavelength, $\sigma$ RCS, $S_{min}$ sensitivity | Monostatic 77 GHz: $R_{max} \approx 250$ m |
| 7 | LiDAR Photon Count | $N_{ph} = \dfrac{E_{p} \lambda}{hc} \cdot \eta$ | $E_{p}$ pulse energy, $\eta$ quantum efficiency | $N_{ph} \sim 10^{9}$ per pulse |
| 8 | Camera Pinhole | $x_{img} = f \cdot \dfrac{X_{cam}}{Z_{cam}}$ | $f$ focal length, $X_{cam}, Z_{cam}$ camera-frame coordinates | Valid for $Z_{cam} \gg f$ |
| 9 | Test Pyramid Mass | $T_{MIL} : T_{SIL} : T_{HIL} : T_{DIL} : T_{Track} = 10^{6} : 10^{5} : 10^{4} : 10^{2} : 10^{1}$ | Number of test iterations per dev cycle | Cost rises ~100× per level up |
| 10 | Mean Distance Between Failures | $MDBF = \dfrac{D_{total}}{N_{fault}}$ | $D_{total}$ km, $N_{fault}$ count | $MDBF \to \infty$ for L5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that **TTC is undefined when $v_{rel} \le 0$** (the lead vehicle is faster than, or equal to, the host). Always state the **pre-condition $v_{rel} > 0$** before writing the formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Simulation environments and test tracks underpin the entire **homologation chain** that puts an ADAS feature into a production car. Without them:
- The cost of physical crash tests would be **prohibitive** (each Euro NCAP test costs ≈ **€50,000** and destroys the vehicle).
- Edge cases (a child chasing a ball into the street at midnight in the rain) would be **statistically impossible to encounter on public roads** within a development cycle.
- The legal liability under **product-liability law** could not be defended without **traceable, reproducible test evidence**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Disengagement Rate and the MDBF

The Disengagement Rate (DR) and the Mean Distance Between Failures (MDBF) are **inverse metrics** of the same underlying statistical process. Let us derive the relationship between them.

**Step 1 — Define the stochastic process.**
Let $T_i$ be the autonomous driving time (in hours) between the $(i-1)^{th}$ and $i^{th}$ disengagement. We assume $T_i$ are **i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed)** exponential random variables with rate parameter $\lambda$. The exponential assumption is standard in reliability engineering for systems with constant hazard rate within a maintenance interval.

The probability density function is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean time between failures.**

$$MTBF = \mathbb{E}[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$MTBF = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\therefore MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Convert time-based MTBF to distance-based MDBF.**

The vehicle travels at an average speed $v_{avg}$ (in km/h). The mean distance accumulated in one MTBF interval is:

$$MDBF = v_{avg} \cdot MTBF = \frac{v_{avg}}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Express the Disengagement Rate per kilometre.**

The disengagement rate is the **expected number of disengagements per kilometre**:

$$DR = \frac{1}{MDBF} = \frac{\lambda}{v_{avg}}$$

**Step 5 — Express the Disengagement Rate per 1000 km (industry standard, California DMV):**

$$DR_{1k} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{v_{avg}} = \frac{1000}{MDBF}$$

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example (typical L4 robotaxi fleet):**

Given $v_{avg} = 36$ km/h, $\lambda = 0.002$ disengagements per hour:

$$MDBF = \frac{36}{0.002} = 18{,}000 \text{ km}$$

$$DR_{1k} = \frac{1000}{18000} \approx 0.0556 \text{ disengagements per 1000 km}$$

This corresponds to **one disengagement every 18,000 km**, which is in line with Waymo's 2023 California disclosure (~0.022 per 1000 km).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stopping Distance (Full Kinematic Chain)

The stopping distance is the **integral of velocity** from the instant a hazard is detected to the instant the vehicle comes to rest. The vehicle first travels the **driver/algorithm reaction distance** $d_{r}$ and then the **braking distance** $d_{b}$.

**Step 1 — Reaction phase (constant velocity $v_0$):**

$$d_{r} = v_{0} \cdot t_{r}$$

**Step 2 — Braking phase (kinetic energy → heat).**
By Newton's second law, the deceleration $a = \mu g$. The velocity evolves as:

$$v(t) = v_0 - \mu g \cdot t$$

The vehicle stops at $t_{stop} = \dfrac{v_0}{\mu g}$. The braking distance is:

$$d_{b} = \int_{0}^{t_{stop}} v(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{v_0/\mu g} (v_0 - \mu g t) \, dt$$

$$= \left[ v_0 t - \frac{1}{2} \mu g t^{2} \right]_{0}^{v_0/\mu g}$$

$$= v_0 \cdot \frac{v_0}{\mu g} - \frac{1}{2} \mu g \left( \frac{v_0}{\mu g} \right)^{2}$$

$$= \frac{v_0^{2}}{\mu g} - \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \mu g} = \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two phases:**

$$d_{stop} = d_{r} + d_{b} = v_0 t_{r} + \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \mu g}$$

This is the equation tabulated in Section 2.4. Converting $v_0$ from km/h to m/s via $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{km/h}}{3.6}$ is the most common KTU mark-loss step.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Radar Maximum Range (Radar Equation)

A monostatic FMCW automotive radar emits $P_t$ watts of chirp power through an antenna of gain $G$, which propagates to a target of **radar cross-section (RCS)** $\sigma$ and back-scatters to the same antenna. The received power at the antenna feed is:

$$P_{r} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$$

The radar can detect this echo only if $P_{r} \ge S_{min}$, where $S_{min}$ is the **minimum detectable signal** set by the noise floor of the receiver. Setting $P_r = S_{min}$ and solving for $R$:

$$R_{max}^{4} = \frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}}$$

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}}}$$

**Numerical check** (typical 77 GHz Bosch front radar):
- $P_t = 0.01$ W (10 mW per chirp)
- $G = 10^{3}$ (30 dBi)
- $\lambda = 3.9 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\sigma = 10$ m² (small car)
- $S_{min} = 10^{-13}$ W (–100 dBm)

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{0.01 \cdot (10^{3})^{2} \cdot (3.9 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \cdot 10}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot 10^{-13}}}$$

$$\approx \sqrt[4]{\frac{0.01 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 1.52 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 10}{1984.4 \cdot 10^{-13}}}$$

$$\approx \sqrt[4]{\frac{152}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}}} \approx \sqrt[4]{7.66 \times 10^{11}} \approx 296 \text{ m}$$

This matches the **250 m specification** after including processing losses.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Simulation Tiers

| Tier | Hardware Realism | Environment Realism | Iteration Time | Cost per Run | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MIL | None (math) | High (Carla, PreScan) | seconds | **₹0** | Algorithm design, regression |
| SIL | None (compiled C) | High | seconds | **₹0** | Code-gen parity, MISRA check |
| HIL | Real ECU | Real (RT-sim) | milliseconds | **₹50 / min** | I/O, bus, fault injection |
| DIL | Real vehicle | Mock (screen) | minutes | **₹5000 / run** | HMI, comfort, driver acceptance |
| Track | Real vehicle | Real (objects) | hours | **₹50 000 / run** | Euro NCAP, type approval |
| Public | Real vehicle | Real (chaos) | days | **₹10 lakh / month** | Data collection, corner cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 100×-per-level cost ramp is the fundamental reason** that OEMs exhaust the lower tiers (especially MIL + SIL) before committing to a single HIL or track run. A typical AD programme runs **billions of MIL scenarios, millions of SIL, thousands of HIL, hundreds of DIL, and dozens of track tests** before public release.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
