## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate clearly between Model-in-the-Loop (MIL), Software-in-the-Loop (SIL), and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing in the context of an Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) system. Mention the typical tool used at each level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MIL (1 mark):** The control algorithm is implemented as a Simulink/Stateflow model and executed on a desktop PC. The "vehicle" is a pure mathematical model (point-mass, bicycle, or 7-DoF). Tool: MATLAB/Simulink. Advantage: fastest iteration, suitable for millions of regression runs in CI/CD.
- **SIL (1 mark):** Production C/C++ code is auto-generated from the model (or hand-coded) and re-executed on the host. Used to verify **model-to-code parity** through back-to-back testing. Tool: Embedded Coder, Vector vTESTstudio.
- **HIL (1 mark):** The **real production ECU** is plugged into a real-time simulator (dSPACE, NI VeriStand, ETAS LABCAR) that emulates the CAN/LIN bus, sensors, and vehicle dynamics. Used to verify timing, I/O, and fault-injection behaviour at millisecond fidelity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Reality Gap" in ADAS simulation. State the standard industrial threshold and name one technique used to reduce it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Reality Gap is the measurable deviation between the statistical distribution of a simulated sensor's output and the distribution of the corresponding real sensor's output, expressed as a percentage of the real mean.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\Delta_{real} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{\vert \mu_{sim}(i) - \mu_{real}(i) \vert}{\mu_{real}(i)} \times 100\%$
- **Threshold & technique (1 mark):** Industry accepts $\Delta_{real} \le 5\%$ for an L3 camera stack. Reduction techniques include **domain randomisation**, **noise injection calibration** (matching the real noise power spectral density), and **neural sensor models** trained on real data (e.g., NVIDIA's learned LiDAR).

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Module-4 question carries an internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the V-Model of validation for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system, indicating where MIL, SIL, HIL, and Proving-Ground tests fit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Left arm — decomposition (3 marks):**
   - **Step 1: ODD & Hazard Analysis (HARA)** — define the Operational Design Domain (e.g., highway, dry, day, 0–130 km/h). Identify hazards such as "rear-end collision due to false lead-vehicle drop-out".
   - **Step 2: Functional Safety Requirements** — assign an ASIL level (typically **ASIL B** for ACC). Specify the Safe State (e.g., "if radar fails, freeze speed at last valid value").
   - **Step 3: System & Software Architecture** — decompose the ACC into perception (radar + camera fusion), planning (target selection, time-gap policy), control (PI speed controller), and actuation (throttle/brake by-wire).
   - **Step 4: Detailed Design & Code Generation** — produce the Simulink model and auto-generate C code.

2. **Bottom of the V — implementation (1 mark):**
   - The C code is flashed onto the production ECU (e.g., NXP S32K3 + radar front-end).

3. **Right arm — validation (3 marks):**
   - **MIL** — run 10⁶ cut-in scenarios in PreScan to tune the PI gains.
   - **SIL** — verify that auto-generated C produces identical throttle output to the model within 0.1 %.
   - **HIL** — fault-inject "radar CAN bus timeout" and verify the ECU enters the safe state within 100 ms.
   - **Proving Ground** — execute the Euro NCAP ACC test protocol on the certified track (straight 1 km, lead vehicle at 50 km/h, host at 80 km/h; verify zero contact).

**Sketch (text-rendered block diagram, 1 mark):**
```
V&V Levels (Top to Bottom):
[System] ◄───────────► [System Test]    ← Track
[Subsys] ◄───────────► [Integration]    ← DIL
[Unit]   ◄───────────► [Unit Test]      ← MIL/SIL/HIL
[Code]   ◄───────────► [Code Review]    ← Static analysis
```

**(b) An AEB system is to be validated on a test track. The host vehicle approaches a stationary target at 60 km/h. The system is expected to achieve an avoidance speed reduction of 30 km/h before contact. The friction coefficient is μ = 0.7 and the reaction time is t_r = 0.2 s (automated, not human). Compute (i) the available reaction distance, (ii) the braking distance required, and (iii) comment on whether the design meets the safety goal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
$v_0 = 60 \, \dfrac{km}{h} = \dfrac{60}{3.6} = 16.67 \, m/s$
Required final speed: $v_f = 30 \, km/h = 8.33 \, m/s$

**Step 2 — (i) Reaction distance (2 marks):**

$$d_r = v_0 \cdot t_r = 16.67 \times 0.2 = 3.33 \, m$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Braking distance to slow from 16.67 m/s to 8.33 m/s (3 marks):**
Deceleration: $a = \mu g = 0.7 \times 9.81 = 6.867 \, m/s^2$

Using $v_f^{2} = v_0^{2} - 2 a d_b$:

$$d_b = \frac{v_0^{2} - v_f^{2}}{2 a} = \frac{(16.67)^{2} - (8.33)^{2}}{2 \times 6.867}$$

$$= \frac{277.89 - 69.39}{13.734} = \frac{208.5}{13.734} \approx 15.18 \, m$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Safety-goal comment (2 marks):**
Total stopping distance = 3.33 + 15.18 = **18.51 m**. If the target is detected at, say, 25 m, then a margin of 6.5 m remains. The system **meets the safety goal comfortably** for this scenario. However, for higher host speeds (≥ 80 km/h) or lower friction (wet asphalt, μ = 0.4), the same algorithm would NOT meet the goal, and the controller must trigger the brake **before** t_r expires (predictive TTC threshold, typically TTC < 2.7 s).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Compare the features of CARLA, MATLAB/PreScan, and dSPACE HIL as test environments for an L2 ADAS stack. Recommend a workflow that uses all three sequentially. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **CARLA (open-source, photo-realistic) (2 marks):**
   - Built on Unreal Engine. Open-source, free for research. Strong for **vision-based perception** (semantic segmentation, depth, instance masks). Native Python API. Weakness: vehicle dynamics are simplified (bicycle model), so control validation is approximate.

2. **MATLAB/PreScan (commercial, multi-physics) (2 marks):**
   - Closed-form sensor models with adjustable noise, configurable road networks, and Simulink co-simulation. Industry standard for **OEM algorithm development**. Strength: tight integration with Control System Toolbox and Model Predictive Control. Cost: ₹15–25 lakh per seat.

3. **dSPACE HIL (real-time, certified) (2 marks):**
   - Microsecond-level real-time execution on dedicated SCALEXIO hardware. Used for **certification-grade ECU validation** under ISO 26262. Cost: ₹1–5 crore per bench.

4. **Sequential workflow (1 mark):**
   - **Phase 1:** Develop perception/planning in CARLA on a public cloud (1 million scenarios/day).
   - **Phase 2:** Port the algorithm to MATLAB/PreScan, tune control gains using measured CAN signals.
   - **Phase 3:** Auto-generate production C code, deploy on dSPACE SCALEXIO, run fault-injection.
   - **Phase 4:** Validate on the OEM proving ground (e.g., ARAI Pune, Mahindra Chakan, M-City Michigan).

**(b) A 77 GHz FMCW radar has $P_t = 5$ mW, $G = 1500$ (≈ 31.8 dBi), $\lambda = 3.9$ mm, $\sigma = 5$ m², and $S_{min} = 5 \times 10^{-14}$ W. (i) Calculate $R_{max}$. (ii) Comment on how the maximum range changes for a motorcycle (σ ≈ 1 m²). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — (i) Substitute into the radar equation (5 marks):**

$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{P_{t} G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}}}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma = 0.005 \times (1500)^{2} \times (3.9 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times 5$$

$$= 0.005 \times 2.25 \times 10^{6} \times 1.521 \times 10^{-5} \times 5$$

$$= 8.555 \times 10^{-1} \approx 0.8555$$

Compute the denominator:
$$(4\pi)^{3} S_{min} = 1984.4 \times 5 \times 10^{-14} = 9.922 \times 10^{-11}$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{0.8555}{9.922 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.622 \times 10^{9}$$

Fourth root:
$$R_{max} = \sqrt[4]{8.622 \times 10^{9}} \approx 170.9 \, m$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Motorcycle case (2 marks):**
Since $R_{max} \propto \sqrt[4]{\sigma}$, for $\sigma = 1$ m² (motorcycle):

$$R_{max}^{bike} = R_{max}^{car} \times \sqrt[4]{\frac{1}{5}} = 170.9 \times 0.6687 \approx 114.3 \, m$$

**Comment:** Detection range **falls by ~33 %** for a motorcycle. This is a major SOTIF concern and a primary reason for **sensor fusion with camera and LiDAR** — radar alone can under-detect VRUs (Vulnerable Road Users) at long range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Unit conversion slip:** Forgetting to divide km/h by **3.6** when plugging into $d_{stop} = v t + v^{2}/(2 \mu g)$. A frequent **–1 mark** error.
> 2. **Mixing up MIL and SIL:** Many students write "SIL uses real ECU". It does **not** — SIL runs the C code on the **host PC**; HIL uses the real ECU. This conflation costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(4\pi)^{3}$ term** in the radar equation and writing $R^{2}$ instead of $R^{4}$ is a **–3 mark** disaster.
> 4. **Omitting the SOTIF standard (ISO 21448)** in V-Model answers loses the **1 mark** for "applicable standards" line.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **V-Model** is the KTU-recommended framework; align every design step with a matching test step on the right arm.
- [x] **Test pyramid order** (cheaper to more expensive): **MIL → SIL → HIL → DIL/VIL → Proving Ground → Public Road**.
- [x] **Reality Gap $\le$ 5 %** is the industry acceptance threshold for camera/LiDAR models.
- [x] **Disengagement Rate (per 1000 km)** and **MDBF** are inverse metrics: $DR_{1k} = 1000 / MDBF$.
- [x] **Stopping distance** = $v_0 t_r + v_0^{2}/(2 \mu g)$; always convert km/h → m/s by **÷ 3.6**.
- [x] **Radar range** $\propto \sqrt[4]{\sigma}$: motorbikes (small $\sigma$) lose **~33 %** range vs cars.
- [x] **TTC $< 2.7$ s** is the standard AEB activation threshold; TTC is undefined for $v_{rel} \le 0$.
- [x] **ISO 26262** covers functional safety; **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** covers intended-functionality safety (perception failures). Both must be cited.
- [x] **OpenSCENARIO** describes *dynamic* content; **OpenDRIVE** describes *static* road networks — both are ASAM standards.
- [x] **Cost ramp** is **~100× per tier**: do **millions** of MIL before a single HIL run.
- [x] **Euro NCAP AEB Pedestrian / Cyclist / Car-to-Car** are the most common track-tested ADAS protocols in KTU questions.
- [x] **Sim-to-Real reduction** = domain randomisation + noise injection + learned sensor models.
- [x] **Track facilities** of relevance: ARAI Pune, M-City Michigan, AstaZero Sweden, JARI Japan, IDIADA Spain.
- [x] Always state the **ODD** (Operational Design Domain) before quoting any safety number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Validation Processes and Safety Assessments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Validation Processes and Safety Assessments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validation** is the *confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence* that a particular product (an Autonomous Driving / ADAS function) fulfills the **intended use, user needs, and operational requirements** in its **Operational Design Domain (ODD)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, validation is one half of the famous **V&V (Verification & Validation) couplet** that underpins the entire ADAS/AD development lifecycle: - **Verification** → *"Are we building the product RIGHT?"* — Confirms the implementation matches the design specification (analytical, white-box, simulation-based). - **Validation** → *"Are we building the RIGHT product?"* — Confirms the system meets the user's safety, comfort, and regulatory expectations in the real world (black-box, empirical, evidence-based). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4, Unit 3):** Validation of an ADS must demonstrate an *acceptable level of safety* comparable to a *competent, careful human driver* (the so-called "Positive Risk Balance" defined in **UN-R157** for ALKS and analogous frameworks). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a pharmaceutical company developing a new painkiller. - **Verification** is the lab test: *"Does the molecule bind to the target receptor as the chemistry model predicts?"* - **Validation** is the multi-year, multi-thousand-patient **clinical trial**: *"Does the pill actually relieve pain safely in real human beings across diverse populations?"* For autonomous vehicles, validation is the equivalent of a **gigantic, statistically-robust "clinical trial" of driving**. You cannot prove safety by reasoning alone; you must demonstrate it with **astronomical amounts of evidence** — billions of simulated kilometers plus millions of physical kilometers — because the *universe of "edge cases"* (children chasing balls, black ice, construction zones, sun glare) is practically infinite. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot (High-Yield) | Term | Formal Meaning | Units / Scale | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ODD** (Operational Design Domain) | The specific operating conditions (weather, road type, time of day, geofence) the system is designed for. | Categorical (taxonomy) | | **OEDR** (Object and Event Detection & Response) | Sub-task of perceiving objects and reacting appropriately. | Boolean / Latency (ms) | | **HARA** | Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (per ISO 26262). | Qualitative + Risk Score | | **ASIL** | Automotive Safety Integrity Level (A → D, D = most stringent). | Discrete (QM, A, B, C, D) | | **SOTIF** | Safety Of The Intended Functionality (ISO 21448). | Coverage / Residual Risk | | **MRM** | Minimal Risk Manoeuvre (fallback to a safe stop). | Time (s), Distance (m) | | **DDT** | Dynamic Driving Task (sustained lateral + longitudinal control). | Continuous | > [!NOTE] > **Constants to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:** > - **10⁹ km** ≈ target validation distance commonly cited to demonstrate AV safety parity with human drivers (≈ 100 fatalities / 10⁹ km human baseline). > - **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** threshold for **ASIL D = 10⁻⁸ h⁻¹** (i.e., 1 failure in ~114,000 years of operation). > - **TTC (Time-To-Collision) < 1.5 s** is universally used as the threshold for *imminent collision* in AEB validation. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The V-Model of Validation (Adapted for ADAS/AD) The **V-Model** is the foundational framework used by OEMs and Tier-1s. It pairs each *decomposition* (specification) phase on the left with a corresponding *integration* (validation) phase on the right. **Why the V?** Because every level of abstraction on the left (concept → system → software → hardware) must be *tested* at an equivalent integration level on the right. **The 5-Step Logic Flow:** 1. **Concept Phase (Left) ↔ Vehicle Acceptance Test (Right):** The *highest* level of validation — does the *whole car* behave safely on a closed proving ground? (Proving ground + on-road pilots). 2. **System Requirements ↔ Integration Test:** Validate sub-system interaction (e.g., does the AEB controller + radar + braking system meet the *system-level* latency budget?). 3. **Software/Hardware Architecture ↔ Software/Hardware Integration Test:** Unit-tested modules integrated and tested on ECU hardware. 4. **Module Design ↔ Unit Test:** Each algorithm (lane detection, MPC controller) tested in isolation. 5. **Coding ↔ Static Analysis (no direct counterpart on the right slope).** ### 2.2 The "X-in-the-Loop" Testing Pyramid This is the *single most testable* concept in Module 4 for KTU. Validation is **not** a single activity — it is a layered pyramid where lower layers are cheap/fast/abstract and higher layers are expensive/slow/real. | Loop Level | Full Name | What is Tested? | Pros | Cons | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **MIL** | Model-in-the-Loop | Pure algorithmic model in MATLAB/Simulink. | Cheapest, fastest, full coverage of logic. | No real-time; no hardware realism. | | **SIL** | Software-in-the-Loop | Compiled C/C++ code (auto-generated from model) on a PC. | Detects code-level bugs; software regressions. | No processor timing; no I/O. | | **HIL** | Hardware-in-the-Loop | Real ECU hardware stimulated by a real-time simulator of plant dynamics. | Tests ECU + sensor interfaces + bus latencies (CAN/LIN/FlexRay/Ethernet). | Very expensive rig; complex to maintain. | | **DIL** | Driver-in-the-Loop | A *human* drives a *real* or *simulated* vehicle, evaluating HMI, comfort, trust. | Captures subjective comfort, UX, edge cases a coder misses. | Subjective; non-repeatable. | | **ViL** | Vehicle-in-the-Loop | Real vehicle + virtual surrounding traffic (simulated environment). | Tests full sensor stack + actuators + compute in controlled "infinite" scenarios. | Requires a proving ground + AR/V2X setup. | | **Field Test** | On-road public testing | Real vehicle in real traffic. | Highest fidelity for *unknown unknowns*. | Slow; dangerous; cannot scale to 10⁹ km. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Pearl:** The *only* way to achieve the **10⁹ km** validation target economically is to do **~99.9% in MIL/SIL/HIL simulation** and use the physical fleet (ViL + Field) for *targeted confirmation* of the *residual* corner cases. This is called the **"Simulation-First"** approach championed by Waymo, Cruise, and Mobileye. ### 2.3 Safety Standards Landscape (Must-Memorize Triad) > [!NOTE] > **The 3 Pillars of AV Safety Standards:** > 1. **ISO 26262** (Functional Safety) — Addresses *systematic and random hardware failures*. > 2. **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** — Addresses *hazards from intended function* (e.g., a perception algorithm mis-classifies a white truck against a bright sky). > 3. **ISO 21434** (Cybersecurity Engineering) — Addresses *threats from malicious actors* (CAN injection, GPS spoofing, OTA hijack). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **mathematically grounded in the official ISO 26262-5:2018, ISO 21448:2022, and ASAM OpenSCENARIO standards**. Memorize the units and typical orders of magnitude. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Risk Score for HARA** (severity × exposure × controllability) | $R = S \times E \times C$ | $S,E,C \in \lbrace 1,2,3 \rbrace$ → $R \in \lbrace 1..27 \rbrace$ | | 2 | **ASIL Determination Table** | $\text{ASIL} = f(S,E,C)$ | QM (no requirement) or A, B, C, D | | 3 | **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | ASIL D: $\text{PFH} < 10^{-8} \, \text{h}^{-1}$ | | 4 | **Hardware Architectural Metric — SPFM** (Single-Point Fault Metric) | $\text{SPFM} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \lambda_{SPF}}{\sum \lambda}$ | ASIL D: $\geq 99\%$, ASIL B: $\geq 90\%$ | | 5 | **Latent Fault Metric — LFM** | $\text{LFM} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \lambda_{LF,\text{ detected}}}{\sum \lambda_{LF}}$ | ASIL D: $\geq 90\%$ | | 6 | **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | Imminent: $\text{TTC} < 1.5$ s | | 7 | **Required Deceleration (AEB)** | $a_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{(\Delta v)^{2}}{2 \cdot \Delta d}$ | $a_{\text{req}} < \mu \cdot g$ for feasibility | | 8 | **Required Sample Size for Statistical Validation** | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | $Z$ = confidence z-score; $E$ = margin of error | | 9 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Hours | | 10 | **SOTIF Coverage Ratio** (validated scenarios / total scenarios) | $C_{\text{SOTIF}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{accepted}} + N_{\text{mitigated}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Target: $\geq 95\%$ | | 11 | **Disengagement Rate (CA DMV metric)** | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Disengagements}}{\text{km Driven}}$ | Disengagements per 1000 km | > The vertical bar (e.g., $|x|$) is intentionally rendered as $\lbrace \cdot \rbrace$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve table integrity. Where absolute value is required, use $\vert \cdot \vert$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Validation frameworks are not academic luxuries. They directly determine: - **Type-approval / homologation** in the EU (UNECE WP.29 GRVA / R157) and USA (FMVSS + state laws). - **Insurance premium calculations** for autonomous fleets. - **Liability assignment** in crash investigations (who pays: OEM? Tier-1? Software vendor?). - **Investment decisions** — VCs discount unvalidated startups because "regulatory moat" requires demonstrable safety. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: ASIL Determination from a Hazard Scenario This is a *classic 14-mark KTU ESE problem*. The examiner typically gives a scenario like: *"An AEB function may fail to detect a stationary pedestrian at a crosswalk, leading to collision."* You must derive the ASIL. **Step 1 — Identify the Hazard and the Malfunctioning Behaviour** > Hazard: AEB does not apply maximum braking when a stationary child is detected in front. > Malfunctioning behaviour: *Missed object detection* leading to *no actuation* of the brake request. **Step 2 — Determine Severity (S)** Severity classifies the *worst-case harm to a person* if the hazard occurs. | S Class | Description | Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | S0 | No injuries | — | | S1 | Light to moderate injuries | Bruises, whiplash | | S2 | Severe to life-threatening injuries | Trauma, broken bones | | **S3** | **Life-threatening or fatal injuries** | Pedestrian fatality | **Derivation logic:** A pedestrian struck at 50 km/h by a 1.5-tonne vehicle has a fatality probability $> 50\%$. So $S = 3$. **Step 3 — Determine Exposure (E)** Exposure is the *probability* that the vehicle is *in the operating situation* where the hazard could occur. | E Class | Description | Probability | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | E0 | Incredibly unlikely | $< 0.001\%$ | | E1 | Very low probability | $\approx 0.1\%$ | | E2 | Low probability | $\approx 1\%$ | | **E3** | **Medium probability** | $\approx 10\%$ | | **E4** | **High probability** | $\geq 50\%$ | **Derivation logic:** A child could appear at a crosswalk on *any* urban drive. A typical commuter faces a crosswalk $\geq 50\%$ of the time. So $E = 4$. **Step 4 — Determine Controllability (C)** Controllability is the *probability* that a *competent, attentive driver* (or other road users) can avoid the harm. | C Class | Description | Controllable by Driver? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | C0 | Controllable in general | $> 99\%$ | | C1 | Simply controllable | $\approx 90\%$ | | C2 | Normally controllable | $\approx 50\%$ | | **C3** | **Difficult to control or uncontrollable** | $< 10\%$ | **Derivation logic:** A stationary child appearing from behind a parked car at < 1.5 s of warning cannot be avoided by any human reflex. So $C = 3$. **Step 5 — Look Up the ASIL in the Risk Graph** The ASIL risk graph (ISO 26262-3:2018, Figure B.2) yields: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Given } & S = 3, \quad E = 4, \quad C = 3, \\[4pt] \text{ASIL} & = \text{ASIL D} \quad \text{(highest integrity level)}. \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** AEB for pedestrian protection is **ASIL D** — the same level demanded for airbags and electronic stability control. > [!NOTE] > **Why the "D" is significant:** ASIL D imposes PFH $\leq 10^{-8}\,\text{h}^{-1}$, requires dual-channel hardware redundancy, and demands > 99% SPFM. Achieving this with a *single* camera + *single* radar is impossible — hence modern AEBs use *sensor fusion* with diverse modalities. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Sample-Size for Statistical Disengagement Validation **Problem:** A fleet operator reports **7 disengagements in 50,000 km** of urban autonomous driving. The regulator demands a 95% confidence interval that the true disengagement rate is below 1 per 5,000 km. Is the fleet compliant? **Step 1 — Compute Observed Rate** $$ \begin{aligned} \hat{p} &= \dfrac{7}{50000} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{disengagements / km} \\[6pt] \hat{p}_{5k} &= 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000 = 0.7 \text{ disengagements per 5,000 km}. \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Build 95% Confidence Interval Using Normal Approximation** The standard error for a proportion is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SE}(\hat{p}) &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}} \\[6pt] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.99986}{50000}} \\[6pt] &\approx \sqrt{2.8 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-5}. \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the 95% Confidence Bound ($Z_{0.025} = 1.96$)** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{\text{upper}} &= \hat{p} + 1.96 \cdot \text{SE} \\[6pt] &= 1.4 \times 10^{-4} + 1.96 \times 5.3 \times 10^{-5} \\[6pt] &\approx 1.4 \times 10^{-4} + 1.04 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt] &\approx 2.44 \times 10^{-4} \text{ disengagements / km}. \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Translate to "per 5,000 km"** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{\text{upper},5k} &= 2.44 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000 = 1.22 \text{ disengagements per 5,000 km}. \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verdict** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{\text{upper},5k} &= 1.22 \;\; > \;\; 1.0 \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \textbf{NOT COMPLIANT}. \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute Required Sample Size for Compliance** To bring $p_{\text{upper},5k} \le 1.0$ at 95% confidence: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{req}} &= \left(\dfrac{Z_{0.025}}{\Delta}\right)^{2} \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p}) \\[6pt] \Delta &= \dfrac{1}{5000} - 1.4 \times 10^{-4} = 6 \times 10^{-5} \\[6pt] N_{\text{req}} &= \left(\dfrac{1.96}{6 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{2} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt] &\approx (32667)^{2} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt] &\approx 1.07 \times 10^{9} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt] &\approx 1.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ km}. \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The fleet needs to drive $\approx 3\times$ more kilometres to statistically prove compliance at the 95% confidence level. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Validation Method | Validation Question | Best Loop Level | Why? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | "Does my MPC controller satisfy stability?" | **MIL** | Pure math, no hardware needed. | | "Is my generated C code functionally equivalent to the model?" | **SIL** | Detects compiler / overflow / type issues. | | "Does the ECU meet latency budgets under bus load?" | **HIL** | Real CPU + real CAN. | | "Is the lane-keep haptic feedback comfortable to humans?" | **DIL** | Subjective UX must be human-rated. | | "Does the perception stack detect a 30-cm child mannequin at 80 m?" | **ViL** | Real sensors + controlled scene. | | "Is the car safe in *unanticipated* traffic?" | **Field Test** | Only real traffic can throw unknown unknowns. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The V-Model of Validation (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Concept Phase
Item Definition] --> B[System Level
Functional Requirements] B --> C[Software and Hardware
Architectural Design] C --> D[Module Level Design] D --> E[Code Implementation] E --> F[Static Analysis
MISRA C Compliance] F --> G[Unit Testing
Module Level] G --> H[Software and Hardware
Integration Testing] H --> I[System Integration
Testing on HiL] I --> J[Vehicle Acceptance
Proving Ground Tests] J --> K[Field Validation
Public Road] E -.->|paired validation| G D -.->|paired validation| H C -.->|paired validation| I B -.->|paired validation| J A -.->|paired validation| K ``` > **Reading guide:** The downward arrows (left to right top) depict the *decomposition* of the system from concept to code. The dotted upward arrows depict the *integration & validation* steps. Each requirement on the left is *directly traceable* to a corresponding test on the right — this **traceability** is mandatory for ISO 26262 audits. ### 4.2 X-in-the-Loop Testing Pyramid (Mermaid Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph level1 [Pyramid Apex - Highest Fidelity] FT[Field Testing
Public Road] end subgraph level2 [Upper Tier] VIL[Vehicle in the Loop] DIL[Driver in the Loop] end subgraph level3 [Middle Tier - Hardware] HIL[Hardware in the Loop] end subgraph level4 [Lower Tier - Software] SIL[Software in the Loop] end subgraph level5 [Base - Mathematical Model] MIL[Model in the Loop] end MIL --> SIL --> HIL --> DIL HIL --> VIL --> FT style FT fill:#f99 style VIL fill:#fc9 style DIL fill:#fc9 style HIL fill:#ff9 style SIL fill:#9f9 style MIL fill:#9cf ``` > **Reading guide:** Each upward tier adds *fidelity* and *cost* while *shrinking* the test population you can afford to run. Modern AV programmes spend ~90% of validation effort at the MIL/SIL/HIL base. ### 4.3 HARA + ASIL Determination Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR A[Item Definition
AEB System] --> B[Situational Analysis
Driving Scenarios] B --> C[Identify Hazards
e.g. No brake on stationary child] C --> D[Assess Severity S] C --> E[Assess Exposure E] C --> F[Assess Controllability C] D --> G[Risk Graph Lookup] E --> G F --> G G --> H{ASIL Level} H --> I[QM - No Requirement] H --> J[ASIL A] H --> K[ASIL B] H --> L[ASIL C] H --> M[ASIL D] M --> N[Safety Goal
PFH less than 1e-8 per hour] style M fill:#f44 style L fill:#f84 style K fill:#fc4 style J fill:#ff4 style I fill:#cfc ``` > **Reading guide:** Every ADAS feature begins with a HARA — the output is a *Safety Goal* with a specific ASIL that *propagates* down into hardware and software requirements. ### 4.4 SOTIF Validation Loop (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR A[System Specification] --> B[Identify Triggering Conditions] B --> C[Run Scenario Catalog] C --> D[Area 1 - Known Safe] C --> E[Area 2 - Known Unsafe] C --> F[Area 3 - Unknown Unknown] D --> G[Acceptable] E --> H[Functionality Modification] H --> A F --> I[Triggering Condition Discovery] I --> B style G fill:#9f9 style H fill:#fc9 style I fill:#f99 ``` > **Reading guide:** ISO 21448 (SOTIF) divides the validation space into three regions. The goal is to **shrink Area 2 and Area 3 by improving the system** until Area 1 dominates — typically requiring $>$95% SOTIF coverage. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Distinguish between **Verification** and **Validation** in the context of autonomous driving. Why is validation considered fundamentally more difficult? **Model Answer:** > **Verification** checks that the product is *built correctly* (i.e., the implementation conforms to its specification). It is largely analytical — achieved through inspections, code reviews, unit tests, and simulation. > > **Validation** checks that the *right product* has been built — i.e., the system behaves safely under real-world conditions for the intended user. It is empirical and requires evidence of *acceptable risk* in the ODD. > > Validation is harder because the operational domain of an AV is effectively **infinite** (weather, traffic, road geometry, edge cases) and a complete enumeration is computationally and economically infeasible. Statistical proofs of safety at the 10⁹ km level require massive simulation + real-world fleets. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is **SOTIF (ISO 21448)**? How does it *complement* ISO 26262 in ADAS safety assurance? **Model Answer:** > **SOTIF — Safety Of The Intended Functionality** (ISO 21448:2022) addresses hazards arising from the *intended* operation of a system, *not* from hardware failures. Examples include a perception algorithm misclassifying a stationary white truck against a bright sky, or a planner producing a trajectory that confuses other drivers. > > ISO 26262 alone is *insufficient* for ML/AI-based ADAS because: > - Most AV failures are **systematic** (algorithmic) rather than **random** (hardware). > - SOTIF covers *triggering conditions* (weather, edge cases) that ISO 26262 was never designed for. > - SOTIF mandates a **validation cycle** that iteratively shrinks the "known unsafe" and "unknown unknown" regions until the "known safe" region dominates. > > In modern AV stacks, **ISO 26262 + ISO 21448 + ISO 21434** are applied *jointly* to cover functional safety, SOTIF, and cybersecurity respectively. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **X-in-the-Loop testing pyramid** (MIL, SIL, HIL, DIL, ViL, Field) used for ADAS validation. For each level, state *what is tested*, *one advantage*, and *one limitation*. **(b) [7 Marks]** An L2 ADAS feature (Adaptive Cruise Control) has the hazard: *"ACC may accelerate the host vehicle towards a slower lead vehicle during a cut-in scenario."* Perform a complete **HARA** and determine the **ASIL**. Justify each of S, E, C. **Model Solution:** **(a) X-in-the-Loop Pyramid:** | Level | What is Tested | Advantage | Limitation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **MIL** | Algorithm in MATLAB/Simulink | Very fast, full coverage of logic states | No real processor timing | | **SIL** | Compiled code on PC | Catches code-level bugs early | No real I/O or bus | | **HIL** | Real ECU + simulated plant | Captures CPU/bus latency | Very expensive rig | | **DIL** | Human in simulator | Captures subjective HMI comfort | Non-repeatable, subjective | | **ViL** | Real car + virtual traffic | Real sensors in controlled scenario | Requires proving ground | | **Field** | Real car in real traffic | Highest realism | Slow, unsafe to scale | (1 mark per row's *correct pairing* + 1 mark for the concluding statement on the *simulation-first* strategy.) **(b) HARA Solution:** - **Hazard:** ACC fails to decelerate during cut-in → rear-end collision with a slower lead vehicle. - **Severity S = 3**: At highway speeds (≥ 80 km/h), failure to brake against a slower lead vehicle causes a high-energy rear-end crash with significant injury probability. - **Exposure E = 4**: Cut-in events are *very common* in dense highway traffic; a typical commuter experiences multiple cut-ins per drive. - **Controllability C = 2**: An attentive driver *can* usually brake manually, but with only ~1–2 s warning, recovery is *moderately* likely. - **Risk graph lookup**: $S=3, E=4, C=2 \Rightarrow$ **ASIL C**. - (Bonus 1 mark for mentioning that the derived *Safety Goal* is: *"The ACC function shall apply sufficient deceleration to maintain a safe time gap within 300 ms of cut-in detection, with PFH $\leq 10^{-7}\,\text{h}^{-1}$."*) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **V-Model** for ADAS validation. Discuss the role of *traceability* in satisfying ISO 26262 audits. **(b) [7 Marks]** A simulated AV fleet accumulates **3 collisions in 2 × 10⁵ km** of urban driving. The regulatory target is to demonstrate, with **95% confidence**, that the *true* collision rate is below **1 per 10⁵ km**. Using the normal-approximation test, determine whether the fleet is compliant. If not, calculate the *minimum additional kilometres* required. **Model Solution:** **(a) V-Model & Traceability:** - The V-Model has a left arm (*decomposition*: Concept → System → Software/HW architecture → Module design → Code) and a right arm (*integration & validation*: Unit test → SW/HW integration → System integration on HiL → Vehicle acceptance → Field validation). - The right-arm tests are *paired* with left-arm requirements — this bidirectional pairing is called **traceability**. - For ISO 26262 audits, every ASIL-derived **Safety Goal** must be traceable *up* to a hazard, *down* to a software unit, and *out* to a passing test case. - (Award 5 marks for the V-Model explanation + 2 marks for the traceability discussion.) **(b) Statistical Compliance Test:** - Observed rate: $\hat{\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ per km. - For the normal approximation with rare events, the standard error is: $$\text{SE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{\lambda}}{N}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 8.66 \times 10^{-6}.$$ - 95% upper confidence bound: $$\lambda_{\text{upper}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} + 1.96 \times 8.66 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per km}.$$ - Translate target: $1 / 10^{5} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ per km. - **Verdict:** $3.2 \times 10^{-5} > 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ ⇒ **NOT COMPLIANT**. - Required kilometres: To achieve $\lambda_{\text{upper}} \leq 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$, the fleet must drive approximately: $$N_{\text{req}} \approx \left(\dfrac{1.96}{\Delta}\right)^{2} \hat{\lambda} \approx \left(\dfrac{1.96}{0.5 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{2} (1.5 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 2.3 \times 10^{6} \text{ km}.$$ - So $\approx 2.1 \times 10^{6}$ **additional** kilometres must be driven. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "PFH" with "failure rate per km":** PFH is *per hour*, not per km. Always convert km → hours using the expected average speed before comparing to ISO 26262 thresholds. > 2. **Forgetting the "1.96" Z-score:** KTU examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if you compute a point estimate without a confidence interval — regulatory validation is always *probabilistic*, not deterministic. > 3. **Treating ASIL D as a "more advanced" version of ASIL A:** ASIL is *not* a quality grade — it is a *process stringency* assigned by the risk graph. You cannot "upgrade" an ASIL B feature to ASIL D by adding tests. > 4. **Skipping traceability:** A V-Model answer without mentioning *traceability* between left-arm requirements and right-arm tests will lose at least 2 marks in 14-mark questions. > 5. **Confusing Verification with Validation:** Writing *"validation = running tests"* will cost you 1–2 marks. Validation is specifically about *real-world* evidence of acceptable risk. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **V&V**: Verification = "right product" / "built right"; Validation = "right product" / "built right for the user." - ✅ **The 3 Standards Triad**: ISO **26262** (Functional Safety) + ISO **21448** (SOTIF) + ISO **21434** (Cybersecurity). - ✅ **ASIL**: A, B, C, D — derived from the **risk graph** $S \times E \times C$, with D demanding PFH $\leq 10^{-8}\,\text{h}^{-1}$. - ✅ **X-in-the-Loop Pyramid** (bottom → top): MIL → SIL → HIL → DIL → ViL → Field. Each tier adds fidelity *and* cost. - ✅ **Simulation-First Strategy** is the only economically viable route to the **10⁹ km** validation target. - ✅ **TTC** is the dominant safety metric for AEB / FCW; threshold typically **< 1.5 s** for "imminent collision." - ✅ **Statistical validation** is mandatory — you need a *confidence interval*, not a point estimate. - ✅ **HARA** is the entry point of every ISO 26262 work product; **Safety Goals** propagate from it. - ✅ **SOTIF Coverage** is iterative — Areas 2 and 3 must shrink toward Area 1 until $\geq 95\%$ validated. - ✅ **Traceability** is *non-negotiable* in ISO 26262 audits — every requirement must link to a test, every test must link to a requirement. - ✅ **Disengagement rate** is *not* a safety metric (it is a *progress* metric); safety is measured in collisions per km or PFH.

topics.title: Maintenance and Troubleshooting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance and Troubleshooting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Maintenance and Troubleshooting** in the context of Autonomous Driving and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) refers to the systematic set of procedures, diagnostic protocols, calibration routines, software management practices, and fault-isolation methodologies employed to ensure that sensors, ECUs (Electronic Control Units), actuators, communication networks, and perception-planning algorithms of an autonomous vehicle operate at their specified performance, safety, and reliability levels throughout the vehicle's operational life.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this domain encompasses:

- **Preventive Maintenance (PM)**: Scheduled calibration, sensor cleaning, firmware validation, and hardware health checks.
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)**: AI/ML-driven forecasting of component degradation using telemetry, CAN bus data, and digital twins.
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM)**: Reactive repair procedures post-failure identification.
- **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)**: Real-time monitoring triggering maintenance only when KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) cross thresholds.
- **Diagnostic Troubleshooting**: Hierarchical fault-tree analysis, OBD-II/III scanning, HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) verification, and SIL (Software-in-the-Loop) regression testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Maintenance of an autonomous vehicle is fundamentally different from traditional ICE vehicles because failure of a single perception sensor (e.g., LiDAR) or an outdated HD map can degrade the entire SAE Level 3–5 driving stack without triggering an obvious mechanical symptom.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Human Pilot and Aircraft Maintenance**

Think of an autonomous vehicle as a **commercial airliner with no human pilot**:

| Autonomous Vehicle Component | Aircraft Equivalent |
|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Camera, LiDAR, Radar | Pilot's eyes, radar, altimeter |
| ECU & Domain Controller | Flight Management Computer (FMC) |
| HD Map + Localization | Air traffic control + GPS |
| Drive-by-wire actuators | Control surfaces (ailerons, flaps) |
| Diagnostic OBD port | Black box + maintenance logbook |

Just as a commercial aircraft undergoes:
- **Pre-flight checks** (analogous to **sensor calibration** before every autonomous drive),
- **Scheduled A/B/C/D checks** (analogous to **preventive maintenance intervals**),
- **Real-time engine health monitoring (EHM)** (analogous to **predictive diagnostics via CAN/Ethernet**),
- **Black box retrieval after incidents** (analogous to **post-event data logging and forensic analysis**),

…the autonomous vehicle requires a parallel, rigorous maintenance and troubleshooting regime — but at far higher complexity because the "pilot" is software, and software bugs are silent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** standards in ADAS maintenance are typically measured against **ISO 26262 (Functional Safety)**, **ISO 21448 (SOTIF – Safety of the Intended Functionality)**, **ASIL-D (Automotive Safety Integrity Level D)**, and **UNECE R157 (Automated Lane Keeping)** regulatory thresholds.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in ADAS Maintenance

The following standardized metrics define the maintenance envelope:

- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — target: **> 10,000 hours** for radar sensors.
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — target: **< 2 hours** for Level 3+ ADAS faults.
- **Availability (A)** — target: **≥ 99.99%** (the "four nines") for sensor stacks.
- **Failure Rate (λ)** — typically expressed in **FIT (Failures In Time = 10⁻⁹ failures/hour)**.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** — ISO 26262 metric: target **≥ 99%** for ASIL-D systems.
- **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** — for ASIL-D: **< 10⁻⁸ /hour**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Maintenance Architecture

The maintenance of an autonomous driving system is organized into **four hierarchical layers**, mirroring the SAE J3016 driving automation levels:

### Layer 1: Sensor & Hardware Layer (Physical Maintenance)
- Cleaning of camera lenses (LiDAR windows use anti-soiling coatings).
- Torque re-specification of mounting brackets after vibration exposure.
- Replacement of desiccant cartridges in LiDAR units to prevent internal fogging.
- Inspection of wiring harnesses for **chafing, rodent damage, and connector corrosion** (per **USCAR-2 / LV214** standards).

### Layer 2: Perception & Localization Layer (Calibration Maintenance)
- **Intrinsic calibration**: Removing lens distortion parameters (focal length $f$, principal point $c_x, c_y$, radial/tangential distortion coefficients $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$).
- **Extrinsic calibration**: Determining the 6-DoF transformation $\begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix}$ between sensors (rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ and translation vector $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$).
- **Online recalibration**: Triggered when ego-motion consistency check between IMU, wheel odometry, and GNSS exceeds **0.5° heading drift** or **0.1 m positional drift**.

### Layer 3: Software & Algorithm Layer (OTA & Software Maintenance)
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** with cryptographic signing per **UNECE R156 (Software Update Management System – SUMS)**.
- **A/B partitioning** for fail-safe rollback.
- **Differential (delta) updates** to minimize bandwidth (~95% size reduction).
- Regression testing of perception/planning stacks against **scenario-based test catalogues**.

### Layer 4: Functional Safety & Cybersecurity Layer
- Continuous monitoring per **ISO 26262 ASIL decomposition**.
- Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) on CAN/Ethernet buses.
- Fault-Tolerant Time Interval (FTTI) budgeting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Maintenance hierarchy MUST be answered in order from hardware → software. Examiners specifically test if students understand that **software updates without sensor recalibration = unsafe vehicle**.

## 2.2 Diagnostic & Troubleshooting Methodology

The troubleshooting process in autonomous vehicles follows the **ODIS (Open Diagnostic and Information System)** paradigm standardized by ISO 22900 (MVCI – Modular Vehicle Communication Interface):

1. **Fault Detection (FD)**: DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code) generation via OBD-II P0/U0/C0/B0 codes.
2. **Fault Confirmation**: Recurrence check across **N driving cycles** (typically 2–3).
3. **Fault Isolation**: Bisection algorithm narrowing fault to ECU / harness / sensor / software.
4. **Fault Rectification**: Repair, recalibration, or reflashing.
5. **Verification**: HIL/SIL test or road-test under controlled conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless; range 0–1 |
| 2 | Failure Rate (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ in **FIT** (10⁻⁹/h) |
| 3 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ | For constant failure rate |
| 4 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{detected}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | ISO 26262 metric |
| 5 | Probability of Failure per Hour | $PFH$ | ASIL-D: $< 10^{-8}$ /h |
| 6 | Pinhole Camera Model | $s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $K$ = intrinsic matrix |
| 7 | Lens Distortion | $x_{\text{distorted}} = x(1+k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + [2p_1 xy + p_2(r^2 + 2x^2)]$ | Brown-Conrady model |
| 8 | Sensor Fusion (Kalman) | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | Optimal for linear-Gaussian |
| 9 | CAN Bus Latency | $t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{(8n + 64)}{f_{\text{baud}}}$ | For standard 11-bit ID |
| 10 | Differential OTA Size | $\Delta_{OTA} = \dfrac{B_{\text{new}} - B_{\text{old}}}{B_{\text{old}}} \times 100\%$ | Typically 90–95% reduction |
| 11 | LiDAR Beam Divergence | $\theta_{\text{div}} = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi D}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $D$ = aperture |
| 12 | Radar Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $B$ = bandwidth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Caveat**: In KTU answer sheets, the formula table above is **NOT** to be reproduced verbatim. Instead, students must select the 2–3 formulas relevant to the specific question asked and explain the variables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet Operators (e.g., Waymo, Cruise, Tesla Robotaxi)**: Use PdM to schedule maintenance windows during low-demand hours, maximizing fleet availability.
- **OEMs (e.g., Mercedes DRIVE PILOT, BMW Personal Pilot L3)**: Must comply with **UNECE R157** which mandates OTA management + maintenance logs as legal proof of roadworthiness.
- **Insurance & Forensics**: Maintenance records serve as legal evidence in post-accident litigation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability and Availability of an ADAS Sensor Stack

### Problem Statement
An autonomous vehicle's perception stack contains:
- 1 forward-facing camera (MTBF = 15,000 h)
- 1 forward radar (MTBF = 20,000 h)
- 4 surround-view cameras (each MTBF = 18,000 h)
- 1 LiDAR (MTBF = 12,000 h)

The system fails (and triggers a Minimum Risk Manoeuvre – MRM) if **any one** sensor fails. Compute the system MTBF, availability (assuming MTTR = 1.5 h), and PFH.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Model the system as a series reliability block diagram.**

For a series system (failure of any component = system failure), the overall failure rate $\lambda_s$ is the sum of individual failure rates.

$$
\lambda_i = \dfrac{1}{MTBF_i}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\lambda_{\text{cam}} = \dfrac{1}{15000} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{radar}} = \dfrac{1}{20000} = 5.000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{surround}} = \dfrac{1}{18000} = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{LiDAR}} = \dfrac{1}{12000} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 2: Sum the failure rates for the four surround cameras.**

$$
\lambda_{\text{surround,total}} = 4 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-5} = 22.222 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 3: Sum all failure rates for the series system.**

$$
\lambda_s = (6.667 + 5.000 + 22.222 + 8.333) \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\lambda_s = 42.222 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

$$
\lambda_s = 4.2222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 4: Compute System MTBF.**

$$
MTBF_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{1}{4.2222 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2368 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{2368}{2368 + 1.5}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{2368}{2369.5} \approx 0.999367 \approx 99.94\%
$$

**Step 6: Compute PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour).**

For an exponential distribution:

$$
PFH \approx \lambda_s = 4.2222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 7: Compare against ASIL-D threshold.**

ASIL-D requires $PFH < 10^{-8}$ /h. Our system **fails this requirement**, indicating the perception stack needs **redundancy** (e.g., dual LiDAR, sensor diversity voting).

### Analytical Interpretation Matrix

| Metric | Computed | ASIL-D Threshold | Verdict |
|--------|----------|------------------|---------|
| MTBF | 2368 h | — | Low for series architecture |
| Availability | 99.94% | ≥ 99.99% | Marginal |
| PFH | $4.22 \times 10^{-4}$ /h | $< 10^{-8}$ /h | **NOT MET** |
| Diagnostic Coverage | Required ≥ 99% | — | Sensor health monitoring mandatory |

**Engineering Conclusion**: The series architecture must be replaced by a **2-out-of-3 redundant voting system** with **degraded mode fallback** to meet ASIL-D requirements.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pinhole Camera Intrinsic Matrix Calibration Error Propagation

### Problem Statement
A forward camera has intrinsic matrix:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

If $f_x$ drifts by $\Delta f_x$ due to temperature, derive the resulting pixel error $\Delta u$ at image coordinate $(u, v)$ for a world point at depth $Z$.

### Solution

The projection equation is:

$$
u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} + c_x
$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to $f_x$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial f_x} = \dfrac{X}{Z}
$$

Therefore, the error propagation is:

$$
\Delta u = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial f_x} \cdot \Delta f_x = \dfrac{X}{Z} \cdot \Delta f_x
$$

For a typical automotive forward camera ($f_x = 1200$ px, baseline shift $\Delta f_x = 2$ px), and an object at $X = 2$ m laterally and $Z = 50$ m depth:

$$
\Delta u = \dfrac{2}{50} \times 2 = 0.08 \text{ pixels}
$$

This 0.08-pixel drift is below detection thresholds, but accumulates over temperature cycles — necessitating **online intrinsic recalibration** every 5000 km or 6 months (whichever is earlier).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ADAS Maintenance Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle Operating Cycle Start] --> B[Sensor Health Self-Check]
    B --> C{All Sensors Healthy?}
    C -- No --> D[Trigger MRM - Minimum Risk Manoeuvre]
    D --> E[Log DTC to OBD-II Memory]
    E --> F[Display Driver Warning]
    C -- Yes --> G[Run Online Calibration Check]
    G --> H{Calibration Drift Within Tolerance?}
    H -- No --> I[Schedule Service Visit]
    I --> J[Dealer / Service Center Calibration]
    H -- Yes --> K[Activate Normal Driving Mode]
    K --> L[Continuous Telemetry Monitoring]
    L --> M{Predictive Maintenance Threshold Crossed?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Predictive Alert - Schedule Proactive Service]
    M -- No --> O[End of Cycle - Normal Shutdown]
    N --> O
    J --> O
    F --> O
```

## 4.2 Troubleshooting Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Driver Reports ADAS Malfunction] --> Q[Connect OBD-II Scanner]
    Q --> R{DTC Present?}
    R -- No --> S[Check Sensor Cleaning Status]
    S --> T[Inspect Wiring & Connectors]
    T --> U[Verify Software Version]
    R -- Yes --> V[Read DTC Code - P0/U0/C0/B0]
    V --> W{DTC Category?}
    W -- P0 --> X[Powertrain-Related Sensor Fault]
    W -- U0 --> Y[Network Communication Fault]
    W -- C0 --> Z[Chassis Sensor Fault]
    W -- B0 --> AA[Body Control Fault]
    X --> AB[Run Sensor Calibration]
    Y --> AC[Check CAN/Ethernet Bus Integrity]
    Z --> AD[Replace Chassis Sensor]
    AA --> AE[Reflash Body ECU Firmware]
    AB --> AF[Verify Fix - HIL Test]
    AC --> AF
    AD --> AF
    AE --> AF
    AF --> AG{Issue Resolved?}
    AG -- Yes --> AH[Close DTC - Document Repair]
    AG -- No --> AI[Escalate to OEM Engineering]
```

## 4.3 Sensor Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SC1[Service Center Entry] --> SC2[Vehicle on Level Surface]
    SC2 --> SC3[Calibration Target Placement - Per OEM Spec]
    SC3 --> SC4[Target Distance - Typically 1m to 5m]
    SC4 --> SC5[Launch OEM Calibration Tool]
    SC5 --> SC6{Camera Calibration}
    SC5 --> SC7{LiDAR Calibration}
    SC5 --> SC8{Radar Alignment}
    SC6 --> SC9[Compute Intrinsic K Matrix]
    SC7 --> SC10[Verify Point Cloud Density]
    SC8 --> SC11[Verify Boresight Angle ± 0.1°]
    SC9 --> SC12[Save Calibration to ECU]
    SC10 --> SC12
    SC11 --> SC12
    SC12 --> SC13[Print Calibration Certificate]
    SC13 --> SC14[Vehicle Released to Customer]
```

## 4.4 OTA Software Update Pipeline (SUMS per UNECE R156)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O1[OEM Develops Software Update] --> O2[Internal QA & Regression Testing]
    O2 --> O3[Cryptographic Signing - RSA-2048]
    O3 --> O4[Upload to OEM Cloud Server]
    O4 --> O5[Vehicle Downloads Delta Package via 4G/5G]
    O5 --> O6[Verify Digital Signature]
    O6 --> O7{Signature Valid?}
    O7 -- No --> O8[Reject Update - Log Security Event]
    O7 -- Yes --> O9[Stage in Inactive Partition]
    O9 --> O10[Pre-Install Health Verification]
    O10 --> O11{Pre-Install Checks Pass?}
    O11 -- No --> O12[Abort & Notify User]
    O11 -- Yes --> O13[Apply Update to Active Partition]
    O13 --> O14[Reboot ECU Stack]
    O14 --> O15[Post-Install Self-Test]
    O15 --> O16{Self-Test Pass?}
    O16 -- No --> O17[Rollback to Previous Partition]
    O16 -- Yes --> O18[Update Successful - Log to SUMS Registry]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR). How are they used to compute the availability of an ADAS sensor?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **MTBF (1 mark)**: Mean Time Between Failures is the expected elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system, measured in hours. For ADAS sensors like automotive radar, typical MTBF values are in the range of 10,000–25,000 hours.

- **MTTR (1 mark)**: Mean Time To Repair is the average time required to diagnose, repair, and restore a failed component to operational status. For ADAS sensor replacement in service centers, typical MTTR is 0.5–2 hours.

- **Availability Formula (1 mark)**:
$$
A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$
Availability represents the fraction of operational time over total time. For an autonomous vehicle fleet, the target availability must be ≥ 99.99% to comply with Level 3+ operational safety requirements.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is Over-The-Air (OTA) update, and why is cryptographic signing critical for ADAS software updates?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **OTA Definition (1 mark)**: OTA refers to the wireless delivery and installation of firmware/software updates to vehicle ECUs via cellular (4G/5G) or Wi-Fi networks, eliminating the need for physical service-center visits.

- **Cryptographic Signing – Purpose (1 mark)**: Cryptographic signing (typically using RSA-2048 or ECDSA-P256) ensures that the update package is **authentic** (originated by the legitimate OEM) and **unaltered** (no tampering during transit).

- **Criticality (1 mark)**: Per **UNECE R156 (SUMS – Software Update Management System)**, every OTA update must be digitally signed. Without this, an attacker could inject malicious code that alters perception/planning logic, leading to catastrophic safety failures. The signature verification occurs **before** any data is written to flash memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical maintenance architecture of an autonomous driving system. Discuss the role of preventive, predictive, and corrective maintenance in ensuring ASIL-D compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forward radar sensor has MTBF = 25,000 h and MTTR = 2 h. If the diagnostic coverage is 95% and the system must achieve ASIL-D (PFH < 10⁻⁸/h), determine whether the radar alone meets the requirement. If not, suggest a redundancy strategy. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchical Maintenance Architecture (7 Marks):**

The autonomous driving maintenance architecture is structured in **four hierarchical layers**:

1. **Sensor & Hardware Layer (1.5 Marks)**: Includes physical inspection of camera lenses, LiDAR windows, radar radomes, ultrasonic transducers, and wiring harnesses. Maintenance tasks include cleaning, torque re-verification, and replacement of desiccants. Compliance with **USCAR-2** and **LV214** connector specifications is mandatory.

2. **Perception & Localization Layer (1.5 Marks)**: Involves intrinsic and extrinsic calibration of sensors. Intrinsic calibration corrects lens distortion using the Brown-Conrady model, while extrinsic calibration determines the 6-DoF pose between sensors via checkerboard targets or AprilTag fiducials.

3. **Software & Algorithm Layer (2 Marks)**: Encompasses OTA updates, A/B partitioning for fail-safe rollback, and regression testing against scenario catalogues. Compliance with **UNECE R156 (SUMS)** is legally mandated.

4. **Functional Safety & Cybersecurity Layer (2 Marks)**: Continuous monitoring per **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** with intrusion detection on CAN/Ethernet buses, FTTI budgeting, and fail-operational fallback strategies.

**Preventive Maintenance (PM)** schedules routine calibration (every 5,000 km or 6 months). **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** uses ML models trained on telemetry to forecast failures 50–200 hours in advance. **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** is reactive repair post-failure. Together, these three strategies minimize PFH below the ASIL-D threshold of $10^{-8}$/h.

**(b) Radar Reliability Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- MTBF = 25,000 h
- MTTR = 2 h
- Diagnostic Coverage (DC) = 95% = 0.95

**Step 1**: Compute the failure rate.

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{25000} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute availability.

$$
A = \dfrac{25000}{25000 + 2} = \dfrac{25000}{25002} = 0.99992 = 99.992\%
$$

**Step 3**: Compute undetected failure rate (relevant to PFH).

$$
\lambda_{\text{undetected}} = (1 - DC) \times \lambda = 0.05 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 4**: Compare against ASIL-D threshold.

The total failure rate $\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ /h is **far above** the ASIL-D PFH threshold of $10^{-8}$/h. Therefore, **the radar alone does NOT meet ASIL-D requirements.**

**Step 5: Redundancy Strategy (Suggested)**:

Deploy a **dual-redundant radar configuration** with cross-monitoring. For a parallel redundant system with identical radars:

$$
\lambda_{\text{system}} = \lambda_1 \times \lambda_2 \times T_{\text{common-mode}} \approx (4 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times t
$$

For $t = 1$ hour mission time:

$$
\lambda_{\text{system}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ /h}
$$

This is **closer to but still above** the ASIL-D threshold. Therefore, **triple-modular redundancy (TMR)** with majority voting is recommended:

$$
\lambda_{\text{TMR}} \approx \lambda^3 \times t^2 \approx (4 \times 10^{-5})^3 \times 1 \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ /h}
$$

This comfortably meets ASIL-D requirements, but adds cost and computational complexity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the fault diagnostic procedure for an autonomous vehicle using the OBD-II system. Discuss DTC code categories (P0, U0, C0, B0) with examples specific to ADAS sensors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the complete OTA update pipeline as mandated by UNECE R156 (SUMS). Why is A/B partitioning essential, and how does the rollback mechanism work in case of an update failure? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) OBD-II Diagnostic Procedure (7 Marks):**

The **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, Generation 2)** system is a standardized self-diagnostic and reporting capability mandated across all vehicles sold in markets adhering to **CARB (California Air Resources Board)** regulations.

**Procedure (5 Steps):**

1. **Connect OBD-II Scanner** (1 Mark): The scanner plugs into the standardized **J1962 connector** (16-pin, typically under the dashboard). It communicates with ECUs using **ISO 15765 (CAN)**, **ISO 14230 (KWP2000)**, or **ISO 9141** protocols.

2. **Read DTCs** (1 Mark): Diagnostic Trouble Codes are categorized:
   - **P0xxx (Powertrain)** — e.g., **P0A1F**: Battery Energy Control Module malfunction.
   - **U0xxx (Network/Communication)** — e.g., **U0100**: Lost Communication with ECM/PCM; **U0126**: Lost Communication with Steering Angle Sensor Module (critical for Lane Keep Assist).
   - **C0xxx (Chassis)** — e.g., **C0040**: Right Front Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit malfunction.
   - **B0xxx (Body)** — e.g., **B1000**: ECU Internal Failure.

3. **Interpret DTCs** (1 Mark): Cross-reference with OEM service manual to identify root cause.

4. **Isolate Fault** (1.5 Marks): Use bisection methodology — disconnect suspected sensor, check wiring continuity, measure supply voltage (typically 12 V or 5 V).

5. **Repair & Clear DTC** (1.5 Marks): Replace faulty component, run verification drive cycle (typically 2–3 cycles), then clear DTC using scanner.

**ADAS-Specific DTC Examples**: Radar sensor misalignment → **C1AC1** (OEM-specific); Camera module failure → **B1318**; LiDAR communication loss → **U0235**.

**(b) OTA Update Pipeline per UNECE R156 (7 Marks):**

**UNECE R156** mandates that all software updates affecting vehicle safety, emissions, or type-approval must be managed through a **Software Update Management System (SUMS)** certified by the regulatory authority.

**Pipeline Stages:**

1. **OEM-Side Development & Testing** (1 Mark): Update is developed, undergoes HIL/SIL regression testing, and is signed using **RSA-2048 / ECDSA-P256**.

2. **Cloud Distribution** (1 Mark): Signed delta package uploaded to OEM's secure cloud server (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure).

3. **Vehicle Download** (1 Mark): Vehicle downloads package via 4G/5G/Wi-Fi during ignition-off or charging phase (for EVs).

4. **Cryptographic Verification** (1 Mark): ECU verifies signature against OEM's public key stored in Hardware Security Module (HSM). Invalid signature → **immediate rejection**.

5. **Staging on Inactive Partition** (1 Mark): Update is written to the inactive A/B partition, leaving the active partition untouched until verification succeeds.

**A/B Partitioning Rationale**: If the new software fails self-test, the ECU can **boot from the previous partition** within milliseconds, ensuring the vehicle is never bricked.

**Rollback Mechanism**: After update installation and reboot, the ECU runs **post-install self-tests**. If failures are detected (e.g., perception module fails to initialize), the bootloader automatically switches to the previous partition and logs the failure to the OEM's SUMS registry. The driver is notified via the HMI to schedule a service appointment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1**: Writing MTBF and MTTR formulas without explaining the **units** → loses 0.5 mark per formula.
> - **Mistake 2**: Confusing **ASIL-D PFH threshold ($10^{-8}$/h)** with **SIL (Industrial Safety Integrity Level)** — these are **different standards**.
> - **Mistake 3**: Forgetting to mention **cryptographic signing** in OTA questions — this is mandatory in any 14-mark OTA answer.
> - **Mistake 4**: Listing DTC categories (P0/U0/C0/B0) without giving an **ADAS-specific example** for each — examiners expect autonomous-vehicle context.
> - **Mistake 5**: Skipping the **rollback mechanism** in OTA answers — this is a high-weight sub-part.
> - **Mistake 6**: Not drawing **block diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions — KTU marking scheme typically allocates 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintenance in autonomous vehicles** is **not just mechanical** — it spans sensor calibration, software updates, and cybersecurity monitoring.
- **MTBF / MTTR / Availability** are foundational reliability metrics. Availability $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ must be ≥ **99.99%** for Level 3+ autonomy.
- **PFH threshold for ASIL-D is $10^{-8}$/h** — single sensors rarely meet this; **redundancy (TMR, dual-radar)** is essential.
- **DTC codes** follow ISO 15031-6: P0 (Powertrain), U0 (Network), C0 (Chassis), B0 (Body). ADAS faults typically appear under **U0** (communication loss) or **C0** (sensor).
- **OTA updates** must comply with **UNECE R156 (SUMS)** — cryptographic signing, A/B partitioning, rollback, and SUMS registry logging are all mandatory.
- **Sensor calibration** = intrinsic (lens distortion per Brown-Conrady model) + extrinsic (6-DoF $R, t$ between sensors). Required every **5,000 km or 6 months**.
- **LiDAR** uses anti-soiling coatings + desiccant cartridges to prevent internal fogging.
- **Radar range resolution**: $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ — requires wide bandwidth FMCW chirps.
- **Camera projection**: $s[u, v, 1]^T = K [R \mid t] [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$ — drift in $f_x$ causes pixel error $\Delta u = (X/Z) \cdot \Delta f_x$.
- **Predictive maintenance** uses ML on telemetry to forecast failures 50–200 hours in advance — reduces fleet downtime by 30–40%.
- **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop)** and **SIL (Software-in-the-Loop)** testing are mandatory verification tools after any software update.
- **Cybersecurity**: CAN bus intrusion detection is now an **OTA-maintained subsystem**, not a one-time install.
- **Common student errors**: confusing ASIL with SIL, omitting units in formulas, forgetting rollback mechanism, and writing generic ICE-vehicle maintenance procedures instead of ADAS-specific ones.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Issues in ADAS and Autonomous Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common Issues in ADAS and Autonomous Systems

# Common Issues in ADAS and Autonomous Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Issues in ADAS and Autonomous Systems** refer to the systematic set of technical, environmental, ethical, legal, and human-factor challenges that degrade the reliability, safety, and societal acceptance of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and fully Autonomous Vehicles (AVs). These issues span the perception–planning–action pipeline and arise from sensor limitations, algorithmic edge cases, adversarial conditions, cybersecurity threats, regulatory ambiguity, and the unresolved **handover problem** between machine and human control.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these issues are categorized under **Module 4 (Autonomous Driving Challenges and Future Directions)**, mapping directly to:

| Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|----------------|---------------------------|
| **CO4** – Analyze failure modes, ethical dilemmas, and societal impacts | Analyze / Evaluate |
| **CO5** – Evaluate real-world deployment readiness | Evaluate |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "New Driver" Metaphor**
> Think of an autonomous vehicle as a *newly licensed teenage driver*. It performs brilliantly on sunny highways (its comfort zone), but the moment it encounters fog, an unsignalized intersection, a child chasing a ball, or a construction zone with faded lane markings — situations called **edge cases** or **corner cases** — it hesitates, misjudges, or fails outright. The "common issues" are exactly the catalogue of scenarios, sensor blind spots, ethical puzzles, and legal grey zones that distinguish a *demo* from a *product*.

A second analogy: ADAS is a **chain of sensors and algorithms**. As the old engineering adage goes, *"a chain is only as strong as its weakest link."* In AVs, the perception link (camera in glare), the localization link (GPS in a tunnel), the planning link (unwritten traffic rules), and the actuation link (brake latency) all represent potential failure points.

---

### 1.3 The Eight Pillars of Common Issues

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus**: Examiners consistently frame Module 4 questions around these eight pillars. Memorize the taxonomy.

1. **Sensor-level issues** — noise, occlusion, weather degradation, range limits
2. **Perception & AI issues** — edge cases, dataset bias, adversarial attacks
3. **Localization & Mapping issues** — GPS dropouts, HD map staleness, dynamic obstacles
4. **Decision-making & Planning issues** — ethical dilemmas, multi-agent prediction
5. **V2X Communication issues** — latency, packet loss, security
6. **Cybersecurity issues** — spoofing, ransomware, CAN bus injection
7. **Human factors** — handover, over-trust, mode confusion
8. **Legal, ethical & regulatory issues** — liability, the Trolley Problem, homologation

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Reliability Metric (Bolded Constant)**: The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) defines **ASIL-D (Automotive Safety Integrity Level D)** as the highest safety classification, demanding a **probabilistic metric of random hardware failure ≤ 10⁻⁸ per hour** (i.e., less than one failure in 100 million operating hours). ADAS components targeting autonomous emergency braking or steering must comply.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor-Level Issues — The "Degraded Perception" Problem

Autonomous stacks rely on a **sensor suite**: cameras, LiDAR, radar, ultrasonic, and GNSS/IMU. Each has a unique degradation profile.

| Sensor | Strength | Common Failure Mode | Environmental Trigger |
|--------|----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Camera** | Color, semantic context | Glare, low light, occlusion | Sun, night, fog, snow |
| **LiDAR** | Precise 3D geometry | Range drop, point cloud sparsity | Heavy rain, snow, dust |
| **Radar** | Velocity, weather-robust | Low angular resolution, ghost targets | Multi-path reflections |
| **Ultrasonic** | Close-range parking | Range cap (~5 m), slow update | High-speed scenarios |
| **GNSS/IMU** | Global position | Multi-path, tunnel outage | Urban canyon, tunnels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sensor-Independence Rule**: To claim **SAE Level 3+** autonomy, no two sensors may share the same physical failure mode. This is why modern AVs use *complementary* (not *redundant*) sensing — camera + radar + LiDAR each fail differently.

### 2.2 Reliability Mathematics

The availability (uptime fraction) of a multi-sensor perception stack is governed by **parallel reliability theory**:

$$
R_{\text{system}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]
$$

where $R_i(t)$ is the reliability of the $i$-th sensor at time $t$, and $N$ is the number of *parallel* (independent) sensing channels.

For components with **constant hazard rate** $\lambda_i$ (Exponential failure distribution):

$$
R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}
$$

The **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** is:

$$
\text{MTBF}_i = \frac{1}{\lambda_i}
$$

The **system-level MTBF** for parallel sensors becomes:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \int_0^{\infty} \left[1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - e^{-\lambda_i t})\right] dt
$$

---

## 2.3 Perception & AI Issues — Edge Cases and the Long Tail

> [!NOTE]
> The single largest unsolved problem in autonomous driving is the **"long tail"** of rare events. Waymo's CEO has publicly stated that 99% of the perception problem is solved; the remaining 1% contains millions of edge cases.

**Key sub-issues:**

- **Dataset bias** — Training data over-represents highways and under-represents construction zones in developing countries.
- **Adversarial perturbations** — Specially crafted stickers on stop signs can fool CNN classifiers (Eykholt et al., 2018).
- **Domain shift** — A model trained in California may fail in Kerala monsoons.
- **Open-set recognition** — The vehicle encounters object classes never seen in training.

---

## 2.4 Localization & HD Map Issues

GNSS position accuracy degrades from **±2 m (open sky)** to **±50 m or complete outage (urban canyon)**. This forces reliance on **sensor-based localization** using LiDAR scan-matching or visual odometry, but these methods drift:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{drift}}(t) = \mathbf{p}_{\text{true}} + \int_0^{t} \mathbf{b}_{\text{IMU}}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

where $\mathbf{b}_{\text{IMU}}$ is the time-varying IMU bias vector. **HD map staleness** is the second killer: a roadworks site may render a 6-month-old map obsolete overnight.

---

## 2.5 Cybersecurity Attack Surfaces

The attack surface of an AV spans **>100 ECUs** connected via **CAN bus**, **Ethernet (100BASE-T1)**, **Wi-Fi (V2X)**, **Bluetooth**, and **cellular (C2C)**. Common attacks:

| Attack Type | Vector | Consequence |
|-------------|--------|-------------|
| **GPS Spoofing** | Fake satellite signals | Vehicle redirected to wrong lane |
| **LiDAR Spoofing** | IR laser replay | Phantom obstacles → emergency brake |
| **CAN Injection** | OBD-II port | Unauthorised brake/steer commands |
| **Ransomware** | Infotainment | Fleet-wide immobilisation |
| **Adversarial Vision** | Printed patches | Stop sign misread as speed-limit sign |

---

## 2.6 Ethical & Legal Dilemmas — The Trolley Problem

The classic thought experiment applied to AVs: when a collision is unavoidable, **whom should the car prioritize?** KTU examiners frequently use the Moral Machine experiment (MIT, 2018) as a reference. Legal frameworks such as the **UN Regulation No. 157 (ALKS)** and **India's Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act 2019** remain ambiguous on liability.

---

## 2.7 Human Factors — The Handover Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **handover problem** is the single biggest obstacle to **Level 3** autonomy. A human driver, disengaged for 8 minutes, requires **5–40 seconds** to regain situational awareness — longer than many accident timelines.

The takeover request (TOR) latency budget is governed by:

$$
t_{\text{reaction}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{move}} + t_{\text{cognitive}}
$$

where each term is bounded. Studies (Mercedes, 2019) showed that drivers in **Level 3** systems **failed to re-engage safely** in real traffic, leading Mercedes to **abandon Level 3** development.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas Table** — Memorize for the 14-mark numerical/analytical question.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Exponential reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 2 | Parallel system reliability | $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i)$ | Bounded $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | Series system reliability | $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod_i R_i$ | Bounded $[0,1]$ |
| 4 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Hours |
| 5 | Failure-in-time | $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9}$ | Failures per $10^{9}$ device-hours |
| 6 | ASIL-D target | $\text{PMHF} \le 10^{-8}$ | Per hour |
| 7 | LiDAR range equation (peak) | $P_R = \frac{P_T \, G_T \, G_R \, \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Watts (R in metres) |
| 8 | SNR of a camera sensor | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 9 | Braking distance (kinematic) | $d_b = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | Metres |
| 10 | Reaction time budget | $t_{\text{react}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{move}} + t_{\text{cog}}$ | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: These formulas underpin ISO 26262 functional safety certification, ADAS liability litigation, sensor procurement decisions, and insurance risk modelling.
