# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parallel System Reliability of a 3-Sensor Stack

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A Level-3 autonomous vehicle uses three independent perception channels — Camera ($R_C$), LiDAR ($R_L$), and Radar ($R_R$) — connected in parallel. Derive the system reliability expression and compute it for $\lambda_C = 5 \times 10^{-5}/\text{h}$, $\lambda_L = 8 \times 10^{-5}/\text{h}$, $\lambda_R = 3 \times 10^{-5}/\text{h}$, at $t = 10{,}000$ hours.

### Step 1 — Write the parallel reliability formula

For $N$ independent components in parallel, system *survival* occurs if **at least one** component works. Thus:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[1 - R_i(t)\bigr]
$$

For three channels ($N = 3$):

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 1 - \bigl[1 - R_C(t)\bigr]\bigl[1 - R_L(t)\bigr]\bigl[1 - R_R(t)\bigr]
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the product

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 1 - \bigl[1 - R_C - R_L - R_R + R_C R_L + R_C R_R + R_L R_R - R_C R_L R_R\bigr]
$$

Simplifying:

$$
R_{\text{sys}}(t) = R_C + R_L + R_R - R_C R_L - R_C R_R - R_L R_R + R_C R_L R_R
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the exponential reliability model

For each sensor with constant hazard rate $\lambda_i$:

$$
R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}
$$

Hence:

$$
R_C = e^{-5\times10^{-5} \times 10{,}000} = e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
R_L = e^{-8\times10^{-5} \times 10{,}000} = e^{-0.8}
$$

$$
R_R = e^{-3\times10^{-5} \times 10{,}000} = e^{-0.3}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerically evaluate the exponentials

$$
e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

$$
e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493
$$

$$
e^{-0.3} \approx 0.7408
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the parallel reliability expression

$$
R_{\text{sys}} = 0.6065 + 0.4493 + 0.7408 - (0.6065)(0.4493) - (0.6065)(0.7408) - (0.4493)(0.7408) + (0.6065)(0.4493)(0.7408)
$$

Compute each product term-by-term:

$$
(0.6065)(0.4493) = 0.2725
$$

$$
(0.6065)(0.7408) = 0.4493
$$

$$
(0.4493)(0.7408) = 0.3328
$$

$$
(0.6065)(0.4493)(0.7408) = 0.2019
$$

### Step 6 — Final substitution and sum

$$
R_{\text{sys}} = (0.6065 + 0.4493 + 0.7408) - (0.2725 + 0.4493 + 0.3328) + 0.2019
$$

$$
R_{\text{sys}} = 1.7966 - 1.0546 + 0.2019
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{sys}} \approx 0.9439}
$$

### Step 7 — Engineering interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The combined 3-sensor stack has a **94.39%** probability of surviving 10,000 hours without total perception failure — a substantial improvement over the weakest single sensor (LiDAR at 44.93%). This justifies the industry-standard *sensor fusion* approach.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Distance under Sensor Latency

**Problem:** An AV travels at $v = 90$ km/h. The perception-to-actuation pipeline introduces a latency of $t_l = 200$ ms. Friction coefficient $\mu = 0.7$, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute total stopping distance.

### Step 1 — Convert units

$$
v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Distance covered during latency (thinking distance)

$$
d_{\text{latency}} = v \cdot t_l = 25 \times 0.2 = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Braking distance using kinematic equation

$$
d_b = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}
$$

$$
d_b = \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \frac{625}{13.734}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_b \approx 45.51 \text{ m}}
$$

### Step 4 — Total stopping distance

$$
d_{\text{total}} = d_{\text{latency}} + d_b = 5.0 + 45.51 = 50.51 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 5 — Critical insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **latency term alone adds 5 m** — equivalent to a 1.4-car-length margin. Reducing $t_l$ from 200 ms to 50 ms recovers 3.75 m, often the difference between a near-miss and a collision at highway speeds.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answers provided as per KTU valuation key pattern — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for significance.**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the "Long Tail" problem in autonomous driving perception. Why is it considered the central unsolved issue of Level-4 autonomy?**

**Model Answer:**
The long-tail problem refers to the **infinite variety of rare, unusual, or never-before-seen scenarios** (e.g., a kangaroo crossing in Australia, a handcart loaded with hay, or a three-wheeler carrying an oversized load) that an AV must handle but which constitute a small fraction of training data. Each rare event may individually have a probability below $10^{-6}$, yet the **cumulative probability of encountering *some* edge case per kilometre is high**. It is central to Level-4 autonomy because Level-4 demands *no human fallback* — even a single unhandled case per billion kilometres is unacceptable to regulators.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 example + 1 Level-4 reasoning)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the "handover problem" in SAE Level-3 autonomous vehicles? Why did Mercedes-Benz abandon Level-3 development?**

**Model Answer:**
The handover problem is the difficulty of **safely transferring control from the autonomous system back to a human driver** during a critical event. After extended disengagement, a human requires **5–40 seconds** to regain situational awareness — longer than the **3-second** accident avoidance window at highway speeds. Mercedes-Benz abandoned Level-3 (Drive Pilot in Germany, 2019) because real-world trials showed drivers **failed to re-engage safely** during traffic-jam pilot scenarios, exposing the manufacturer to unacceptable liability under UN R157.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 numerical latency + 1 commercial consequence)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Both Question A and Question B must be fully solved mentally — only one is to be written in the exam.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** *With neat block diagrams, classify and explain the **eight major categories of common issues** encountered in ADAS and autonomous vehicles. For each category, provide **one real-world example** and the **corresponding SAE level at which the issue becomes critical**.  **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

The eight categories of common issues in ADAS/AV systems, as aligned with the KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus, are:

1. **Sensor-Level Issues** — Cameras fail in glare/fog; LiDAR range drops in heavy rain; radar suffers multi-path ghost targets in tunnels. *Example:* Tesla Model S (2016) failed to engage brakes against a white truck against bright sky — *camera failed to discriminate*. *Critical from SAE L2 onwards.*

2. **Perception & AI Issues** — Edge cases (a child wearing a Halloween costume resembling a green light), adversarial patches, dataset bias, open-set recognition. *Example:* Adversarial sticker on a stop sign (Eykholt et al., 2018). *Critical from L3 onwards.*

3. **Localization & HD-Map Issues** — GPS outage in tunnels, IMU drift over time, HD-map staleness during roadworks. *Example:* Uber ATG fatality (Tempe, 2018) involved imperfect HD-map state classification. *Critical from L3 onwards.*

4. **Decision & Planning Issues** — Trolley dilemma, multi-agent interaction prediction, unwritten social rules (e.g., waving a pedestrian through). *Critical from L4 onwards.*

5. **V2X Communication Issues** — Latency (>10 ms degrades platooning), packet loss, message authentication. *Example:* ETSI ITS-G5 packet collisions. *Critical for cooperative L4.*

6. **Cybersecurity Issues** — GPS spoofing, CAN-bus injection, OTA ransomware. *Example:* Jeep Cherokee hack (Miller & Valasek, 2015) — remote control of steering and brakes. *Critical from L3 onwards.*

7. **Human-Factor Issues** — Handover delay, over-trust (drivers napping in L2), mode confusion. *Example:* Tesla Autopilot fatal crashes (2016, 2018, 2019) involved driver over-trust. *Critical for L3.*

8. **Legal & Ethical Issues** — Liability allocation between driver, manufacturer, software vendor; compliance with UN R157 (ALKS), ISO 26262, ISO 21448 (SOTIF). *Critical from L3 onwards.*

*(7 marks: 1 mark per category × 6 + 1 mark for the consolidated diagram explanation)*

---

**(b)** *A perception stack has **three independent sensors** with hazard rates $\lambda_1 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$/h, $\lambda_2 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$/h, $\lambda_3 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$/h. Compute the **system reliability at t = 5000 hours** assuming parallel redundancy. Compare with the **single most reliable sensor alone**.  **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Single-sensor reliabilities (Exponential model):**

$$
R_1 = e^{-\lambda_1 t} = e^{-2 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000} = e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048
$$

$$
R_2 = e^{-4 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

$$
R_3 = e^{-6 \times 10^{-5} \times 5000} = e^{-0.3} \approx 0.7408
$$

**Step 2 — Parallel system reliability formula:**

$$
R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)(1 - R_3)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each complementary term:**

$$
(1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)(1 - R_3) = (0.0952)(0.1813)(0.2592)
$$

$$
= 0.0952 \times 0.1813 = 0.01726
$$

$$
= 0.01726 \times 0.2592 = 0.004474
$$

**Step 4 — System reliability:**

$$
R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 0.004474 = 0.9955
$$

**Step 5 — Best single sensor:**

$$
R_{\text{best single}} = 0.9048
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and inference:**

$$
\Delta R = 0.9955 - 0.9048 = 0.0907 \;(\text{9.07% improvement})
$$

The parallel triple-sensor stack provides **99.55% reliability** versus **90.48%** for the best individual sensor — a **9-fold reduction** in failure probability. This is the engineering justification for **sensor fusion redundancy** in ASIL-D systems.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for formula + 2 marks for individual reliability + 2 marks for parallel computation + 1 mark comparison + 1 mark engineering interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** *Explain the **cybersecurity attack surface** of an autonomous vehicle with a layered block diagram. Discuss in detail **(i) GPS spoofing, (ii) LiDAR spoofing, and (iii) CAN-bus injection attacks** — their mechanisms, consequences, and mitigation strategies.  **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Layered Attack-Surface Diagram (textual block diagram):**

$$
\text{AV Attack Surface} = \begin{cases}
\text{Perception Layer: Camera, LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic} \\
\text{Communication Layer: V2X (DSRC/C-V2X), Wi-Fi, BT, Cellular} \\
\text{Control Layer: CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, Automotive Ethernet} \\
\text{Backend Layer: OTA servers, Cloud APIs, Fleet services}
\end{cases}
$$

**(i) GPS Spoofing (Communication Layer)**

- **Mechanism**: An attacker broadcasts counterfeit GNSS signals at higher power than the legitimate satellites, causing the receiver's tracking loops to lock onto the fake signal.
- **Consequence**: The vehicle's reported position drifts by **hundreds of metres** within seconds; the planning module believes the vehicle is on a parallel road or off-route, triggering unsafe rerouting.
- **Mitigation**: Multi-constellation GNSS (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + IRNSS), IMU dead-reckoning bridging, **Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring (RAIM)**, and cross-checking position against LiDAR-derived landmarks.

**(ii) LiDAR Spoofing (Perception Layer)**

- **Mechanism**: A pulsed infrared laser (e.g., 905 nm, matching the LiDAR wavelength) is fired into the LiDAR receiver, mimicking the return of a phantom obstacle at a controlled distance.
- **Consequence**: The perception stack detects a "ghost" obstacle and the planner triggers an emergency brake — a **denial-of-service attack** in the physical world.
- **Mitigation**: Encoded pulse patterns, time-of-flight consistency checks, multi-echo analysis, and sensor fusion with radar (which is immune to optical spoofing).

**(iii) CAN-Bus Injection (Control Layer)**

- **Mechanism**: The Controller Area Network (CAN) lacks authentication; any node can transmit arbitration-winning frames. Attackers connect via the **OBD-II port** (Miller & Valasek, 2015 Jeep hack) or via compromised telematics units and inject brake or steering messages.
- **Consequence**: Unauthorised activation of brakes, steering, throttle, or gear shifts at highway speeds — a direct threat to life.
- **Mitigation**: CAN authentication (e.g., AUTOSAR SecOC, message authentication codes), gateway ECUs with intrusion detection, network segmentation, and secure boot.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for diagram + 2 marks per attack (mechanism + consequence + mitigation))*

---

**(b)** *An autonomous vehicle travels at **$v = 108$ km/h** on a highway. The perception-to-brake pipeline has a latency of **$t_l = 250$ ms**. The road is wet with friction coefficient **$\mu = 0.5$**. Compute:*
- *(i) the distance travelled during the latency (thinking distance),*
- *(ii) the braking distance,*
- *(iii) the total stopping distance,*
- *(iv) the percentage contribution of the latency term to total distance.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit conversion:**

$$
v = 108 \text{ km/h} = 108 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 30 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Latency distance:**

$$
d_{\text{latency}} = v \cdot t_l = 30 \times 0.25 = 7.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Braking distance:**

$$
d_b = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g} = \frac{(30)^2}{2 \times 0.5 \times 9.81} = \frac{900}{9.81}
$$

$$
d_b \approx 91.74 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Total stopping distance:**

$$
d_{\text{total}} = d_{\text{latency}} + d_b = 7.5 + 91.74 = 99.24 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Latency percentage contribution:**

$$
\% \text{ latency} = \frac{7.5}{99.24} \times 100 \approx 7.56\%
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering inference:**

> [!NOTE]
> Although the latency term contributes only **7.56%** of total distance, the absolute **7.5 m** margin is critical. Reducing $t_l$ from 250 ms to 50 ms (a 5× speed-up) recovers **6 m** — enough to prevent a collision in many AEB scenarios. This is why AV stacks target **end-to-end latencies below 100 ms** for highway AEB.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for steps 1–5 + 1 mark engineering comment + 0.5 mark rounding discipline)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up Series vs Parallel reliability** — Students frequently use the wrong formula. **Memory aid**: Parallel = "any one works" → product of failures subtracted from 1; Series = "all must work" → product of reliabilities.
> 2. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF** — MTBF includes repair time, MTTF is for non-repairable components. ECUs are repairable (MTBF); sensors are typically non-repairable (MTTF).
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** — km/h to m/s is mandatory in braking-distance problems. *Always* show the conversion step.
> 4. **Mis-stating the handover time** — It is **5–40 seconds**, not "a few seconds." This range is examined.
> 5. **Omitting the engineering interpretation** — KTU's 2024 Scheme rewards **"application-level"** answers. Always close with a one-line engineering significance.
> 6. **Vague ethical answers** — Don't just say "it depends"; reference **UN R157**, **Moral Machine**, or **ISO 21448 SOTIF**.
> 7. **Missing cybersecurity specifics** — Name the attack (e.g., "GPS spoofing"), the vector (e.g., "OBD-II port"), and the countermeasure (e.g., "SecOC authentication").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Common Issues**

- [x] **Eight pillars of issues**: Sensor, Perception/AI, Localization, Planning, V2X, Cybersecurity, Human Factors, Legal/Ethical.
- [x] **Long-tail problem** = the infinite rare-event distribution; central to L4 challenge.
- [x] **Handover problem**: 5–40 s human re-engagement > 3 s accident window → Level-3 showstopper.
- [x] **Mercedes Drive Pilot** abandoned Level-3 (2019) due to handover failures.
- [x] **Sensor-independence rule**: complementary sensing is mandatory (camera ≠ LiDAR ≠ radar failure modes).
- [x] **Parallel reliability**: $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - R_i)$; exponential $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$.
- [x] **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$; **ASIL-D PMHF target** $\le 10^{-8}$/hour.
- [x] **Stopping distance** = $d_{\text{latency}} + v^2/(2\mu g)$ — latency term is small % but critical absolute metres.
- [x] **Cybersecurity triad**: GPS spoofing, LiDAR spoofing, CAN injection → mitigate via RAIM, pulse-coding, SecOC.
- [x] **Regulatory anchors**: UN R157 (ALKS), ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO 21448 (SOTIF), India's MV Act 2019.
- [x] **Trolley problem + Moral Machine** (MIT 2018) is the canonical ethics reference.
- [x] **Tesla Autopilot crashes (2016, 2018, 2019)** and **Uber ATG fatality (Tempe 2018)** are board-favourite real-world citations.
- [x] **HD-map staleness** + **GNSS urban canyon** are the dual localization killers.
- [x] **Mode confusion** and **over-trust** are the dominant human-factor failures of L2 systems.
- [x] Always close numerical answers with an **engineering interpretation sentence** — this is where the **last 1 mark** lives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagnostic Tools and Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diagnostic Tools and Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive Diagnostics** in the context of **Autonomous Driving and ADAS** refers to the systematic set of on-board and off-board **electronic procedures, hardware tools, and software applications** used to detect, isolate, identify, record, and rectify faults within the Electronic Control Units (ECUs) of a vehicle. These tools enable technicians and engineers to communicate with the in-vehicle network, read fault memory, view live sensor data, perform actuator tests, and re-flash ECUs.

The governing international standards include:
- **ISO 15031** — Road vehicles — Communication between vehicle and external equipment for emissions-related diagnostics (OBD-II).
- **ISO 14229** — Unified Diagnostic Services (UDS) — application layer diagnostic protocol.
- **ISO 15765** — Diagnostics over Controller Area Network (DoCAN).
- **SAE J1962** — Diagnostic Connector (the standard 16-pin OBD-II J1962 trapezoidal connector).
- **SAE J1939** — Recommended practice for serial control and communications heavy duty vehicle network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, Generation II)** is a **legally mandated, standardized self-diagnostic capability** in all petrol cars sold in India, the EU, and the US since **2001 (petrol)** and **2004 (diesel)**. It is the *minimum* diagnostic interface an automotive engineer must master before exploring UDS or OEM-specific systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car as a Patient

Imagine taking your vehicle to a workshop. The mechanic is not a "car doctor" by metaphor alone — **diagnostics is literally medicine applied to vehicles**:

| Human Medicine Analogy | Automotive Diagnostics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient walking in with a fever | Vehicle arrives with a MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) glowing |
| Doctor asks "where does it hurt?" | Scan tool reads **DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes)** from ECU memory |
| Doctor checks pulse, BP, blood report | Live data stream from sensors (RPM, MAF, O2, steering angle) |
| Doctor orders an MRI/CT scan | **Actuator test** — commanding injectors, ABS valves to fire |
| Doctor writes a prescription | ECU **re-flashing / coding** with new calibration |
| Specialist doctor (cardiologist) | OEM-specific tools (e.g., **Techstream** for Toyota, **ISTA** for BMW) |

The **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** is the vehicle's way of "telling you it feels sick," but the actual *diagnosis* requires a **scan tool** plugged into the **OBD-II port** to communicate with the ECUs over the **CAN (Controller Area Network) bus**.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for ADAS Diagnostics

| Term | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit — a small computer inside the car (e.g., Engine ECU, ADAS ECU, Radar ECU) |
| **DTC** | Diagnostic Trouble Code — a 5-character alphanumeric code like `P0301` |
| **MIL** | Malfunction Indicator Lamp — the "Check Engine" light on the dashboard |
| **Freeze Frame** | Snapshot of all sensor values the instant a fault was logged |
| **PID** | Parameter ID — a request for a specific live data value |
| **UDS** | Unified Diagnostic Services — modern ISO-standard diagnostic protocol |
| **Flash / Re-flash** | Overwriting the firmware inside an ECU |
| **Scan Tool** | Hardware device + software that runs diagnostic requests |
| **CAN Bus** | Two-wire differential serial bus (CAN-H and CAN-L) connecting ECUs |
| **ADAS Calibration** | Precise optical/radar alignment of cameras, LiDAR, radar sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Coverage in ADAS** is *much deeper* than in powertrain systems because a sensor misalignment of even **0.1°** can shift a target's perceived position by **~1.7 m at 1 km distance** for a forward-facing camera. ADAS diagnostics therefore combine **functional fault codes** with **geometric / calibration diagnostics**.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Context

In the **2024 Scheme NEP-aligned syllabus** for PEAUT632, this topic supports the following Course Outcomes (COs):
- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Apply diagnostic and validation tools to assess the functional integrity of ADAS subsystems.*
- It is tested typically through **direct conceptual questions** (Part A — 3 marks) and **scenario / comparison questions** (Part B — 14 marks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Diagnostic Architecture Stack

Modern vehicle diagnostics operates as a **layered stack**, similar to the OSI networking model:

1. **Physical Layer** — Wired link (CAN-H / CAN-L twisted pair, or Ethernet 100BASE-T1 in newer ADAS ECUs).
2. **Data Link Layer** — Framing protocol (ISO 11898-1 CAN, FlexRay, or Automotive Ethernet).
3. **Transport / Network Layer** — ISO-TP (ISO 15765-2) for multi-frame messages larger than 8 bytes.
4. **Application Layer** — UDS services (ISO 14229) or OBD-II services (SAE J1979).
5. **Diagnostic Tester** — Off-board scan tool with manufacturer-specific software.

## 2.2 OBD-II Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) Structure

A standard OBD-II DTC is a **5-character alphanumeric identifier** following the format:

$$ \text{DTC} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{System letter}} \;\; \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Origin digit}} \;\; \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Fault group digit}} \;\; \underbrace{YY}_{\text{Fault-specific digits}} $$

Where:
- **First character (Letter)** identifies the vehicle system affected.
- **Second character (Digit)** distinguishes between generic (SAE) and manufacturer-specific.
- **Third character (Digit)** indicates the fault subsystem group.
- **Fourth & Fifth characters (Digits)** identify the specific fault.

| 1st Letter | System | 2nd Digit | Origin | 3rd Digit | Subsystem Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Powertrain (Engine, Transmission) | 0 | Generic (SAE) | 1, 2 | Fuel / Air metering |
| **B** | Body (Airbag, Lighting, Wipers) | 1 | Manufacturer-specific | 3 | Ignition / Misfire |
| **C** | Chassis (ABS, Steering, Suspension) | 2 | Generic (SAE) | 4 | Auxiliary emissions |
| **U** | Network (CAN, UDS, Communication) | 3 | Reserved | 5 | Cruise / Idle control |
| | | | | 6 | Computer / Output circuit |
| | | | | 7, 8 | Transmission |

**Reference standard:** **SAE J2012** defines the DTC format and the **P0xxx** family is the universal "lawyer-readable" set.

## 2.3 UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services) — ISO 14229

UDS is the **de-facto global standard** for off-board diagnostics. It defines services via 1-byte **Service IDs (SIDs)**. The most important UDS services:

| SID (Hex) | Service Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **0x10** | DiagnosticSessionControl | Switch between Default, Programming, Extended sessions |
| **0x11** | ECUReset | Hard or soft reset of ECU |
| **0x14** | ClearDiagnosticInformation | Erase DTC memory |
| **0x19** | ReadDTCInformation | Read stored / pending / permanent DTCs |
| **0x22** | ReadDataByIdentifier | Read a specific DID (Data Identifier) |
| **0x23** | ReadMemoryByAddress | Read raw memory address |
| **0x27** | SecurityAccess | Seed-and-key authentication |
| **0x2E** | WriteDataByIdentifier | Write to a DID (configuration) |
| **0x34** | RequestDownload | Initiate flash download |
| **0x36** | TransferData | Transmit flash data blocks |
| **0x37** | RequestTransferExit | Finalize flash |
| **0x3E** | TesterPresent | Keep-alive heartbeat |
| **0x85** | ControlDTCSetting | Enable / disable DTC logging |

**Positive response rule:** A successful UDS response increments the SID by **0x40**. Example: `0x10 → 0x50`, `0x19 → 0x59`, `0x22 → 0x62`.

## 2.4 SAE J1962 OBD-II Connector Pinout

The 16-pin trapezoidal connector — **legally standardized** by SAE J1962 — pin assignments:

| Pin | Signal | Pin | Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manufacturer discretionary | 9 | Manufacturer discretionary |
| 2 | J1850 Bus + (legacy) | 10 | J1850 Bus − (legacy) |
| 3 | Manufacturer discretionary | 11 | Manufacturer discretionary |
| 4 | Chassis Ground | 12 | Manufacturer discretionary |
| 5 | Signal Ground | 13 | Manufacturer discretionary |
| **6** | **CAN High (ISO 15765)** | 14 | CAN Low (ISO 15765) |
| 7 | K-Line (ISO 9141-2) | 15 | L-Line (ISO 9141-2) |
| 8 | Manufacturer discretionary | **16** | **Battery +12V (unswitched)** |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Quantum (CAN)** | $\text{tq} = \dfrac{(\text{BRP}+1)}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$ | seconds | CAN bit-timing setup |
| **Nominal Bit Time** | $T_{\text{bit}} = \text{tq}\cdot\left(S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}\right)$ | seconds | Period of 1 CAN bit |
| **CAN Bit Rate** | $R_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{bit}}}$ | bps | Determines bus throughput |
| **Sample Point (%)** | $\text{SP} = \dfrac{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}}{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typically 75–87.5% |
| **Propagation Delay Budget** | $t_{\text{prop,budget}} \le T_{\text{prop}}\cdot\text{tq}$ | seconds | Max bus cable delay |
| **ADAS Angular Error → Linear Error** | $d_{\text{lat}} = L \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{err}})$ | metres | Camera misalignment impact |
| **Diagnostic Cycle Time (MIL on)** | $t_{\text{MIL}} \le \text{2 driving cycles}$ (Type A) | cycles | OBD-II compliance |
| **DTC Status Byte Mask** | `bit7=testFailed`, `bit6=testNotCompleted`, `bit5=confirmedDTC` | bit-flag | DTC clearing logic |

**Standard CAN bus bit rates** for diagnostics: **125 kbps (CAN-FD legacy)**, **500 kbps (CAN Classic, OBD-II standard)**, **1 Mbps and above (CAN-FD for ADAS)**.

## 2.6 Why Diagnostic Tools Matter for ADAS

ADAS-equipped vehicles have **30–100+ ECUs**, including dedicated ECUs for:
- Front-facing camera
- 77 GHz radar
- Surround-view LiDAR
- Domain controller (autonomy ECU)

Each requires **functional diagnostics** (sensor health, calibration data validity) and **geometric calibration** (alignment to the vehicle reference axis). Tools like **CSC-Tool SE** (for radar), **Autel IA800** (for camera calibration), and **OEM factory tools** (e.g., Tesla Toolbox, Toyota Techstream) are indispensable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Decoding the DTC `P0301`

**Problem:** A customer reports an illuminated Check Engine Light. A scan tool retrieves DTC `P0301`. Diagnose the meaning.

**Step 1 — Split the DTC into characters.**

$$ \underbrace{P}_{\text{letter}} \;\; \underbrace{0}_{\text{origin}} \;\; \underbrace{3}_{\text{group}} \;\; \underbrace{01}_{\text{fault ID}} $$

**Step 2 — Interpret each character using SAE J2012 / J2012DA.**

The first character **`P`** identifies the system as **Powertrain** (engine, transmission, related emissions).

The second character **`0`** is the **origin digit**:

$$ Y_{2}=0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Generic (SAE-standardized) — readable on ANY OBD-II scan tool worldwide.} $$

The third character **`3`** identifies the **fault subsystem group**:

$$ Y_{3}=3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Ignition System OR Misfire (per SAE J2012).} $$

The fourth and fifth characters **`01`** identify the **specific fault within that group**. SAE reserves code `01` for "Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected."

**Step 3 — Assemble the final diagnosis.**

$$ \boxed{\text{P0301} \;\equiv\; \text{Powertrain, Generic, Ignition / Misfire Group, Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected}} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering implications and next diagnostic steps.**

- Inspect spark plug / coil on cylinder 1.
- Read **Freeze Frame** data: engine RPM, load, coolant temp at the moment the fault was set.
- Check for fuel injector fault (cross-counting of misfires via PID `0x0B`).
- For ADAS context: an unstable engine idle can corrupt camera frame timestamps — a *cascaded* fault.

> [!NOTE]
> **Manufacturer-specific** codes use a second digit of `1` (e.g., `P1336` is a Ford-specific code, while `P0301` is generic). This is a **common KTU exam question** — make sure to explain both origin digits `0` (generic) and `1` (manufacturer-specific).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Decoding `C1234`

**Problem:** A scan tool shows `C1234` on a 2022 sedan. Interpret.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Letter } C &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Chassis system (ABS, steering, suspension, brake-by-wire).} \\
\text{Origin } 1 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Manufacturer-specific (NOT generic).} \\
\text{Group } 2 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Per SAE J2012: "Brake system / Wheel speed sensor" subgroup.} \\
\text{Fault ID } 34 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Specific code — must reference OEM service manual.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ \boxed{\text{C1234} \;\equiv\; \text{Manufacturer-specific chassis / brake-system fault, code ID 34.}} $$

Because the **second digit is 1**, the precise definition is *not* in the public SAE list — it must be looked up in the OEM workshop manual. **This is a classic KTU Part A trap question.**

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — CAN Bit Timing Derivation

**Problem:** A CAN Classic bus uses a CAN controller clock of $f_{\text{CLK}} = 16 \text{ MHz}$ and a Baud Rate Prescaler of $\text{BRP} = 3$. The bit timing register is set to: $S_{\text{sync}}=1$, $T_{\text{prop}}=2$, $T_{\text{seg1}}=6$, $T_{\text{seg2}}=3$. Compute the bit rate and sample point.

**Step 1 — Compute the time quantum $\text{tq}$.**

$$
\text{tq} = \frac{(\text{BRP}+1)}{f_{\text{CLK}}} = \frac{(3+1)}{16\times 10^{6}} = \frac{4}{16\times 10^{6}} = 0.25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 250 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total nominal bit time $T_{\text{bit}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{bit}} &= \text{tq}\cdot\left(S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}\right) \\
&= 250 \text{ ns}\cdot\left(1+2+6+3\right) \\
&= 250 \text{ ns}\cdot 12 \\
&= 3\,000 \text{ ns} = 3\;\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bit rate.**

$$
R_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{bit}}} = \frac{1}{3\times 10^{-6}} \approx 333\,333 \text{ bps} \approx 333.33 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sample point percentage.**

$$
\text{SP} = \frac{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}}{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}} \times 100\% = \frac{1+2+6}{12}\times 100\% = 75\%
$$

$$ \boxed{\text{Bit rate } \approx 333.33 \text{ kbps},\quad \text{Sample point} = 75\%} $$

A 75% sample point is at the **lower edge of the CiA (CAN in Automation) recommended range (75–87.5%)**, suitable for slow/short buses. **Higher sample points (~87.5%) are preferred for long or noisy automotive harnesses** to allow more time for signal propagation before sampling.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — UDS Service Frame Walk-Through

**Problem:** A scan tool requests a UDS ReadDataByIdentifier for DID `0xF190` (Vehicle Identification Number). Decode the request and the positive response.

**Step 1 — Construct the request frame on CAN.**

$$
\text{Request: SID} = 0x22,\; \text{DID}_{\text{MSB}} = 0xF1,\; \text{DID}_{\text{LSB}} = 0x90
$$

CAN frame payload (little-endian DID): `22 90 F1` (3 bytes).

**Step 2 — Positive response SID.**

$$
\text{Response SID} = 0x22 + 0x40 = 0x62
$$

**Step 3 — Response frame payload.**

CAN frame: `62 90 F1` + VIN bytes (typically 17 ASCII chars). For a 17-character VIN, total payload = 20 bytes — **exceeds the 8-byte CAN Classic single frame limit**, so the response is sent using **ISO 15765-2 (ISO-TP) transport protocol** with First Frame (FF), Consecutive Frames (CF), and Flow Control (FC) handshaking.

**Step 4 — Engineering insight.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISO-TP First Frame (FF)} &: \text{PCI type}=0x1,\; \text{length field} = 20 \text{ (0x14)} \\
\text{Consecutive Frame (CF)} &: \text{PCI type}=0x2,\; \text{SN increments mod 16} \\
\text{Flow Control (FC)} &: \text{PCI type}=0x3,\; \text{BS, STmin}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ \boxed{\text{Modern UDS over CAN is always ISO-TP-segmented for payloads} > 7 \text{ bytes (CAN Classic)}.} $$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Layers vs. ADAS Fault Type

| Fault Type | Detection Layer | Tool Category | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera lens dirty | Driver / Surround ECU vision algorithm | OEM scan tool + camera self-test | Cleaning + recalibration |
| Radar sensor offset | Radar ECU + vehicle dynamics cross-check | Radar calibration target + scan tool | Geometric recalibration |
| Brake-by-wire latency | Chassis DTC `Cxxxx` | OEM-specific diagnostic tool | Firmware re-flash |
| CAN bus off (node dropped) | Network DTC `Uxxxx` | Scope + DBC file analyzer | Wiring / termination check |
| Software bug in ADAS stack | OEM engineering diagnostic suite | Engineering test bench (HIL) | OTA or wired re-flash |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End ADAS Diagnostic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Vehicle Arrives at Workshop] --> nodeB[Visual Inspection and MIL Status]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is MIL ON?}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD[Check ADAS Sensors Visually]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE[Connect Scan Tool to J1962 Port]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Read DTCs via OBD-II Service 03]
    nodeF --> nodeG[DTCs Found]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Read Freeze Frame via Service 02]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Read Live Data PIDs via Service 01]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Identify Fault Subsystem]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Powertrain Fault?}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeL[Generic OBD-II Scan Tool Sufficient]
    nodeK -- No --> nodeM{Chassis / ADAS Fault?}
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeN[OEM Specific Tool Required]
    nodeM -- No --> nodeO[Network Communication Fault]
    nodeO --> nodeP[CAN Bus Scope Analysis]
    nodeN --> nodeQ[Perform Actuator Test]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Geometric Recalibration of ADAS Sensors]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Clear DTCs via Service 04]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Test Drive Verification]
    nodeT --> nodeU[MIL Status Recheck]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Close Job and Document]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — UDS Diagnostic Session and Service Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[TesterPresent 0x3E] --> nodeS2[DiagnosticSessionControl 0x10]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[SecurityAccess 0x27 Seed]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Tester Computes Key]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[SecurityAccess 0x27 Key]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6{Authenticated?}
    nodeS6 -- No --> nodeS7[ECU Responds 0x7F Negative]
    nodeS6 -- Yes --> nodeS8[ReadDTCInformation 0x19]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[ReadDataByIdentifier 0x22]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[RequestDownload 0x34]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11[TransferData 0x36 Block N]
    nodeS11 --> nodeS12[More Blocks]
    nodeS12 -- Yes --> nodeS11
    nodeS12 -- No --> nodeS13[RequestTransferExit 0x37]
    nodeS13 --> nodeS14[ECUReset 0x11]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — OBD-II Connector and Signal Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeOBD[SAE J1962 OBD-II Connector Pin 16]
    nodeOBD -- Pin 16 +12V --> nodePWR[Permanent Battery Feed]
    nodeOBD -- Pin 4 Chassis GND --> nodeGND[Chassis Ground]
    nodeOBD -- Pin 5 Signal GND --> nodeSGND[Signal Ground]
    nodeOBD -- Pin 6 CAN High --> nodeCANH[CAN Bus High Wire]
    nodeOBD -- Pin 14 CAN Low --> nodeCANL[CAN Bus Low Wire]
    nodeCANH --> nodeECU1[Engine ECU]
    nodeCANL --> nodeECU1
    nodeCANH --> nodeECU2[ABS ECU]
    nodeCANL --> nodeECU2
    nodeCANH --> nodeECU3[ADAS Domain Controller]
    nodeCANL --> nodeECU3
    nodeECU1 --> nodeTERM1[120 Ohm Termination]
    nodeECU3 --> nodeTERM2[120 Ohm Termination]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Diagnostic Tool Software Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeUI[User Interface Layer Technician Console]
    nodeUI --> nodeAPP[Application Layer OBD II UDS Tester Logic]
    nodeAPP --> nodeAS[Application Services SAE J1979 ISO 14229]
    nodeAS --> nodeTP[Transport Layer ISO 15765 2 ISO TP]
    nodeTP --> nodeDLL[Data Link Layer CAN Classic or CAN FD]
    nodeDLL --> nodePHY[Physical Layer CAN High CAN Low Twisted Pair]
    nodePHY --> nodeHW[USB to CAN Hardware Interface]
    nodeHW --> nodeVEH[Vehicle In Vehicle Network]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams above are written with **alphanumeric node IDs** (nodeA, nodeOBD, etc.) and **plain uppercase alphanumeric labels** to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards. Each diagram is intended to be a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**, mapping interactions between the diagnostic tester, the vehicle, and the ECUs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between OBD-II and UDS diagnostic protocols. Mention the standard document number for UDS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | OBD-II | UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services) |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | SAE J1979 (application layer) + ISO 15765 (CAN transport) | **ISO 14229** (application layer) |
| **Purpose** | **Legally mandated** emissions and powertrain monitoring | **Manufacturer-defined** comprehensive ECU services (flash, coding, calibration) |
| **Mandatory** | Yes, by law on all petrol cars post-2001 | No, but de-facto OEM standard |
| **DTC codes** | P0xxx generic; P1xxx manufacturer-specific | Same; but services are far richer |
| **Scope** | Mostly powertrain | All ECUs (engine, ADAS, body, infotainment) |
| **Services** | Read DTCs, Live Data, Freeze Frame, Clear DTCs | 25+ services (flashing, security, I/O control, routine control) |

**Key exam-ready line:** *"OBD-II is the legally required 'lawyer-readable' subset; UDS is the engineering-grade superset."*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the structure of an OBD-II DTC. Identify what each character of the DTC `B1342` represents.**

**Model Answer:**

The 5-character OBD-II DTC follows the format `XYYYY` per **SAE J2012**.

Decoding `B1342`:

$$
\underbrace{B}_{\text{Body system}} \;\; \underbrace{1}_{\text{Manufacturer-specific}} \;\; \underbrace{3}_{\text{Group: SRS or Lighting subgroup}} \;\; \underbrace{42}_{\text{Specific fault code 42}}
$$

- **First character `B`** — Body system (airbags, lighting, central locking, HVAC).
- **Second character `1`** — **Manufacturer-specific** origin (NOT generic SAE — needs OEM manual).
- **Third character `3`** — Per SAE J2012, this digit (when in the body range) typically refers to an **SRS / Restraint / Lighting subgroup**.
- **Fourth & fifth `42`** — Specific fault ID assigned by the OEM.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a modern automotive diagnostic system. Briefly describe the role of each layer (Physical, Data Link, Transport, Application, Tester).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CAN Classic controller uses $f_{\text{CLK}} = 8 \text{ MHz}$, $\text{BRP} = 1$, with bit-timing segments $S_{\text{sync}}=1$, $T_{\text{prop}}=3$, $T_{\text{seg1}}=4$, $T_{\text{seg2}}=2$. Calculate the (i) bit rate and (ii) sample point percentage.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

The diagnostic system is organized in **5 functional layers**, mirroring the OSI model:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer**
- Wired medium: differential pair of CAN-H and CAN-L wires (twisted, 120 Ω characteristic impedance).
- Voltage levels: CAN-H ≈ 3.5 V (dominant), CAN-L ≈ 1.5 V (dominant); both ≈ 2.5 V (recessive).
- Connector: **SAE J1962** 16-pin trapezoid.

**Layer 2 — Data Link Layer**
- Implements **ISO 11898-1** (CAN Classic) framing.
- 11-bit (standard) or 29-bit (extended) CAN-ID, plus arbitration, ACK, CRC.
- Supports arbitration without corruption.

**Layer 3 — Transport Layer (Network)**
- **ISO 15765-2 (ISO-TP)** handles messages > 8 bytes via segmentation.
- Frame types: **Single Frame (SF), First Frame (FF), Consecutive Frame (CF), Flow Control (FC)**.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer**
- **OBD-II services** (SAE J1979) for legal diagnostics.
- **UDS services** (ISO 14229) for engineering diagnostics.

**Layer 5 — Diagnostic Tester / Tool Software**
- Off-board device (laptop + USB-to-CAN dongle + OEM software).
- Displays live data, DTCs, freeze frames; commands actuator tests; re-flashes firmware.

**Block diagram** — refer to the diagnostic tool software stack Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.4.

**Engineering significance:** Layering ensures **abstraction** — a UDS application developer doesn't need to know CAN bit timing; a CAN firmware engineer doesn't need to know UDS services.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:** $f_{\text{CLK}} = 8 \text{ MHz}$, $\text{BRP} = 1$, $S_{\text{sync}}=1$, $T_{\text{prop}}=3$, $T_{\text{seg1}}=4$, $T_{\text{seg2}}=2$.

**(i) Compute $\text{tq}$:**

$$
\text{tq} = \frac{(\text{BRP}+1)}{f_{\text{CLK}}} = \frac{(1+1)}{8\times 10^{6}} = \frac{2}{8\times 10^{6}} = 0.25\,\mu\text{s} = 250 \text{ ns}
$$

**Compute total nominal bit time $T_{\text{bit}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{bit}} &= \text{tq}\cdot\left(S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}\right) \\
&= 250 \text{ ns}\cdot(1+3+4+2) \\
&= 250 \text{ ns}\cdot 10 = 2\,500 \text{ ns} = 2.5\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute bit rate:**

$$
R_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{bit}}} = \frac{1}{2.5\times 10^{-6}} = 4\times 10^{5}\text{ bps} = 400\,\text{kbps}
$$

**(ii) Compute sample point percentage:**

$$
\text{SP} = \frac{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}}{S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}}}\times 100\% = \frac{1+3+4}{10}\times 100\% = 80\%
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{bit}} = 400\,\text{kbps},\qquad \text{Sample Point} = 80\%}
$$

A **80% sample point** sits comfortably inside the **CiA-recommended 75–87.5% window**, suitable for mid-length automotive CAN harnesses.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With respect to UDS (ISO 14229), explain the following services with their service IDs and a one-line purpose:
   (i) `0x10` DiagnosticSessionControl
   (ii) `0x19` ReadDTCInformation
   (iii) `0x27` SecurityAccess
   (iv) `0x34` RequestDownload
   (v) `0x3E` TesterPresent
   (vi) `0x22` ReadDataByIdentifier
   (vii) `0x14` ClearDiagnosticInformation

**(b) [7 Marks]** Decode the following DTCs: `P0171`, `C0561`, `U0100`. State the affected system, origin (generic vs. manufacturer-specific), and the subsystem group for each.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

| SID (Hex) | Service Name | Purpose (one-line) |
|---|---|---|
| **0x10** | **DiagnosticSessionControl** | Switches ECU between Default, Programming, Extended sessions |
| **0x19** | **ReadDTCInformation** | Retrieves stored, pending, or permanent DTCs from ECU memory |
| **0x27** | **SecurityAccess** | Performs seed-and-key authentication before secure operations |
| **0x34** | **RequestDownload** | Initiates firmware download into ECU flash memory |
| **0x3E** | **TesterPresent** | Keep-alive heartbeat sent by tester to maintain diagnostic session |
| **0x22** | **ReadDataByIdentifier** | Reads a specific Data Identifier (DID) — e.g., VIN, calibration version |
| **0x14** | **ClearDiagnosticInformation** | Erases DTC memory (often used after repair) |

**Positive response rule:** SID + 0x40 (e.g., 0x10 → 0x50, 0x19 → 0x59, 0x27 → 0x67).

**Negative response:** ECU returns `0x7F` + original SID + **NRC (Negative Response Code)**. Example NRCs:
- `0x12` — Sub-function not supported.
- `0x14` — Response too long.
- `0x22` — Conditions not correct (e.g., session mismatch).
- `0x33` — Security access required.
- `0x72` — General programming failure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Decoding `P0171`:**

$$
\underbrace{P}_{\text{Powertrain}} \;\; \underbrace{0}_{\text{Generic SAE}} \;\; \underbrace{1}_{\text{Fuel or air metering subgroup}} \;\; \underbrace{71}_{\text{System too lean Bank 1}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Powertrain, generic, fuel-metering group, **"System Too Lean (Bank 1)"** — typically caused by vacuum leak, weak fuel pump, dirty MAF sensor, or clogged injector.

---

**Decoding `C0561`:**

$$
\underbrace{C}_{\text{Chassis}} \;\; \underbrace{0}_{\text{Generic SAE}} \;\; \underbrace{5}_{\text{Cruise or idle control subgroup}} \;\; \underbrace{61}_{\text{Specific fault ID 61}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Chassis, generic, cruise/idle subgroup. Per SAE J2012, code `0561` corresponds to **"ABS Disabled Signal Message Counter Incorrect"** or cruise/brake-related fault — exact meaning is OEM-dependent but the *group and origin* are fixed.

---

**Decoding `U0100`:**

$$
\underbrace{U}_{\text{Network}} \;\; \underbrace{0}_{\text{Generic SAE}} \;\; \underbrace{1}_{\text{Communication subgroup}} \;\; \underbrace{00}_{\text{Lost communication with ECM or PCM A}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Network, generic, communication subgroup, **"Lost Communication With ECM/PCM 'A'"** — the ADAS domain controller has lost CAN frames from the Engine ECU for ≥ 5 seconds.

**ADAS implication:** Any `Uxxxx` DTC during an ADAS function engagement triggers **safe-state fallback** (limp-home mode); the vehicle may refuse Level 2+ autonomy until communication is restored.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the standard document number** (e.g., UDS = ISO 14229, OBD-II CAN = ISO 15765, DTC format = SAE J2012). Examiners reserve **at least 1 mark** for the standard reference.
> 2. **Mixing up DTC origin digits.** `0` = generic, `1` = manufacturer-specific. Reversing them is a **favourite trick question**.
> 3. **Skipping units** in CAN timing answers. Always write kbps, μs, ns, and %.
> 4. **Not stating the sample-point valid range** (75–87.5% per CiA). Examiners love this.
> 5. **Omitting the positive-response SID rule** in UDS answers — `SID + 0x40` is a high-yield 1-marker.
> 6. **Confusing K-Line (pin 7) with CAN-L (pin 14)** on the J1962 connector — pin-number recall is tested.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **OBD-II** = legally mandated, **SAE J1979** services, **J1962** connector, 16-pin trapezoidal.
- ✅ **UDS** = ISO 14229, ≥ 25 services, used for **re-flashing, coding, security, calibration**.
- ✅ **DTC format** = 5 characters per SAE J2012: **Letter + Origin (0 generic, 1 OEM) + Group + Specific**.
- ✅ **First-letter system map**: `P` (Powertrain), `B` (Body), `C` (Chassis), `U` (Network).
- ✅ **CAN bus bit rate formula**: $R_{\text{bit}} = f_{\text{CLK}}\;/\;\big[(\text{BRP}+1)\cdot(S_{\text{sync}}+T_{\text{prop}}+T_{\text{seg1}}+T_{\text{seg2}})\big]$.
- ✅ **Time quantum**: $\text{tq} = (\text{BRP}+1)/f_{\text{CLK}}$.
- ✅ **Sample point** must lie in **75–87.5%** per CiA recommendation.
- ✅ **J1962 critical pins**: **4** (Chassis GND), **5** (Signal GND), **6** (CAN-H), **14** (CAN-L), **16** (+12 V).
- ✅ **ISO 15765** = Diagnostics over CAN (DoCAN); **ISO 15765-2** = ISO-TP transport (handles > 8-byte payloads via FF/CF/FC).
- ✅ **UDS positive response rule**: response SID = request SID + **0x40**.
- ✅ **UDS negative response**: `0x7F` + request SID + NRC (e.g., `0x12`, `0x22`, `0x33`).
- ✅ **ADAS-specific diagnostics**: camera/radar/LiDAR **geometric calibration**, software version verification (DID `0xF1xx`), and **safe-state fallback triggers** when `Uxxxx` DTCs are active.
- ✅ **Freeze Frame** = snapshot of sensor values the moment a DTC was set — a high-yield diagnostic evidence.
- ✅ **OEM-specific tools** (Techstream, ISTA, ODIS, Volvo VIDA, FCA wiTECH) are required for non-generic (`x1xxx`) codes.
- ✅ **Generic OBD-II scan tool** is **NOT sufficient** for ADAS sensor recalibration or ECU re-flashing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Best Practices for Autonomous Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Best Practices for Autonomous Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autonomous Vehicle (AV) Maintenance** is a *holistic, multi-disciplinary engineering discipline* that encompasses the systematic inspection, calibration, software updating, hardware servicing, data-driven diagnostics, and continuous performance verification of an autonomous vehicle's perception, decision-making, actuation, and connectivity subsystems — carried out across the vehicle's entire operational life cycle to ensure **functional safety (ISO 26262 / SOTIF ISO 21448)**, **reliability**, **regulatory compliance**, and **operational readiness**.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, AV maintenance is classified into three engineering tiers:

| Maintenance Tier | Scope | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier-1: Operational Maintenance** | Cleaning, sensor wipe, fluid top-up, pre-trip inspection | Per shift / per km |
| **Tier-2: Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Calibration, firmware updates, mechanical servicing | Time/mileage based |
| **Tier-3: Predictive/Corrective Maintenance** | AI-driven fault prediction, root-cause analysis, overhaul | Condition/data based |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Hospital Patient" Analogy

Imagine an autonomous vehicle is a **patient in a high-tech hospital** who is allowed to walk around the city on their own, but is constantly wired to a remote medical monitoring system.

- The **LiDARs and cameras** are the patient's *eyes* — they get dirty (dust, mud, rain) and need cleaning just like spectacles.
- The **AI compute platform** is the *brain* — it needs regular "mental health check-ups" via software updates, much like a person needs learning and memory refresh.
- The **steering, brake, and throttle actuators** are the patient's *muscles and joints* — they wear out with use and need physiotherapy (lubrication, alignment, replacement).
- The **HD maps and V2X communications** are the patient's *memory of the world and ability to hear others* — both must be kept current.
- The **diagnostic telemetry stream** is the *heart-rate monitor and ECG* that doctors use to predict a heart attack *before* it happens. AVs use the same idea: predict sensor/actuator failure **before** it occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a hospital patient cannot rely on a single annual check-up, an AV cannot rely on periodic garage visits. It needs **continuous, real-time, data-driven, multi-tier maintenance** — this is the central philosophy of AV maintenance best practices.

## 1.3 Why Maintenance is Mission-Critical for AVs

A conventional car's brake failure is dangerous. A **Level 3+ AV's sensor miscalibration can be catastrophic** because the vehicle is *delegating human decision-making* to software. Hence, the **ASIL-D (Automotive Safety Integrity Level – highest)** classification of AV perception and actuation functions makes maintenance non-negotiable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** Maintenance protocols for AVs must comply with **ISO 26262** (functional safety), **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (Safety of the Intended Functionality), **UN-R157** (Automated Lane Keeping Systems), and **AIS-140 / AIS-189** standards applicable in India for connected and autonomous vehicles.

## 1.4 Primary Subsystems Requiring Maintenance

1. **Perception Stack:** LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GNSS/IMU
2. **Compute & Software Stack:** SoC, OS, perception/planning stack, ML models
3. **Actuation Stack:** Steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire, throttle-by-wire, gear actuators
4. **Connectivity Stack:** 4G/5G, V2X, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GNSS
5. **Energy Stack:** HV battery, BMS, thermal management
6. **Data Stack:** HD map database, fleet learning, event data recorder
7. **Cybersecurity Stack:** HSM, TPM, intrusion detection, OTA signing infrastructure

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of AV Maintenance Best Practice

Maintenance of an autonomous vehicle is governed by **five engineering pillars**. Each pillar addresses a distinct failure mode and is mandatory for a KTU 2024 scheme answer on this topic.

### Pillar 1 — Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance

- Time- or mileage-based interventions performed even when no fault is observed.
- Examples: every 10,000 km — re-calibrate LiDAR/camera extrinsics; every 25,000 km — replace brake-by-wire hydraulic fluid; quarterly — verify HD map currency.
- Goal: arrest **degradation** before it crosses a safety threshold.

### Pillar 2 — Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance

- Uses **live telemetry, machine learning, and remaining-useful-life (RUL) estimators** to forecast failures.
- Sensors monitored: motor current signature, brake pad thickness (via embedded sensor), LiDAR photodiode degradation, battery cell impedance drift.
- Model inputs: vibration FFT, temperature profile, current waveforms, CAN bus error frames.

### Pillar 3 — Corrective (Reactive) Maintenance

- Triggered *after* a fault is logged. Involves root-cause analysis (RCA), 8D report, fishbone diagram.
- Critical for software defects, sensor replacement, actuator overhaul.

### Pillar 4 — Software & Data Maintenance

- **OTA (Over-the-Air) updates** for OS, perception models, planning policies, HD map tiles.
- A/B deployment with shadow-mode validation.
- Rollback capability on failed updates.
- Data retention policy: typically **3–7 years** for EDR/event data.

### Pillar 5 — Cybersecurity Maintenance

- Continuous vulnerability scanning, intrusion detection, key rotation, OTA signing key custody.
- Patch latency target: **critical CVE < 72 hours**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### Table 2.1 — Sensor Maintenance Matrix (Critical for 14-Mark Answers)

| Sensor | Failure Mode | Inspection Frequency | Cleaning Method | Calibration Method | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LiDAR** | Window contamination, photodiode aging, beam misalignment | Daily visual + monthly quantitative | Microfibre + isopropyl alcohol | Extrinsic (lidar↔vehicle) on calibration rig | ±0.1° angular, ±2 cm at 50 m |
| **Camera** | Lens fouling, IR cut filter degradation, ISP drift | Daily clean, weekly focus check | Air-blow + optical wipe | Intrinsic + extrinsic checkerboard | ±0.05° angular |
| **Radar** | Radome delamination, phase noise | Monthly visual, quarterly RF test | Mild detergent | Boresight on anechoic chamber target | ±0.2° angular |
| **Ultrasonic** | Transducer fouling, diaphragm crack | Pre-trip tap test | Water rinse | Range check against known obstacle | ±2 cm at 2 m |
| **GNSS/IMU** | Multipath, gyro bias drift, lever-arm error | Quarterly accuracy audit | N/A (sealed) | RTK/PPK with surveyed base | ±2 cm position, ±0.05° heading |
| **Wheel Odometry** | Tire pressure, slip, scaling | Weekly TPMS check | N/A | Scale factor on known track | ±0.5% distance |

> [!NOTE]
> When writing the LiDAR angular tolerance, do **not** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables. Use `\vert` for absolute value, e.g., tolerance $= \vert \theta_{\text{meas}} - \theta_{\text{true}} \vert \le 0.1°$.

### Table 2.2 — Maintenance Metrics / KPIs (Formula Cheat Sheet)

| Metric | Formula (LaTeX-safe) | Engineering Meaning | Target / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{i}}{N}$ | Average uptime between failures | Higher is better |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{i}}{N}$ | Average downtime per failure | Lower is better |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational | $\ge 0.999$ for AV |
| **RUL (Remaining Useful Life)** | $\text{RUL}(t) = t_{\text{EOL}} - t$ | Predicted time/p cycles left | Used for predictive scheduling |
| **Sensor Health Index (SHI)** | $\text{SHI} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{meas}} - x_{\text{ref}} \vert}{x_{\text{ref, max}}}$ | Normalised 0–1 degradation score | $\ge 0.85$ operational |
| **Calibration Residual (e)** | $e = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^{2} + (\Delta y)^{2} + (\Delta \theta)^{2}}$ | Combined extrinsic error | $< 1$ cm, $< 0.1°$ |
| **OTA Patch Latency (T_p)** | $T_{p} = t_{\text{deployed}} - t_{\text{CVE disclosed}}$ | Time from vulnerability to fleet patch | $< 72$ h for critical |

Where $t_{i}$ = operating time before $i$-th failure, $r_{i}$ = repair time for $i$-th failure, $N$ = total number of failures in observation window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Availability equation $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$** is the single most-tested formula in maintenance-related questions. Memorise it and know what each term physically represents.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet Operators (e.g., robotaxi companies)** use these metrics to compute **cost-per-km**, **dispatch readiness**, and **insurance premiums**.
- **OEMs** use them as inputs to **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and to validate SOTIF arguments before regulatory homologation.
- **Regulators** use them to decide on type-approval conditions and post-deployment surveillance intervals.

## 2.4 Key Engineering Trade-offs (Often Asked in 14-Mark Answers)

1. **Over-Maintenance vs Safety:** Excessive PM increases downtime and cost but reduces fault probability. The optimum balances **availability** vs **maintenance cost**.
2. **OTA vs Recall:** A defect fixed by OTA is faster and cheaper than a physical recall. However, OTA introduces **rollback risk** if the patch is defective.
3. **Sensor Cleaning vs Sensor Wear:** Repeated mechanical wiping causes micro-scratches. Choose **coatings (hydrophobic, anti-soil)** to reduce cleaning frequency.
4. **HD Map Freshness vs Bandwidth:** Map diffs of a city can be 10s of MB/day. Optimise via **tiled differential updates**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Availability Equation

The Availability equation is derived from the fundamental definition of operational readiness.

### Step 1 — Define the Observation Window

Consider a system observed over a long time interval $T_{\text{obs}}$. During this window, the system alternates between two states:
- **Operational (UP)** for cumulative time $T_{\text{up}}$
- **Under Repair (DOWN)** for cumulative time $T_{\text{down}}$

$$T_{\text{obs}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Fractional Uptime

By definition, **Availability $A$** is the fraction of total time the system is in the operational state:

$$A = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{obs}}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce MTBF and MTTR

For a system that experiences $N$ failures, the average time between two consecutive failures is MTBF and the average repair duration is MTTR:

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{N}, \quad \text{MTTR} = \dfrac{T_{\text{down}}}{N}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Availability Expression

$$T_{\text{up}} = N \cdot \text{MTBF}, \quad T_{\text{down}} = N \cdot \text{MTTR}$$

Substituting into the equation for $A$:

$$A = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

The $N$ cancels:

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for system availability, applicable to AV compute, sensor, and actuation subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation assumes *renewal process* behaviour — failures are independent and identically distributed. For AVs with non-stationary degradation (e.g., battery aging), more complex models like the **Weibull hazard function** are used, but KTU examinations expect the MTBF/MTTR form.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing AV Fleet Availability

**Problem:** A robotaxi fleet logs the following over a quarter:

- Total operating hours: $T_{\text{up}} = 12{,}000$ h
- Total downtime for repairs: $T_{\text{down}} = 60$ h
- Number of unscheduled failures: $N = 15$

Compute the MTBF, MTTR, and Availability.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — MTBF:

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{N} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \text{ h}}{15} = 800 \text{ h}$$

Step 2 — MTTR:

$$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{T_{\text{down}}}{N} = \dfrac{60 \text{ h}}{15} = 4 \text{ h}$$

Step 3 — Availability:

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{800}{800 + 4} = \dfrac{800}{804} = 0.9950$$

i.e., **99.50% availability**. For a Level-4 robotaxi, the industry target is $A \ge 0.999$ (i.e., "three nines"), so this fleet falls short and requires a **predictive maintenance upgrade**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Sensor Health Index (SHI) Calculation

**Problem:** A forward-facing camera's measured lateral position error during a calibration audit is $x_{\text{meas}} = 4.2$ cm versus a reference $x_{\text{ref}} = 0$ cm. The maximum allowable deviation is $x_{\text{ref,max}} = 10$ cm. Compute the SHI and decide if re-calibration is needed.

**Solution:**

$$\text{SHI} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{meas}} - x_{\text{ref}} \vert}{x_{\text{ref,max}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert 4.2 - 0 \vert}{10} = 1 - 0.42 = 0.58$$

Since $\text{SHI} = 0.58 < 0.85$ (operational threshold), the camera **must be re-calibrated** before the next dispatch.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — RUL Prediction Using Linear Degradation

**Problem:** A brake pad wear sensor shows wear progressing as $w(t) = 0.05 t + 0.10$ mm, where $t$ is in thousands of km. The replacement threshold is $w_{\text{EOL}} = 2.0$ mm. Compute the RUL at the current odometer $t = 12{,}000$ km.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Current wear at $t = 12$ (i.e., 12,000 km):

$$w(12) = 0.05(12) + 0.10 = 0.60 + 0.10 = 0.70 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2 — Solve $w(t) = w_{\text{EOL}}$ for end-of-life $t_{\text{EOL}}$:

$$0.05 \, t_{\text{EOL}} + 0.10 = 2.0 \Rightarrow 0.05 \, t_{\text{EOL}} = 1.9 \Rightarrow t_{\text{EOL}} = 38 \text{ (thousand km)}$$

Step 3 — RUL:

$$\text{RUL} = t_{\text{EOL}} - t = 38 - 12 = 26 \text{ (thousand km)} = 26{,}000 \text{ km}$$

The brake pad has **26,000 km of life remaining**. Schedule a maintenance check at 25,000 km to allow a safety margin.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Maintenance Strategy

| Symptom Observed | Likely Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Gradual sensor accuracy drift | **Predictive** | Continuous degradation allows forecasting |
| Sudden firmware crash | **Corrective + OTA** | Software rollback needed |
| Daily soiling of camera lens | **Preventive (daily clean)** | High frequency, low cost |
| Random CAN bus error frames | **Diagnostic + Predictive** | Indicates intermittent fault |
| Battery cell voltage divergence | **Predictive (BMS-driven)** | Slow aging with warning signs |
| Crash event | **Corrective + Forensic** | Full inspection, SOTIF re-validation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — AV Maintenance Workflow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Vehicle in Service]) --> nodeB{Daily Pre-Trip Check}
    nodeB -- Pass --> nodeC[Dispatch into Autonomous Mode]
    nodeB -- Fail --> nodeD[Quarantine to Bay]
    nodeC --> nodeE{Telemetry Stream}
    nodeE -- Anomaly Detected --> nodeF[Predictive Engine: RUL Estimator]
    nodeF -- RUL less than threshold --> nodeG[Schedule Maintenance Slot]
    nodeF -- RUL greater than threshold --> nodeC
    nodeE -- Normal --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeH[Maintenance Bay: Diagnosis]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Root Cause}
    nodeI -- Software --> nodeJ[OTA Patch with Rollback Plan]
    nodeI -- Hardware --> nodeK[Sensor or Actuator Replacement]
    nodeI -- Calibration --> nodeL[Re-Calibration on Test Rig]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Shadow Mode Validation]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN{Pass Quality Gate}
    nodeN -- Yes --> nodeO[Re-certify and Return to Fleet]
    nodeN -- No --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeO --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AV Maintenance Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PerceptionLayer
        nodePer1[LiDAR Sensor]
        nodePer2[Camera Sensor]
        nodePer3[Radar Sensor]
        nodePer4[GNSS/IMU Sensor]
        nodePer5[Ultrasonic Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer
        nodeC1[On-board SoC]
        nodeC2[Perception Stack]
        nodeC3[Planning Stack]
        nodeC4[Control Stack]
    end
    subgraph ActuationLayer
        nodeAc1[Steer-by-Wire]
        nodeAc2[Brake-by-Wire]
        nodeAc3[Throttle-by-Wire]
    end
    subgraph ConnectivityLayer
        nodeCon1[V2X Modem]
        nodeCon2[5G/4G Link]
        nodeCon3[Cloud OTA Server]
    end
    subgraph MaintenanceLayer
        nodeMx1[Health Monitor]
        nodeMx2[Predictive Engine]
        nodeMx3[OTA Manager]
        nodeMx4[Digital Twin]
        nodeMx5[Calibration Rig Interface]
    end
    nodePer1 --> nodeC1
    nodePer2 --> nodeC1
    nodePer3 --> nodeC1
    nodePer4 --> nodeC1
    nodePer5 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3 --> nodeC4
    nodeC4 --> nodeAc1
    nodeC4 --> nodeAc2
    nodeC4 --> nodeAc3
    nodeCon2 --> nodeCon3
    nodeCon3 --> nodeMx3
    nodeMx1 --> nodeMx2
    nodeMx3 --> nodeC1
    nodeMx2 --> nodeMx4
    nodeMx5 --> nodeC1
    nodeCon1 --> nodeC1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Maintenance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1{Is fault continuous or intermittent?}
    nodeQ1 -- Continuous --> nodeQ2[Run Bench Diagnostic]
    nodeQ1 -- Intermittent --> nodeQ3[Increase Telemetry Sampling]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ4{Calibration Error more than 0.1 deg}
    nodeQ4 -- Yes --> nodeQ5[Schedule Re-calibration]
    nodeQ4 -- No --> nodeQ6[Schedule Component Replacement]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ7{Reproducible in 24 h}
    nodeQ7 -- Yes --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ7 -- No --> nodeQ8[Log as Anomaly, Continue Monitoring]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ9[Update Service Record and Re-dispatch]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ9
    nodeQ8 --> nodeQ9
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers above use **alphanumeric prefixes** (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodePer1`, `nodeMx3`) and labels are **plain uppercase text without markdown formatting** to ensure Mermaid compilation reliability.

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Calibration Rig Setup (Text-Based)

A typical AV sensor calibration rig consists of:

- **Optical Bench** (vibration-isolated granite table)
- **Target Boards** (checkerboard for camera, retro-reflective for LiDAR, corner reflectors for radar)
- **Surveyed Reference Markers** (Total Station positioned, sub-mm accuracy)
- **Vehicle Lift / Wheel Aligner** (for ground-truth pose)
- **GNSS Base Station** (for IMU lever-arm and heading reference)
- **Software Toolchain** (Kalibr, OpenCV, MATLAB Automated Driving Toolbox)

The vehicle is driven (or pushed) into a known pose. Each sensor's intrinsic and extrinsic parameters are estimated via **bundle adjustment** or **least-squares optimisation**, and residuals are recorded. A pass requires $e = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta \theta)^2}$ to be within tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

> Differentiate between **preventive** and **predictive** maintenance strategies in the context of autonomous vehicles. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive | Predictive |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Time or mileage | Live condition data |
| Basis | Schedule | ML/RUL model |
| Cost | Lower data cost, higher labour | Higher tooling cost, lower downtime |
| Example | Camera lens cleaning every shift | RUL-based LiDAR replacement forecast |

(3 marks: 1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark per valid example)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

> List **four** types of data that must be retained in an autonomous vehicle's event data recorder (EDR) to support post-incident maintenance and forensic analysis.

**Model Answer:**

1. Vehicle dynamic state — speed, acceleration, yaw rate, steering angle (1 mark)
2. Perception snapshot — detected object list, semantic segmentation at the time of incident (1 mark)
3. Planning and control decisions — selected trajectory, mode flag, fallback engagement (0.5 mark)
4. System health — sensor health indices, fault codes, last calibration timestamp (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the **five pillars of maintenance best practices** for autonomous vehicles, with specific examples of maintenance tasks for the perception stack. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A sensor in an AV has been observed to drift gradually. Derive the **availability equation** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ and compute the availability for a fleet with $\text{MTBF} = 720$ h and $\text{MTTR} = 8$ h. Comment on whether this meets the **three-nines (99.9%)** target for a Level-4 robotaxi. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Pillars of Maintenance Best Practices** (7 marks = ~1.4 marks per pillar):

1. **Preventive Maintenance** — Scheduled cleaning of camera lenses, monthly LiDAR window inspection, quarterly brake fluid replacement. (1.5)
2. **Predictive Maintenance** — Use ML on vibration/current signatures to forecast actuator failure. Use SHI thresholds. (1.5)
3. **Corrective Maintenance** — Root-cause analysis on fault codes, 8D report workflow. (1)
4. **Software/Data Maintenance** — OTA updates for perception models, HD map tile refresh, A/B shadow-mode validation. (1.5)
5. **Cybersecurity Maintenance** — Patch latency < 72 h, key rotation, intrusion detection. (1.5)

*(Total 7 marks distributed as above.)*

**(b) Derivation and Computation** (7 marks):

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$T_{\text{obs}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{obs}}} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$A = \dfrac{720}{720 + 8} = \dfrac{720}{728} = 0.9890 = 98.90\%$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

Since $A = 98.90\% < 99.90\%$, this fleet **does not meet** the Level-4 three-nines availability target. Recommendation: reduce MTTR by deploying **predictive maintenance** and **on-board spare diagnostics**, or extend MTBF via more robust sensor sourcing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Describe the **sensor maintenance matrix** for an autonomous vehicle's perception stack, with at least three examples including inspection frequency and calibration tolerance. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A brake pad wear follows the linear degradation model $w(t) = 0.04t + 0.20$ mm, where $t$ is in thousands of km. The end-of-life threshold is $w_{\text{EOL}} = 2.0$ mm. If the current odometer reading is $t = 18{,}000$ km, compute the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** and recommend a maintenance schedule. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sensor Maintenance Matrix — Explanation** (7 marks = ~1.5 marks per sensor type, with bonus for tabulation):

- **LiDAR:** Daily visual inspection of window; monthly quantitative check. Cleaning with isopropyl alcohol + microfibre. Calibration on a target rig with tolerance $\vert \Delta\theta\vert \le 0.1°$ and range error $\le 2$ cm at 50 m. (2)
- **Camera:** Daily clean with air-blow; weekly focus check. Intrinsic + extrinsic calibration using checkerboard with $\vert \Delta\theta\vert \le 0.05°$. (2)
- **Radar:** Monthly radome visual; quarterly RF test in anechoic chamber. Boresight tolerance $\le 0.2°$. (2)
- **Ultrasonic (bonus):** Pre-trip tap test; range check tolerance $\le 2$ cm at 2 m. (1)

*(Tabulating the answer earns the full 7 marks.)*

**(b) RUL Computation and Maintenance Schedule** (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Current wear** (1.5 marks):

$$w(18) = 0.04(18) + 0.20 = 0.72 + 0.20 = 0.92 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for end-of-life** (2 marks):

$$0.04 \, t_{\text{EOL}} + 0.20 = 2.0 \Rightarrow t_{\text{EOL}} = \dfrac{1.80}{0.04} = 45 \text{ (thousand km)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute RUL** (1.5 marks):

$$\text{RUL} = t_{\text{EOL}} - t = 45 - 18 = 27 \text{ (thousand km)} = 27{,}000 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Maintenance Schedule** (2 marks):

- **Inspection check** at 25,000 km (1,000 km margin) — non-intrusive wear measurement.
- **Replacement** scheduled at 27,000 km or earlier if any non-linear wear excursion is observed.
- **Trigger condition:** if measured wear exceeds predicted wear by more than 15%, escalate to corrective replacement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AV Maintenance Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating preventive and predictive maintenance** — Students often write them as synonyms. Preventive is *schedule-based*; predictive is *condition/ML-based*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in MTBF/MTTR computation** — Always state "MTBF = 800 h", not just "800". KTU evaluators check units strictly.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation of $A$** — Many students jump straight to the formula. A 14-mark question on availability typically allocates 3–4 marks specifically for the derivation.
> 4. **Ignoring cybersecurity and OTA** — A complete answer must cover *both* hardware and software maintenance. Pure mechanical answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Using markdown bold `**` or pipes `|` inside Mermaid labels or tables** — While not a content penalty, in technical writing questions this costs presentation marks.
> 6. **Failing to link maintenance to functional safety standards (ISO 26262 / SOTIF)** — Always cite the relevant standard in any maintenance strategy answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autonomous Vehicle Maintenance** is a *multi-tier* discipline covering **operational, preventive, predictive, corrective, and software/cyber** activities.
- The **five pillars** are: Preventive, Predictive, Corrective, Software/Data, Cybersecurity — *memorise all five for 14-mark answers.*
- The **MTBF/MTTR/Availability** triad is the single most-tested quantitative topic. Key formula:

$$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}, \quad \text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N}, \quad \text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum r_i}{N}$$

- **Sensor calibration tolerances to remember:** LiDAR $\le 0.1°$, Camera $\le 0.05°$, Radar $\le 0.2°$, Ultrasonic $\le 2$ cm at 2 m, GNSS $\le 2$ cm.
- **RUL computation** for linear degradation: $\text{RUL} = t_{\text{EOL}} - t$, where $t_{\text{EOL}}$ comes from $w(t) = w_{\text{EOL}}$.
- **Sensor Health Index (SHI)** uses an absolute-value form — write it as $\vert x_{\text{meas}} - x_{\text{ref}} \vert$, **never** with pipe symbols inside markdown tables (use `\vert` or `\mid`).
- **Standards to cite:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO 21448 SOTIF, UN-R157, AIS-140/189.
- **OTA updates** are preferred over physical recalls for software defects, but require shadow-mode validation and rollback capability.
- **Predictive maintenance** beats preventive maintenance on cost-per-km for AV fleets — it converts calendar-based interventions to *condition-based* ones.
- **Cybersecurity patch latency target** for critical CVEs is **< 72 hours** in the fleet.
- **Digital twin** of the vehicle can be used to *simulate* maintenance outcomes before any physical intervention.
- Always close a maintenance answer with a **standards citation and a measurable KPI** (MTBF/MTTR/A/SHI/RUL) — that is what examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Future Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT632 - AUTONOMOUS DRIVING AND ADAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Future Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Case Studies" in Autonomous Driving?

A **case study** in the context of autonomous driving refers to a detailed, real-world investigation of a specific autonomous vehicle (AV) deployment, an Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) rollout, a regulatory pilot project, or a notable accident/incident involving self-driving technology. The objective is to extract engineering lessons, performance metrics, sensor-stack insights, and societal impact from the field deployment of SAE Level 2 through Level 5 systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A case study in AV/ADAS is a *systematic engineering review* of an operational autonomous system — covering its **OEDR** (Object and Event Detection and Response), **operational design domain (ODD)**, **sensor fusion architecture**, **decision policy**, and **post-deployment performance data** (disengagement rate, miles per intervention, collision rate, etc.).

## 1.2 What are "Future Trends"?

**Future trends** denote the emerging technologies, regulatory frameworks, business models, and research directions that are expected to shape the next generation of autonomous driving over the 5–15 year horizon. These include **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication, **5G/6G connectivity**, **edge-cloud computing**, **AI-driven end-to-end planning**, **digital twins**, and **Level 4 robotaxi commercialization**.

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education** framework, this topic primarily maps to **CO4** (Analyze real-world autonomous systems and evaluate future technology trajectories) and touches **Bloom's Level L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine teaching a teenager to drive:
- A **case study** is like reviewing a *driving school report card* — what worked, what failed, where the student almost crashed at a roundabout.
- A **future trend** is like reading *automotive engineering magazines from 10 years in the future* — predicting electric powertrains, smart highways, and cars that talk to each other.
